topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Basics - Aspects of Network Security - Attacks - Different Types - Hackers - Crackers - Common Intrusion Techniques - Trojan Horse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Security Basics — The Foundation of Cyber Defense

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Computer Security* refers to the collective measures, policies, protocols, and tools implemented to protect the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA Triad)** of computer systems, networks, and the data processed within them against unauthorized access, modification, destruction, or disruption.

### The CIA Triad — The Holy Trinity of Information Security

| Pillar | Symbol | Definition | Real-World Analogy |
|--------|--------|------------|--------------------|
| **Confidentiality** | 🔒 | Ensures that data is accessible **only** to those authorized to view it. | A locked diary — only the owner holds the key. |
| **Integrity** | ✅ | Guarantees that data is **accurate, consistent, and unaltered** during storage or transit. | A sealed legal envelope — tampering is immediately visible. |
| **Availability** | 🌐 | Ensures that systems and data are **accessible when needed** by authorized users. | A 24/7 hospital ER — open whenever an emergency arises. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Beyond CIA — Extended Security Goals:**
> - **Authentication** — Verifying *who* you are.
> - **Authorization** — Defining *what* you are allowed to do.
> - **Non-Repudiation** — Ensuring a sender cannot deny having sent a message.
> - **Accountability** — Tracing actions back to a specific entity.

### 1.2 Aspects of Network Security

Network security is not a single product — it is a **layered, multi-dimensional discipline**. KTU 2024 Scheme identifies the following core aspects:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven Pillars of Network Security (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Physical Security** — Locked server rooms, biometric access.
> 2. **Perimeter Security** — Firewalls, DMZ (Demilitarized Zone).
> 3. **Network Security** — VPNs, IDS/IPS, segmentation.
> 4. **Application Security** — Secure coding, WAF (Web App Firewalls).
> 5. **Data Security** — Encryption (AES-256, RSA-2048), hashing (SHA-256).
> 6. **Endpoint Security** — Antivirus, EDR, host-based firewalls.
> 7. **User Security** — Awareness training, MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Security as a Castle

> [!NOTE]
> **🏰 The Castle Defense Analogy:**
> Imagine your network is a **medieval castle** protecting the kingdom's treasure (data).
> - **Walls & Moat** → Firewalls & Network Perimeter.
> - **Guards at Gate** → Authentication & Access Control.
> - **Watchtowers** → Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS).
> - **Treasure Vault** → Encryption & Data-at-Rest protection.
> - **Royal Decrees** → Security Policies.
> - **Scouts & Messengers** → VPNs securing communication.
>
> An attacker (the enemy army) must bypass **all** these layers — a concept known as **Defense in Depth**.

---

## 1.4 Hackers vs. Crackers — The Ethical Divide

| Attribute | **Hacker** (White Hat) | **Cracker** (Black Hat) | **Grey Hat** |
|-----------|-------------------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| **Intent** | Ethical, educational, defensive | Malicious, destructive, criminal | Mixed / exploratory |
| **Authorization** | Always authorized | Never authorized | Sometimes unauthorized |
| **Goal** | Find & report vulnerabilities | Steal, damage, extort | Report but demand reward |
| **Legal Status** | Legal | Illegal (IT Act 2000, India) | Legally ambiguous |
| **Example** | Penetration testers | Ransomware operators | Bug bounty hunters who overstep |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Other Categories of Threat Actors (KTU Syllabus Highlights):**
> - **Script Kiddies** — Use pre-built tools without understanding.
> - **Hacktivists** — Politically/socially motivated (e.g., Anonymous).
> - **Insider Threats** — Malicious employees with legitimate access.
> - **State-Sponsored Actors (APT)** — Advanced Persistent Threats backed by nations.
> - **Phreakers** — Attackers of telephone/PBX systems.

---

## 1.5 Trojan Horse — The Wolf in Sheep's Clothing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Trojan Horse** (or simply *Trojan*) is a type of **malicious software (malware)** that disguises itself as a legitimate, useful, or benign program to trick users into installing it. Once executed, it performs hidden malicious actions — such as creating backdoors, stealing data, or downloading additional payloads — without the user's knowledge.

### 🐴 The Greek Mythology Analogy

The ancient Greeks gifted a giant wooden horse to Troy, claiming it was a peace offering. Hidden inside were enemy soldiers who, at night, opened the gates and destroyed the city. Similarly, a Trojan **appears** to be a gift (free game, PDF, utility), but **carries a hidden payload** inside.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike viruses or worms, Trojans **do not self-replicate**. They rely entirely on **social engineering** to be installed by the victim.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Attacks — Formal Taxonomy & Classification

Cyber attacks can be classified along multiple axes. The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the following structured taxonomy:

### A. Based on **Attacker's Level of Activity**

| Type | Description | Detection Difficulty | Example |
|------|-------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Passive Attack** | Eavesdropping without altering data | Very Hard | Sniffing, Traffic Analysis |
| **Active Attack** | Modifies, fabricates, or disrupts data | Moderate (leaves traces) | MITM, DoS, Masquerade |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Passive Attacks violate **Confidentiality** only. Active attacks violate **Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability** — making them far more dangerous.**

### B. Based on **Network Location / OSI Layer**

| OSI Layer | Attack Type | Tool/Method |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Layer 1 (Physical) | Wiretapping, Tapping | Physical cable interception |
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | MAC Flooding, ARP Spoofing | `macof`, `arpspoof` |
| Layer 3 (Network) | IP Spoofing, Smurf Attack | `hping3`, ICMP flood |
| Layer 4 (Transport) | SYN Flood, Port Scanning | `nmap`, `LOIC` |
| Layer 7 (Application) | XSS, SQL Injection, Phishing | Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP |

### C. Common Intrusion Techniques (KTU Module 1 Focus)

#### 1. **Scanning / Reconnaissance**
- **Port Scanning** — Identifying open services using `nmap`.
- **Vulnerability Scanning** — Tools: Nessus, OpenVAS.
- **Network Mapping** — Tools: `traceroute`, Netdisco.

#### 2. **Social Engineering**
Manipulating **human psychology** rather than technical systems.

- **Phishing** — Fraudulent emails mimicking legitimate entities.
- **Spear Phishing** — Targeted phishing at specific individuals.
- **Vishing** — Voice-based phishing (phone calls).
- **Baiting** — Leaving infected USB drives in public places.
- **Pretexting** — Fabricating a scenario to extract information.

#### 3. **Password Attacks**
- **Brute Force** — Tries every possible combination.
- **Dictionary Attack** — Uses a wordlist of common passwords.
- **Credential Stuffing** — Reuses leaked credentials from other breaches.

#### 4. **Malware-Based Attacks**
- Virus, Worm, **Trojan**, Ransomware, Spyware, Rootkit, Adware.

#### 5. **Session Hijacking**
Stealing a valid session token (e.g., cookies) to impersonate a user.

#### 6. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)**
Attacker secretly relays/alters communication between two parties.

#### 7. **Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)**
Overwhelming a system with traffic to make it unavailable.

---

## 2.2 Trojan Horse — Detailed Anatomy

### Trojan Classification by Payload (KTU Highlight)

| Trojan Type | Hidden Function |
|-------------|-----------------|
| **Backdoor Trojan** | Creates remote access for attacker (e.g., Back Orifice) |
| **Banking Trojan** | Steals financial credentials (e.g., Zeus, Emotet) |
| **RAT (Remote Access Trojan)** | Full remote control of victim's machine |
| **Keylogger Trojan** | Records every keystroke |
| **Downloader Trojan** | Downloads additional malware post-infection |
| **Info-Stealer Trojan** | Exfiltrates documents, credentials, browser data |
| **DDoS Trojan** | Turns victim into a bot for DDoS attacks |

### Infection Vectors — How Trojans Enter

1. **Email Attachments** — Disguised as invoices, CVs, photos.
2. **Free Software / Cracked Apps** — Bundled with trojans.
3. **Malicious Websites / Drive-by Downloads**.
4. **USB / Removable Media**.
5. **Instant Messaging & Social Media Links**.

### Trojan Life Cycle (5 Stages)

```
Stage 1: Delivery → Stage 2: Installation → Stage 3: Concealment
                              ↓
        Stage 4: Execution (Payload) → Stage 5: Propagation / Persistence
```

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Security Metrics

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|------------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Entropy (Information Theory)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)$ | Measures uncertainty/predictability of data |
| **Password Strength (bits)** | $E = \log_2(R^L)$ | $R$ = character set size, $L$ = length |
| **Brute Force Time** | $T = \dfrac{R^L}{G}$ | $G$ = guesses/sec by attacker |
| **Avalanche Effect** | $\geq 50\%$ bit change for 1-bit input change | Cryptographic strength indicator |
| **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) | $\dfrac{\sum \text{Downtime}}{\text{Number of Incidents}}$ | Recovery metric |
| **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) | $\dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Reliability metric |
| **Risk** | $R = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Risk assessment foundation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used by SOC (Security Operations Center) analysts, CISSP-certified professionals, and in risk assessment frameworks like **NIST SP 800-30** and **ISO 27005**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Defense Mapping

| Attack Type | CIA Violated | Primary Defense | Recovery Strategy |
|-------------|--------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Sniffing (Passive) | Confidentiality | Encryption (TLS/SSL, VPN) | Certificate rotation |
| MITM (Active) | Confidentiality + Integrity | Mutual TLS, Certificate Pinning | Re-authentication |
| DoS / DDoS | Availability | Rate Limiting, Anycast, CDN | Failover, scrubbing centers |
| SQL Injection | Integrity + Confidentiality | Parameterized Queries, WAF | Patching, input validation |
| Phishing | Confidentiality | User Awareness Training, MFA | Password reset, breach notification |
| Trojan Horse | All three (CIA) | Antivirus, EDR, Least Privilege | Reimage system, credential rotation |
| Brute Force | Confidentiality | Account Lockout, CAPTCHA, MFA | Rate limiting, password policy |

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Password Entropy & Brute Force Time

### Step 1: Define Password Search Space

If a password uses a character set of size $R$ and has length $L$, the total number of possible passwords is:

$$
S = R^L
$$

**Example:** A password of length 8 using lowercase + digits ($R = 26 + 10 = 36$):

$$
S = 36^8 = 2{,}821{,}109{,}907{,}456 \approx 2.82 \times 10^{12}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Entropy in Bits

$$
H = \log_2(R^L) = L \cdot \log_2(R)
$$

For our example:

$$
H = 8 \times \log_2(36) = 8 \times 5.17 = 41.36 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3: Derive Average Brute-Force Time

Assuming an attacker guesses $G$ passwords per second, the average time to crack (assuming attacker tries half the search space on average):

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{R^L}{2G}
$$

**Engineering Example:** Modern GPU rigs achieve $G \approx 10^{11}$ guesses/sec on MD5 hashes.

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{36^8}{2 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{2.82 \times 10^{12}}{2 \times 10^{11}} \approx 14.1 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **divide by 2** for the average case. The **worst-case** is $\frac{R^L}{G}$, but the **expected value** is $\frac{R^L}{2G}$.

### Step 4: Compare Against Strong Password (12 chars, mixed set)

With $R = 94$ (all printable ASCII) and $L = 12$:

$$
S = 94^{12} \approx 4.75 \times 10^{23}
$$

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{4.75 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 10^{11}} \approx 2.37 \times 10^{12} \text{ seconds} \approx 75{,}200 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Increasing password length from 8 → 12 characters increased crack time from **seconds** to **millennia** — demonstrating the **exponential power of password length** over complexity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Shannon's Entropy for Attack Detection

When monitoring network traffic, anomaly detection systems use entropy to flag suspicious uniformity (e.g., encrypted traffic, or DDoS packets with identical payloads).

**Step 1: Compute source IP entropy**

$$
H(IP) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2(p_i)
$$

Where $p_i$ is the probability of observing source IP $i$.

**Step 2: Interpretation Thresholds**

$$
H_{\text{normal}} \approx 15 \text{ to } 20 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(typical enterprise)}
$$

$$
H_{\text{DDoS}} \approx 8 \text{ to } 12 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(botnet uniformity)}
$$

**Step 3: Detection Rule**

$$
\text{If } H(IP) < H_{\text{threshold}} \Rightarrow \text{Alert: Possible DDoS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use:** Tools like **Zeek (Bro IDS)** and **Splunk** dashboards use entropy-based detection in enterprise SOCs.

---

## 3.4 Trojan Detection — Heuristic Logic Tree

A signature-based antivirus engine uses the following simplified decision flow:

$$
\text{Decision}(x) =
\begin{cases}
\text{INFECTED}, & \text{if } \text{SignatureMatch}(x) = 1 \\
\text{SUSPICIOUS}, & \text{if } \text{Entropy}(x) > 7.0 \text{ and } \text{NoSignature}(x) = 1 \\
\text{CLEAN}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $\text{Entropy}(x) = -\sum_{b} p_b \log_2(p_b)$ over byte distribution of file $x$.
- High entropy (>7.0) suggests **packing/encryption** — a common Trojan obfuscation technique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why modern **heuristic + behavioral AV** (like Windows Defender, Kaspersky) outperforms pure signature-based detection for **zero-day Trojans**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a Hacker and a Cracker. List any two categories of threat actors.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Hacker** | **Cracker** |
|-----------|------------|-------------|
| Intent | Ethical / Defensive | Malicious / Offensive |
| Authorization | Always authorized | Never authorized |
| Legality | Legal | Illegal (IT Act 2000 §66) |

**Two categories of threat actors:**
1. **Insider Threat** — Employees with legitimate access who misuse privileges.
2. **Script Kiddie** — Unskilled attackers using pre-built exploitation tools.

*(3 marks — 1 for differentiation, 1 each for categories)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is a Trojan Horse? Why is it considered different from a virus or worm?

**Model Answer:**

A **Trojan Horse** is malicious software that disguises itself as a legitimate application to deceive users into installing it. Once activated, it performs hidden malicious actions such as opening backdoors, stealing data, or downloading additional payloads.

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | Trojan | Virus | Worm |
|---------|--------|-------|------|
| Self-Replication | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Needs User Action | ✅ Yes | Sometimes | ❌ No |
| Spreads via Network | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Disguise | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for differences, 1 for example like Back Orifice)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1 Comprehensive]**

**(a)** Explain the **CIA Triad** in detail. Discuss how each component can be compromised with a real-world attack example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify the **different types of network attacks** (passive vs active). Describe any **three common intrusion techniques** used by attackers with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Answer for Q.A(a): CIA Triad

**1. Confidentiality (2 marks)**
Confidentiality ensures that sensitive information is accessed **only by authorized parties**. Real-world compromise example: **Edward Snowden (2013)** leaked classified NSA documents — a catastrophic breach of confidentiality. Technical defenses include **AES-256 encryption**, **TLS 1.3**, and **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**.

**2. Integrity (2 marks)**
Integrity guarantees that data has **not been tampered with** during storage or transmission. Real-world compromise: **Stuxnet (2010)** — a worm that altered Iranian centrifuge PLC code, causing physical destruction while displaying normal readings. Defenses: **SHA-256 hashing**, **digital signatures (RSA-PSS)**, **HMAC**.

**3. Availability (2 marks)**
Availability ensures systems are **operational when needed**. Real-world compromise: **Dyn DNS DDoS Attack (Oct 2016)** using the **Mirai botnet** — took down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit across the US/EU. Defenses: **Anycast routing**, **CDNs (Cloudflare, Akamai)**, **rate limiting**, **redundant systems**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A robust security architecture must balance all three — over-emphasizing one (e.g., encryption without backups) compromises the others.

---

#### 📝 Model Answer for Q.A(b): Attack Classification & Intrusion Techniques

**Part 1 — Classification (3 marks)**

| Type | Description | Detects? | Example |
|------|-------------|----------|---------|
| **Passive** | Eavesdrops without altering data | Very hard | Wireshark packet capture |
| **Active** | Modifies/forges data | Leaves traces | ARP poisoning |

**Part 2 — Three Intrusion Techniques (4 marks)**

**1. Social Engineering (1.5 marks)**
Manipulating human psychology. Example: A fake IT helpdesk call asking for employee credentials. Defense: **Security awareness training, MFA**.

**2. Brute-Force Password Attack (1.5 marks)**
Systematically tries all password combinations. Modern GPU rigs test **$10^{11}$ MD5 hashes/sec**. Defense: **Account lockout, CAPTCHA, bcrypt hashing with high work factor**.

**3. Port Scanning / Reconnaissance (1 mark)**
Using `nmap` to identify open services and OS fingerprints. Defense: **Stealth firewall rules, port-knocking, intrusion prevention systems**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 1]**

**(a)** Define a **Trojan Horse**. Explain its **architecture, types, and infection lifecycle** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **various aspects of network security**. Explain the **Defense-in-Depth** strategy with a layered block diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Answer for Q.B(a): Trojan Horse

**1. Definition (1 mark)**
A Trojan Horse is a non-self-replicating malware that masquerades as legitimate software to trick users into installation and execution.

**2. Architecture (2 marks)**

```
+-----------------------------------+
|  Legitimate-Looking Wrapper       |  ← User-facing (e.g., game installer)
+-----------------------------------+
|  Dropper / Loader                 |  ← Extracts hidden payload
+-----------------------------------+
|  Encrypted / Packed Payload       |  ← Hidden malicious code
+-----------------------------------+
|  Persistence Mechanism            |  ← Registry keys, scheduled tasks
+-----------------------------------+
|  Command & Control (C2) Beacon    |  ← Communicates with attacker
+-----------------------------------+
```

**3. Types (2 marks)**
- **Backdoor Trojan** — e.g., Back Orifice, SubSeven
- **Banking Trojan** — e.g., Zeus, Emotet
- **RAT (Remote Access Trojan)** — e.g., DarkComet, NanoCore
- **Info-Stealer** — e.g., RedLine, Raccoon

**4. Infection Lifecycle (2 marks)**

**Stage 1 — Delivery:** Email attachment, fake software bundle, malicious ad.
**Stage 2 — Execution:** User double-clicks; dropper installs payload silently.
**Stage 3 — Persistence:** Adds registry entry (`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`).
**Stage 4 — C2 Communication:** Phone home via HTTPS, DNS tunneling, or Tor.
**Stage 5 — Action:** Keylogging, ransomware deployment, lateral movement.

---

#### 📝 Model Answer for Q.B(b): Network Security Aspects & Defense-in-Depth

**1. Aspects of Network Security (4 marks)**

| Aspect | Function |
|--------|----------|
| Physical Security | Restricts physical access to hardware |
| Perimeter Security | Firewalls, DMZ protect boundary |
| Network Security | VPNs, IDS/IPS, network segmentation |
| Endpoint Security | Antivirus, EDR on individual devices |
| Application Security | Secure SDLC, WAFs, code reviews |
| Data Security | AES encryption, hashing, DLP |
| User Security | Training, MFA, least privilege |

**2. Defense-in-Depth Strategy (3 marks)**

A multi-layered security approach where **no single layer's failure** compromises the entire system. Like an onion — the attacker must peel back **every** layer:

- **Layer 1:** Physical locks, CCTV, biometrics.
- **Layer 2:** Firewalls (e.g., pfSense, Palo Alto), DMZ.
- **Layer 3:** IDS/IPS (Snort, Suricata), 802.1X port authentication.
- **Layer 4:** Host-based firewalls, EDR, application whitelisting.
- **Layer 5:** Encryption (AES-256), tokenization, secure backups.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reference:** See **Section 4.7 Defense-in-Depth Block Diagram** above.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Resulting in Mark Deductions):**
> 1. **Confusing Hacker with Cracker** — Writing "all hackers are criminals" loses 1 mark. Always clarify the **ethical/legal distinction**.
> 2. **Listing Trojan as a virus** — Trojans are **NOT self-replicating**. This is a frequent error worth **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark questions** — KTU examiners **mandate** diagrams for full marks. A question on Trojan lifecycle or Defense-in-Depth without a diagram attracts **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Writing only theoretical content for attack questions** — Always pair each attack with: **(a) Real-world example, (b) Defense mechanism**. Generic answers lose marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the "Why" in CIA Triad** — Don't just define; mention **what is compromised** and **how**.
> 6. **Mixing up OSI layer attacks** — Layer 7 ≠ Layer 3. Be precise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Foundations**

### 🔐 Core Concepts
- ✅ **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (plus Authentication, Non-Repudiation).
- ✅ **Network Security Aspects** — 7 layers (Physical → User).
- ✅ **Attack Types** — Passive (eavesdropping) vs Active (modification/disruption).
- ✅ **Hackers vs Crackers** — Authorized vs Unauthorized; ethical vs malicious.

### 🧑‍💻 Threat Actors to Remember
- ✅ White Hat, Black Hat, Grey Hat, Script Kiddie, Hacktivist, Insider, APT.

### ⚔️ Common Intrusion Techniques
- ✅ Scanning, Social Engineering, Password Attacks, Malware, MITM, DoS/DDoS, Session Hijacking.

### 🐴 Trojan Horse Essentials
- ✅ **Definition** — Disguised malware, non-self-replicating.
- ✅ **Types** — Backdoor, Banking, RAT, Keylogger, Info-Stealer, DDoS.
- ✅ **Lifecycle** — Delivery → Installation → Concealment → Execution → Propagation.
- ✅ **Vs Virus/Worm** — No replication, needs user action, disguises itself.

### 📐 Key Formulas
- ✅ Entropy: $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$
- ✅ Password Search Space: $S = R^L$
- ✅ Brute Force Avg Time: $T = \dfrac{R^L}{2G}$
- ✅ Avalanche Effect: $\geq 50\%$ bit change

### 🎯 Exam Strategy
- ✅ Always pair **attack + defense** in answers.
- ✅ Include **at least one diagram** in 7-mark questions.
- ✅ Use **real-world examples** (Stuxnet, Mirai, Snowden) for higher marks.
- ✅ Memorize **3 Trojan types** and **3 intrusion techniques** with examples.

> **🎓 End of Module 1 Notes — Cyber Security (PEECT864)**
