topics.title: Virus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Virus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Virus**: A *computer virus* is a piece of **malicious software (malware)** that **self-replicates** by inserting its own code into other computer programs, data files, or system areas, and requires **explicit user action** (or a host program) to spread from one system to another. The virus remains **dormant** until a specific *trigger condition* is met, after which it activates its *payload* (destructive or disruptive routine).

According to Fred Cohen's classical 1986 formal definition, a virus is *"a program that can 'infect' other programs by modifying them to include a possibly evolved copy of itself."* The three mandatory properties that distinguish a virus from other malware are:

1. **Self-replication** – it copies itself into new hosts.
2. **Dependency on a host** – it cannot execute as a standalone .exe (a worm can).
3. **Trigger-based activation** – it waits for a logical event (date, file open, user login, etc.) before delivering the payload.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology Checklist** (Frequently asked in 2-mark definitions):
> - **Payload** → The malicious code that performs the damage (data deletion, encryption, backdoor opening, etc.).
> - **Trigger** → The logical condition (logic bomb) that activates the payload.
> - **Host** → The legitimate program, file, or boot sector that carries the viral code.
> - **Signature** → A unique byte-pattern used by antivirus software to identify a known virus.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **biological virus** like the flu:

| Biological Virus | Computer Virus |
|-----------------|----------------|
| Hijacks a living cell's machinery | Hijacks a legitimate program (`host.exe`) |
| Injects its RNA/DNA into the cell | Inserts its code into the host file |
| Cell produces more viruses | Host program now spreads the virus when run |
| Needs a sneeze/cough to spread (host action) | Needs you to open the file (user action) |
| Symptoms appear after incubation | Payload triggers only on a specific event |

> [!TIP]
> **Why a virus is NOT a worm:** A worm is like an airborne virus — it can fly across a network on its own. A computer virus is like a virus that needs you to shake hands with an infected person. **Worms are autonomous, viruses need a carrier.**

---

### 1.3 High-Yield Classification Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Virus Categories as per KTU Module-1 Syllabus:**
> - **Boot Sector Virus** – infects the Master Boot Record (MBR).
> - **File Infector Virus** – attaches to `.exe` or `.com` files.
> - **Macro Virus** – written in scripting languages of office apps (VBA).
> - **Multipartite Virus** – infects both boot sector and files.
> - **Stealth Virus** – hides from the OS by intercepting calls.
> - **Polymorphic Virus** – encrypts itself with a new key each replication.
> - **Metamorphic Virus** – rewrites its own code entirely (no fixed signature).
> - **Companion Virus** – creates a same-named file with `.com` extension (DOS-era).
> - **Email Virus** – spreads via mail attachments (e.g., ILOVEYOU).
> - **Worm** (often co-classified) – self-propagates over networks (Morris, Code Red).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Virus — The Three Logical Blocks

Every virus, irrespective of its type, is composed of **three logical blocks**. KTU examiners repeatedly test this.

1. **Infection Mechanism (Replication Module)**
   - The routine that locates new host files/disks and copies the viral code into them.
   - Uses system calls like `FindFirstFile` / `FindNextFile` (Windows) or `readdir` (Linux).
   - Decides the *infection strategy*: prepend, append, cavity, or entry-point obfuscation.

2. **Trigger (Logic Bomb / Activation Condition)**
   - Boolean expression evaluated on every host execution.
   - Examples: `if (system_date == "April 1") fire_payload();` (Chernobyl/CIH).
   - Counter-based triggers: "infect the 10th file then activate."

3. **Payload (Damage Routine)**
   - The actual malicious effect. May be **benign** (message popup) or **destructive** (MBR overwrite, file encryption for ransomware, data exfiltration).
   - Payload is what gives the virus its *threat rating*.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Infection Lifecycle

The textbook lifecycle has **4 phases** (must be drawn in every KTU answer):

1. **Dormant Phase** – Virus is idle, waiting for the trigger event. (Optional phase; some viruses skip it.)
2. **Propagation Phase** – Virus copies itself into host files/sectors. This is the *replication* stage.
3. **Triggering Phase** – The pre-defined condition is met; the activation routine runs.
4. **Execution Phase** – The payload fires → damage is done.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Core Metrics, Equations & Limits for Computer Viruses**

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Reproduction Number** $R_0$ | $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta}{\gamma}$ | Dimensionless; if $R_0 > 1$, the infection spreads. |
| 2 | **Vulnerable Hosts** $\dfrac{dS}{dt}$ | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S \cdot I$ | Hosts per unit time |
| 3 | **Infected Hosts** $\dfrac{dI}{dt}$ | $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I - \gamma \cdot I$ | Hosts per unit time |
| 4 | **Removed/Recovered** $\dfrac{dR}{dt}$ | $\dfrac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I$ | Hosts per unit time |
| 5 | **Epidemic Threshold** | $R_0 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \beta = \gamma$ | Critical equilibrium point |
| 6 | **Doubling Time** $T_d$ | $T_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\beta - \gamma}$ | Time units (sec/min/hr) |
| 7 | **Hash Signature Length** | $L_{sig} = n \;\text{bytes}$ (typically $n \in [16, 64]$) | Bytes |
| 8 | **Detection Rate** $P_d$ | $P_d = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ (True Positive Rate) | $0 \le P_d \le 1$ |
| 9 | **False Positive Rate** $P_{fp}$ | $P_{fp} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $0 \le P_{fp} \le 1$ |
| 10 | **Encryption Key Bits** | $k \in \{64, 128, 256\}$ for polymorphic engines | Bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The SIR (Susceptible–Infected–Removed) model is used by **CERT-In**, **CDAC** (India), and **Symantec Threat Intelligence Labs** to forecast malware outbreaks in enterprise networks. The epidemic threshold $R_0$ is the single most important number for any cyber-epidemiologist.

---

### 2.4 Why the Three Blocks Matter — Engineering View

- **Modularity** allows the virus writer to *upgrade* one block (e.g., a new payload) without rewriting the whole virus — this is why antivirus vendors must use **heuristic / behavior-based** detection, not just signatures.
- **Polymorphism** changes the *infection mechanism* block (different encryption key every time) but keeps the payload identical.
- **Metamorphism** rewrites the *entire* virus, including the infection and payload code, defeating signature scanners completely.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SIR Epidemic Model for Computer Viruses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Set (Classical Kulkarni / Bailey Model):**
> 1. Closed network of $N$ homogeneous hosts ($N = S + I + R$ is constant).
> 2. Each infected host makes $\beta$ contact attempts per unit time (infection *rate*).
> 3. Each infected host is removed (cleaned, patched, isolated) at rate $\gamma$.
> 4. The population is large enough that stochastic fluctuations are negligible → deterministic ODE model.

**Step 1 — Rate of change of Susceptible hosts.**
A susceptible host becomes infected when it makes *contact* with an infected one. The number of such contacts per unit time is proportional to the product $S \cdot I$. Therefore, the rate of *loss* in $S$ is $\beta S I$.

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \, S(t) \, I(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Rate of change of Infected hosts.**
Infected hosts *gain* one new member whenever a susceptible gets infected ($+\beta S I$). They *lose* a member at rate $\gamma$ when they are cleaned/removed. Hence:

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \, S(t) \, I(t) \;-\; \gamma \, I(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Rate of change of Removed hosts.**
Once cleaned, hosts either become immune (patched OS) or are quarantined. They do not re-enter $S$.

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \, I(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Population conservation.**
Adding the three equations, every term on the right cancels, confirming $N$ is invariant.

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} + \frac{dI}{dt} + \frac{dR}{dt} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = S(t) + I(t) + R(t)
$$

**Step 5 — Deriving the Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$.**
At the start of an outbreak ($t = 0$), $S \approx N$ and $I$ is very small. The infection grows only if $\dfrac{dI}{dt} > 0$:

$$
\beta \, S \, I - \gamma \, I > 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \beta S > \gamma
$$

Substituting $S = N$ at $t = 0$:

$$
R_0 \;=\; \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} \;\;\text{(with normalized population)}\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Decision Rule (Examiner Loves This):**
> - $R_0 < 1$: infection dies out → antivirus strategy is sufficient.
> - $R_0 = 1$: **endemic threshold** → critical boundary, patch immediately.
> - $R_0 > 1$: infection becomes an **epidemic** → network segmentation required.

**Step 6 — Doubling Time (early outbreak, $S \approx N$).**
For $R_0 > 1$ in the early phase, $I(t)$ grows exponentially:

$$
I(t) \;\approx\; I_0 \, e^{(\beta - \gamma) t}
$$

Setting $I(t) = 2 I_0$:

$$
2 I_0 = I_0 \, e^{(\beta - \gamma) T_d} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\beta - \gamma}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style)

> A campus LAN has $N = 1000$ hosts. A macro virus infects 1 host and propagates at $\beta = 0.0005$ per host·hr. The IT team disinfects at $\gamma = 0.2$ per hr. Determine if the virus becomes an epidemic and compute the doubling time.

**Solution:**

$$
R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} = \frac{0.0005 \times 1000}{0.2} = \frac{0.5}{0.2} = 2.5
$$

Since $R_0 = 2.5 > 1$, **the virus WILL become an epidemic**. Each infected host will, on average, infect 2.5 more before being removed.

$$
T_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\beta N - \gamma} = \frac{0.6931}{0.5 - 0.2} = \frac{0.6931}{0.3} \approx 2.31 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Conclusion:** The IT team must either reduce $\beta$ (network segmentation, email filters) or raise $\gamma$ (faster patching) such that $\beta N \le \gamma$ (i.e., disinfect every host within ~2 hours).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detection Techniques

| Technique | Principle | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Signature-based** | Byte-pattern matching against a database | Fast, low false-positive | Fails against polymorphic / zero-day |
| **Heuristic (Static)** | Code-structure analysis (e.g., entropy, packed sections) | Detects unknown viruses | Higher false-positives |
| **Behavior-based (Sandbox)** | Execute in VM, monitor syscalls | Catches metamorphics | Slow, evasion via sleep timers |
| **Integrity Checking** | Hash baseline of critical files | Detects any modification | Misses pre-existing infection |
| **Anomaly-based (ML)** | Statistical profile of normal behavior | Adaptive | Requires training data, costly FP |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often write "antivirus uses signatures only" — this is **outdated**. Modern products (Kaspersky, CrowdStrike, Defender ATP) use a **hybrid** of all five techniques.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logical Anatomy of a Computer Virus (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Virus Binary] --> B[Infection Mechanism Module]
    A --> C[Trigger Logic Bomb Module]
    A --> D[Payload Damage Module]

    B --> B1[Locate Host Files]
    B --> B2[Copy Viral Code]
    B --> B3[Restore Control to Host]

    C --> C1{Condition Met?}
    C1 -- No --> E[Stay Dormant]
    C1 -- Yes --> D
    E --> F[Host Program Runs Normally]

    D --> D1[Execute Malicious Action]
    D1 --> D2[Data Corruption]
    D1 --> D3[Backdoor Open]
    D1 --> D4[Ransomware Encryption]
    D1 --> D5[Information Theft]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#ffb300,color:#000000
```

---

### 4.2 Virus Lifecycle — State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant: Virus introduced
    Dormant --> Propagation: Host program executed
    Propagation --> Triggering: Infection routine copies code
    Triggering --> Dormant: Condition not yet met
    Triggering --> Execution: Trigger condition TRUE
    Execution --> Damage: Payload fires
    Damage --> [*]

    note right of Dormant: Virus idle, no activity
    note right of Propagation: Self-replication begins
    note right of Triggering: Logic bomb evaluation
    note right of Execution: Payload delivered
```

---

### 4.3 Virus Classification Hierarchy (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Computer Virus] --> V1[Boot Sector]
    V --> V2[File Infector]
    V --> V3[Macro]
    V --> V4[Stealth]
    V --> V5[Polymorphic]
    V --> V6[Metamorphic]
    V --> V7[Multipartite]
    V --> V8[Companion]

    V1 --> V1A[Infects MBR of HDD or floppy]
    V2 --> V2A[Appends or prepends to .EXE .COM]
    V3 --> V3A[VBA scripts in Office documents]
    V4 --> V4A[Hides from OS by hooking interrupts]
    V5 --> V5A[Encrypts body with rotating key]
    V6 --> V6A[Rewrites entire code via permutation]
    V7 --> V7A[Combines boot and file infection]
    V8 --> V8A[Creates same-name .COM to run first]

    style V fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style V5 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style V6 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.4 Propagation Flow in a Network (SIR-Inspired)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Patient Zero: Host A] -->|Email attachment| Q1[Host B]
    Q1 -->|File share| Q2[Host C]
    Q2 -->|USB drive| Q3[Host D]
    Q3 -->|Network share| Q4[Host E]

    AV[Antivirus Engine] -.->|Detects at E| Q4
    AV -.->|Quarantine and patch| Q3
    AV -.->|Heuristic flag| Q2

    style P fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style AV fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define a computer virus. List any four types of viruses with one example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A computer virus is a self-replicating malicious program that attaches itself to a legitimate host program or file and spreads when the host is executed, requiring user action to propagate.

Four types:
1. **Boot Sector Virus** – Example: *Brain Virus* (1986, infected floppy MBR).
2. **File Infector Virus** – Example: *CIH/Chernobyl* (1998, infected `.exe`).
3. **Macro Virus** – Example: *Melissa* (1999, MS Word VBA).
4. **Polymorphic Virus** – Example: *Storm Worm* (changes encryption key per infection).

---

**Q2. Differentiate between a virus and a worm. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|
| Host Required | **Yes**, attaches to a program/file | **No**, standalone executable |
| User Action | Needs user to open/run infected file | Propagates **autonomously** over network |
| Speed of Spread | Slow (depends on user behavior) | Very fast (exponential) |
| Payload | Usually yes (destructive) | Optional; primarily consumes bandwidth |
| Example | ILOVEYOU (2000) | Morris Worm (1988), Code Red (2001) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **anatomy of a computer virus** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each component in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The anatomy of a computer virus consists of three core modules:

1. **Infection Mechanism Module:**
   This is the *replication engine* of the virus. When the infected host program is executed, this module first takes control. It searches for *new, uninfected* host files or boot sectors using OS calls (e.g., `FindFirstFile` in Windows, `readdir` in Linux). It then injects a copy of the viral code into these targets using one of several strategies:
   - **Prepending** – viral code placed before the host's code.
   - **Appending** – viral code placed at the end, with a jump instruction at the entry point.
   - **Cavity Infection** – viral code inserted into unused gaps (e.g., PE section padding).
   - **Entry-Point Obfuscation** – modifies the host's entry point to point to the virus first.
   After injection, control is returned to the host so that the program appears to function normally — this stealth behavior is critical for survival.

2. **Trigger Module (Logic Bomb):**
   This module contains a *Boolean predicate* evaluated periodically. Types of triggers include:
   - **Date/Time trigger** – e.g., fires on a specific date (CIH activated on April 26).
   - **Counter trigger** – fires after the virus has replicated N times.
   - **Event trigger** – fires on a user action (e.g., opening an application).
   - **复合 Trigger** – combination of the above.
   The trigger module keeps the virus dormant and undetected until the condition is satisfied, which maximizes spread before detection.

3. **Payload Module:**
   This is the *malicious effect* that gives the virus its threat rating. Payloads range from benign (displaying a message) to highly destructive (deleting files, encrypting data for ransomware, opening a backdoor for remote control, or exfiltrating credentials). Modern Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) often use a *download-and-execute* payload that fetches additional malware from a Command-and-Control (C2) server.

> **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the visual block diagram (mandatory to score full marks — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if no diagram is drawn).

---

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **lifecycle of a computer virus**. List the four phases and explain what happens in each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The lifecycle of a computer virus consists of **four phases**, which must be drawn in sequence:

**Phase 1 – Dormant Phase:**
The virus is idle within the host system. It does not perform any malicious activity. Some viruses skip this phase entirely and proceed directly to propagation. During dormancy, the virus may still be present in the file system but is not active.

**Phase 2 – Propagation Phase:**
Triggered when the infected host file is executed. The virus's infection mechanism module takes control, locates new target files or boot sectors, and copies itself into them. Each newly infected file becomes a *new vector* for spread. This phase can repeat exponentially: a single infection can lead to hundreds in minutes.

**Phase 3 – Triggering Phase:**
After propagation, the trigger (logic bomb) module evaluates its condition. If the condition is *not* met, the virus returns to the dormant phase. If the condition *is* met, it proceeds to the execution phase.

**Phase 4 – Execution Phase:**
The payload module fires. The malicious action is performed — this is the moment the user (and antivirus software) notices the damage. Damage may include data deletion, system corruption, file encryption (ransomware), or backdoor installation.

> **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes (State Transition Diagram — mandatory to score full marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Drawing the lifecycle as a *circular* diagram instead of a *linear state-transition* — examiners expect a clear left-to-right or top-to-bottom state machine.
> 2. Forgetting to label the **Dormant phase** as *optional* — many students assume it is mandatory, which is technically incorrect.
> 3. Not providing a *real virus example* for each phase (e.g., CIH's April 26 trigger) — this costs 1–2 marks.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the **different types of computer viruses** with examples. Differentiate between **polymorphic** and **metamorphic** viruses in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Major Types of Viruses:**

1. **Boot Sector Virus** – Infects the Master Boot Record (MBR) of a hard disk or floppy. Loads into memory at boot time, before any antivirus. *Example: Brain (1986), Stoned.*
2. **File Infector Virus** – Attaches itself to executable files (`.exe`, `.com`, `.sys`). Either prepends, appends, or overwrites parts of the file. *Example: CIH/Chernobyl (1998), Jerusalem (1987).*
3. **Macro Virus** – Written in macro languages (VBA) embedded in Office documents (Word, Excel). Spreads via email attachments. *Example: Melissa (1999), ILOVEYOU (2000).*
4. **Multipartite Virus** – Hybrid that infects both boot sectors and executable files, making it harder to remove. *Example: Tequila (1991).*
5. **Stealth Virus** – Hides its presence by intercepting OS read calls and returning the *original* uninfected version of the file. *Example: Brain (uses stealth), Whale.*
6. **Polymorphic Virus** – Encrypts its body with a different key every time it replicates, and may also use different encryption algorithms. The only constant is the small *decryption stub*. *Example: Storm Worm, Marburg.*
7. **Metamorphic Virus** – Rewrites its *entire* code (no encryption) at every replication using code permutation, register swapping, and instruction substitution. There is *no constant byte sequence* between variants. *Example: Simile (2002), Zmist.*
8. **Companion Virus** – Creates a file with the same name as the host but a different extension (e.g., `file.com` paired with `file.exe`); the OS executes the `.com` first. *Example: Globe (DOS-era).*
9. **Email Virus** – Spreads via email; often uses social engineering. *Example: ILOVEYOU, MyDoom.*
10. **Worm** (often co-classified) – Self-propagates over networks *without* user intervention. *Example: Morris (1988), Code Red (2001), Blaster (2003), Conficker (2008).*

**Polymorphic vs Metamorphic — Detailed Comparison:**

| Feature | Polymorphic Virus | Metamorphic Virus |
|---|---|---|
| **Core technique** | **Encryption** of body with rotating key | **Code rewriting** (no encryption) |
| **Constant bytes** | Decryption stub remains same | **Zero** — every byte changes |
| **Code logic** | Identical payload, only encrypted form varies | Entire code restructured per copy |
| **Detection difficulty** | Hard (signature misses encrypted body) | **Very hard** — even heuristic struggles |
| **Size** | Body size roughly constant | Size may vary significantly |
| **Example** | Storm Worm | Simile, Zmist |

The fundamental difference: **polymorphism changes appearance but not logic**; **metamorphism changes both appearance and logic** with each replication.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **various virus detection and prevention techniques** in detail. How does a heuristic-based antivirus engine work? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Virus Detection Techniques:**

1. **Signature-Based Detection** – Maintains a database of known virus byte-patterns (signatures, typically 16–64 bytes long). Scans files and compares them. *Limitation: zero-day and polymorphic viruses evade this.*
2. **Heuristic-Based Detection** – Analyzes the *static structure* of code without executing it. Checks for suspicious patterns: entry-point modification, presence of encryption loops, unusual section entropy, missing section names, and use of `Format` or `Delete` system calls. Flags suspicious files for quarantine.
3. **Behavior-Based Detection (Sandboxing)** – Executes the suspect file in an *isolated virtual machine* and monitors its system calls, file modifications, registry changes, and network connections. Any anomalous behavior (e.g., mass file deletion, outbound C2 traffic) triggers an alert.
4. **Integrity Checking (Checksums)** – Computes cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-256) of critical system files and stores them in a secure baseline. Any change in the hash indicates tampering.
5. **Anomaly-Based / ML Detection** – Builds a statistical profile of *normal* program behavior (CPU usage, file access patterns, network calls). Uses machine learning (Random Forest, Deep Learning) to flag deviations. Used by modern EDRs like CrowdStrike and Microsoft Defender ATP.

**How a Heuristic Engine Works (Step-by-Step):**

1. **File is submitted for scanning** (on-access or on-demand).
2. **Disassembler** converts the binary into assembly instructions.
3. **Rule Engine** applies a set of pre-defined *heuristic rules*. Common rules include:
   - Rule 1: Program contains an encryption-decryption loop with a rotating key → suspicious (possible polymorphic).
   - Rule 2: Program modifies its own entry point → suspicious.
   - Rule 3: Program searches for `.exe` files and writes to them → suspicious.
   - Rule 4: Program calls `Format C:` or mass-deletion APIs → high-risk.
4. **Weighted Score** is computed; if it crosses a threshold (e.g., 70/100), the file is flagged.
5. **Quarantine / Sandbox Submission** — flagged file is moved to a sandbox for behavior-based analysis.
6. **Decision** — clean, suspicious, or malicious, communicated to the user and the central threat intelligence cloud.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Detection Section:**
> 1. Writing *only* about signature-based detection — outdated and incomplete. Modern AV is **multi-layered**.
> 2. Confusing *heuristic* (static) with *behavioral* (dynamic) — they are different.
> 3. Not explaining the *threshold* concept in heuristic engines — examiners expect it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 / Virus**

- **Definition:** A virus is a self-replicating program that needs a **host** and **user action** to spread.
- **Three Modules:** Infection Mechanism + Trigger (Logic Bomb) + Payload.
- **Four Phases:** Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution.
- **Polymorphic vs Metamorphic:** Polymorphic uses **encryption**; Metamorphic uses **code rewriting**.
- **Worm ≠ Virus:** Worms are **standalone** and self-propagate over networks; viruses need a host.
- **SIR Model:** $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$. If $R_0 > 1$, it is an **epidemic**.
- **Doubling Time:** $T_d = \ln 2 / (\beta - \gamma)$ during early outbreak.
- **Detection Methods:** Signature, Heuristic, Behavioral (Sandbox), Integrity Checksum, Anomaly/ML — modern AV uses **all five**.
- **Famous Examples to Remember:** Brain (boot sector), Melissa (macro), ILOVEYOU (email), CIH (trigger-based), Code Red (worm), Storm (polymorphic), Simile (metamorphic).
- **High-Yield 2-Mark Definitions:** Payload, Trigger, Host, Signature, Heuristic, Sandbox, MBR.
- **Always draw a diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions — failing to do so costs **at least 1 mark** per KTU valuation norms.
- **Mention at least one real-world virus** in every descriptive answer to score high on KTU's "Application" CO (CO2/CO3).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Worm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Worm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **computer worm** is a standalone, self-replicating, malicious software program (malware) that propagates itself across computer networks by exploiting security vulnerabilities in the target systems, without requiring human interaction or the attachment of itself to an existing host file. Unlike a virus, a worm is a complete, independent program capable of autonomous execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A worm is a self-contained malicious program that duplicates itself and spreads autonomously through computer networks, consuming bandwidth and system resources, often carrying a *payload* that can damage, steal, or encrypt data on the infected host.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **biological flu virus in a crowded, connected city**. A computer virus is like a flu that needs a person to *touch* a doorknob (a host file) to spread. A **worm**, on the other hand, is like a flu that has *sprouted tiny wings* — it flies from person to person (computer to computer) on its own, exploiting the *open windows* (network vulnerabilities) in every house it finds. You do not need to sneeze on a file; the worm simply walks through the network door by itself.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Payload** | The malicious code carried by the worm (e.g., backdoor, ransomware, data wiper) |
| **Propagation Vector** | The network method the worm uses to spread (SMTP, SMB, RPC, P2P) |
| **Vulnerability Exploit** | The specific software flaw the worm leverages to gain entry |
| **Botnet** | A network of worm-infected "zombie" machines controlled by an attacker |
| **Zero-Day Exploit** | A worm that targets a flaw unknown to the software vendor |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love to ask the difference between a *Virus* and a *Worm*. Memorize that a **worm does NOT need a host file** and **does NOT require user interaction** for propagation.

### 1.4 Core Operational Metrics

- **Bandwidth Saturation Threshold:** Worms can consume up to **100%** of available network bandwidth during a scan-based outbreak.
- **Infection Velocity:** Modern worms (e.g., Code Red) infected over **359,000 hosts in under 14 hours**.
- **Default Propagation Rate:** Scanning worms (e.g., Slammer) can probe **thousands of IPs per second**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Worm — Structural Breakdown

A typical worm consists of four logical components, each of which is a KTU exam favorite:

1. **Reconnaissance / Scanning Module**
   - Generates IP addresses or enumerates network neighbors to find vulnerable targets.
   - Strategies: *Random scan*, *sequential scan*, *local subnet preference*, *hit-list scan*, *topological scan*.

2. **Exploitation / Attack Module**
   - Carries the exploit code (e.g., buffer overflow, SQL injection, credential stuffing) targeting a known CVE.
   - Exploits vulnerabilities in operating systems, services (IIS, SMB, RPC), or applications.

3. **Replication / Payload Delivery Module**
   - Once the target is compromised, the worm transfers a copy of itself to the victim.
   - Often bundled with a secondary *payload* (e.g., DDoS agent, backdoor, rootkit).

4. **Command & Control (C2) Beacon**
   - Establishes communication with the attacker's C2 server using HTTP, IRC, or DNS tunneling.

### 2.2 Worm Propagation Models

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the **Simple Epidemic Model** and the **Two-Factor Model**.

#### 2.2.1 Simple Epidemic Model (SEM)

Models worm spread analogous to biological disease transmission in a closed population.

Let:
- $N$ = Total number of vulnerable hosts in the network
- $I(t)$ = Number of infected hosts at time $t$
- $S(t)$ = Number of susceptible (uninfected) hosts at time $t$
- $\beta$ = Pairwise infection rate constant
- $S(t) = N - I(t)$

The rate of new infections is governed by:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot S(t)$$

Substituting $S(t) = N - I(t)$:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot \big(N - I(t)\big)$$

The solution is a logistic growth curve:

$$I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + e^{-\beta N (t - t_0)}}$$

where $t_0$ is the inflection time when $I(t) = \dfrac{N}{2}$.

#### 2.2.2 Two-Factor Model (Improved)

The SEM ignores two real-world factors: (a) human countermeasures (patching, firewalling) and (b) network congestion slowing the worm. The **Two-Factor Model** (introduced by Zou, Gong, Towsley) modifies SEM with:

- A time-varying infection rate $\beta(t)$ that decreases as patches are applied.
- A countermeasure removal rate $R(t)$ representing disinfected hosts.

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta(t) \cdot I(t) \cdot S(t) - R(t)$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Meaning |
|--------------------|------------|---------------------|
| Worm Infection Rate (SEM) | $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$ | $\beta$: pair rate, $N$: population |
| Logistic Solution | $I(t) = \dfrac{N}{1 + e^{-\beta N(t - t_0)}}$ | $t_0$: half-infection time |
| Basic Reproduction Number | $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\gamma}$ | Avg. hosts one worm infects before death/disinfection |
| Epidemic Threshold | $R_0 > 1$ | Worm *will* spread |
| Epidemic Suppression | $R_0 \le 1$ | Worm *dies out* |
| Scanning Rate (Code Red) | $\approx 6.0$ scans/second | Random 32-bit IP space |
| Slammer Scan Rate | $\approx 4000$ packets/second | UDP/1434 exploit |
| Cohen's Epidemic Threshold | $T = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Critical scan-rate fraction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** The condition $R_0 > 1$ means the worm outbreak is *epidemic*. If $R_0 \le 1$, the worm will die out naturally. This is mathematically identical to the SIR model from epidemiology.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Defense Engineering:** Worms (ethically) are used in *penetration testing* to assess organizational resilience.
- **IDS/IPS Tuning:** Studying worm signatures trains anomaly-detection systems (e.g., Snort, Suricata).
- **Cyber Insurance & Risk Modeling:** Insurers use epidemic models to estimate downtime loss.
- **Critical Infrastructure Protection:** Stuxnet (2010), a state-sponsored worm, demonstrated the kinetic-cyber threat to SCADA systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Simple Epidemic Model Solution

**Step 1 — State the Differential Equation.**

The infection rate is proportional to the product of infected and susceptible hosts:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot S(t)$$

**Step 2 — Express Susceptible Population.**

Since total population is constant (no births/deaths) for a closed network:

$$S(t) = N - I(t)$$

Substituting into Step 1:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot \big(N - I(t)\big)$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables.**

$$\frac{dI}{I(N - I)} = \beta \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Partial Fraction Decomposition.**

$$\frac{1}{I(N - I)} = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I} \right]$$

*Reasoning:* Using the identity $\dfrac{1}{X(N-X)} = \dfrac{1}{N}\left(\dfrac{1}{X} + \dfrac{1}{N-X}\right)$, which is algebraically verifiable.

**Step 5 — Integrate Both Sides.**

$$\frac{1}{N} \int \left( \frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I} \right) dI = \beta \int dt$$

$$\frac{1}{N} \big[ \ln \vert I \vert - \ln \vert N - I \vert \big] = \beta t + C$$

$$\frac{1}{N} \ln \left( \frac{I}{N - I} \right) = \beta t + C$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate Both Sides.**

$$\frac{I}{N - I} = e^{N(\beta t + C)} = A \cdot e^{N \beta t}$$

where $A = e^{NC}$ is the integration constant.

**Step 7 — Solve for $I(t)$.**

$$I = A e^{N \beta t} (N - I)$$

$$I = A N e^{N \beta t} - A e^{N \beta t} I$$

$$I (1 + A e^{N \beta t}) = A N e^{N \beta t}$$

$$I(t) = \frac{A N e^{N \beta t}}{1 + A e^{N \beta t}} = \frac{N}{1 + \frac{1}{A} e^{-N \beta t}}$$

**Step 8 — Apply Initial Condition at $t = t_0$ (where $I = N/2$).**

At $t = t_0$, $I(t_0) = N/2$:

$$\frac{N}{2} = \frac{N}{1 + \frac{1}{A} e^{-N \beta t_0}}$$

$$2 = 1 + \frac{1}{A} e^{-N \beta t_0} \implies \frac{1}{A} = e^{N \beta t_0}$$

**Step 9 — Final Logistic Solution.**

$$\boxed{I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + e^{-N \beta (t - t_0)}}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worm vs. Virus vs. Trojan

| Attribute | Worm | Virus | Trojan Horse |
|-----------|------|-------|--------------|
| Needs Host File? | **No** | **Yes** | Yes (masquerades as legitimate) |
| User Interaction Required? | **No** | Yes (often) | Yes (to install) |
| Self-Replicating? | **Yes** | Yes | **No** |
| Primary Vector | **Network vulnerabilities** | Files, email attachments | Social engineering |
| Carrier File Required? | No | **Yes** (.exe, .doc) | Yes |
| Propagation Speed | **Very fast** (minutes to hours) | Slow (requires user action) | Slow (requires user trust) |
| Example | Morris, Slammer, Code Red | ILOVEYOU, Melissa | Zeus, Emotet |
| Typical Payload | DDoS, Backdoor, Ransomware | File corruption, data theft | Backdoor, keylogger |

### 3.3 Classification of Worms (Analytical Matrix)

| Class | Mechanism | Example |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| **Email Worm** | Propagates via email address books; sends itself as attachment | ILOVEYOU (2000) |
| **Internet / Scanning Worm** | Randomly scans IP space for vulnerable services | Code Red (2001) |
| **File-Sharing Worm** | Spreads via P2P networks (Kazaa, LimeWire) | Benjamin (1999) |
| **Instant Messaging Worm** | Spreads through IM protocols (MSN, IRC) | Chatoshi |
| **Mobile Worm** | Propagates via Bluetooth, SMS, MMS | Cabir (2004) |
| **Fileless / Memory Worm** | Resides in RAM, no filesystem footprint | Poweliks |
| **Polymorphic Worm** | Encrypts/rewrites its own code per generation | Zmist |
| **Metamorphic Worm** | Restructures its entire code at each infection | Simile |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Worm Lifecycle — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Worm Activated on Host H0] --> B[Reconnaissance Module]
    B --> C{Vulnerable Target Found?}
    C -- No --> D[Rescan New IP Range]
    D --> B
    C -- Yes --> E[Exploitation Module]
    E --> F[Gain Remote Shell or Privilege]
    F --> G[Payload Transfer to Target]
    G --> H[Execute Worm on New Host H1]
    H --> I[Activate Secondary Payload]
    I --> J{Command & Control Beacon}
    J -- Yes --> K[Report to Attacker C2 Server]
    J -- No --> L[Trigger Local Damage e.g. DDoS, Ransomware]
    K --> M[Continue Propagation Loop]
    L --> M
    M --> B
```

### 4.2 Worm Propagation Phases — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase1[Phase 1: Slow Start]
        P1A[Few Initial Infections] --> P1B[Localized Spread]
    end
    subgraph Phase2[Phase 2: Exponential Growth]
        P2A[Logistic Curve Region] --> P2B[Bandwidth Saturation]
    end
    subgraph Phase3[Phase 3: Saturation]
        P3A[Most Vulnerable Hosts Compromised] --> P3B[Defenses Activated]
    end
    subgraph Phase4[Phase 4: Slowdown]
        P4A[Remaining Hosts Patched] --> P4B[Outbreak Quelled]
    end
    Phase1 --> Phase2
    Phase2 --> Phase3
    Phase3 --> Phase4
```

### 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Architecture Against Worms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Perimeter] --> B[Firewalls: Block Unused Ports]
    B --> C[IDS/IPS: Signature and Anomaly Detection]
    C --> D[Network Segmentation: VLANs and Zero Trust]
    D --> E[Endpoint Protection: EDR and Anti-Malware]
    E --> F[Patch Management: Timely CVE Remediation]
    F --> G[Application Whitelisting]
    G --> H[User Awareness Training]
    H --> I[Backup and Disaster Recovery]
    I --> J[Incident Response Plan]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]  
*Define a computer worm. How is it different from a computer virus?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **computer worm** is a standalone, self-replicating malicious program that propagates across computer networks by exploiting security vulnerabilities, **without requiring user intervention** and **without attaching itself to a host file**.

**Differences from a virus (2 marks):**
1. A worm is an **independent executable program**, whereas a virus is a **code fragment** that requires a host file (executable, document, macro) to attach itself to.
2. A worm propagates **autonomously over the network**, while a virus typically requires **user action** (opening a file, executing a macro) to spread.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark, Differences = 2 marks (1 mark each).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]  
*List any three types of worms and give one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Example |
|------|---------|
| 1. **Email Worm** — spreads via email address harvesting | ILOVEYOU |
| 2. **Internet/Scanning Worm** — exploits network service vulnerabilities | Code Red |
| 3. **File-Sharing Worm** — uses P2P networks for distribution | Benjamin |

*(1 mark per correct pair; any three valid types accepted.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Simple Epidemic Model (SEM)** for worm propagation. Derive its logistic growth equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Two-Factor Model** and explain how it improves upon the basic epidemic model. Mention the role of $R_0$ (basic reproduction number) in determining whether a worm will cause an outbreak. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Simple Epidemic Model (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**

The SEM is derived from classical epidemiology. It treats worm propagation as analogous to disease spread in a closed, fully susceptible population.

- $N$ = total vulnerable hosts in the network
- $I(t)$ = number of infected hosts at time $t$
- $S(t) = N - I(t)$ = number of susceptible hosts
- $\beta$ = pairwise infection rate per unit time

**Step 2 — The Differential Equation (2 marks):**

The rate of new infections is proportional to encounters between infected and susceptible hosts:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot S(t) = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot \big(N - I(t)\big)$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of the Logistic Solution (3 marks):**

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dI}{I(N - I)} = \beta \, dt$$

Applying partial fractions:

$$\frac{1}{N} \left( \frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I} \right) dI = \beta \, dt$$

Integrating:

$$\frac{1}{N} \ln \left( \frac{I}{N - I} \right) = \beta t + C$$

Solving for $I(t)$ and applying initial condition $I(t_0) = N/2$:

$$\boxed{I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + e^{-N \beta (t - t_0)}}}$$

This is the **logistic (S-shaped) curve** describing the worm's infection trajectory.

---

#### Part (b) — Two-Factor Model and $R_0$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Limitations of SEM (2 marks):**

SEM assumes:
- Constant infection rate $\beta$ (ignores patching)
- No removal/disinfection of infected hosts
- Infinite bandwidth (ignores network congestion)

In reality, defenders patch systems and firewalls block scans, so $\beta$ *decreases* over time.

**Step 2 — Two-Factor Model Equation (2 marks):**

The Two-Factor Model (Zou et al., 2002) introduces a time-varying $\beta(t)$ and a removal rate $R(t)$:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta(t) \cdot I(t) \cdot S(t) - R(t)$$

- $\beta(t)$ decreases as countermeasures are deployed
- $R(t)$ accounts for disinfected, patched, or quarantined hosts
- This model captures the *slowdown phase* of real outbreaks (e.g., Code Red, Nimda)

**Step 3 — The Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$ (3 marks):**

The basic reproduction number is the average number of secondary infections caused by one infected host in a fully susceptible population:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$$

where $\gamma$ is the removal/disinfection rate.

**Decision rule:**
- If $R_0 > 1$ — the worm causes an **epidemic outbreak**
- If $R_0 \le 1$ — the worm **dies out** without intervention

This is mathematically equivalent to the classical epidemiological threshold theorem. Defenders aim to push $R_0 \le 1$ by raising $\gamma$ (faster patching/disinfection) or lowering $\beta$ (firewalls, IDS, segmentation).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]

**(a)** Describe the **architecture of a typical network worm**, listing its main components. Explain each component's function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **lifecycle of a scanning worm** from initial infection to secondary payload execution. Discuss two real-world examples: **Code Red** and **Slammer**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Worm Architecture (7 Marks)

A network worm has **four main components**:

1. **Reconnaissance / Scanning Module (2 marks):**
   - Generates a list of candidate victim IP addresses.
   - Strategies: *random scan* (Code Red), *sequential scan*, *local-preference scan* (Nimda), *hit-list scan*, *topological scan* (uses information from infected host).
   - Function: Maximize spread by efficiently discovering new vulnerable targets.

2. **Exploitation / Attack Module (2 marks):**
   - Carries the **exploit code** for a specific vulnerability (e.g., CVE-2001-0500 buffer overflow in IIS for Code Red).
   - Gains unauthorized access via buffer overflows, RPC vulnerabilities, weak credentials, or default passwords.

3. **Replication / Payload Module (2 marks):**
   - Transfers a copy of the worm to the compromised host.
   - Often bundles a **secondary payload** — a backdoor, DDoS agent, rootkit, or ransomware.

4. **Command & Control (C2) Beacon (1 mark):**
   - Establishes an outbound channel to the attacker's C2 server using HTTP, IRC, DNS, or Tor.
   - Receives updated instructions, exfiltrates data, or coordinates DDoS attacks.

#### Part (b) — Lifecycle and Real-World Examples (7 Marks)

**Lifecycle of a Scanning Worm (5 marks):**

1. **Initial Seeding:** Attacker infects one or a few hosts manually.
2. **Scanning Phase:** Worm generates random / targeted IP addresses and probes for vulnerable services.
3. **Exploitation Phase:** Once a vulnerable host is found, the worm sends the exploit payload (e.g., a buffer overflow string).
4. **Compromise & Transfer:** The worm copies itself to the victim and begins execution.
5. **Reactivation:** The newly infected host re-enters the scanning phase, leading to **exponential propagation**.

**Real-World Examples (2 marks):**

- **Code Red (2001):** Exploited **IIS Index Server buffer overflow** (CVE-2001-0500). Used a *random 32-bit IP scan*. In **14 hours**, it infected **~359,000 hosts**. Carried a payload that defaced websites and launched a DDoS attack on www.whitehouse.gov.

- **Slammer / Sapphire (2003):** Exploited **SQL Server UDP port 1434** buffer overflow. A tiny **376-byte** worm that achieved a scan rate of **~4,000 packets/second**, doubling infections every **8.5 seconds**. Caused widespread internet slowdowns and bank ATM failures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Worm and Virus:** Writing that a worm "needs a host file" — instant 1-mark penalty.
> 2. **Forgetting the Term $S(t) = N - I(t)$:** When deriving SEM, students often forget to substitute this — leading to an unsolvable ODE. Always substitute *before* separating variables.
> 3. **Skipping the $R_0$ Threshold Rule:** Examiners specifically look for the rule "$R_0 > 1$ means outbreak, $R_0 \le 1$ means containment." Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Memorizing without Understanding:** Students rote-memorize $I(t) = N / (1 + e^{-\beta N (t - t_0)})$ but cannot explain $t_0$ — losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams in Lifecycle Questions:** A 7-mark lifecycle question without a labeled diagram is penalized **1–2 marks**. Always draw the propagation cycle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Worms**

- **Definition:** A worm is a self-replicating, standalone malware that propagates over networks **without user interaction and without a host file**.
- **Key Contrast:** Virus = needs host + user action. Worm = standalone + autonomous. Trojan = no replication.
- **Architecture:** Four modules — *Reconnaissance, Exploitation, Replication/Payload, C2 Beacon*.
- **SEM Equation:** $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$.
- **Logistic Solution:** $I(t) = \dfrac{N}{1 + e^{-N \beta (t - t_0)}}$ — produces the famous **S-shaped infection curve**.
- **Two-Factor Model:** Adds time-varying $\beta(t)$ and removal $R(t)$ to reflect real-world patching and congestion.
- **Epidemic Threshold:** $R_0 = \beta N / \gamma$. Outbreak occurs if $R_0 > 1$.
- **Major Worms to Memorize:**
  - *Morris Worm (1988)* — first internet worm; exploited Unix sendmail/finger.
  - *ILOVEYOU (2000)* — VBS email worm; $10B+ damage.
  - *Code Red (2001)* — IIS buffer overflow; 359k hosts in 14 hours.
  - *Slammer (2003)* — SQL Server; 376-byte worm; fastest spread ever.
  - *Stuxnet (2010)* — targeted SCADA/PLC systems; first known cyber-physical weapon.
  - *Conficker (2008)* — Windows SMB exploit; built massive botnet.
  - *WannaCry (2017)* — ransomware worm via EternalBlue (SMB).
- **Defense Layers (in order):** Firewall → IDS/IPS → Network Segmentation → EDR → Patch Management → Backups.
- **High-Yield Exam Keywords:** *Self-replicating, autonomous, no host file, network vulnerability, payload, epidemic model, $R_0$, scanning worm, botnet.*
- **Cohen's Epidemic Threshold (bonus):** $T = 1/\sqrt{N}$ — fraction of hosts that must be immune to halt worm spread.
- **Always Include a Diagram** in lifecycle questions — examiners reward visual clarity.
- **Never Confuse Terms:** *Worm ≠ Virus ≠ Trojan ≠ Ransomware* — each is a distinct malware category in KTU's classification scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Threats - Sources of Security Threats - Motives - Target Assets and Vulnerabilities - Consequences of Threats - E-mail Threats - Web-threats - Intruders and Hackers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Threats - Sources of Security Threats - Motives - Target Assets and Vulnerabilities - Consequences of Threats - E-mail Threats - Web-threats - Intruders and Hackers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Security Threat?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (William Stallings, *Cryptography and Network Security: Principles and Practice*)**
> A **security threat** is any potential circumstance, event, or action that can intentionally or accidentally exploit a vulnerability in an information system, causing harm to the system's assets, operations, or reputation. A threat, by itself, is a *possibility* — it becomes a successful attack only when it finds a **vulnerability** to exploit.

In simpler engineering terms:
$$\text{Threat} \xrightarrow{\text{exploits}} \text{Vulnerability} \xrightarrow{\text{results in}} \text{Attack} \rightarrow \text{Damage}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Threat** = What can go wrong (the danger).
> - **Vulnerability** = The weakness that allows the threat to happen (the unlocked door).
> - **Risk** = The probability and impact of the threat being realized.
> - **Attack** = The actual occurrence of the threat exploiting the vulnerability.

### 🏠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Vault"

Imagine your home is a computer system:
- **Threat** = A burglar who *might* come to your street.
- **Vulnerability** = The unlocked back window of your house.
- **Asset** = The jewellery, cash, and laptop inside.
- **Consequence** = Theft of valuables, violation of privacy, psychological trauma.
- **Countermeasure** = Installing a lock, an alarm system, and CCTV cameras.

Just as you cannot remove the burglar from the street (the threat is ever-present), in cyber security you cannot *eliminate* threats — you can only **reduce vulnerabilities** and **protect assets**.

---

## 1.2 Sources of Security Threats

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies threat sources into three primary categories:

| Source Type | Description | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Natural / Environmental** | Uncontrollable physical events | Earthquakes, floods, fires, power surges, lightning |
| **Unintentional (Human Error)** | Accidental actions by legitimate users | Misconfigured servers, lost USB drives, accidental deletion, weak password choice |
| **Intentional (Malicious)** | Deliberate actions by adversaries | Hackers, insiders, nation-state APT groups, ransomware gangs, disgruntled employees |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** When asked to "list sources of security threats," always mention the **three-tier classification** (Natural, Unintentional, Intentional). Examiners specifically look for this hierarchy in 3-mark answers.

---

## 1.3 Motives Behind Security Threats

Attackers are not random — they are driven by specific motivations. Understanding motive helps in threat modelling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Canonical Motives (Recurring KTU Question)**
> 1. **Financial Gain** — Credit card theft, ransomware, banking trojans.
> 2. **Espionage** — Corporate or state-sponsored intelligence gathering.
> 3. **Ideology (Hacktivism)** — Political, religious, or social agendas (e.g., Anonymous).
> 4. **Revenge / Insider Anger** — Disgruntled employees sabotaging their organization.
> 5. **Challenge / Ego / Curiosity** — Script kiddies and hobbyist hackers testing skills.
> 6. **Terrorism** — Cyberterrorists targeting critical infrastructure (power grids, hospitals).
> 7. **Cyberwarfare** — Nation-state attacks for strategic advantage (Stuxnet, SolarWinds).

---

## 1.4 Target Assets and Vulnerabilities

### 1.4.1 Target Assets (What attackers want)

| Asset Class | Concrete Examples |
|:---|:---|
| **Hardware** | Servers, routers, IoT devices, storage media |
| **Software** | Operating systems, application code, licensed tools |
| **Data** | PII, PHI, intellectual property, financial records, login credentials |
| **People** | Employees, customers, executives (social engineering targets) |
| **Services** | Network availability, DNS, email, cloud-hosted applications |
| **Reputation & Trust** | Brand image, customer confidence |

### 1.4.2 Common Vulnerabilities (The unlocked doors)

- **Software bugs** (buffer overflows, injection flaws, unpatched CVEs)
- **Misconfigurations** (default passwords, open ports, public S3 buckets)
- **Weak authentication** (no MFA, password reuse, predictable passwords)
- **Human factors** (phishing susceptibility, social engineering)
- **Design flaws** (lack of encryption, insecure protocols like Telnet, FTP)
- **Zero-day vulnerabilities** (unknown flaws with no available patch)

---

## 1.5 Consequences of Threats

> [!WARNING]
> **The CIA Triad** — the cornerstone of information security. Every threat violates at least one pillar:

$$\text{Security} = f(\text{Confidentiality}, \text{Integrity}, \text{Availability})$$

| CIA Pillar | Definition | Consequence if Violated | Real-World Example |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Confidentiality** | Keeping data private | Data breach, identity theft, corporate espionage | Facebook–Cambridge Analytica scandal (2018) |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring data is unaltered | Tampered records, financial fraud, malware injection | Stuxnet modifying Iranian centrifuges |
| **Availability** | Systems accessible when needed | Service outage, ransomware, DoS | Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack (2021) |

Beyond the CIA triad, modern frameworks also include:
- **Authenticity** — verifying the identity of users and data origin.
- **Accountability** — non-repudiation through audit trails.
- **Non-repudiation** — sender cannot deny sending a message.

---

## 1.6 E-mail Threats (E-mail as an Attack Vector)

E-mail remains the **#1 initial-access vector** in real-world breaches (Verizon DBIR consistently reports ~70–80% of incidents start with email).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Four E-mail Threats (KTU Syllabus Mandate)**
>
> 1. **Phishing** — Fraudulent emails impersonating legitimate entities to steal credentials or trick users into installing malware. Variants:
>    - *Spear Phishing* — targeted at a specific individual/organization.
>    - *Whaling* — targeting senior executives (CEO Fraud / BEC).
>    - *Vishing* — voice phishing (phone-based).
>    - *Smishing* — SMS phishing.
> 2. **Spam / Unsolicited Bulk Email (UBE)** — Unsolicited messages, often used as a delivery channel for other attacks.
> 3. **Malware Attachments** — Infected files (.exe, .docm, .pdf with embedded JavaScript, .iso, .lnk) that execute upon opening.
> 4. **E-mail Spoofing & Business Email Compromise (BEC)** — Forging the sender address to appear as a trusted party; BEC caused **>\$2.7 billion in losses in 2022** (FBI IC3 report).

### Defense Mechanisms
- **SPF (Sender Policy Framework)** — validates sending IP via DNS.
- **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** — cryptographic signature on outgoing mail.
- **DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance)** — policy layer combining SPF + DKIM.
- **User awareness training** and **anti-phishing gateways** (e.g., Proofpoint, Mimecast).

---

## 1.7 Web-Threats (Web as an Attack Vector)

The modern web is the largest attack surface in any organization. The **OWASP Top 10** is the de facto industry standard for web application security risks.

> [!NOTE]
> **OWASP Top 10 (2021 Edition) — Most Tested in KTU**
> 1. **A01: Broken Access Control** — Users accessing data/functions they shouldn't.
> 2. **A02: Cryptographic Failures** — Weak/missing encryption (e.g., storing passwords as MD5).
> 3. **A03: Injection** — SQL Injection, NoSQL Injection, OS Command Injection, LDAP Injection.
> 4. **A04: Insecure Design** — Architectural flaws that cannot be fixed by good code.
> 5. **A05: Security Misconfiguration** — Default credentials, verbose error messages, open cloud storage.
> 6. **A06: Vulnerable & Outdated Components** — Unpatched libraries, frameworks, OS.
> 7. **A07: Identification & Authentication Failures** — Weak passwords, missing MFA, credential stuffing.
> 8. **A08: Software & Data Integrity Failures** — Insecure CI/CD pipelines, unsigned updates.
> 9. **A09: Security Logging & Monitoring Failures** — No audit trail for incidents.
> 10. **A10: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** — Forcing the server to make unintended requests.

### 1.7.1 SQL Injection — The Classic Web Threat
A malicious SQL statement inserted into an input field, executing unintended database commands.

**Example vulnerable PHP code:**
```php
$query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" . $_GET['user'] . "'";
```
If an attacker inputs `' OR '1'='1' --`, the query becomes:
```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' --'
```
This **bypasses authentication** and returns all rows.

**Defense:** *Parameterized queries / Prepared statements.*

### 1.7.2 Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
Injecting malicious client-side scripts into web pages viewed by other users.
- **Reflected XSS** — payload in URL/request.
- **Stored XSS** — payload persisted in database.
- **DOM-based XSS** — payload in client-side JavaScript.

**Defense:** Output encoding, Content Security Policy (CSP), input sanitization.

---

## 1.8 Intruders and Hackers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Stallings):** An **intruder** is a person (or program acting on behalf of a person) who attempts to gain unauthorized access to a system or to perform an unauthorized action. The term **hacker** is sometimes used synonymously, but more precisely refers to an individual with deep technical knowledge who probes and experiments with systems.

### 1.8.1 Classes of Intruders (Stallings Taxonomy)

| Class | Skill Level | Motivation | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Masquerader** | Variable | Personal gain, espionage | Outsider who steals credentials and pretends to be a legitimate user |
| **Misfeasor** | Authorized user, abuses privileges | Curiosity, revenge, financial | Employee accessing customer credit card data they don't need |
| **Clandestine User** | High | Seeks to evade detection; long-term access | APT actor inside a network for months |

### 1.8.2 The Six Hats of Hackers (Classic KTU Question)

| Type | Hat Color | Description |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **White Hat** | ⚪ Ethical | Security professionals who test with permission (penetration testers) |
| **Black Hat** | ⚫ Malicious | Criminals who break systems for personal gain or damage |
| **Grey Hat** | 🩶 Ambiguous | May violate laws but without malicious intent (e.g., publicizing vulnerabilities) |
| **Script Kiddie** | 🟢 Beginner | Uses pre-built tools without understanding underlying mechanisms |
| **Hacktivist** | 🟡 Ideological | Motivated by political/social causes (e.g., Anonymous) |
| **State-Sponsored / APT** | 🔴 Nation-state | Highly resourced, long-term, strategic (Lazarus Group, APT29) |

### 1.8.3 Hacker Behaviour Pattern
1. **Reconnaissance** — gather information (passive: Shodan, Google Dorks; active: port scanning, Nmap).
2. **Scanning** — identify open ports, services, vulnerabilities.
3. **Gaining Access** — exploit a vulnerability.
4. **Maintaining Access** — install backdoors, rootkits, persistence mechanisms.
5. **Covering Tracks** — clear logs, hide files, use encryption.

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Threat-Vulnerability-Asset (TVA) Risk Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental relationship governing all of information security risk analysis is:**
> $$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Asset Value}$$
>
> In practical KTU/industry usage, this is operationalized using **ALE (Annual Loss Expectancy)**.

### Step-by-step logic of the model

1. **Identify the Asset** — what are we protecting? (e.g., a database of 1 million customer records).
2. **Assign Asset Value (AV)** — the financial / strategic worth of the asset.
3. **Determine Exposure Factor (EF)** — the fraction of asset value lost in a single breach (0 < EF ≤ 1).
4. **Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**:
   $$\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$$
5. **Determine Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** — the expected number of incidents per year.
6. **Compute Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)**:
   $$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$
7. **Compare ALE to Cost of Countermeasure (CoC)** — implement control if:
   $$\text{ALE}_{\text{before control}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after control}} > \text{Cost of Control}$$

### Real-world engineering utility
- **Insurance underwriting** for cyber-liability policies.
- **ROI justification** for security budgets before a CISO board.
- **Compliance** with ISO 27005 and NIST SP 800-30 risk management frameworks.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table. It is the single highest-yield reference for numerical KTU problems on this module.**

| # | Formula / Term | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Asset Value | $AV$ | ₹ / USD — monetary or qualitative (High/Medium/Low) |
| 2 | Exposure Factor | $EF$ | Dimensionless, $0 < EF \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Single Loss Expectancy | $\text{SLE} = AV \times EF$ | Currency |
| 4 | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | $ARO$ | Events per year (e.g., 0.5 = once every 2 years) |
| 5 | Annual Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times ARO$ | Currency per year |
| 6 | Residual Risk | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{original}} - \text{Control effectiveness}$ | After controls applied |
| 7 | Total Risk | $R_{\text{total}} = T \times V \times A$ | Threat × Vulnerability × Asset |
| 8 | Cost-Benefit of Control | $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{CoC}}{\text{CoC}}$ | Return on Security Investment (decimal or %) |
| 9 | Attack Probability (Bayesian) | $P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Vuln}) = \dfrac{P(\text{Vuln} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack})}{P(\text{Vuln})}$ | Bayesian update after intel |
| 10 | Password Entropy | $H = L \times \log_2(N)$ | $L$ = length, $N$ = character set size (bits) |

---

## 2.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical 7-mark KTU question)

> **Problem:** A company has a customer database worth ₹10,00,000. A successful breach exposes 60% of its value. Historically, such breaches occur once every 4 years. The company wants to install a firewall costing ₹50,000/year that reduces the breach likelihood to once every 10 years and lowers exposure to 25%. Should the firewall be purchased?

**Step 1 — Compute SLE (before control):**
$$\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = ₹6{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE (before control):**
$$\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ per year}$$
$$\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = ₹1{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE (after control):**
$$\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$$
$$\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = \dfrac{1}{10} = 0.10 \text{ per year}$$
$$\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.10 = ₹25{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Annual Savings:**
$$\text{Savings} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 1{,}50{,}000 - 25{,}000 = ₹1{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Cost-Benefit Decision:**
$$\text{Net Benefit} = 1{,}25{,}000 - 50{,}000 = ₹75{,}000 > 0$$

✅ **Yes, the firewall should be purchased.** The control provides a net annual benefit of ₹75,000.

---

## 2.4 Threat Classification Frameworks

> [!NOTE]
> **Three classification axes used in KTU exam answers:**

1. **By Source**: Natural / Unintentional / Intentional.
2. **By Intent**: Accidental vs. Deliberate (malicious).
3. **By Effect on CIA**: Disclosure (Confidentiality), Deception (Integrity), Disruption (Availability), Usurpation (Authenticity).

The fourth dimension — **STRIDE** (Microsoft's threat modelling taxonomy) — is industry-standard:

| Letter | Threat | Violated Property |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **S** | Spoofing | Authenticity |
| **T** | Tampering | Integrity |
| **R** | Repudiation | Non-repudiation |
| **I** | Information Disclosure | Confidentiality |
| **D** | Denial of Service | Availability |
| **E** | Elevation of Privilege | Authorization |

---

## 2.5 Risk Severity Matrix (Standard 5×5)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the most commonly expected diagram in KTU 14-mark questions on "consequences of threats."**

| | **Low Impact (1)** | **Medium Impact (2)** | **High Impact (3)** | **Critical Impact (4)** | **Catastrophic Impact (5)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Very High Likelihood (5)** | Medium (5) | High (10) | High (15) | Critical (20) | Critical (25) |
| **High Likelihood (4)** | Medium (4) | Medium (8) | High (12) | Critical (16) | Critical (20) |
| **Medium Likelihood (3)** | Low (3) | Medium (6) | High (9) | High (12) | Critical (15) |
| **Low Likelihood (2)** | Low (2) | Low (4) | Medium (6) | High (8) | High (10) |
| **Rare Likelihood (1)** | Low (1) | Low (2) | Low (3) | Medium (4) | Medium (5) |

$$\text{Risk Score} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}$$
- **Score 1–6:** Low → Accept / monitor.
- **Score 7–12:** Medium → Mitigate with controls.
- **Score 13–19:** High → Mandatory mitigation.
- **Score 20–25:** Critical → Avoid the risk or seek executive sign-off.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Risk Equation from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the expression $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ rigorously so that you can present it as a model answer.

### Step 1 — Define the Asset and its Value
Let the asset have a monetary value $AV$. This represents the total loss if the asset is completely destroyed.

### Step 2 — Define the Exposure Factor
The **Exposure Factor (EF)** is defined as the percentage of asset value that is lost in a single successful threat event:
$$EF = \frac{\text{Value Lost in One Incident}}{AV}, \quad 0 < EF \leq 1$$

### Step 3 — Derive the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)
The expected monetary loss from a single occurrence of the threat:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= \text{Value Lost in One Incident} \\
&= EF \times AV
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **per-incident financial impact**.

### Step 4 — Define the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)
$ARO$ is the estimated frequency with which a threat is expected to occur per year, based on historical data, threat intelligence, or actuarial tables.

Examples:
- $ARO = 1$ → expected once per year.
- $ARO = 0.1$ → expected once every 10 years.
- $ARO = 365$ → expected daily.

### Step 5 — Derive the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)
Multiply the per-incident loss by the expected number of incidents per year:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times ARO \\
&= (EF \times AV) \times ARO
\end{aligned}$$

This gives the **expected annual financial loss** to the organization from that specific threat.

### Step 6 — Add the Countermeasure
If a control is applied, both $EF$ and $ARO$ typically decrease. Let:
- $EF_c$ = new exposure factor after control.
- $ARO_c$ = new annualized rate after control.
- $CoC$ = annual cost of the control.

The **residual ALE** is:
$$\text{ALE}_{\text{residual}} = (EF_c \times AV) \times ARO_c$$

### Step 7 — Cost-Benefit Decision Rule
The control is **financially justified** if and only if:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{original}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{residual}} &> \text{CoC} \\
(EF \times AV \times ARO) - (EF_c \times AV \times ARO_c) &> \text{CoC}
\end{aligned}$$

The **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** is then:
$$\text{ROSI} = \frac{(EF \times AV \times ARO) - (EF_c \times AV \times ARO_c) - CoC}{CoC}$$

This is the **complete derivation** of the industry-standard risk formula. ✍️

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threat × Consequence × Countermeasure

> [!TIP]
> **This 4-column matrix is the perfect "table answer" for 14-mark questions asking you to "explain various security threats, their consequences, and countermeasures."**

| # | Threat Type | Source / Motive | Consequence (CIA Violation) | Typical Countermeasure |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Virus / Worm** | Malicious code author, financial / disruption | Integrity + Availability | Antivirus, patch management, network segmentation |
| 2 | **Trojan Horse** | Disguised malware, financial | Confidentiality + Integrity | Application whitelisting, user awareness |
| 3 | **Ransomware** | Criminal gangs, financial | Availability (data encrypted) | Backups (3-2-1 rule), EDR, no-MFA policy removal |
| 4 | **Phishing** | Fraudsters, financial | Confidentiality (credentials) | SPF/DKIM/DMARC, anti-phishing gateway, training |
| 5 | **DoS / DDoS** | Hacktivists, extortion, competition | Availability | Rate limiting, scrubbing centers, CDN, WAF |
| 6 | **SQL Injection** | Hackers, financial / defacement | Confidentiality + Integrity | Parameterized queries, WAF, least privilege DB accounts |
| 7 | **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** | Hackers, session hijacking | Confidentiality | Output encoding, CSP, input validation |
| 8 | **MITM (Man-in-the-Middle)** | Network attackers, espionage | Confidentiality + Integrity | TLS 1.3, certificate pinning, HSTS |
| 9 | **Insider Threat** | Disgruntled employee, financial | All three (CIA) | Least privilege, DLP, UEBA, audit logging |
| 10 | **Zero-Day Exploit** | APT groups, espionage | Variable | Virtual patching, behaviour-based detection (EDR/XDR) |
| 11 | **Password Brute-Force** | Hackers, account takeover | Confidentiality | Account lockout, MFA, CAPTCHA, strong password policy |
| 12 | **Drive-by Download** | Compromised websites, malware delivery | Integrity + Availability | Browser sandbox, ad-blockers, endpoint protection |
| 13 | **Rogue AP / Evil Twin** | Wi-Fi attackers, sniffing | Confidentiality | 802.1X, WPA3-Enterprise, certificate validation |
| 14 | **Privilege Escalation** | Internal / external attackers, persistence | Authorization failure | RBAC, regular permission audits, principle of least privilege |
| 15 | **Backdoor / Rootkit** | APT / advanced attackers, persistence | Integrity + Availability | Integrity checking (Tripwire), secure boot, EDR |

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Deduction: Password Strength

A frequently asked KTU sub-question: *"Why are longer passwords stronger?"*

### Step 1 — Define Entropy
For a password of length $L$ drawn from a character set of size $N$, the **search space** is:
$$S = N^L$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Bits
The information-theoretic **entropy** in bits is:
$$H = \log_2(S) = L \times \log_2(N)$$

### Step 3 — Example Computations

| Password | Length $L$ | Charset size $N$ | $S = N^L$ | $H$ (bits) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `1234` | 4 | 10 (digits) | $10^4 = 10{,}000$ | $\approx 13.3$ |
| `Pa55word` | 8 | 26 (lowercase) | $26^8 \approx 2 \times 10^{11}$ | $\approx 37.6$ |
| `P@55w0rd!` | 9 | 94 (printable ASCII) | $94^9 \approx 5 \times 10^{17}$ | $\approx 58.9$ |
| `correct horse battery staple` | 28 | 26 + space | $27^{28} \approx 10^{40}$ | $\approx 134$ |

### Step 4 — Time to Crack
Assuming a cracking rig performs $10^{12}$ guesses/second:
$$T_{\text{crack}} = \frac{S}{2 \times 10^{12}} \text{ seconds}$$

- `Pa55word` → ~100 seconds.
- `P@55w0rd!` → ~3 days.
- 28-character passphrase → longer than the age of the universe.

### Step 5 — Implication for Security Policy
NIST SP 800-63B now recommends:
- **Minimum length 8** (preferably 12+) for user-chosen passwords.
- **Maximum length 64** or more.
- **Drop forced periodic rotation** unless compromise is suspected.
- **Drop composition rules** (no mandatory "must contain symbols") — they cause predictable patterns like `Password1!`.

---

## 3.4 Bayesian Threat Update (Analytical Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Beyond syllabus but useful for high-scoring analytical answers:**

When a vulnerability is disclosed, the conditional probability of an attack changes. Using Bayes' theorem:
$$P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Vuln Published}) = \frac{P(\text{Vuln Published} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack})}{P(\text{Vuln Published})}$$

This is the basis of **threat intelligence feeds** and the rationale behind **emergency patching windows** (typically 24–72 hours for critical CVEs).

## 4.4 Phishing Attack Anatomy (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Attacker] -->|crafts| A2[Forged Email<br/>mimics Bank or IT Admin]
    A2 -->|sent via| A3[Bulk SMTP<br/>or compromised server]
    A3 -->|delivered to| A4[Victim Inbox]
    A4 -->|user clicks| A5{Decision}
    A5 -->|clicks link| A6[Phishing Landing Page]
    A5 -->|opens attachment| A7[Malware Payload]
    A6 -->|submits| A8[Harvested Credentials]
    A7 -->|executes| A9[Remote Access / Ransomware]
    A8 --> A10[Account Takeover]
    A9 --> A10

    A1 -.spoofs sender.-> A2
    A1 -.may use SPF DKIM bypass.-> A3

    style A1 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style A4 fill:#FFE66D
    style A6 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style A7 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style A8 fill:#C0392B,color:#fff
    style A9 fill:#C0392B,color:#fff
    style A10 fill:#900,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.5 SQL Injection — Attack & Defence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ATTACK
        A1[Attacker input field] -->|enters| A2[Malicious SQL String<br/>UNION SELECT password FROM users --]
        A2 -->|app concatenates| A3[Final SQL Query]
        A3 -->|executes on| A4[(Database)]
        A4 -->|returns| A5[Sensitive Data or Auth Bypass]
    end

    subgraph DEFENCE
        D1[User Input] -->|goes to| D2[Prepared Statement<br/>with Bound Parameters]
        D2 -->|pre-compiled| D3[Query Plan]
        D3 -->|treats input as| D4[Data only, never as Code]
        D4 -->|executes on| D5[(Database)]
    end

    A5 -. .->|Result| Attacker

    style A1 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style A5 fill:#900,color:#fff
    style D2 fill:#52B788,color:#fff
    style D4 fill:#52B788,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.6 Hacker Classification Matrix (Hat-Colour View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hackers] --> H1[White Hat<br/>Ethical Pen-Tester]
    H --> H2[Black Hat<br/>Malicious Criminal]
    H --> H3[Grey Hat<br/>Ambiguous]
    H --> H4[Script Kiddie<br/>Uses Pre-built Tools]
    H --> H5[Hacktivist<br/>Political Agenda]
    H --> H6[State Sponsored APT<br/>Nation-State Resources]

    H2 --> H2a[Phishing Operators]
    H2 --> H2b[Ransomware Gangs]
    H2 --> H2c[Carding Fraudsters]

    H6 --> H6a[APT29 Cozy Bear]
    H6 --> H6b[Lazarus Group]
    H6 --> H6c[Stuxnet Actors]

    style H1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    style H2 fill:#000000,color:#fff
    style H3 fill:#808080,color:#fff
    style H4 fill:#90EE90
    style H5 fill:#FFD700
    style H6 fill:#DC143C,color:#fff
```
