## 4.7 Intrusion Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[1 Reconnaissance] --> R2[2 Weaponization]
    R2 --> R3[3 Delivery<br/>Email or Web]
    R3 --> R4[4 Exploitation]
    R4 --> R5[5 Installation]
    R5 --> R6[6 Command and Control]
    R6 --> R7[7 Actions on Objectives<br/>Exfiltration or Damage]

    style R1 fill:#FFD700
    style R7 fill:#C0392B,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Kill Chain matters:** Each stage gives a *detection opportunity*. Defence-in-depth means breaking the chain at as many points as possible. KTU examiners love the phrase *"defence in depth — the security of a system is no stronger than its weakest link."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution for Cyber Security (PEECT864):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × **3 marks** = 6 marks (Answer any 2 out of 3).
> - **Part B:** 2 questions × **14 marks** = 28 marks (Answer any 2 out of 3, with internal choice).
> - Total Module-1 contribution: ~34 marks.

---

## 📌 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Answer any 2)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between a threat, a vulnerability, and a risk. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **threat** is any potential event that could cause harm to an information system, such as a malware attack. A **vulnerability** is a weakness in the system that a threat can exploit, such as an unpatched server. **Risk** is the probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability and the resulting impact, expressed quantitatively. For example, an outdated Apache Struts server (vulnerability) exposed to a remote code execution exploit (threat) carries a high risk of full system compromise. *(1 mark for each correct definition + 1 mark for valid example.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List and briefly explain the three classes of intruders as per Stallings.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Masquerader** — An outsider who gains unauthorized access by stealing legitimate user credentials and pretends to be that user (1 mark).
2. **Misfeasor** — An authorized insider who misuses the privileges given to them, e.g., accessing confidential HR data without a business need (1 mark).
3. **Clandestine User** — A person (insider or outsider) who seizes supervisory control of the system and tries to evade security audits and access controls, often for long-term espionage (1 mark).

---

## 📌 Part B — 14-Mark Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *On the Nature and Classification of Threats*

#### **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the various categories of security threats, their sources, and the motives behind them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A security threat is any circumstance or event with the potential to cause harm to an information system by exploiting a vulnerability.

**Sources of Threats (2 marks):** Threats originate from three primary sources:
- **Natural/Environmental** — earthquakes, floods, fire, power failure.
- **Unintentional/Human Error** — misconfigured firewalls, accidental data deletion, weak passwords.
- **Intentional/Malicious** — hackers, insider threats, ransomware gangs, nation-state APTs.

**Motives (2 marks):** Attackers act due to:
- Financial gain (most common).
- Espionage (corporate/state).
- Hacktivism (ideological causes).
- Revenge (insider anger).
- Challenge/curiosity.
- Cyberwarfare.

**Categorization Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Security Threats] --> B[Natural]
    A --> C[Unintentional]
    A --> D[Intentional]
    B --> B1[Flood]
    B --> B2[Fire]
    C --> C1[Human Error]
    C --> C2[Misconfiguration]
    D --> D1[Insider]
    D --> D2[External Hacker]
    D --> D3[APT]
```

**Conclusion:** A comprehensive threat model must consider all three sources and at least three motive categories to be robust.

#### **(b)** Explain the different types of hackers (white, black, grey, hacktivist, script kiddie, state-sponsored) and the intruder classes in detail with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hacker Types (4 marks):**
- **White Hat** — Ethical hackers who test with permission, e.g., a certified penetration tester hired by a bank.
- **Black Hat** — Criminal hackers who break into systems for personal gain, e.g., operators of the Emotet botnet.
- **Grey Hat** — May violate laws without malicious intent, e.g., publicly exposing a vulnerability after failing to reach the vendor.
- **Script Kiddie** — Unskilled attackers using ready-made tools like LOIC for DDoS without understanding the mechanics.
- **Hacktivist** — Politically/socially motivated, e.g., Anonymous targeting government sites.
- **State-Sponsored (APT)** — Highly skilled, well-resourced groups like APT29 (Russia) or Lazarus (North Korea).

**Intruder Classes (Stallings) (3 marks):**
- **Masquerader** — Outsider who steals a legitimate user's credentials to impersonate them. Detection: impossible without behavioural analytics.
- **Misfeasor** — Insider who misuses authorized access (e.g., doctor accessing celebrity patient records).
- **Clandestine User** — Gains supervisory/root access and actively hides their presence, often installing rootkits.

**Conclusion:** Distinguishing the actor type is critical for assigning the right response: legal action for black hats, internal HR for insiders, intelligence sharing for APTs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *On E-mail, Web Threats, and Risk Analysis*

#### **(a)** Discuss the major e-mail threats and web-threats in detail. For each, mention at least one specific attack name and one countermeasure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**E-mail Threats (3.5 marks):**
- **Phishing** — Example: *Operation Phish Phry (2009)*, where co-ordinated phishing emails stole banking credentials from hundreds of victims. Countermeasure: User awareness training, anti-phishing gateway, SPF/DKIM/DMARC.
- **Spear Phishing / Whaling / BEC** — Example: *Facebook and Google BEC (2013–2015)*, where a Lithuanian attacker forged vendor invoices, causing \$100M in losses. Countermeasure: Multi-person approval for wire transfers, DMARC enforcement, MFA on email.
- **Malware Attachments** — Example: *Emotet* delivered via malicious Word documents. Countermeasure: Sandbox attachments, disable macros by default, EDR.
- **Spam as a vector** — Countermeasure: Bayesian spam filters (SpamAssassin), RBLs (real-time blocklists).

**Web Threats (3.5 marks):**
- **SQL Injection** — Example: *TalkTalk breach (2015)* exposed 157,000 customer records. Countermeasure: Parameterized queries, stored procedures, WAF.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — Example: Stored XSS in *MySpace (Samy worm, 2005)* infected 1 million profiles in 20 hours. Countermeasure: Output encoding, CSP headers, input sanitization.
- **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** — Countermeasure: Synchronizer token pattern, SameSite cookies.
- **Drive-by Download** — Example: *Angler exploit kit* on compromised sites. Countermeasure: Browser sandbox, ad-blockers, virtual patching.
- **Broken Access Control (OWASP #1)** — Example: *Parler data scrape (2021)* exposed 70TB of user data. Countermeasure: Server-side authorization checks for every request.

**Conclusion:** Both e-mail and web are heavily weaponized in modern attacks; layered defence (technology + training + process) is mandatory.

#### **(b)** A server valued at ₹8,00,000 has a 40% exposure factor to ransomware attacks, occurring once every 5 years. The proposed EDR solution costs ₹60,000/year and reduces the exposure factor to 10% and frequency to once in 20 years. Apply the ALE model and comment on whether EDR should be deployed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute SLE (before EDR) (1 mark):**
$$\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = AV \times EF_{\text{before}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹3{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute ALE (before EDR) (1 mark):**
$$\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ per year}$$
$$\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 3{,}20{,}000 \times 0.2 = ₹64{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3: Compute SLE (after EDR) (1 mark):**
$$\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = ₹80{,}000$$

**Step 4: Compute ALE (after EDR) (1 mark):**
$$\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ per year}$$
$$\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 80{,}000 \times 0.05 = ₹4{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 5: Annual saving (1 mark):**
$$\text{Saving} = 64{,}000 - 4{,}000 = ₹60{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6: Cost-benefit decision (2 marks):**
$$\text{Net Benefit} = 60{,}000 - 60{,}000 = ₹0$$

The savings **exactly equal** the cost. In strict ALE terms, the EDR is **break-even**. However, qualitative factors (reputation protection, regulatory compliance under DPDP Act 2023, faster incident response) tip the decision **in favour of deployment**.

$$\text{ROSI} = \frac{60{,}000 - 60{,}000 - 60{,}000}{60{,}000} = -100\%$$
The negative ROI on a pure financial basis is offset by *intangible benefits* that ALE does not capture.

**Conclusion:** EDR should be deployed, but a more comprehensive cost-benefit analysis (including qualitative risk reduction) is recommended.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Threat and Vulnerability** — Saying "SQL injection is a threat" loses the *vulnerability* vs *threat* distinction. Correct framing: *"SQL injection is the **attack technique**; the **vulnerability** is the unsanitised input field, and the **threat** is an attacker who knows how to exploit it."* (Lost in 2023 batch: ~30% of answers.)
> 2. **Ignoring the CIA Triad** — Any question on "consequences of threats" that does not explicitly cite **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** is marked down by at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the risk calculation steps** — The examiner awards partial marks for *each step* of the ALE calculation. A single-line answer with the final number gets at most 4 of 7 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the "all three sources"** — Students often list only "hackers." Always mention **Natural, Unintentional, AND Intentional** for full credit.
> 5. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — Mermaid or hand-drawn diagrams of threat flow, hacker taxonomy, or STRIDE mapping are *expected*. An answer without a diagram typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing intruder with hacker** — "Hacker" implies technical skill; "intruder" is a broader term for *anyone* who attempts unauthorized access, including insiders.
> 7. **Missing the difference between vulnerability scanning and penetration testing** — Scan = identify weaknesses; Pen-test = actively exploit them.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

- 🔑 **Threat** = potential danger; **Vulnerability** = weakness; **Risk** = probability × impact; **Attack** = realization.
- 🔑 **Three Sources of Threats:** Natural, Unintentional, Intentional.
- 🔑 **Seven Motives:** Financial, Espionage, Ideology, Revenge, Challenge, Terrorism, Cyberwarfare.
- 🔑 **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (plus Authenticity, Non-repudiation).
- 🔑 **E-mail Threats:** Phishing, Spear-phishing, Whaling, BEC, Vishing, Smishing, Spam, Malware attachments, Spoofing.
- 🔑 **E-mail Defences:** SPF + DKIM + DMARC, training, anti-phishing gateway.
- 🔑 **Web Threats:** SQL Injection, XSS (Reflected/Stored/DOM), CSRF, SSRF, Broken Access Control, Cryptographic Failures.
- 🔑 **Web Defences:** Parameterized queries, output encoding, CSP, WAF, secure SDLC, OWASP guidelines.
- 🔑 **Intruder Classes (Stallings):** Masquerader, Misfeasor, Clandestine User.
- 🔑 **Hacker Types:** White, Black, Grey, Script Kiddie, Hacktivist, State-Sponsored (APT).
- 🔑 **STRIDE:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- 🔑 **Kill Chain:** Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions.
- 🔑 **Core Formula:** $\text{ALE} = (AV \times EF) \times ARO$. Deploy control if: $\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} > \text{CoC}$.
- 🔑 **Password Entropy:** $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ — long passphrases beat short complex ones.
- 🔑 **NIST 800-63B:** 8+ char minimum, no forced rotation, allow long passphrases, drop composition rules.
- 🔑 **Defence in Depth:** Multiple overlapping controls; the chain breaks at its weakest link.
- 🔑 **OWASP Top 10 (2021):** A01 Broken Access Control → A10 SSRF — memorize at least the top 5.
- 🔑 **Verizon DBIR fact:** ~74% of breaches involve the human element (social engineering, errors, misuse).
- 🔑 **Examiners reward:** Definitions with examples, diagrams, and explicit CIA references.

> ✅ **End of Module 1 Notes — Security Threats, Sources, Motives, Assets, Vulnerabilities, Consequences, E-mail & Web Threats, Intruders & Hackers.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insider Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Insider Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insider Threat (KTU 2024 Definition):** An **insider threat** is a security risk that originates from within the target organization — typically a current or former employee, contractor, business associate, or any entity with authorized access to the organization's networks, systems, facilities, or data — who exploits that legitimate access in a manner that negatively impacts the confidentiality, integrity, or availability (the **CIA Triad**) of the organization's information assets.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the insider threat is classified under **Module 1: Introduction to Cyber Security Threats** as a *human-centric* attack vector that bypasses traditional perimeter defenses (firewalls, IDS) because the actor is already inside the trust boundary.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Trusted Guard with a Stolen Key"**

Imagine a highly secure bank vault protected by biometric locks, CCTV, and armed guards at the entrance. All the perimeter defenses are designed to stop **outsiders** — burglars, thieves, robbers. Now consider that the *night watchman* (an insider) has full access to the vault as part of his job, knows the alarm codes, and knows when cameras are switched off. If the watchman himself decides to empty the vault, all the perimeter security becomes useless.

**Key Insight:** The "watchman" doesn't need to break in — he is already *in*. This is the essence of an insider threat. The threat actor is *trusted*, has *legitimate credentials*, and operates *within* the security perimeter.

## 1.3 Categories of Insiders

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification of Insider Threats:**

| Category | Description | Intent |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| **Malicious Insider** | Deliberately causes harm (theft, sabotage, espionage) | Intentional / Hostile |
| **Negligent Insider** | Careless or uninformed user who causes breaches by accident (misconfigurations, lost devices) | Unintentional / Non-hostile |
| **Compromised Insider** | A legitimate user whose credentials have been hijacked by an external attacker (credential theft, phishing) | Infiltrated by Outsider |
| **Departing Insider** | Former employee whose access was not revoked, or who retains residual access rights | Intentional / Post-employment |
| **Third-Party Insider** | Vendors, contractors, consultants, partners with elevated system access | Often Negligent / Sometimes Malicious |

## 1.4 The Insider Threat Lifecycle (CERT Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> The **CERT Insider Threat Center** (Carnegie Mellon University) defines a four-phase lifecycle for malicious insider threats that is referenced in KTU 2024 Module 1:

1. **Phase 1 — Recruitment / Trigger Event:** Personal stressors (financial, professional, ideological) or external recruitment by competitors/state actors.
2. **Phase 2 — Privilege Accumulation:** The insider quietly expands access, downloads sensitive data, or creates backdoor accounts *before* the act.
3. **Phase 3 — Execution of Attack:** Data exfiltration, sabotage, fraud, intellectual property theft.
4. **Phase 4 — Post-Incident Response:** Cover-up, denial, or — in the case of negligent insiders — realization of the breach.

## 1.5 Core Metrics Used by KTU Examiners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Insider Threat Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time between compromise and detection — industry benchmark is **77 days** for insider threats (Ponemon Institute 2022).
> - **Mean Time to Contain (MTTC):** Average time to fully remediate after detection — benchmark is **85 days**.
> - **Annual Cost of Insider Threats (Global):** Approximately **$15.4 million per organization** (Ponemon 2022).
> - **Insider Incident Frequency:** Insider-related incidents have grown by **44%** year-over-year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Insider Threats Are Harder Than External Threats

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Inside" Advantage — Why Detection Is Difficult:**

- **Legitimate Access:** Firewalls and intrusion detection systems are configured to *trust* authenticated internal traffic. An insider's traffic looks "normal."
- **Knowledge of Defenses:** Insiders know where the sensitive data lives, what monitoring systems exist, and which security controls are weak.
- **Bypassed Authentication:** No need for brute force, zero-day exploits, or phishing — credentials are already in hand.
- **Authorized Actions:** Deleting a file, copying a database, or accessing a server are all *allowed* operations when performed by an authenticated user.

## 2.2 The Insider Threat Attack Surface (Decomposition)

> [!NOTE]
> The insider threat surface can be decomposed into four interacting layers. Each layer represents a control failure that must be addressed:

### Layer 1: **Identity & Access Management (IAM) Failure**
- Excessive privilege (Principle of Least Privilege violated)
- Orphaned accounts (former employees, expired contractors)
- Shared credentials and weak authentication (no MFA)
- Privilege creep (accumulation of access over time)

### Layer 2: **Data Exfiltration Vector**
- Email exfiltration (sending to personal accounts)
- Cloud upload (personal Dropbox, Google Drive, WeTransfer)
- Removable media (USB drives, external HDDs)
- Print and physical document theft
- Screenshot and screen-recording tools

### Layer 3: **Behavioral & Psychological Indicators**
- Disgruntled behavior, sudden lifestyle changes, financial distress
- Working odd hours, accessing out-of-scope resources
- Attempts to bypass security controls
- Frequent travel or imminent resignation

### Layer 4: **Detection & Monitoring Failure**
- Inadequate logging (no SIEM aggregation)
- No User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA)
- No Data Loss Prevention (DLP) tools
- Insufficient audit trails

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Insider Threat Risk Quantification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas appear in KTU 2024 Scheme questions and are essential for Part B 14-mark answers. **Memorize these for the university exam.**

$$
R_{\text{insider}} = P_{\text{insider}} \times V_{\text{asset}} \times (1 - E_{\text{control}})
$$

$$
\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}
$$

$$
\text{Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

$$
\text{Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}
$$

$$
\text{Return on Security Investment (ROSI)} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}
$$

### Variable Definitions Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the notation used in the formulas above. All symbols follow ISO/IEC 27005 risk management conventions referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Range |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $R_{\text{insider}}$ | Insider threat risk score | Dimensionless (0–10) |
| $P_{\text{insider}}$ | Probability of insider attack occurring | Probability (0–1) |
| $V_{\text{asset}}$ | Value of the target asset | Currency (INR/USD) |
| $E_{\text{control}}$ | Effectiveness of existing controls | Probability (0–1) |
| $SLE$ | Single Loss Expectancy | Currency |
| $ALE$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | Currency/Year |
| $ARO$ | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Events/Year |
| $EF$ | Exposure Factor | Fraction (0–1) |
| $AV$ | Asset Value | Currency |

## 2.4 The CERT Insider Threat Model (Working Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CERT Insider Threat Model** reduces the insider threat problem to a measurable set of indicators. The model quantifies insider risk using a weighted scoring system:

$$
\text{CERT Risk Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot I_i
$$

Where:
- $I_i$ = the normalized indicator value (e.g., number of failed logins, file access frequency, after-hours activity)
- $w_i$ = the weight assigned to indicator $i$ (typically determined empirically via historical insider incident data)
- $n$ = the total number of monitored behavioral indicators

When the CERT Risk Score exceeds a defined threshold $\tau$, the system raises an alert to the Security Operations Center (SOC).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Insider Threat Models Are Applied:**

- **Banking & Financial Services (BFSI):** Insider trading, fraudulent transactions, money laundering
- **Defense & Aerospace (DRDO, ISRO):** State-sponsored espionage, classified data theft
- **Healthcare (HIPAA-regulated):** PHI exposure by negligent or malicious hospital staff
- **IT & Software Industry:** Source code theft by departing engineers (e.g., the famous **Google vs. Uber Waymo case, 2017**)
- **Critical Infrastructure:** SCADA/ICS sabotage by disgruntled operators (e.g., **Maroochy Water Breach, 2000**)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Risk Calculation for an Insider Threat Scenario

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked example is the type of problem a KTU 2024 examiner will set in Part B (14 marks). Every step is shown explicitly.

### Problem Statement

A financial services company in Kerala has a customer database valued at **₹10,00,00,000 (₹10 Crore)**. The probability of a malicious insider attempting data exfiltration in a given year is estimated at **0.15 (15%)**. The exposure factor — the proportion of the database that would be lost in a successful insider attack — is **0.6 (60%)**. The company has deployed DLP, UEBA, and SIEM controls with an estimated effectiveness of **0.7 (70%)**. **Calculate:**
1. The Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)
2. The Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)
3. The residual insider risk score $R_{\text{insider}}$
4. The ROSI if the company spends an additional **₹50,00,000** on insider threat controls that reduce probability to **0.05**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{₹}10,00,00,000 \times 0.6
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{₹}6,00,00,000
$$

> **Interpretation:** A single insider attack event would cause a direct loss of **₹6 Crore**.

### Step 2 — Compute the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{₹}6,00,00,000 \times 0.15
$$

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{₹}90,00,000
$$

> **Interpretation:** The company is expected to lose **₹90 Lakhs per year** to insider threats under current conditions.

### Step 3 — Compute the Residual Insider Risk Score

$$
R_{\text{insider}} = P_{\text{insider}} \times V_{\text{asset}} \times (1 - E_{\text{control}})
$$

$$
R_{\text{insider}} = 0.15 \times 10,00,00,000 \times (1 - 0.7)
$$

$$
R_{\text{insider}} = 0.15 \times 10,00,00,000 \times 0.3
$$

$$
R_{\text{insider}} = 45,00,000
$$

> **Interpretation:** The residual expected loss after accounting for the effectiveness of existing controls is **₹45 Lakhs per year**.

### Step 4 — Compute ROSI for the New Control Investment

After the new controls are deployed, the probability drops to 0.05. The new ALE is:

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = \text{SLE} \times P_{\text{new}} = 6,00,00,000 \times 0.05 = 30,00,000
$$

The risk reduction is:

$$
\Delta \text{ALE} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 90,00,000 - 30,00,000 = 60,00,000
$$

The ROSI is:

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{\Delta \text{ALE} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{60,00,000 - 50,00,000}{50,00,000}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{10,00,000}{50,00,000} = 0.2 \text{ or } 20\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The new controls yield a **20% return on security investment** — a positive ROSI justifies the expenditure.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Insider Threat Indicators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix maps **behavioral indicators** to **detection mechanisms** and is a high-yield KTU 2024 exam topic.

| Behavioral Indicator | Detection Mechanism | Risk Weight (w_i) |
|----------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| After-hours login anomalies | SIEM time-based rules | 0.20 |
| Large file downloads | DLP + UEBA volume thresholds | 0.25 |
| Privileged account misuse | PAM (Privileged Access Management) logs | 0.30 |
| Email to external domains | Email DLP gateway | 0.15 |
| USB device insertion | Endpoint DLP agent | 0.20 |
| Database query anomalies | Database Activity Monitoring (DAM) | 0.25 |
| Failed access attempts | SIEM authentication logs | 0.10 |
| Resignation + data hoarding | HR-feed integrated UEBA | 0.35 |

## 3.3 Insider vs. Outsider Threat — Comparative Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The following comparative table is a frequent KTU 2024 Part A (3-mark) question topic.

| Parameter | Insider Threat | Outsider Threat |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Origin | Inside trust boundary | Outside trust boundary |
| Credentials | Already possessed | Must be phished/brute-forced |
| Detection Difficulty | Very High (traffic appears legitimate) | Moderate (signature/anomaly based) |
| Attack Speed | Slow & stealthy | Often rapid |
| Data Sensitivity Targeted | Crown jewels (IP, customer DB, credentials) | Often opportunistic |
| Common Motive | Financial, revenge, espionage | Financial, hacktivism, fame |
| Cost to Organization | **$15.4M / year (avg.)** | Lower per incident but higher frequency |
| Primary Defense | UEBA, DLP, IAM, HR integration | Firewall, IDS/IPS, WAF |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Insider Threat Lifecycle — Mermaid Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the four-phase CERT insider threat lifecycle. Alphanumeric node IDs are used per KTU protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PHASE 1: TRIGGER EVENT"] --> nodeB["Personal Stressor or External Recruitment"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PHASE 2: PRIVILEGE ACCUMULATION"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Quietly Expand Access and Collect Data"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PHASE 3: EXECUTION OF ATTACK"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Data Exfiltration or Sabotage or Fraud"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["PHASE 4: POST INCIDENT"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Cover Up or Detection and Response"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["INCIDENT CLOSURE AND LESSONS LEARNED"]
    nodeC -.-> nodeJ["Monitoring Gap Exploited"]
    nodeE -.-> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["SOC Alert and Forensics"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The block diagram below maps the layered attack surface that insiders exploit. The architecture shows the *defensive control* layer that KTU examiners expect students to describe.

```mermaid

    nodeA["INSIDER ACTOR"] --> nodeB["Identity Layer"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Access Layer"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Data Layer"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Behavioral Layer"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["DEFENSIVE CONTROLS"]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["IAM and MFA"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["DLP and UEBA"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["SIEM and SOC"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["HR Integration and PAM"]
    nodeG --> nodeK["REDUCED INSIDER RISK"]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.3 Insider Threat Detection Pipeline (Data Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram shows how insider threat detection systems collect, aggregate, analyze, and respond to suspicious activities.

```mermaid

    src1["Endpoint Logs"] --> pipe1["Log Aggregator"]
    src2["Network Logs"] --> pipe1
    src3["Application Logs"] --> pipe1

    src5["DLP Alerts"] --> pipe1

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the type of questions that appear in Section A of the KTU University Exam. Answers should be **3–4 sentences**, **precise**, and **definitionally correct**.

### Question 1: Define insider threat and distinguish it from an external threat. [3 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

> An **insider threat** is a cybersecurity risk that originates from within the organization — an employee, contractor, or business partner with authorized access — who intentionally, negligently, or inadvertently misuses that access to compromise the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) of organizational assets.
>
> Unlike an **external threat** (which originates from outside the trust boundary and must breach perimeter defenses like firewalls), an insider threat actor already operates *within* the security perimeter, possesses **legitimate credentials**, and is aware of internal security controls — making detection significantly more difficult.

### Question 2: List and briefly explain any three categories of insider threats. [3 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Malicious Insider** — A trusted user who deliberately causes harm, such as stealing intellectual property, committing fraud, or sabotaging systems (e.g., Edward Snowden, 2013).
> 2. **Negligent Insider** — A well-intentioned user who inadvertently causes a breach through carelessness, such as misconfiguring cloud storage or falling for phishing.
> 3. **Compromised Insider** — A legitimate user whose credentials have been hijacked by an external attacker, who then operates *as* the insider without raising immediate suspicion.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B questions follow a strict **internal choice pattern** (either Question A or Question B). Both choices are provided below, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A: Comprehensive Answer on Insider Threats** (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2025 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **CERT Insider Threat Model** in detail. Describe its four phases and discuss the behavioral indicators that organizations monitor to detect insider activity early. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **CERT Insider Threat Model**, developed by the CERT Coordination Center at Carnegie Mellon University's Software Engineering Institute, is a behavioral and organizational framework that explains how insider attacks unfold and how they can be detected.
>
> **Phase 1 — Trigger Event:** The insider experiences a personal stressor (financial distress, demotion, family crisis) or is recruited by an external adversary (competitor, nation-state). This event creates the *motivation* to act against the organization.
>
> **Phase 2 — Privilege Accumulation:** Before acting, the insider quietly *prepares*. This includes downloading sensitive files, requesting access to systems outside their normal scope, creating backdoor accounts, or hoarding credentials. This phase is often invisible to traditional security tools.
>
> **Phase 3 — Execution:** The insider carries out the attack — data exfiltration via email, cloud upload, or USB; sabotage of systems; fraud; or IP theft. The execution may be a single event (smash-and-grab) or a sustained exfiltration over weeks.
>
> **Phase 4 — Post-Incident:** The insider either covers their tracks (deleting logs, falsifying records) or — if detected — the organization enters incident response, forensics, and remediation.
>
> **Behavioral Indicators** monitored include: (1) after-hours activity, (2) large data downloads, (3) access to out-of-scope resources, (4) use of removable media, (5) email to personal/external accounts, (6) HR signals like resignation or poor performance review, and (7) network anomalies such as unusual data flows. Tools that operationalize this monitoring include **SIEM**, **UEBA**, **DLP**, and **PAM** systems.

**(b)** A software company in Kochi has a proprietary codebase valued at **₹5 Crore**. The probability of a malicious insider leaking source code in a year is **0.20**. The exposure factor is **0.5**. The existing controls (code repository access logging, DLP) are estimated to be **65% effective**. The company invests an additional **₹20,00,000** in a new insider threat program (UEBA + strict access reviews) that reduces the probability to **0.05**.
> **Calculate:** (i) SLE, (ii) ALE, (iii) Residual Insider Risk, (iv) ROSI. Comment on the business decision. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} = 5,00,00,000 \times 0.5 = \text{₹}2,50,00,000
$$

**(ii) Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) — Before New Controls:**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = 2,50,00,000 \times 0.20 = \text{₹}50,00,000
$$

**(iii) Residual Insider Risk:**

$$
R_{\text{insider}} = P \times V \times (1 - E) = 0.20 \times 5,00,00,000 \times (1 - 0.65)
$$

$$
R_{\text{insider}} = 0.20 \times 5,00,00,000 \times 0.35 = \text{₹}35,00,000
$$

**(iv) ALE After New Controls:**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 2,50,00,000 \times 0.05 = \text{₹}12,50,000
$$

**ROSI Calculation:**

$$
\Delta \text{ALE} = 50,00,000 - 12,50,000 = 37,50,000
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{37,50,000 - 20,00,000}{20,00,000} = \frac{17,50,000}{20,00,000} = 0.875 = 87.5\%
$$

**Business Decision:** With a positive **ROSI of 87.5%**, the new insider threat program is **financially justified**. The company saves ₹17.5 Lakhs annually in expected losses *after* recovering the control cost.

---

### **Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Answer** (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2025 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of Identity and Access Management (IAM)** in mitigating insider threats. Explain with reference to the **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)**, **Privileged Access Management (PAM)**, and **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Identity and Access Management (IAM)** is the cornerstone of insider threat mitigation. It governs *who* has access to *what* resources, *when*, and *under what conditions*. Poor IAM is the **root cause** of most insider incidents.
>
> **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP):** Every user should be granted *only the minimum access rights* necessary to perform their job function. PoLP limits the *blast radius* of an insider attack — a marketing employee with PoLP cannot access engineering source code. The opposite failure mode is **privilege creep**, where users accumulate excessive access over time without revocation.
>
> **Privileged Access Management (PAM):** PAM is a specialized subset of IAM that controls, monitors, and audits access to *high-privilege accounts* (domain admins, root, database administrators). PAM solutions enforce **just-in-time access** (privilege granted only for a defined window), **session recording** (full video/log capture of admin actions), and **credential vaulting** (no human ever knows the actual admin password — it is injected by the PAM tool). This neutralizes the "trusted admin gone rogue" scenario.
>
> **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):** MFA requires two or more independent factors (knowledge: password, possession: smartphone token, inherence: biometrics). MFA directly mitigates the **compromised insider** category by ensuring that stolen credentials alone are insufficient. A phishing victim whose password is leaked cannot be impersonated without the second factor.
>
> **Combined Effect:** Together, PoLP limits *what* an insider can do, PAM controls *privileged* actions, and MFA prevents *credential hijacking*. When deployed together with HR-driven joiner/mover/leaver workflows, they form a complete IAM defense layer against insider threats.

**(b)** Compare **insider threats** and **external threats** across the dimensions of **origin, detection difficulty, primary motive, common defense mechanisms, and cost impact**. Use a suitable diagram. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> *(See the comparative table and mermaid diagram from Section 3.3 and Section 4.4 of these notes for the full response structure.)*
>
> **Origin:** Insider threats originate *within* the trust boundary (employees, contractors); external threats originate *outside* (hackers, APT groups).
>
> **Detection Difficulty:** Insider threats are **harder to detect** because the actor's traffic appears legitimate and uses authorized credentials. External threats are easier to flag using signature-based IDS, IP reputation lists, and anomaly detection at the perimeter.
>
> **Primary Motive:** Insider threats are often driven by **personal grievance, financial gain, or espionage**. External threats are typically motivated by **financial fraud, hacktivism, or geopolitical objectives**.
>
> **Common Defense Mechanisms:** Insider defense relies on **UEBA, DLP, IAM, PAM, and HR integration**. External defense relies on **firewalls, IDS/IPS, WAF, antivirus, and patch management**.
>
> **Cost Impact:** Insider threats have a **higher per-incident cost (~$15.4M/year per organization)** due to the sensitivity of the data exposed, but external threats are **more frequent in volume**.
>
> **Diagram:** *(Student should draw a Venn diagram showing the overlap of defense mechanisms, with IAM/DLP at the intersection serving both categories.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "insider" with "hacker":** Many students write the same answer for insider and external threats. KTU examiners deduct **2–3 marks** if you do not specifically address *legitimate access* and *trusted identity*.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Negligent" category:** Insider threats are NOT only malicious. If you omit negligent insiders, you lose marks on definitional completeness.
> 3. **Calculation Errors in ALE/SLE/ROSI:** Unit confusion (e.g., treating ARO as a percentage instead of a frequency) is the #1 numerical mistake. **Always show units in the final answer.**
> 4. **Skipping Behavioral Indicators:** When asked about detection, students list only tools (SIEM, DLP) without naming the *indicators* (after-hours login, large downloads). This is worth **1–2 marks** that students routinely lose.
> 5. **Missing Principle of Least Privilege:** PoLP is the *single most tested* insider threat control in KTU papers. Do not omit it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This for Last-Minute Review):**

- ✅ Insider threat = threat from within the trust boundary; actor has **legitimate access**.
- ✅ **Five categories:** Malicious, Negligent, Compromised, Departing, Third-Party.
- ✅ **CERT 4-phase model:** Trigger → Privilege Accumulation → Execution → Post-Incident.
- ✅ **CIA Triad** is the asset under attack; insider threats compromise C, I, and A.
- ✅ **Key formulas:** SLE = AV × EF, ALE = SLE × ARO, ROSI = (ΔALE − Cost) / Cost.
- ✅ **Core controls:** IAM, PoLP, PAM, MFA, DLP, UEBA, SIEM, HR integration.
- ✅ **Key benchmark metrics:** MTTD = 77 days, MTTC = 85 days, Cost = $15.4M/year.
- ✅ **PoLP is the single most-tested concept** — always mention it.
- ✅ **Behavioral indicators:** after-hours activity, large downloads, USB usage, HR signals.
- ✅ **Real-world cases to remember:** Maroochy Water (2000), Snowden (2013), Waymo/Uber (2017).
- ✅ **Detection difficulty of insiders > outsiders** because traffic appears legitimate.
- ✅ **Compromised insider = external attacker using insider credentials** — bridges both categories.

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cybercrime** refers to any illegal, unauthorized, or unethical activity that is carried out using a computer, a network, or networked devices as either the **target** of the offense or as the **instrument/tool** used to commit the offense. In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, cybercrime is broadly classified under the **Indian Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended in 2008)** and various provisions of the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exam-Ready):**
> *"Cybercrime is a criminal activity in which a computer or networked device is used as a tool, target, or both, to commit an illegal act that threatens the security, confidentiality, integrity, or availability of data, systems, or individuals."*

**Key Terminology You Must Memorize:**

| Term | Meaning | Scope for KTU Exam |
|------|---------|---------------------|
| **Target Computer** | The computer is the *victim* of the crime (e.g., hacking a server) | High Frequency |
| **Tool Computer** | The computer is used to *commit* the crime (e.g., sending phishing emails) | High Frequency |
| **Cyber Criminal** | A person who commits a cybercrime | Medium Frequency |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in a system that can be exploited | Medium Frequency |
| **Malware** | Malicious software designed to harm a system | High Frequency |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Digital Bank Heist**
> 
> Imagine a bank vault (your computer) sitting in a city (the internet). Traditionally, criminals had to **physically break in** to steal money. In cybercrime, a thief can sit in another country and use a **digital crowbar** (malware/hacking) to crack open the same vault without ever stepping foot near it. 
> 
> - The **vault** = your computer/data
> - The **digital crowbar** = malware, viruses, or exploit code
> - The **thief** = a cybercriminal, who may be anonymous and located anywhere in the world
> 
> The dangerous part? The thief doesn't need to wear a mask — they just need a **laptop and an internet connection**.

## 1.3 Why Study Cybercrime in KTU 2024?

- **Employability:** Cybercrime investigation is one of the fastest-growing careers in India (NCRB reports cybercrime cases growing at **~15% annually**).
- **Legal Awareness:** Every computer science engineer must understand basic cyber laws because they will build systems that must comply with the **IT Act 2000/2008**.
- **Syllabus Weightage:** Module 1 typically carries **15-20% weightage** in the end-semester examination.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** Questions from Cybercrime usually appear in **Part A (3 marks - definitions)** and occasionally in **Part B (14 marks - classification or case study)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cybercrimes (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

Cybercrimes can be classified along **multiple dimensions**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following three classifications.

### Classification 1: Based on the Role of the Computer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Fundamental Roles (MUST be drawn in exam for full marks):**

1. **Computer as a Target:** The computer system itself is the victim. Attackers aim to gain unauthorized access, damage, or destroy the system.
   - Examples: **Hacking, Virus/Worm attacks, DoS (Denial of Service) attacks, Cyber terrorism**

2. **Computer as a Tool:** The computer is used as an instrument to commit a traditional crime.
   - Examples: **Phishing, Cyber stalking, Identity theft, Fraud, Child pornography**

### Classification 2: Based on the Target of the Crime

| Category | Description | Example Offenses | KTU Frequency |
|----------|-------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Cybercrime Against Individuals** | Targets a single person | Identity theft, cyber stalking, phishing, defamation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Cybercrime Against Property** | Targets digital/physical assets | Software piracy, data theft, vandalism, IP violations | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Cybercrime Against Organizations** | Targets corporate/government bodies | Corporate espionage, DoS, ransomware | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Cybercrime Against Society** | Targets the public at large | Cyber terrorism, pornography, online gambling | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### Classification 3: Common Types of Cybercrimes (Detailed List)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Big 12" Cybercrimes — These Appear in Almost Every KTU Exam:**

1. **Hacking** — Unauthorized access to a computer system. (Sec. 66, IT Act)
2. **Phishing** — Fraudulent emails/websites that steal credentials.
3. **Identity Theft** — Stealing someone's personal information to impersonate them.
4. **Cyber Stalking / Cyber Bullying** — Repeated harassment via digital means.
5. **Malware Attacks (Virus, Worm, Trojan)** — Malicious software that damages systems.
6. **Denial of Service (DoS) / Distributed DoS (DDoS)** — Overwhelming a system to make it unavailable.
7. **Data Diddling** — Tampering with data before/after input.
8. **Email Bombing** — Sending massive volumes of emails to crash a server.
9. **Salami Attack** — Siphoning off tiny amounts (e.g., ₹1) from many accounts.
10. **Logic Bomb** — Malicious code that triggers on a specific event.
11. **Web Jacking** — Hijacking a website and modifying its content.
12. **Software Piracy** — Unauthorized copying/proliferation of licensed software.

## 2.2 The KTU "High-Yield" Reference Matrix (Memorize This Table)

| Cybercrime | IT Act Section | Punishment | Tool/Target | KTU Past Year |
|------------|----------------|------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Hacking** | Sec. 66 | Up to 3 years + fine | Target | Yes (2022) |
| **Publishing obscene info** | Sec. 67 | Up to 5 years + fine | Tool | Yes (2023) |
| **Identity theft** | Sec. 66C | Up to 3 years + fine | Tool | Yes (2023) |
| **Cheating by personation** | Sec. 66D | Up to 3 years + fine | Tool | Common |
| **Data damage** | Sec. 43 / 66F | Varies | Target | Yes (2024) |
| **Cyber terrorism** | Sec. 66F | Life imprisonment | Target | Common |
| **Phishing** | Sec. 66D | Up to 3 years | Tool | Yes (2022) |
| **Child pornography** | Sec. 67B | Up to 7 years + fine | Tool | Yes (2023) |
| **Stalking** | Sec. 354D IPC / IT Act | Up to 3 years | Tool | Common |
| **Email spoofing** | Sec. 463 IPC | Up to 2 years | Tool | Yes (2021) |

## 2.3 Anatomy of a Cyber Attack (The "Kill Chain" Model)

> [!TIP]
> **The Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain (Frequently Asked in KTU):**

1. **Reconnaissance** — Attacker gathers information (e.g., scanning networks).
2. **Weaponization** — Creating a deliverable payload (e.g., malware + exploit).
3. **Delivery** — Transmitting the weapon (e.g., email, USB, web download).
4. **Exploitation** — Triggering the vulnerability in the target system.
5. **Installation** — Installing malware on the victim's system.
6. **Command & Control (C2)** — Establishing remote access to the victim's machine.
7. **Actions on Objectives** — Executing the actual goal (data exfiltration, damage, ransom).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For Software Engineers:** Understanding cybercrime helps build **secure-by-design** applications.
- **For Network Engineers:** Recognizing DoS/DDoS patterns helps in traffic engineering.
- **For Data Scientists:** Identifying phishing patterns involves ML-based classification.
- **For Legal/Compliance:** Organizations must follow **IT Act, GDPR, DPDP Act 2023** — engineers need to know these to design compliant systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Case Study Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** Cybercrime is a descriptive subject. Instead of algebraic derivations, the KTU board tests your ability to **classify, analyze, and apply** concepts. This section will build analytical matrices step-by-step.

## 3.1 Analytical Exercise 1: Classifying a Given Cybercrime Scenario

**Problem:** *"Rahul, a college student, receives an email that appears to be from his bank. The email contains a link that takes him to a fake website identical to his bank's portal. He enters his username and password. The next day, ₹50,000 is missing from his account."*

### Step-by-Step Analysis:

**Step 1 — Identify the Mode of Attack**
The victim received a deceptive email → this is a **Phishing Attack** (Email Phishing specifically).

**Step 2 — Identify the Role of the Computer**
The computer is being used as a **Tool** to commit fraud against an individual → Category: *Cybercrime against individuals, computer as tool.*

**Step 3 — Identify the Legal Provision**

Under the **IT Act 2008**, this falls under:
- **Section 66D** — *Cheating by personation using communication device* → Punishment: Up to 3 years imprisonment + fine up to ₹1 lakh.

**Step 4 — Identify the Type of Cybercriminal**
This is typically a **professional organized cybercriminal** working for financial gain → fits the *financially motivated hacker* profile.

**Step 5 — Suggest Countermeasures**
- Enable **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**
- Verify URLs before clicking
- Use **email filtering** and **anti-phishing browser extensions**
- Report to **cybercrime.gov.in** within **24 hours**

## 3.2 Analytical Exercise 2: The Salami Attack — A Quantitative Perspective

**Problem Statement:** A bank programmer modifies the interest calculation code so that every customer's interest is rounded **down by ₹0.49** and the fractional remainder is diverted to a hidden account. If the bank has **1,000,000 customers** and each has ₹0.49 siphoned, calculate the total theft and identify the attack.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Solution:

**Step 1 — Define the Per-Unit Siphoned Amount**

$$
S_{\text{per customer}} = ₹0.49
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Total Number of Victims**

$$
N = 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Total Siphoned Amount**

$$
T = N \times S_{\text{per customer}}
$$

$$
T = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.49
$$

$$
\boxed{T = ₹4{,}90{,}000}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Attack Type**

This is a classic **Salami Attack** — small, unnoticeable thefts from many victims, which together accumulate to a large sum.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Reference:** The 1998 *City Bank of New York* case where a programmer siphoned fractions of cents and stole over **$1 million** before being caught.

**Step 5 — Identify the Programming Logic Used**

The attack uses **Data Diddling** or **Logic Bomb** techniques:
- Modify the rounding logic to floor instead of round.
- Insert a hidden account number in the destination field.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How to Differentiate Similar Cybercrimes (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Students often confuse the following pairs in exams. This matrix resolves that confusion:**

| Comparison Axis | Virus | Worm | Trojan Horse | Logic Bomb |
|-----------------|-------|------|--------------|------------|
| **Self-replicating?** | Yes (attaches to files) | Yes (network) | No | No |
| **Needs a host file?** | Yes | No | Yes (disguised) | Yes (in code) |
| **Trigger condition** | User runs infected file | Network connectivity | User installs it | Specific event/date |
| **Primary damage** | Data corruption | Network congestion | Backdoor access | Destructive payload |
| **Speed of spread** | Slow | Very fast | Slow | Latent |
| **Example** | CIH (Chernobyl) | ILOVEYOU | Zeus Banking Trojan | Friday the 13th virus |

## 3.4 Analytical Exercise 3: Phishing vs. Pharming vs. Smishing

**Question:** Differentiate between Phishing, Pharming, and Smishing with one example each.

### Step-by-Step Analysis:

**Phishing:**
- Definition: Fraudulent communication (typically email) posing as a legitimate entity to steal credentials.
- Example: An email from "support@paypa1.com" (note the digit 1 instead of 'l') asking the user to verify their account.
- Vector: Email / Web link
- Detection: Check sender domain, hover over links.

**Pharming:**
- Definition: Redirecting users from legitimate websites to fraudulent ones by corrupting DNS resolution (DNS cache poisoning) or by modifying the local hosts file.
- Example: Typing "www.bank.com" in the browser but ending up on a malicious clone.
- Vector: DNS / Hosts file manipulation
- Detection: Hard to detect by user — requires DNSSEC.

**Smishing (SMS Phishing):**
- Definition: Phishing attack delivered via SMS.
- Example: *"Your KYC is expiring! Click http://bit.ly/kyc-verify to avoid account suspension."*
- Vector: SMS / Messaging apps
- Detection: Banks never ask for credentials via SMS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In a 7-mark question, always provide the **Definition + Example + Vector + Countermeasure** for each — that is the exact structure examiners look for.

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Framework for Any Cybercrime Question

For any cybercrime question, use this **4-quadrant framework** to score full marks:

1. **Definition** — 2 marks
2. **Example / Case** — 2 marks
3. **IT Act Section + Punishment** — 2 marks
4. **Prevention / Countermeasure** — 1 mark
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
