# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of IDS – The Three Classification Axes

IDS systems can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**, each appearing frequently in KTU Module 2 questions:

| # | Classification Axis | Types Produced |
|---|--------------------|----------------|
| **Axis A** | Deployment Location | Network-Based (NIDS), Host-Based (HIDS), Hybrid |
| **Axis B** | Detection Methodology | Signature-Based, Anomaly-Based, Stateful Protocol Analysis, Hybrid |
| **Axis C** | Response Capability | Passive IDS, Active IDS (IPS) |

### 2.2 Axis A — Based on Deployment Location

#### 2.2.1 Network-Based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)

- **Placement:** Strategic chokepoints — between firewall and internal LAN, or at network demilitarised zones (DMZ).
- **Scope:** Monitors **all traffic flowing across a subnet**.
- **Mechanism:** Operates in **promiscuous mode**, sniffing packets via a TAP or SPAN port.
- **Examples:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek (Bro), Cisco NetFlow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Cannot analyse **encrypted traffic** without SSL/TLS inspection. Also blind to attacks within a single host.

#### 2.2.2 Host-Based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)

- **Placement:** Installed **on the individual endpoint/server** being protected.
- **Scope:** Monitors system calls, file-system changes, log files (e.g., `/var/log/auth.log` in Linux), registry edits.
- **Mechanism:** Compares current system state against a **known-good baseline**.
- **Examples:** OSSEC, Tripwire, AIDE, Windows Defender ATP.

#### 2.2.3 Hybrid / Distributed IDS (DIDS)

- Combines multiple NIDS + HIDS agents reporting to a **central management console (SIEM)**.
- Examples: OSSIM (AlienVault), IBM QRadar, Splunk Enterprise Security.

### 2.3 Axis B — Based on Detection Methodology (THE MOST EXAMINED AXIS)

#### 2.3.1 Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)

- **Core Idea:** Maintain a database of known attack patterns (signatures/rule-sets).
- **Technique:** Pattern matching (e.g., Snort rules), string matching, regex.
- **Analogy:** *A wanted-poster wall — police arrest anyone matching a printed face.*
- **Pros:** Very low false-positive rate; high accuracy for known attacks.
- **Cons:** **Zero-day attacks** bypass it completely.
- **Detection Latency:** Instantaneous for matched patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics in Signature IDS:**
> - **Database Size:** typically **1,000 – 50,000 rules**.
> - **Matching Speed:** measured in **Mbps** (Mega-packets per second).
> - **Snort Rule Format (KTU Favourite):** `action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (options)`

#### 2.3.2 Anomaly-Based Detection

- **Core Idea:** Builds a **statistical/ML model of "normal" behaviour**; flags deviations.
- **Sub-Types:**
  * **Statistical Anomaly Detection** — mean, variance, multivariate Gaussian.
  * **Protocol Anomaly** — checks RFC compliance (e.g., flagging a TCP packet with SYN+FIN set).
  * **Traffic Rate Anomaly** — DDoS detection via entropy of source IPs.
  * **ML-Based** — KNN, SVM, Random Forest, Autoencoders.
- **Analogy:** *A thermostat that screams when room temperature deviates from 22 °C.*
- **Pros:** Can detect **previously unseen (zero-day)** attacks.
- **Cons:** Higher false-positive rate (FP); requires training period.

#### 2.3.3 Stateful Protocol Analysis

- **Core Idea:** Tracks the **stateful behaviour** of protocols (e.g., FTP, DNS) by validating expected state transitions.
- **Mechanism:** Maintains a state table — flags anomalies like *SMTP MAIL command before HELO*.
- **Pros:** Detects protocol-level attacks (smurf, ping-of-death).
- **Cons:** Computationally expensive; protocol-specific.

#### 2.3.4 Hybrid Detection

- Combines signature + anomaly + stateful methods in parallel or as a decision-fusion ensemble.
- **Modern Reality:** Almost every commercial IDS (Snort 3.x, Suricata) uses a hybrid engine.

### 2.4 Axis C — Based on Response Capability

| Type | Action | Terminates Attack? |
|------|--------|--------------------|
| **Passive IDS** | Logs + Alerts only | ❌ No |
| **Active IDS (IPS)** | Drops packets, blocks IPs, resets connections | ✅ Yes |
| **Inline IDS** | Placed in-line (like firewall) | ✅ Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Confused Pair:**
> - **IDS** = *Detection* → Reactive
> - **IPS** = *Prevention* → Proactive

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Detection Performance Metrics

This table is **essential** for the 14-mark analytical questions.

| Metric | Formula | Range / Ideal | Engineering Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Recall / Sensitivity** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | 0 to 1, **ideal = 1** | % of actual attacks correctly detected |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | 0 to 1, **ideal = 0** | % of legitimate traffic wrongly flagged |
| **True Negative Rate (TNR) / Specificity** | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | 0 to 1, **ideal = 1** | % of legitimate traffic correctly allowed |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | 0 to 1, **ideal = 0** | Missed attacks |
| **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | 0 to 1, **ideal = 1** | Of all alerts raised, how many were real attacks |
| **Accuracy** | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | 0 to 1, **ideal = 1** | Overall correctness |
| **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)** | $F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | 0 to 1, **ideal = 1** | Balanced metric |
| **Matthew's Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | -1 to +1, **ideal = +1** | Robust even with imbalanced classes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Confusion Matrix Layout (KTU Board Convention):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual} \backslash \text{Predicted} & = & & \text{Attack} & & \text{Normal} \\
& \text{Attack} & \quad TP & & \quad FN \\
& \text{Normal} & \quad FP & & \quad TN
\end{aligned}$$

> Where: $TP$ = True Positive (attack correctly flagged), $FN$ = False Negative (missed attack), $FP$ = False Positive (false alarm), $TN$ = True Negative (normal traffic correctly passed).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Sector:** Hybrid IDS in core banking switches — Snort (signature) + ML anomaly engine — feeds Splunk SIEM.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA compliance):** HIDS (OSSEC) on patient-record databases monitors PHI access.
- **Cloud (AWS/Azure):** Distributed IDS agents on every EC2 instance, central analytics via GuardDuty / Sentinel.
- **Industrial Control (SCADA/ICS):** Stateful protocol analysis detects Modbus/DNP3 anomalies.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Problem – Computing IDS Detection Metrics

This is the **classic 14-mark analytical question** format. Solve it step-by-step.

> **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU University Exam Pattern):**
>
> A Network IDS was deployed on a corporate gateway for **24 hours**. During this period it processed **10,000 sessions**, of which **800** were genuine attacks and **9,200** were legitimate. The IDS raised alerts on **750** attack sessions correctly, **50** legitimate sessions wrongly, and missed **50** attack sessions.
>
> **Tasks:**
> (a) Construct the **confusion matrix**.
> (b) Compute the **TPR, FPR, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score**.
> (c) Comment on the IDS's effectiveness.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify Raw Counts from the Problem

Reading the problem carefully:

- Total sessions = $N = 10{,}000$
- Actual attacks = $800$
- Actual normal = $9{,}200$
- IDS raised alert **on attack** → $TP = 750$
- IDS raised alert **on normal** → $FP = 50$
- IDS **missed an attack** → $FN = 50$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute the Missing TN Value

$$TN = \text{Actual Normal} - FP$$

$$TN = 9{,}200 - 50 = 9{,}150$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Construct the Confusion Matrix

| | Predicted: Attack | Predicted: Normal | Row Total |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual: Attack** | $TP = 750$ | $FN = 50$ | $800$ |
| **Actual: Normal** | $FP = 50$ | $TN = 9{,}150$ | $9{,}200$ |
| **Column Total** | $800$ | $9{,}200$ | $10{,}000$ |

**Sanity Check:**
$$TP + FN + FP + TN = 750 + 50 + 50 + 9{,}150 = 10{,}000 \ \checkmark$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute True Positive Rate (TPR / Sensitivity)

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$TPR = \frac{750}{750 + 50} = \frac{750}{800} = 0.9375$$

$$\boxed{TPR = 93.75\,\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The IDS correctly detects **93.75 %** of all real attacks.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Compute False Positive Rate (FPR)

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

$$FPR = \frac{50}{50 + 9{,}150} = \frac{50}{9{,}200} \approx 0.00543$$

$$\boxed{FPR \approx 0.543\,\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Only **0.54 %** of legitimate traffic is wrongly flagged — very low false-alarm burden.

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Compute Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$Precision = \frac{750}{750 + 50} = \frac{750}{800} = 0.9375$$

$$\boxed{Precision = 93.75\,\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Of every 100 alerts, **~94 are real attacks**.

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Compute Overall Accuracy

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FN + FP + TN}$$

$$Accuracy = \frac{750 + 9{,}150}{10{,}000} = \frac{9{,}900}{10{,}000} = 0.99$$

$$\boxed{Accuracy = 99.00\,\%}$$

### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Compute F1-Score

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$$

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.9375 \cdot 0.9375}{0.9375 + 0.9375} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8789}{1.875}$$

$$F1 = \frac{1.7578}{1.875} \approx 0.9375$$

$$\boxed{F1 \approx 0.9375 \ \text{(or } 93.75\,\% \text{)}}$$

### 3.1.9 Step 9 — Engineering Commentary

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
|--------|:---:|--------|
| TPR (Recall) | 93.75 % | ✅ Excellent |
| FPR | 0.54 % | ✅ Excellent |
| Precision | 93.75 % | ✅ Excellent |
| Accuracy | 99.00 % | ✅ Excellent |
| F1-Score | 93.75 % | ✅ Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The IDS is **highly effective** for this dataset. However, because **FNR = 6.25 %**, **50 attacks still slipped through**. A production-grade SOC (Security Operations Centre) would deploy a **hybrid (signature + anomaly) IDS** to push FNR below 1 %.

### 3.2 ROC Curve & Threshold Trade-Off (Theoretical Derivations)

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots TPR vs FPR at varying detection thresholds $\theta$.

$$TPR(\theta) = \int_{\theta}^{\infty} f_{\text{attack}}(x)\,dx$$

$$FPR(\theta) = \int_{\theta}^{\infty} f_{\text{normal}}(x)\,dx$$

Where:
- $f_{\text{attack}}(x)$ = probability density function of attack scores.
- $f_{\text{normal}}(x)$ = probability density function of normal scores.
- $\theta$ = detection threshold (high $\theta$ → fewer false alarms but more missed attacks).

> **Area Under Curve (AUC):**
> $$AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR)\, d(FPR)$$
> - $AUC = 1.0$ → perfect classifier.
> - $AUC = 0.5$ → random guessing.
> - $AUC < 0.5$ → worse than random.

### 3.3 Bayesian Decision Theory (Anomaly IDS Foundation)

Let $H_0$ = normal traffic, $H_1$ = attack. Using Bayes' theorem:

$$P(H_1 \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1)}{P(x \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1) + P(x \mid H_0) \cdot P(H_0)}$$

The IDS raises an alert when:

$$P(H_1 \mid x) > \tau \quad \text{(likelihood-ratio threshold)}$$

Or equivalently via the **likelihood ratio test**:

$$\Lambda(x) = \frac{P(x \mid H_1)}{P(x \mid H_0)} > \frac{\tau \cdot P(H_0)}{(1-\tau) \cdot P(H_1)}$$

This is the **mathematical backbone** of every statistical anomaly detector — frequently cited in KTU answer scripts.

**Architectural Explanation:**

2. Each **internal host** runs an **HIDS agent** that monitors logs and system calls.
3. All NIDS + HIDS alerts stream into a **SIEM (Security Information & Event Management)** console.
4. The **Security Analyst** receives correlated, prioritised alerts and triggers incident-response procedures.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Rule:** Part A answers must be **definition + 2 supporting points** to score full 3 marks. A bare definition gets only 1 mark.

### Q1. Differentiate between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | IDS | IPS |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Action on threat** | Detects and alerts only | Detects and **actively blocks** |
| **Deployment** | Out-of-band (passive tap) | In-line in the data path |
| **Risk** | Cannot stop the attack itself | Risk of single-point-of-failure (drops legitimate traffic on false positives) |
| **Engineering view** | Reactive (sensory) | Proactive (motor response) |

### Q2. List and briefly explain any three types of Intrusion Detection Systems based on detection methodology. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Signature-Based IDS:** Detects attacks by matching traffic against a database of known attack patterns (signatures). High accuracy for known attacks, but cannot detect zero-day exploits. *Example:* Snort.
2. **Anomaly-Based IDS:** Builds a statistical model of normal behaviour and flags deviations. Can detect novel attacks, but generates more false positives. *Example:* ML-based traffic analyser.
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Tracks the state of protocol exchanges (e.g., TCP handshake, DNS queries) and flags invalid state transitions. *Example:* Detecting an FTP `STOR` command before authentication.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Drawing only one classification axis and missing the other two (loss of up to **5 marks**).
> - Confusing "intrusion" with "infection" — intrusion is the *act*, not the *malware*.
> - Forgetting to state the **disadvantage** alongside the advantage (full marks require balanced comparison).
> - Spelling "Snort" as "Snortt" or "Tripwire" as "Trip Wire" — technical terms must be precise.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify Intrusion Detection Systems based on **deployment location**. Explain each type with two real-world examples and one limitation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** detection techniques in detail with a comparative analysis.

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Classification Based on Deployment Location [7 Marks]:**

1. **Network-Based IDS (NIDS):**
   - **Definition:** Monitors network traffic at strategic chokepoints across an entire subnet.
   - **How it works:** Placed in **promiscuous mode** on a switch SPAN port or a network TAP, sniffs every packet.
   - **Examples:** Snort, Suricata, Cisco Firepower.
   - **Limitation:** Cannot decrypt SSL/TLS traffic without inspection; blind to host-level activity.

2. **Host-Based IDS (HIDS):**
   - **Definition:** Resides on an individual host and monitors OS-level events.
   - **How it works:** Analyses system calls, file integrity (hash comparison), log files, and registry changes.
   - **Examples:** OSSEC, Tripwire, AIDE.
   - **Limitation:** Consumes host resources; visible to attackers who may try to disable it.

3. **Hybrid / Distributed IDS (DIDS):**
   - **Definition:** Integrates multiple NIDS + HIDS agents into a unified management framework.
   - **Examples:** AlienVault OSSIM, IBM QRadar, Splunk.
   - **Limitation:** High deployment and tuning complexity.

**(b) Signature-Based vs Anomaly-Based Detection [7 Marks]:**

| Aspect | Signature-Based (Misuse) | Anomaly-Based |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **Principle** | Pattern matching against known attack database | Deviation detection from learned normal profile |
| **Data Source** | Packets, payload strings | Statistical features, behavioural metrics |
| **Algorithm** | String/regex matching, Aho-Corasick | Gaussian, clustering, Neural Networks |
| **Detection of Known Attacks** | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good |
| **Detection of Zero-Day** | ❌ Cannot detect | ✅ Detects deviations |
| **False Positive Rate** | Very Low | Relatively High |
| **Training Period** | None | Required (weeks) |
| **Examples** | Snort rules, ClamAV | KNN, Autoencoders, LSTM |

**Conclusion:** Modern deployments adopt a **hybrid IDS** combining both — signature for high accuracy on known threats and anomaly for unknown threats.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** An IDS tested on 5,000 network sessions produced the following: $TP = 850$, $FN = 150$, $FP = 100$, $TN = 3{,}900$. Compute the **TPR, FPR, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score**. Comment on the IDS effectiveness.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Stateful Protocol Analysis** detection method with two real attack scenarios.

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Performance Metric Computation [7 Marks]:**

Given: $TP = 850$, $FN = 150$, $FP = 100$, $TN = 3{,}900$.

**Verification:** $TP + FN + FP + TN = 850 + 150 + 100 + 3{,}900 = 5{,}000 \ \checkmark$

**Step 1 — True Positive Rate (TPR):**

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{850}{850 + 150} = \frac{850}{1{,}000} = 0.85$$

$$\boxed{TPR = 85\,\%}$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate (FPR):**

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{100}{100 + 3{,}900} = \frac{100}{4{,}000} = 0.025$$

$$\boxed{FPR = 2.5\,\%}$$

**Step 3 — Precision:**

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{850}{850 + 100} = \frac{850}{950} \approx 0.8947$$

$$\boxed{Precision \approx 89.47\,\%}$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{5{,}000} = \frac{850 + 3{,}900}{5{,}000} = \frac{4{,}750}{5{,}000} = 0.95$$

$$\boxed{Accuracy = 95\,\%}$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8947 \cdot 0.85}{0.8947 + 0.85} = \frac{1.5210}{1.7447} \approx 0.8719$$

$$\boxed{F1 \approx 87.19\,\%}$$

**Effectiveness Comment [1 Mark]:** The IDS shows **good overall accuracy (95 %)** with a reasonable FPR. However, the **15 % miss rate (FNR)** is concerning for high-security environments — a hybrid signature+anomaly deployment is recommended to drive FNR below 5 %.

**(b) Stateful Protocol Analysis [7 Marks]:**

**Definition [2 Marks]:** Stateful Protocol Analysis (SPA) is a detection method that compares observed protocol behaviour against a model of **expected, RFC-compliant state transitions**. The IDS maintains a state table for each connection and raises an alert when the observed sequence violates the protocol specification.

**How it works [2 Marks]:**
1. The IDS builds a state-machine model for protocols (HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP).
2. Each incoming packet updates the connection's state.
3. Any state transition not present in the legitimate state-machine triggers an alert.

**Real Attack Scenarios [3 Marks]:**

1. **SMTP State Violation:** An attacker issues a `MAIL FROM` command **before** sending the required `HELO/EHLO` greeting. A stateful SMTP IDS flags this as a state-machine violation — characteristic of spambot tools.

2. **FTP Bounce Attack (RFC 959 violation):** The attacker uses the `PORT` command to instruct the FTP server to connect to a third-party host (e.g., an internal database on port 3306). A stateful FTP IDS detects that the `PORT` command's IP does not match the legitimate client IP, raising an alert.

3. **DNS Tunneling Detection:** A stateful DNS analyser notices an abnormally long sequence of `TXT` queries with high-entropy subdomains — typical of data exfiltration over DNS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert (Common 14-Mark Killer):** Many students write "SPA uses signatures" — this is **incorrect**. SPA uses **protocol state machines**, not byte patterns. Spelling this out correctly in the exam earns the methodology marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Section Before Exam):**

- ✅ **Definition:** IDS = *monitors & detects*, IPS = *monitors & blocks*. **Never** use them interchangeably.
- ✅ **3 A's of IDS Functionality:** *Monitor → Analyse → Alert.*
- ✅ **Three Classification Axes:**
  * **Deployment** → NIDS, HIDS, Hybrid/DIDS.
  * **Detection Method** → Signature, Anomaly, Stateful Protocol Analysis, Hybrid.
  * **Response** → Passive IDS, Active IDS / IPS, Inline.
- ✅ **Signature-Based:** Uses **pattern matching**, low FP, **cannot detect zero-days**.
- ✅ **Anomaly-Based:** Uses **statistical / ML models**, can detect zero-days, **higher FP**.
- ✅ **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Tracks **state transitions**, detects protocol misuse.
- ✅ **NIDS Tools:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek. **HIDS Tools:** OSSEC, Tripwire, AIDE.
- ✅ **Confusion Matrix Counts:** Always satisfy $TP + FN + FP + TN = N$ (total traffic).
- ✅ **Key Formulas (Commit to Memory):**
  * $TPR = TP / (TP + FN)$
  * $FPR = FP / (FP + TN)$
  * $Precision = TP / (TP + FP)$
  * $Accuracy = (TP + TN) / N$
  * $F1 = 2 \cdot (P \cdot R) / (P + R)$
- ✅ **ROC Curve:** Plots TPR vs FPR; **AUC = 1** is perfect; **AUC = 0.5** is random.
- ✅ **Bayesian Likelihood Ratio:** $\Lambda(x) = P(x \mid H_1) / P(x \mid H_0)$ — threshold-based detection.
- ✅ **Hybrid IDS = Signature + Anomaly + Stateful** — modern best practice.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Detection Methods — "SASh":** **S**ignature, **A**nomaly, **S**tateful, **h**ybrid.
- ✅ **Golden Exam Line:** *"An IDS is the sensory nervous system of a secure network; an IPS is its reflex arc."*
- ✅ **Most Tested 14-Mark Pattern:** Confusion matrix computation + classification explanation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goals of IDS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Goals of IDS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism — either hardware, software, or a hybrid combination — that monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts. The **Goals of IDS** define the fundamental security objectives that any intrusion detection framework is designed to achieve, forming the operational backbone of proactive cyber defense architectures.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the goals of an IDS are classified along multiple security dimensions, principally the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability), with additional extensions covering **Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, and Accountability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT864 - Module 2):** The goals of an IDS are not just "detecting intrusions." They extend to a multi-layered security framework encompassing prevention, detection, response, deterrence, and post-attack recovery. Examiners frequently test the nuanced differences between *goals* and *functions* of an IDS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building a Digital Security Guard

Imagine a **highly trained security guard** stationed at the entrance of a large corporate office building:

- The guard does not build the building (that's the **firewall** — prevention).
- The guard *watches* who enters, *records* their behavior, *raises alarms* when something suspicious occurs, and *provides evidence* when an incident happens.

In this analogy, the security guard = **IDS**, the building = **network/system**, and the guard's job description (the *goals*) includes:

1. **Watching** every movement → *Monitoring*
2. **Identifying** known thieves from a list → *Detection (Signature-based)*
3. **Spotting** suspicious behavior even without a list → *Detection (Anomaly-based)*
4. **Raising alarms** silently or loudly → *Alerting & Response*
5. **Documenting** everything for later legal use → *Logging & Forensics*
6. **Discouraging** attackers from even trying → *Deterrence*
7. **Helping the system recover** after a breach → *Recovery Support*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The fundamental goal of an IDS is *not* to prevent intrusions (that is the role of firewalls, IPS, and access control). The IDS acts as a **second line of defense** whose primary mission is **detection, documentation, and deterrence**, with the explicit purpose of *knowing* about attacks that bypass preventive controls.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of IDS Goals — The Master List

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify and articulate the following **primary and secondary goals** of an IDS:

### Primary Goals (CIA Triad Anchored)

| # | Goal | Security Dimension |
|---|------|--------------------|
| 1 | Ensure **Confidentiality** of data and resources | CIA — C |
| 2 | Maintain **Integrity** of system and data | CIA — I |
| 3 | Guarantee **Availability** of services | CIA — A |

### Secondary Goals (Extended Security Objectives)

| # | Goal | Functional Purpose |
|---|------|--------------------|
| 4 | **Authentication** verification | Confirm identity of legitimate users |
| 5 | **Authorization** enforcement | Ensure users access only permitted resources |
| 6 | **Accountability** through logging | Maintain audit trails |
| 7 | **Non-Repudiation** | Prevent denial of actions performed |
| 8 | **Deterrence** | Discourage potential attackers |
| 9 | **Response & Recovery support** | Enable post-incident action |
| 10 | **Compliance** | Meet regulatory standards (e.g., PCI-DSS, HIPAA) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "List the goals of IDS," always begin with the **CIA Triad** (3 marks guaranteed) and then expand to **secondary goals** (additional 2-3 marks). This sequence mirrors the KTU valuation key preference order.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of IDS Goals — Detailed Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Detection (The Core Mission)

Detection is the **primary and most fundamental goal** of any IDS. It involves identifying activities that violate security policies or match known attack signatures.

**Sub-objectives of Detection:**
- **Per-event detection**: Real-time flagging of individual suspicious events.
- **Aggregate detection**: Correlating multiple low-severity events to identify a coordinated attack (e.g., a slow port scan spread over hours).
- **Multi-stage attack detection**: Recognizing that events A → B → C together constitute a known attack chain (e.g., reconnaissance → exploitation → privilege escalation).

**Detection mechanisms employed to achieve this goal:**
- **Signature-based detection** (Misuse detection): Compares activity patterns against a database of known attack signatures.
- **Anomaly-based detection**: Establishes a baseline of "normal" behavior and flags deviations.
- **Stateful protocol analysis**: Tracks protocol states to identify sequences that violate specifications.
- **Hybrid detection**: Combines both signature and anomaly approaches for higher accuracy.

### Pillar 2: Prevention (Deterrence & Preemption)

Although prevention is traditionally a firewall function, modern IDS goals include **deterrence** — the act of making the network *less attractive* to attackers.

**Methods:**
- Visible IDS deployment announcements (legal warnings).
- Honeypots and honeynets (decoy systems that lure attackers).
- Banner messages stating "This system is monitored."

> [!NOTE]
> The mere *presence* of an IDS can shift attackers to softer targets, achieving a **defensive goal without active intervention**.

### Pillar 3: Response (Reaction & Containment)

The IDS goal here is to **minimize damage** when an intrusion is detected. This involves:

- **Passive response**: Generate alerts, log events, notify administrators.
- **Active response** (in IPS — Intrusion Prevention Systems): Block traffic, kill sessions, quarantine hosts, modify firewall rules.

The response goal is measured by two key metrics:
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)**: Average time between attack initiation and IDS detection.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)**: Average time between detection and containment.

### Pillar 4: Recovery (Post-Incident Restoration)

After detection and response, the IDS supports recovery by:
- Providing **forensic logs** for incident reconstruction.
- Identifying **compromised systems** for re-imaging.
- Supplying **attack vectors** used so they can be patched.

### Pillar 5: Compliance & Accountability

The IDS supports organizational goals by:
- Maintaining **audit trails** for regulatory compliance (GDPR, SOX, HIPAA, PCI-DSS).
- Enforcing **non-repudiation** — users cannot deny actions logged by the IDS.
- Supporting **legal prosecution** with admissible digital evidence.

## 2.2 Why These Goals Matter — The "Why" Behind Each Pillar

| Goal | Underlying Security Need | Real-World Consequence if Missing |
|------|--------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Confidentiality | Protect sensitive data (PII, trade secrets) | Data breaches, identity theft |
| Integrity | Ensure data is not tampered with | Financial fraud, corrupted records |
| Availability | Services must remain accessible | DDoS outages, business loss |
| Authentication | Verify legitimate users | Impersonation, unauthorized access |
| Authorization | Enforce least-privilege principle | Privilege escalation attacks |
| Accountability | Trace actions to individuals | Inability to prosecute attackers |
| Non-repudiation | Prevent denial of actions | Legal disputes, audit failures |
| Deterrence | Reduce attack likelihood | Increased attack surface |
| Response | Limit breach damage | Prolonged, escalating compromise |
| Recovery | Restore normal operations | Extended downtime, data loss |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IDS Performance Metrics — High-Yield Formulas for KTU**

| Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
|--------|---------|-------------|------|
| True Positive Rate (TPR) / Detection Rate | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Proportion of actual attacks correctly detected | Percentage (0-100%) |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Proportion of normal traffic falsely flagged | Percentage (0-100%) |
| False Negative Rate (FNR) | $FNR = \frac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | Proportion of attacks missed by IDS | Percentage (0-100%) |
| Precision | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Reliability of IDS alerts | Ratio (0-1) |
| Recall (Sensitivity) | $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Same as TPR | Ratio (0-1) |
| F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \times \frac{Precision \times Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | Ratio (0-1) |
| Accuracy | $Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | Percentage |
| Base Rate Fallacy | $P(Attack \vert Alert) = \frac{P(Alert \vert Attack) \times P(Attack)}{P(Alert)}$ | Bayesian probability of true attack given alert | Probability |
| MTTD | $MTTD = \frac{\sum (t_{detected} - t_{occurred})}{N_{incidents}}$ | Average detection delay | Time units |
| MTTR | $MTTR = \frac{\sum (t_{contained} - t_{detected})}{N_{incidents}}$ | Average response time | Time units |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on LaTeX in tables:** All vertical bars (|) for absolute value and conditional probability have been replaced with the LaTeX-safe `\vert` symbol to prevent markdown table rendering failures.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The goals of an IDS are not abstract academic constructs — they directly map to **operational engineering requirements** in:

- **Enterprise networks**: SOC (Security Operations Center) analysts use IDS alerts to triage threats.
- **Critical infrastructure**: SCADA/ICS systems deploy IDS to detect nation-state attacks on power grids.
- **Cloud environments**: AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender embody IDS goals in cloud-native form.
- **IoT ecosystems**: Lightweight IDS variants achieve the same goals on resource-constrained devices.
- **Regulated industries**: Banking, healthcare, and government deploy IDS to satisfy compliance mandates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Goal Classification Framework

## 3.1 Detailed Goal Classification Derivation

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to systematically classify the goals of an IDS. Below is the **exhaustive analytical breakdown** showing how each goal maps to detection methodology, attack class, and security dimension.

### Step 1: Establish the Foundational Security Triad

The three foundational goals of any IDS are derived directly from the **CIA Triad**, which is universally accepted in information security:

$$CIA = \{Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability\}$$

Each of these is a **goal** because it represents a *desired state* the IDS is trying to preserve. The IDS itself does not directly *provide* CIA; rather, it **monitors and reports** on violations of CIA so that human or automated systems can act.

### Step 2: Extend to Authentication, Authorization, Accountability (AAA)

Beyond CIA, the IDS goals extend to the **AAA framework**, which governs identity and access:

$$AAA = \{Authentication, Authorization, Accountability\}$$

The IDS supports these goals by:
- Logging authentication attempts (success/failure).
- Detecting authorization violations (privilege escalation).
- Maintaining accountability trails (who did what, when, from where).

### Step 3: Incorporate Non-Repudiation and Deterrence

The final layer of IDS goals involves **legal and psychological** dimensions:

$$Extended\_Goals = \{NonRepudiation, Deterrence, Response, Recovery, Compliance\}$$

### Step 4: Consolidate the Master Goal Set

The **complete set of IDS goals** under KTU 2024 classification is therefore:

$$
Goals_{IDS} = \{C, I, A\} \cup \{AuthN, AuthZ, Accountability\} \cup \{NonRepudiation, Deterrence, Response, Recovery, Compliance\}
$$

$$
\mid Goals_{IDS} \mid = 11 \text{ distinct objectives}
$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Goal-to-Attack Mapping

For each goal, the IDS must detect specific attack classes. Below is the exhaustive mapping:

| IDS Goal | Attack Class Detected | Example Attack |
|----------|----------------------|----------------|
| Confidentiality | Eavesdropping, data exfiltration | Man-in-the-Middle, packet sniffing |
| Integrity | Unauthorized modification | SQL injection, file tampering |
| Availability | Denial of service | SYN flood, DDoS, ransomware encryption |
| Authentication | Identity spoofing | Password brute force, credential stuffing |
| Authorization | Privilege escalation | Buffer overflow → root shell |
| Accountability | Log tampering, evasion | Log injection, timestamp manipulation |
| Non-Repudiation | Action denial, session hijacking | Cookie theft, replay attacks |
| Deterrence | Pre-attack reconnaissance | Port scanning, OS fingerprinting |
| Response | Active exploitation | Zero-day exploit in progress |
| Recovery | Post-exploitation artifacts | Backdoors, rootkits, persistence mechanisms |
| Compliance | Policy violations | Unauthorized software installation, unencrypted data transmission |

## 3.3 Detection Coverage Derivation

Let $G = \{g_1, g_2, ..., g_n\}$ be the set of IDS goals, and $A = \{a_1, a_2, ..., a_m\}$ be the set of known attack types. A **detection function** $D$ maps each attack to the goals it violates:

$$D: A \rightarrow 2^G$$

For example, a **SQL injection attack** violates:

$$
D(SQLi) = \{Integrity, Confidentiality, Authorization\}
$$

The **coverage** of an IDS is then defined as:

$$
Coverage_{IDS} = \frac{\vert \{g_i \in G : \exists a_j \in A, g_i \in D(a_j)\} \vert}{\vert G \vert}
$$

An ideal IDS has $Coverage_{IDS} = 1.0$ (100% of goals are addressable by detection rules).

## 3.4 Trade-off Analysis: Detection Rate vs. False Positive Rate

A critical analytical concept in IDS goal design is the **inverse relationship** between detection thoroughness and false alarm generation:

$$
\uparrow Detection\_Rate \Rightarrow \uparrow False\_Positive\_Rate \quad (\text{typically})
$$

This trade-off is visualized through the **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve**, where:

$$
TPR = f(FPR)
$$

The optimal IDS operating point is at the **"knee" of the ROC curve** — where small increases in TPR require large increases in FPR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Examiners love asking "What is the biggest challenge in achieving the goals of an IDS?" The correct answer emphasizes the **false positive vs. false negative trade-off**, because:
> - Too many false positives → "alert fatigue" → real attacks ignored (high FNR).
> - Too many false negatives → undetected breaches (failure of the primary goal).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Goal Architecture of IDS

The following diagram presents the complete hierarchical structure of IDS goals as classified under the KTU 2024 scheme:

```mermaid

    B1 --> E1[Detect Eavesdropping and Data Exfiltration]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: IDS Goal Achievement Workflow

The following flowchart illustrates **how** the goals of an IDS are achieved through the detection-response-recovery lifecycle:

```mermaid

    F --> G
    
    G -->|Yes - Known Attack| H[Generate Alert]
    G -->|Yes - Anomaly| I[Behavioral Score]

    I --> K{Score Above Threshold}

    K -->|No| J
    
    H --> L[Log to SIEM]
    H --> M[Notify SOC Analyst]

    N --> O[Active Containment IPS]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IDS Goal Realization

For understanding how the goals are operationally realized, the following block diagram shows the layered architecture:


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the goals of an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) with reference to the CIA Triad.

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**

The goals of an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) are the security objectives it is designed to achieve. The primary goals, anchored in the **CIA Triad**, are:

1. **Confidentiality (1 mark):** The IDS aims to detect and report any unauthorized attempts to access, disclose, or steal sensitive information, ensuring that data is accessible only to authorized entities.

2. **Integrity (1 mark):** The IDS monitors for any unauthorized modification, deletion, or tampering of data and system configurations, ensuring that information remains accurate and trustworthy.

3. **Availability (1 mark):** The IDS identifies Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks and other threats that could disrupt services, ensuring that systems and data remain accessible to legitimate users when needed.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for C, I, A with brief explanation.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between the goals of detection and the goal of deterrence in an IDS.

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**

| Aspect | Detection Goal | Deterrence Goal |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Actively identifying and flagging actual intrusions in progress or after they occur | Discouraging attackers from attempting intrusions in the first place |
| **Mechanism (1 mark)** | Uses signature matching, anomaly analysis, and stateful inspection | Achieved through visible IDS deployment, legal warnings, honeypots, and banner messages |
| **Timing (1 mark)** | Reactive — triggered when an attack is in progress or has occurred | Proactive — operates before any attack begins |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**"With a neat block diagram, explain the various goals of an Intrusion Detection System. Discuss how these goals support the overall security posture of an organization."** `[14 Marks]`

#### Part (a) — Explain the various goals of IDS with a neat diagram. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**

An Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is a security tool designed to monitor network or system activities for malicious behavior. The goals of an IDS are the security objectives it aims to achieve, which extend far beyond simple "intrusion detection."

**Step 2: Classification of Goals (4 marks)**

The goals of IDS can be classified into **three broad categories**:

**A. Primary Goals — CIA Triad:**
- **Confidentiality:** Detect unauthorized data access, eavesdropping, and exfiltration attacks (e.g., packet sniffing, man-in-the-middle).
- **Integrity:** Detect unauthorized modifications, tampering, and corruption of data (e.g., SQL injection, file system tampering).
- **Availability:** Detect attacks that disrupt service access (e.g., DDoS, SYN flood, ransomware).

**B. Secondary Goals — Extended Security:**
- **Authentication:** Detect identity spoofing, credential theft, and brute-force attacks.
- **Authorization:** Identify privilege escalation attempts.
- **Accountability:** Maintain tamper-resistant audit logs of all activities.
- **Non-Repudiation:** Ensure that logged actions cannot be denied by the perpetrator.

**C. Operational Goals — Lifecycle Support:**
- **Deterrence:** Discourage attacks through visible deployment and honeypots.
- **Response and Containment:** Trigger alerts and, in advanced systems, active blocking.
- **Recovery and Forensics:** Provide logs for post-incident investigation and system restoration.
- **Compliance:** Meet regulatory mandates (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS).

**Step 3: Block Diagram (2 marks)**

```
+-----------------------------------------+
|           GOALS OF IDS                 |
+-----------------------------------------+
|  PRIMARY:                               |
|   - Confidentiality                     |
|   - Integrity                           |
|   - Availability                        |
|                                         |
|  SECONDARY:                             |
|   - Authentication                      |
|   - Authorization                       |
|   - Accountability / Non-Repudiation    |
|                                         |
|  OPERATIONAL:                           |
|   - Deterrence                          |
|   - Response & Recovery                 |
|   - Compliance                          |
+-----------------------------------------+
         |                |
         v                v
   [Detection Engine]  [Audit Logs]
```

#### Part (b) — Discuss how these goals support the organizational security posture. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Defense-in-Depth Contribution (2 marks)**

The goals of IDS contribute to the **defense-in-depth strategy** by serving as the second line of defense after preventive controls (firewalls, access control). When preventive measures fail, IDS goals ensure that intrusions are still detected and contained.

**Step 2: Risk Management (2 marks)**

Each goal maps to a specific risk category:
- Confidentiality goal → Mitigates data breach risk.
- Integrity goal → Mitigates fraud and corruption risk.
- Availability goal → Mitigates business continuity risk.
- Accountability goal → Mitigates legal and reputational risk.

**Step 3: Compliance and Legal Support (1.5 marks)**

By achieving the compliance and non-repudiation goals, the IDS provides admissible evidence for legal proceedings and satisfies regulatory audit requirements, reducing the organization's exposure to penalties.

**Step 4: Operational Continuity (1.5 marks)**

The response, recovery, and deterrence goals ensure that the organization can maintain business operations during and after a security incident, with minimal downtime and rapid restoration of services.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**"Explain the primary and secondary goals of IDS. How are these goals achieved through different detection methodologies? Discuss the trade-offs involved."** `[14 Marks]`

#### Part (a) — Explain primary and secondary goals of IDS. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Primary Goals — CIA Triad (3.5 marks)**

The **primary goals** of IDS form the foundation of information security and represent the three pillars of the **CIA Triad**:

1. **Confidentiality (1 mark):** The IDS must detect and report any activity that could lead to unauthorized disclosure of sensitive information. This includes passive attacks (eavesdropping, traffic analysis) and active attacks that exfiltrate data.

2. **Integrity (1 mark):** The IDS monitors for any unauthorized modification, insertion, deletion, or replay of data. This goal protects against attacks such as SQL injection, man-in-the-middle tampering, and malware-induced file corruption.

3. **Availability (1 mark):** The IDS must identify attacks aimed at denying service to legitimate users, such as volumetric DDoS attacks, protocol exhaustion attacks (SYN flood), and ransomware that encrypts critical data.

4. **Interconnection (0.5 marks):** These three goals are interdependent — compromising one often cascades to the others. For example, a successful DoS attack (availability breach) may also be used as a smokescreen for data theft (confidentiality breach).

**Step 2: Secondary Goals — Extended Security Objectives (3.5 marks)**

The **secondary goals** of IDS extend beyond the CIA Triad to encompass identity, access, and legal dimensions:

1. **Authentication (0.7 marks):** Verify the identity of users and detect impersonation, credential theft, and brute-force attacks.

2. **Authorization (0.7 marks):** Ensure that authenticated users access only resources for which they have permissions; detect privilege escalation.

3. **Accountability (0.7 marks):** Maintain detailed, tamper-resistant logs of all system and user activities to trace actions back to specific individuals.

4. **Non-Repudiation (0.7 marks):** Provide cryptographic guarantees that logged actions cannot be denied by the actor.

5. **Deterrence, Response, Recovery, and Compliance (0.7 marks):** These additional goals ensure proactive attack prevention, incident containment, post-incident restoration, and regulatory adherence.

#### Part (b) — How are these goals achieved through detection methodologies? Discuss trade-offs. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Detection Methodologies (3 marks)**

The goals of IDS are achieved through **three primary detection methodologies**:

1. **Signature-based Detection (1 mark):** Compares observed events against a database of known attack patterns (signatures).
   - Achieves: Detection of known attacks, accountability through logging.
   - Limitation: Cannot detect zero-day attacks; goal of detecting novel threats is unmet.

2. **Anomaly-based Detection (1 mark):** Establishes a baseline of normal behavior and flags statistical deviations.
   - Achieves: Detection of unknown attacks, behavioral integrity monitoring.
   - Limitation: High false positive rate, leading to "alert fatigue."

3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis (1 mark):** Tracks protocol states to identify deviations from specifications.
   - Achieves: Detection of protocol-based attacks, availability protection.
   - Limitation: Computationally expensive; requires deep protocol knowledge.

**Step 2: Trade-offs (4 marks)**

| Trade-off | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Detection Rate vs. False Positives (1.5 marks)** | Increasing the detection rate (sensitivity) typically increases the false positive rate. The goal of "detecting all attacks" conflicts with the goal of "maintaining operational efficiency." |
| **Detection Speed vs. Accuracy (1 mark)** | Real-time detection may sacrifice deep analysis accuracy; deep forensic analysis may not meet real-time response goals. |
| **Coverage vs. Performance (1 mark)** | Comprehensive coverage of all 11 goals requires extensive sensors and processing power, impacting network performance. |
| **Privacy vs. Accountability (0.5 marks)** | Deep packet inspection for confidentiality goals may conflict with user privacy expectations and regulations. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "goals" with "functions" or "types":** Students often write "the goals of IDS are signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful." This is **WRONG** — these are *detection methods*, not *goals*. Goals are security objectives (CIA + extended).
> 2. **Omitting the CIA Triad foundation:** Always start with C, I, A as the first three goals. Examiners allocate 3 marks specifically for this.
> 3. **Ignoring secondary goals:** Listing only the CIA Triad without non-repudiation, accountability, or deterrence results in losing 2-3 marks.
> 4. **No diagram in 14-mark questions:** A neat block diagram or flowchart is **mandatory** for full marks in Part (a) of 7-mark sub-questions.
> 5. **Skipping trade-off analysis:** In questions about "how goals are achieved," examiners expect discussion of the **false positive vs. false negative trade-off** and the **ROC curve** concept.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Goals of IDS**

### Must-Know Core Concepts

- ✅ The **primary goals** of IDS are **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** (CIA Triad).
- ✅ The **secondary goals** include **Authentication, Authorization, Accountability, Non-Repudiation, Deterrence, Response, Recovery, and Compliance**.
- ✅ IDS goals are **proactive + reactive** — they detect (reactive) and deter (proactive).
- ✅ The primary goal is **detection**, not prevention (prevention is the firewall's job).

### Key Distinctions to Remember

- ✅ **Goals ≠ Functions**: Goals are *what the IDS aims to achieve*; functions are *how it achieves them*.
- ✅ **Goals ≠ Types**: Types of IDS are NIDS, HIDS, Hybrid IDS; these are categories, not goals.
- ✅ **Goals ≠ Detection Methods**: Signature, anomaly, and stateful are methods, not goals.

### Critical Metrics

- ✅ **TPR** (True Positive Rate) = Detection effectiveness.
- ✅ **FPR** (False Positive Rate) = Alert accuracy.
- ✅ **F1-Score** balances precision and recall.
- ✅ **MTTD** and **MTTR** measure response efficiency.

### Common Exam Traps

- ✅ Writing "to detect intrusions" as the only goal → incomplete answer.
- ✅ Confusing "goals" with "features" or "capabilities."
- ✅ Forgetting the **deterrence** goal (psychological/strategic dimension).
- ✅ Missing the **non-repudiation** and **accountability** goals (legal dimension).

### One-Line Definitions for Quick Recall

- **Confidentiality Goal:** Prevent unauthorized data disclosure.
- **Integrity Goal:** Prevent unauthorized data modification.
- **Availability Goal:** Ensure uninterrupted service access.
- **Authentication Goal:** Verify user identity.
- **Authorization Goal:** Enforce access permissions.
- **Accountability Goal:** Trace actions to individuals.
- **Non-Repudiation Goal:** Prevent denial of performed actions.
- **Deterrence Goal:** Discourage attacks before they occur.
- **Response Goal:** Contain damage after detection.
- **Recovery Goal:** Restore normal operations.
- **Compliance Goal:** Meet regulatory and legal standards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDS Strengths and Limitations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IDS Strengths and Limitations

# Module 2 — Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS): Strengths and Limitations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security technology — hardware or software — that monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts, and produces reports to a management station. The **strengths** of an IDS describe the capabilities that enhance an organization's security posture, while the **limitations** are the inherent weaknesses, blind spots, and operational challenges that prevent an IDS from being a complete security solution on its own.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 expects students to critically evaluate **why an IDS alone is insufficient** — i.e., you must know not only what an IDS *can do*, but also what it *cannot do* and *why it fails* in certain adversarial scenarios. Board questions frequently target this exact "limitations + countermeasure" linkage.

### 1.2 Key IDS Operational Categories (Quick Recall)

| IDS Type | Monitoring Scope | Detection Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **NIDS** (Network-based IDS) | Entire network segments / packet flows | Signature & anomaly on traffic |
| **HIDS** (Host-based IDS) | Single host (logs, syscalls, files) | Local behavioral analysis |
| **Hybrid IDS** | Combined host + network sensors | Correlation of both feeds |
| **Signature-based (Misuse)** | Pattern database matching | Known attack signatures |
| **Anomaly-based** | Statistical / ML baseline | Deviation from normal profile |

> [!NOTE]
> Strengths and limitations differ **sharply** between signature-based and anomaly-based IDS. Always state the *type* of IDS before listing its pros/cons — this is a frequent 3-mark exam discriminator.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Apartment Security Guard

Imagine your apartment complex has two guards at the gate:

- **Guard A (Signature-based IDS)** — He has a printed list of photos of all banned people. If someone whose face matches the list tries to enter, he raises an alarm immediately. He is **fast, accurate for known threats, and never panics about regular residents**. But if a new criminal (whose photo isn't on the list) walks in wearing a different disguise, he **completely misses them**.

- **Guard B (Anomaly-based IDS)** — He has memorized the *typical behavior* of residents (timing, walking pace, bags they carry). If someone acts "oddly," he gets suspicious. He can catch **new, unknown criminals** but often **mistakes the pizza delivery boy for a thief** (false alarm).

Now think of the **complex's CCTV room** as the *management console*. The guards (IDS) can detect, log, and raise alarms — but they **cannot physically stop** the criminal. That's the job of the IPS or security door. **This single insight — IDS detects but does not prevent — is the heart of its limitations.**

### 1.4 The Critical Distinction: IDS vs IPS

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU mistake: writing that an IDS *"blocks"* attacks. **IDS only detects and alerts.** The blocking/inline-dropping variant is an **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)**. Strengths/limitations questions often hinge on this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Strengths of IDS

### A. Detection Capabilities

1. **Real-time / Near-real-time Monitoring**
   - Continuous surveillance of packets, logs, and system calls.
   - Enables prompt incident response before damage escalates.

2. **Centralized Visibility (NIDS advantage)**
   - A single NIDS sensor placed at a choke point (e.g., perimeter router, DMZ) can monitor traffic for the **entire subnet**, providing economical wide-area visibility.

3. **Granular Host-level Forensics (HIDS advantage)**
   - HIDS captures what NIDS cannot see: process execution, file integrity changes, registry edits, rootkit behavior at kernel level.

4. **Detection of Known and Unknown Threats**
   - **Signature IDS** → high accuracy on *known* malware, exploits, and CVE-pattern attacks.
   - **Anomaly IDS** → potential detection of *zero-day* attacks by flagging deviations from baseline behavior.

5. **Policy Enforcement and Compliance Logging**
   - Generates audit trails required by standards like **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, and NIST SP 800-53**.

6. **Stealth / Passive Operation**
   - NIDS operates in **promiscuous mode** on a switched port (via port mirroring / SPAN) — it is *invisible* to attackers, providing surveillance without altering the network.

7. **Extensibility through Rule Updates**
   - Signature databases (e.g., Snort VRT rules, Emerging Threats) can be updated frequently, giving adaptive coverage against newly disclosed CVEs.

8. **Data Source for SIEM / SOC Correlation**
   - IDS alerts feed Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) platforms, enabling **cross-event correlation** and threat intelligence enrichment.

### B. Operational Strengths

| Strength | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| Layered Defense (Defense-in-Depth) | IDS adds a 2nd line of defense behind firewalls |
| Forensic Evidence | Log retention supports post-incident investigation |
| Deterrence | Visible IDS presence discourages casual attackers |
| Scalability (NIDS) | One sensor covers a subnet of hundreds of hosts |

---

## 2.2 Structural Limitations of IDS

### A. Inherent Design Limitations

1. **Detection Without Prevention** *(The #1 KTU Limitation)*
   - IDS is *out-of-band* — it cannot block packets in real time.
   - By the time an alert is generated, the attack may have already succeeded.

2. **False Positive Problem (Anomaly-based IDS)**
   - Legitimate but unusual traffic (e.g., a backup job, a new mobile user) is flagged as malicious.
   - Causes **alert fatigue**, where analysts begin ignoring warnings.

3. **False Negative Problem (Signature-based IDS)**
   - Zero-day attacks and **polymorphic malware** evade signature matching entirely.
   - Encryption of payload hides attack vectors from deep-packet inspection.

4. **Encrypted Traffic Blindness**
   - HTTPS, SSH, TLS-tunneled traffic prevents payload inspection.
   - Modern networks carry **>80% encrypted traffic**, drastically reducing NIDS effectiveness.

5. **High Resource Consumption**
   - Deep packet inspection at line-rate on 10 Gbps+ links demands **multi-core CPUs, large RAM, and high-speed disk I/O**.
   - Cost grows non-linearly with bandwidth.

6. **Evasion Techniques**
   - Attackers use **fragmentation, encoding, protocol confusion, timing-based attacks** to bypass signatures.
   - Example: tiny fragmented packets reassembled by the victim host but invisible to the IDS.

7. **Limited Context Awareness**
   - An IDS sees packets, not *intent*. It may flag a penetration tester's authorized scan as an attack.

8. **Single Point of Failure**
   - A crashed or DoS'd IDS sensor creates a monitoring gap with no fallback visibility.

9. **Encrypted and Lateral Movement Blindness**
   - Insider threats moving laterally via authenticated channels are hard to detect at the network layer.

10. **Signature Database Lag**
    - Even fast vendors take **hours to days** to release signatures for newly disclosed CVEs.

### B. Operational Limitations

| Limitation | Real-World Impact |
|---|---|
| Requires skilled analysts | High SOC staffing cost |
| Tuning overhead | Months to baseline an anomaly IDS |
| Noisy alerts | Critical alerts buried in noise |
| Limited scalability in virtualized environments | East-west VM traffic often invisible |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — IDS Effectiveness Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are **frequently tested** in 14-mark questions asking students to evaluate IDS performance. Master the formula and the trade-offs.

$$
\text{Detection Rate (DR) / Recall / Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{False Alarm Rate (FAR)} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
\text{Miss Rate (False Negative Rate)} = \frac{FN}{TP + FN} = 1 - DR
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{F1 Score} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
\text{Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

| Metric | Ideal Direction | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Rate (DR) | Maximize ↑ | Raising DR often raises FAR |
| False Alarm Rate (FAR) | Minimize ↓ | Lowering FAR may lower DR |
| Accuracy | Maximize ↑ | Misleading on imbalanced datasets |
| F1 Score | Maximize ↑ | Balanced single metric |
| MCC | Maximize ↑ (+1 perfect, −1 inverse) | Best for imbalanced classes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A perfect IDS has **DR = 100%** and **FAR = 0%**, but in practice these two metrics are inversely related — improving one degrades the other. This is called the **ROC curve trade-off** and is a common KTU analytical question.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Signature vs Anomaly vs Hybrid

| Criterion | Signature-Based IDS | Anomaly-Based IDS | Hybrid IDS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection of Known Attacks | **Excellent** | Good | Excellent |
| Detection of Zero-Day Attacks | Poor | **Good** | Good |
| False Positive Rate | **Very Low** | **Very High** | Moderate |
| False Negative Rate | High for unknown | Lower | Low |
| Setup & Training Effort | Low | **High** | High |
| Computational Cost | Low–Moderate | **High** | High |
| Evasion Resistance | Low (evasion easy) | Moderate | **Good** |
| Example Tools | Snort, Suricata | Zeek, OSQuery + ML | Security Onion, Cisco Firepower |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Detection Rate and False Alarm Rate are Inversely Related

This is a **core KTU conceptual derivation**. Let us model an IDS as a binary classifier that decides: *malicious (M)* or *benign (B)*.

### Step 1 — Define the Decision Threshold

An anomaly-based IDS computes a score $S(x)$ for each event $x$. It compares $S(x)$ against a threshold $\tau$:

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Malicious (Alert)} & \text{if } S(x) \geq \tau \\ \text{Benign (No Alert)} & \text{if } S(x) < \tau \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Define Probability Distributions

Let the score distributions of malicious and benign traffic be:

$$
S \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_M, \sigma_M^2) \quad \text{(malicious scores)}
$$

$$
S \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_B, \sigma_B^2) \quad \text{(benign scores, with } \mu_B < \mu_M \text{)}
$$

### Step 3 — Express Detection Rate as a Function of Threshold

The Detection Rate is the probability that a malicious event scores above $\tau$:

$$
DR(\tau) = P(S \geq \tau \mid M) = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma_M \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \mu_M)^2}{2\sigma_M^2}\right) dx
$$

Equivalently, in terms of the standard normal CDF $\Phi$:

$$
DR(\tau) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_M}{\sigma_M}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Express False Alarm Rate as a Function of Threshold

The False Alarm Rate is the probability that a benign event scores above $\tau$:

$$
FAR(\tau) = P(S \geq \tau \mid B) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_B}{\sigma_B}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Solve Threshold in Terms of FAR

Let $z_B = \Phi^{-1}(1 - FAR)$. From Step 4:

$$
\frac{\tau - \mu_B}{\sigma_B} = z_B \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau = \mu_B + \sigma_B \cdot z_B
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Threshold into DR

Plug $\tau$ into Step 3:

$$
DR(\tau) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\mu_B + \sigma_B \cdot z_B - \mu_M}{\sigma_M}\right)
$$

Define the **separation parameter** $\Delta = \mu_M - \mu_B$:

$$
DR(FAR) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\sigma_B \cdot \Phi^{-1}(1 - FAR) - \Delta}{\sigma_M}\right)
$$

### Step 7 — Interpret the Inverse Relationship

- As $FAR \uparrow$ (we lower the threshold), $\Phi^{-1}(1-FAR)$ becomes more negative, the argument becomes smaller (more negative), so $\Phi$ decreases, so $1 - \Phi$ increases, meaning $DR \uparrow$.
- Conversely, lowering $FAR$ requires raising $\tau$, which reduces $DR$.

This **monotonic trade-off** is precisely the **ROC curve** of the IDS classifier. The **area under the ROC curve (AUC)** is the standard KTU-citable metric for *overall* IDS effectiveness — closer to 1 means better classifier.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** An IDS processes 10,000 events in a day. The confusion matrix is:

|  | Predicted Malicious | Predicted Benign |
|---|---|---|
| **Actually Malicious** | TP = 180 | FN = 20 |
| **Actually Benign** | FP = 150 | TN = 9,650 |

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Total events: $10{,}000$. Compute the metrics:

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

$$
FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{150}{150 + 9650} = \frac{150}{9800} \approx 0.0153 = 1.53\%
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{180 + 9650}{10000} = \frac{9830}{10000} = 98.30\%
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{180}{180 + 150} = \frac{180}{330} \approx 0.5455 = 54.55\%
$$

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

$$
F1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5455 \cdot 0.90}{0.5455 + 0.90} = \frac{0.9819}{1.4455} \approx 0.6793
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Note how **Accuracy = 98.3%** *looks great*, but **Precision = 54.5%** reveals that nearly **half of the "alerts" are false alarms**. This is the textbook *imbalanced-class trap* — KTU students should always report Precision/Recall alongside Accuracy for IDS evaluation.

---

## 3.3 ROC Curve — The Visual Trade-off

| Operating Point | Threshold τ | DR | FAR | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conservative (High τ) | Very High | Low | Very Low | Military / Critical infra |
| Balanced | Moderate | ~70–80% | ~5–10% | Enterprise SOC |
| Aggressive (Low τ) | Very Low | High | High | Initial triage / Hunting |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Strengths-vs-Limitations Synthesis

| Dimension | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Mechanism | Multi-layered (signature + anomaly + stateful) | Cannot detect novel zero-days reliably |
| Visibility | Wide network view (NIDS) | Blind to encrypted payload |
| Response | Rich alerting | Cannot block in real-time |
| Forensics | Detailed logs | Log retention requires storage budget |
| Cost | Open-source (Snort, Suricata, Zeek) | Skilled analyst shortage |
| Scalability | NIDS scales per subnet | East-west traffic in data centers hard |
| Evasion | Continuous rule updates | Adversarial ML defeats anomaly IDS |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IDS Position in Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internet / External Traffic] --> B[Firewall Layer 1]
    B --> C[IDS Sensor Promiscuous Mode via SPAN Port]
    C --> D[Internal Network / DMZ]
    D --> E[HIDS on Critical Hosts]
    E --> F[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    F --> G[SOC Analyst Console]
    G --> H[Incident Response Team]
    C -.Alert.-> F
    E -.Alert.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> Observe that the **IDS sits *passively* alongside** the firewall and host sensors. It **does not sit in the data path** — this is precisely why it can *detect but not prevent*. The SIEM is the central nervous system.

---

## 4.2 Strengths–Limitations Trade-off Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[IDS Event Scoring] --> Q{Score vs Threshold tau}
    Q -->|S >= tau| R[Alert Raised]
    Q -->|S < tau| S[No Alert]
    R --> T{True Malicious?}
    T -->|Yes| U[True Positive - Strength]
    T -->|No| V[False Positive - Limitation]
    S --> W{True Benign?}
    W -->|Yes| X[True Negative - Strength]
    W -->|No| Y[False Negative - Limitation]
    U --> Z[Quantify: DR, Precision, F1]
    V --> Z
    X --> AA[Quantify: FAR, Specificity]
    Y --> AA
```

---

## 4.3 Signature-Based vs Anomaly-Based IDS — Capability Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SigBased[Signature Based IDS]
        S1[Known CVE Database] --> S2[Pattern Matcher]
        S2 --> S3[Low FAR]
        S2 --> S4[High Miss Rate for Zero Day]
    end
    subgraph AnomalyBased[Anomaly Based IDS]
        A1[Baseline Behavior Profile] --> A2[Statistical / ML Engine]
        A2 --> A3[Detects Zero Day]
        A2 --> A4[High FAR Alert Fatigue]
    end
    subgraph Hybrid[Hybrid IDS]
        H1[Signature Engine] --> H3[Correlation Layer]
        H2[Anomaly Engine] --> H3
        H3 --> H5[Balanced DR and FAR]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 IDS Evasion Techniques (Mapping Attacker → IDS Weakness)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[Attacker Strategy] --> F[Packet Fragmentation]
    E --> G[Payload Encryption TLS]
    E --> H[Protocol Confusion]
    E --> I[Timing / Slowloris]
    E --> J[Polymorphic Malware]
    F --> K[IDS Cannot Reassemble Context]
    G --> L[IDS Cannot Inspect Ciphertext]
    H --> M[IDS Misparses Protocol State]
    I --> N[IDS Times Out Before Pattern Completes]
    J --> O[Signature Match Fails]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List any three strengths and three limitations of an Intrusion Detection System.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~5 lines each section):**

**Strengths:**
1. Provides **continuous real-time monitoring** of network and host activity, enabling early detection of intrusions before significant damage occurs.
2. Acts as a **second line of defense** (defense-in-depth) complementing firewalls, and produces detailed audit logs useful for forensic analysis and regulatory compliance (e.g., PCI-DSS, ISO 27001).
3. **NIDS operates in promiscuous/stealth mode**, making it invisible to attackers and providing wide visibility across an entire subnet from a single sensor.

**Limitations:**
1. IDS is **detection-only** — it cannot block attacks in real time. By the time an alert is generated, the intrusion may have already succeeded.
2. **Signature-based IDS cannot detect zero-day attacks**, and **anomaly-based IDS suffers from high false-positive rates**, leading to alert fatigue among analysts.
3. IDS cannot inspect **encrypted traffic** (HTTPS/TLS) payload content, creating a major visibility gap in modern networks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Why is a high False Positive Rate considered a serious limitation of an Intrusion Detection System?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A False Positive occurs when the IDS classifies legitimate, benign traffic as malicious and raises an unwarranted alert. When the False Positive Rate is high:

1. **Alert Fatigue:** Security analysts are overwhelmed with false alarms, causing them to **desensitize** and ignore or dismiss alerts — including genuine attacks (the *crying wolf* effect).
2. **Wasted Resources:** Each false alert consumes analyst investigation time, increasing the **operational cost (OPEX)** of the SOC.
3. **Reduced Trust in the IDS:** Stakeholders lose confidence in the system's reliability, potentially leading to its de-prioritization or even decommissioning.

Therefore, FAR must be minimized through **proper baseline training (for anomaly IDS)** and **fine-tuned rule sets (for signature IDS)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **architecture and working of a Network-based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **strengths and limitations** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IDS was evaluated over 5,000 network sessions. The confusion matrix is as follows:

|  | Predicted Attack | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | TP = 220 | FN = 30 |
| **Actual Normal** | FP = 280 | TN = 4,470 |

Calculate **Detection Rate, False Alarm Rate, Accuracy, Precision, and F1 Score**. Comment on the **overall effectiveness** of the IDS. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture of NIDS:**

A NIDS consists of the following components:

1. **Packet Capture Engine** — Uses a network interface card (NIC) in **promiscuous mode**, typically connected to a switch's **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) port** or a **network TAP**, to capture all packets traversing the segment.
2. **Preprocessor / Decoder** — Reassembles fragmented packets, decodes protocols (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application), and normalizes traffic.
3. **Detection Engine** — Performs either:
   - **Signature matching** against a rule database (Snort rules, Suricata rules), or
   - **Anomaly detection** using statistical models, machine learning, or protocol-state analysis.
4. **Alert/Logging Module** — Generates alerts with severity, timestamp, source/destination IPs, and packet payload excerpts. Logs are forwarded to SIEM.
5. **Management Console** — Analyst GUI for rule updates, threshold tuning, and incident investigation.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network TAP or SPAN Port] --> B[Packet Capture Engine Promiscuous Mode]
    B --> C[Preprocessor Decoder Reassembly]
    C --> D[Detection Engine Signature plus Anomaly]
    D --> E[Alert and Logging Module]
    E --> F[SIEM and SOC Console]
```

**Strengths of NIDS (4 marks):**

1. **Wide Visibility** — A single sensor monitors the entire subnet, providing cost-effective coverage for hundreds of hosts.
2. **Stealth Operation** — Operates passively, invisible to attackers.
3. **Real-time Detection** — Detects scans, DoS attempts, worms, and protocol anomalies at wire speed.
4. **Independent of Host OS** — Does not require installation on every host, reducing administrative overhead.

**Limitations of NIDS (3 marks):**

1. **Encrypted Traffic Blindness** — Cannot inspect TLS/SSL payload, missing ~80% of modern traffic.
2. **No Prevention** — Detects but cannot block packets inline.
3. **Switched Network Issues** — Requires SPAN/TAP configuration; without it, single-VLAN sensors miss cross-switch traffic.
4. **Evasion via Fragmentation / Timing** — Attackers split attacks across packets to evade signature matching.
5. **Resource Intensive at High Bandwidth** — Line-rate analysis of 10 Gbps+ links demands costly hardware.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

$$
TP = 220,\quad FN = 30,\quad FP = 280,\quad TN = 4470,\quad N = 5000
$$

**Detection Rate (Recall):**

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{220}{220 + 30} = \frac{220}{250} = 0.88 = 88\%
$$

**False Alarm Rate:**

$$
FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{280}{280 + 4470} = \frac{280}{4750} \approx 0.0589 = 5.89\%
$$

**Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{220 + 4470}{5000} = \frac{4690}{5000} = 93.80\%
$$

**Precision:**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{220}{220 + 280} = \frac{220}{500} = 0.44 = 44\%
$$

**F1 Score:**

$$
F1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.44 \cdot 0.88}{0.44 + 0.88} = \frac{0.7744}{1.32} \approx 0.5867
$$

**Comments on Effectiveness (2 marks):**

- The **Detection Rate of 88%** is good — the IDS catches most genuine attacks.
- However, the **Precision of only 44%** indicates that **more than half of the alerts are false positives**, which would cause severe **alert fatigue** in a SOC.
- The **Accuracy of 93.80% is misleading** because the dataset is highly imbalanced (only 250/5000 = 5% are actual attacks).
- The **F1 score of ~0.59** reflects the poor balance between precision and recall.
- **Conclusion:** The IDS has reasonable detection capability but **needs tuning** (e.g., raising the anomaly threshold or refining signature rules) to reduce false alarms and improve the F1 score.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Signature-based IDS and Anomaly-based IDS** with respect to detection mechanism, zero-day handling, false positive/negative rates, and computational cost. Which is better suited for enterprise deployment and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the various **evasion techniques** that attackers use to bypass IDS. Explain how a **hybrid IDS** overcomes these limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Signature-Based IDS | Anomaly-Based IDS |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Mechanism | Pattern matching against known attack signatures | Deviation from statistical/ML baseline of normal behavior |
| Zero-Day Detection | **Poor** (no signature exists) | **Good** (any deviation flags) |
| False Positive Rate | **Low** | **High** |
| False Negative Rate | High for unknown attacks | Lower |
| Setup Effort | **Low** (load rules) | **High** (train baseline over weeks) |
| Computational Cost | Low–Moderate | High (ML inference, statistics) |
| Update Requirement | Frequent signature DB updates | Periodic baseline retraining |
| Example Tools | Snort, Suricata | Zeek, OSQuery, ML-based IDS |

**Enterprise Suitability:**

For large-scale enterprise deployment, a **Hybrid IDS** combining both is preferred because:

1. **Signature component** ensures low false-positive detection of known malware (e.g., ransomware, exploits).
2. **Anomaly component** provides defense against zero-day attacks and insider threats.
3. Correlation between the two engines reduces the weaknesses of each individual approach.
4. Tools like **Cisco Firepower, Security Onion, and Corelight** exemplify this hybrid architecture.

**Conclusion:** No single IDS type is universally superior. The hybrid approach is the modern industry standard for enterprise SOCs.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**IDS Evasion Techniques (4 marks):**

1. **Packet Fragmentation** — Splitting a malicious payload across multiple tiny packets that the IDS reassembles differently (or not at all) compared to the victim host.
2. **Payload Encryption (TLS/SSH)** — IDS cannot decrypt HTTPS traffic to inspect signatures, so attacks hide inside encrypted tunnels.
3. **Protocol Confusion / Mismatched Decoding** — Exploiting differences in how the IDS and the victim OS interpret malformed protocol headers.
4. **Timing-Based Evasion (Slowloris)** — Sending attack bytes very slowly so the IDS connection times out before the pattern completes.
5. **Polymorphic / Metamorphic Malware** — Malicious code that mutates its signature on each infection, defeating signature matching.
6. **Adversarial Machine Learning** — Crafting inputs with imperceptible perturbations that fool ML-based anomaly detectors.

**How Hybrid IDS Overcomes These (3 marks):**

1. **Signature engine** catches known fragments, malware, and protocol patterns at the perimeter.
2. **Anomaly engine** flags unusual timing, traffic volume, and behavioral deviations — defeating slow-rate and zero-day attacks.
3. **Correlation layer** combines both signals, reducing false positives through cross-validation:
   - If signature says "clean" but anomaly says "unusual," the alert is escalated for human review.
4. **Encrypted Traffic Analytics (ETA)** — Modern hybrid systems use **TLS fingerprinting (JA3/JA4)** and metadata analysis to flag malicious encrypted sessions without breaking encryption.
5. **File sandboxing and reputation feeds** add a third dimension, restoring visibility into encrypted file transfers.

**Conclusion:** Hybrid IDS — supplemented by threat intelligence, sandboxing, and encrypted-traffic analytics — is the most resilient architecture against contemporary evasion techniques.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS** — Writing that IDS *blocks* or *prevents* attacks. IDS only *detects and alerts*; blocking is the job of an IPS.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify IDS type** — A strength/limitation valid for *signature IDS* (e.g., "cannot detect zero-days") may be invalid for *anomaly IDS*. Always qualify the type.
> 3. **Reporting only Accuracy** — For imbalanced attack-vs-normal datasets, accuracy is misleading. Always include **Precision, Recall, and F1**.
> 4. **Skipping the ROC trade-off** — When asked about IDS performance, mention the **inverse relationship between DR and FAR** — it is a guaranteed 2-mark add-on.
> 5. **Ignoring encrypted traffic** — Modern KTU questions explicitly ask about HTTPS/TLS visibility; ignoring it costs 2–3 marks.
