```mermaid
    K3 --> K4[Choose e with gcd e, phi n = 1]
    K4 --> K5[Compute d such that e * d mod phi n = 1]
    K5 --> K6[Public Key: e, n]
    K5 --> K7[Private Key: d, n]
    K6 --> K8[Publish e, n in Directory]
    K7 --> K9[Keep d Secret]
```

### 4.3 DES Feistel Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[64-bit Plaintext Block] --> IP[Initial Permutation IP]
    IP --> L0[L0: Left Half 32 bits]
    IP --> R0[R0: Right Half 32 bits]
    L0 --> R1[R1]
    R0 --> F1[Round Function f with K1]
    F1 --> XOR1[XOR]
    L0 --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> R1
    R1 --> L2[L2]
    R1 --> F2[Round Function f with K2]
    F2 --> XOR2[XOR]
    R0 --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> L2
    L2 --> R2[R2]
    R1 --> F3[Round Function f with K3]
    F3 --> XOR3[XOR]
    L2 --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> R2
    R2 --> SW[Swap L and R after Round 16]
    L2 --> SW
    SW --> INVIP[Inverse Permutation IP inverse]
    INVIP --> OUT[64-bit Ciphertext Block]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hybrid RSA + DES System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ALICE[ALICE - Sender Side]
        A1[Plaintext Data Block] --> A2[DES Engine with Session Key Ks]
        Ks1[Generate Random Session Key Ks] --> A2
        A2 --> A3[Encrypted Data C1]
        Ks1 --> A4[RSA Encrypt Ks with Bob's Public Key eB]
        A4 --> A5[Encrypted Session Key C2]
    end

    subgraph CHANNEL[INSECURE CHANNEL]
        A3 --> TRANS1[Transmit C1]
        A5 --> TRANS2[Transmit C2]
    end

    subgraph BOB[BOB - Receiver Side]
        TRANS1 --> B1[Receive C1]
        TRANS2 --> B2[Receive C2]
        B2 --> B3[RSA Decrypt C2 with Bob's Private Key dB]
        B3 --> B4[Recover Session Key Ks]
        B1 --> B5[DES Decrypt C1 with Ks]
        B4 --> B5
        B5 --> B6[Recover Original Plaintext]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read the Diagrams:** Every block diagram shows the *control flow* (arrows) and *data flow*. In the hybrid architecture, observe how RSA handles **key transport** while DES handles **bulk encryption** — this is the real-world deployment pattern for secure web traffic.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three differences between RSA and DES algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Key Type:** RSA is an **asymmetric (public-key) cryptosystem** using a pair of keys (public + private), whereas DES is a **symmetric (secret-key) cryptosystem** using a single shared secret key.

2. **Block Size:** RSA operates on variable-length blocks determined by the modulus $n$ (typically 1024–2048 bits), while DES operates on fixed **64-bit blocks**.

3. **Encryption Speed:** RSA is computationally **slow** (based on modular exponentiation of large integers), whereas DES is **fast** (based on simple bit-level XOR, permutations, and substitutions, easily implemented in hardware).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the key size of DES and how many rounds does it perform?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Effective Key Size:** **56 bits** (the original 64-bit key contains 8 parity bits used for error detection, leaving 56 bits for cryptographic operations).
- **Number of Rounds:** **16 Feistel rounds**, each using a distinct 48-bit subkey generated by the DES key schedule (PC-1, left circular shifts, PC-2).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A):**
> Students often write *"DES uses a 64-bit key"* — this is **wrong** and loses a mark. Always state **56-bit effective key** (64 bits with 8 parity bits). Also, students confuse *"16 rounds"* with *"16 keys"* — there are 16 rounds, each consuming **one of 16 subkeys** derived from the single 56-bit main key.

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the RSA algorithm with its key generation, encryption, and decryption steps. Illustrate with a small numerical example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the DES algorithm with a neat diagram of its Feistel structure and describe one round operation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**Step 1: Key Generation**
- Choose two primes $p = 5$ and $q = 11$.
- Compute $n = p \cdot q = 55$.
- Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \cdot 10 = 40$.
- Choose $e = 7$ (since $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$).
- Compute $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.
- Testing: $7 \cdot 23 = 161 = 4 \cdot 40 + 1$, so $d = 23$.

**Public Key:** $(e, n) = (7, 55)$
**Private Key:** $(d, n) = (23, 55)$

**Step 2: Encryption** of plaintext $M = 10$:

$$C = M^{e} \bmod n = 10^{7} \bmod 55$$

Intermediate: $10^{2} = 100 \equiv 100 - 55 = 45 \pmod{55}$, $10^{4} \equiv 45^{2} = 2025 \equiv 2025 - 36 \cdot 55 = 2025 - 1980 = 45 \pmod{55}$. So $10^{7} = 10^{4} \cdot 10^{2} \cdot 10 \equiv 45 \cdot 45 \cdot 10 = 20250 \equiv 20250 \bmod 55$. $20250 / 55 = 368.18$, $368 \cdot 55 = 20240$, remainder $10$. Wait — recheck:

$10^{1} \equiv 10$, $10^{2} \equiv 100 \bmod 55 = 45$, $10^{4} \equiv 45^{2} = 2025 \bmod 55$. $2025 / 55 = 36.8$, $36 \cdot 55 = 1980$, remainder $45$. So $10^{4} \equiv 45$. Then $10^{7} = 10^{4} \cdot 10^{2} \cdot 10^{1} \equiv 45 \cdot 45 \cdot 10 = 20250$. $20250 / 55 = 368.18$, $368 \cdot 55 = 20240$, remainder $= 10$.

So $C = 10$. (This is a degenerate case for small $n$ — the example is meant to illustrate the *process*, not for strong security.)

**Step 3: Decryption:**

$$M = C^{d} \bmod n = 10^{23} \bmod 55$$

By Fermat's little theorem, $10^{40} \equiv 1 \pmod{55}$, so $10^{23} = 10^{23}$. Computation confirms $M = 10$. $\checkmark$

---

**Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**DES Feistel Structure Explanation:**

DES operates on 64-bit plaintext blocks using a 56-bit key. The structure consists of:
1. **Initial Permutation (IP)** — fixed bit rearrangement.
2. **16 identical Feistel rounds**, each processing the 64-bit block split into two 32-bit halves $L_i$ and $R_i$.
3. **Final Swap** of $L_{16}$ and $R_{16}$.
4. **Inverse Initial Permutation (IP⁻¹)** — output ciphertext.

**One Round Operation:**

$$L_i = R_{i-1}$$

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus f(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

The round function $f$ performs:
- **Expansion (E-box):** Expand 32-bit $R_{i-1}$ to 48 bits.
- **XOR with $K_i$** (48-bit subkey).
- **Substitution:** Eight S-boxes (6→4 bits each) compress 48 bits to 32 bits.
- **Permutation (P-box):** Fixed bit shuffling.

The subkey $K_i$ is generated by the key schedule: PC-1 selects 56 bits, left circular shifts (1 or 2 positions per round), then PC-2 selects 48 bits to form $K_i$.

(Refer to the Feistel architecture diagram in Section 4.3.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare RSA and DES based on any seven criteria. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the hybrid cryptosystem that combines RSA and DES. Why is such a hybrid approach preferred in practice? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B (a):**

| **S.No.** | **Criterion** | **RSA** | **DES** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Key Type | Asymmetric (public + private) | Symmetric (single shared key) |
| 2 | Block Size | Variable (modulus $n$, typically 1024 bits) | Fixed 64 bits |
| 3 | Key Size | 1024 / 2048 / 4096 bits | 56 bits effective |
| 4 | Rounds | 1 (single modular exponentiation) | 16 Feistel rounds |
| 5 | Speed | Slow (~1000× slower than DES) | Fast (hardware-friendly) |
| 6 | Security Basis | Integer factorization problem | Brute force on $2^{56}$ keys |
| 7 | Key Distribution | Public key openly published | Requires secure channel |
| 8 | Digital Signature | Supported | Not supported |
| 9 | Primary Application | Key exchange, digital signatures | Bulk data encryption |

---

**Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Hybrid Cryptosystem Concept:**

A hybrid cryptosystem combines the strengths of asymmetric (RSA) and symmetric (DES) cryptography. The workflow is:

1. **Sender side:** A random symmetric session key $K_s$ is generated. The actual plaintext is encrypted with DES using $K_s$, producing ciphertext $C_1$. Then $K_s$ itself is encrypted using the recipient's RSA public key $(e, n)$, producing $C_2$.
2. **Transmission:** Both $C_1$ (DES ciphertext) and $C_2$ (RSA-encrypted key) are sent over the insecure channel.
3. **Receiver side:** The recipient first decrypts $C_2$ using their RSA private key $d$ to recover $K_s$. Then they decrypt $C_1$ using $K_s$ to obtain the plaintext.

**Why Hybrid is Preferred:**

- **Speed:** DES is ~1000× faster than RSA, so DES handles bulk data efficiently.
- **Secure Key Exchange:** RSA solves the key distribution problem — the session key $K_s$ is safely delivered without requiring a pre-shared secret.
- **Scalability:** RSA's public-key infrastructure scales to millions of users, while symmetric key management would require $O(n^2)$ keys.
- **Real-World Adoption:** TLS/SSL, PGP, S/MIME all use this exact hybrid model.

(Refer to the hybrid architecture diagram in Section 4.4.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing block size with key size:** Students write *"DES has a 64-bit key"* — it is a **56-bit effective key**.
> 2. **Omitting parity bits in DES:** Always mention 8 parity bits → 64-bit input, 56-bit cryptographic key.
> 3. **Skipping numerical example in RSA:** KTU demands a worked example. Even with tiny primes like $p=5, q=11$, you must show the full key generation, encryption, and decryption arithmetic.
> 4. **Writing only formulas without explanation:** List the equations ($C = M^e \bmod n$, $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \bmod \phi(n)$) AND explain what each variable means.
> 5. **Forgetting the hybrid model:** This is a **favorite 14-mark question**. Students who describe RSA and DES in isolation lose marks — always mention how they cooperate in real systems.
> 6. **Wrong symbol usage:** Use $\phi(n)$ (Euler's totient), not $\varphi$ or "phi-n" — strict notation matters.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **RSA** = **A**symmetric, **P**ublic-key, **S**low, used for key exchange & signatures.
- ✅ **DES** = **S**ymmetric, **S**ecret-key, **F**ast, used for bulk data encryption.
- ✅ RSA modulus: $n = p \cdot q$, where $p, q$ are large primes.
- ✅ Euler's totient: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- ✅ Key relation: $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- ✅ Encryption: $C = M^{e} \bmod n$. Decryption: $M = C^{d} \bmod n$.
- ✅ DES block size: **64 bits**. DES key size: **56 bits** (effective, with 8 parity bits).
- ✅ DES rounds: **16 Feistel rounds**.
- ✅ DES single round: $L_i = R_{i-1}$, $R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus f(R_{i-1}, K_i)$.
- ✅ DES round function $f$ = Expansion → XOR with $K_i$ → S-box substitution → P-box permutation.
- ✅ RSA security basis: **Integer factorization** is computationally hard.
- ✅ DES security basis: **Brute-force on $2^{56}$** keys (broken by EFF in 1998).
- ✅ DES is **insecure today** — superseded by 3DES and AES.
- ✅ RSA is used in TLS/SSL handshakes, digital certificates (X.509), PGP, SSH.
- ✅ **Hybrid model:** RSA encrypts the session key, DES/AES encrypts the data.
- ✅ RSA is ~**1000× slower** than DES/AES — never use RSA for bulk encryption.
- ✅ Both algorithms were published in the same year: **1977**.
- ✅ Remember the comparison direction: RSA addresses *key exchange*, DES addresses *bulk encryption* — together they form the backbone of modern secure communication.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Authentication & Hash Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Message Authentication & Hash Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Message Authentication

**Message Authentication** is the cryptographic mechanism that provides assurance to the receiving party that the received message:
1. Has not been altered (data integrity)
2. Is from the source it claims to be from (source authentication)
3. Has been sent at the intended time (anti-replay / timeliness)

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Cyber Security (PEECT864), message authentication is a fundamental building block that addresses the question: *"Can the receiver verify that the message truly originated from the alleged sender and was not tampered with in transit?"*

$$ \text{Authentication} : \text{Message} \rightarrow \text{Verified Identity + Integrity Proof} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication vs. Confidentiality — KTU Key Distinction**
> Authentication **does not hide** the contents of a message. A message can be in plaintext yet authenticated. Confidentiality (encryption) **does not guarantee** the sender is genuine — anyone with the key could have created the ciphertext.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Hash Functions

A **Cryptographic Hash Function** is a deterministic mathematical function $H$ that accepts an input $M$ of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-size output $h$ called a **message digest**, **hash value**, or **fingerprint**:

$$ H : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n $$

where:
- $\{0,1\}^*$ denotes binary strings of any length
- $\{0,1\}^n$ denotes binary strings of exactly $n$ bits
- Typical values of $n$ in modern standards are **160 bits (SHA-1, RIPEMD-160)**, **256 bits (SHA-256)**, **384 bits (SHA-384)**, and **512 bits (SHA-512)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Wax-Sealed Letter

Imagine you are sending a letter to a friend in another city during medieval times:

| Real-World Analogy | Cryptographic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The letter content | Plaintext message $M$ |
| Your unique signet ring pressed in wax | MAC tag or Digital Signature |
| The wax seal | Authentication tag (HMAC output) |
| A wax seal broken in transit indicates tampering | Hash mismatch detects modification |
| A seal that only you can replicate | Shared secret key $K$ (HMAC) or Private key (Signatures) |
| Reading the letter without breaking seal is impossible | Confidentiality (Encryption — separate goal) |

Just as a sealed letter proves **who sent it** and **whether it was opened**, message authentication proves **the source and integrity** of a digital message.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Information Security (CIA Triad + Authentication)**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — Keeping data secret (Encryption)
> 2. **Integrity** — Detecting modification (Hash functions, MAC)
> 3. **Authentication** — Verifying identity (MAC, Digital Signatures)
>
> Authentication and Integrity are often achieved together using a single mechanism.

## 1.4 Classification of Authentication Functions

The KTU syllabus classifies authentication functions into three broad categories:

1. **Message Encryption** — Using symmetric/asymmetric ciphers to provide implicit authentication (assumes only sender/receiver share the key).
2. **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** — A fixed-size tag generated using a shared secret key.
3. **Hash Function** — A one-way function producing a fixed-length fingerprint.
4. **Digital Signatures** — Asymmetric-key analog of MAC, providing non-repudiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**
> - **MAC** uses a *symmetric* secret key → Both sender & receiver know the key → Cannot prove non-repudiation.
> - **Digital Signature** uses an *asymmetric* key pair → Only sender knows the private key → Provides non-repudiation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Security Requirements for Message Authentication

A robust message authentication scheme must satisfy the following requirements:

| Requirement | Description | Threat Mitigated |
|---|---|---|
| **Disclosure** | Protect message contents from passive eavesdropping | Release of message contents |
| **Traffic analysis** | Hide the source/destination/frequency of communication | Pattern recognition |
| **Masquerading** | Prevent insertion of messages from a fraudulent source | Insertion attack |
| **Content modification** | Detect changes to message contents | Tampering |
| **Sequence modification** | Detect reordering, deletion, or replay of messages | Replay attack |
| **Timing modification** | Detect delay or speedup of message delivery | Replay / delay attack |
| **Source repudiation** | Prevent denial of message origin by the sender | Denial by sender |

## 2.2 Authentication Functions — Operational Analysis

### A. Message Encryption as Authentication

**Symmetric Encryption:** If only sender and receiver share key $K$, then a successfully decrypted message implicitly proves origin.
- **Limitation:** Cannot distinguish between modifications in plaintext vs. ciphertext. Vulnerable to **bit-flipping attacks** in certain modes.

**Asymmetric Encryption:** Anyone can encrypt with the receiver's public key, so this **does NOT provide authentication of sender**.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall: Students often claim "encryption provides authentication." This is only true for **symmetric encryption**, and even then, only against outsiders. It does NOT provide non-repudiation since both parties share the key.

### B. Message Authentication Code (MAC)

A MAC is a cryptographic checksum:

$$ \text{MAC} = C_K(M) $$

where:
- $M$ = variable-length message
- $K$ = shared secret key
- $C$ = MAC generation algorithm
- Output = fixed-length authentication tag

**Verification:**

$$ \text{Valid} \iff V_K(M, \text{MAC}) = \text{Accept} $$

The receiver recomputes the MAC over the received message and compares it with the appended tag.

> [!NOTE]
> MAC differs from Encryption:
> - Encryption is **reversible** (decryptable).
> - MAC is a **one-way function** (irreversible).
> - MAC output length is **fixed** regardless of message length.

### C. Cryptographic Hash Function — Three Key Properties

A secure cryptographic hash function $H(M)$ must satisfy **three fundamental properties**:

#### 1. Pre-image Resistance (One-Way Property)
Given $h = H(M)$, it must be computationally infeasible to find any $M'$ such that $H(M') = h$.

$$ \forall h \in \{0,1\}^n, \;\; \Pr[\text{Attacker finds } M' \text{ s.t. } H(M') = h] \leq \epsilon $$

#### 2. Second Pre-image Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance)
Given $M_1$, it must be computationally infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_2) = H(M_1)$.

$$ \Pr[M_2 \neq M_1 \land H(M_2) = H(M_1)] \leq \epsilon $$

#### 3. Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance)
It must be computationally infeasible to find *any* two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

$$ \Pr[M_1 \neq M_2 \land H(M_1) = H(M_2)] \leq \epsilon $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:**
> - **Pre-image resistance** prevents recovering the original message from its hash.
> - **Second pre-image resistance** prevents forging an *alternate* message matching a given hash.
> - **Collision resistance** prevents finding *any* two messages with the same hash.
>
> Collision resistance is the **strongest** of the three properties. Breaking it (as in MD5/SHA-1) is the most catastrophic.

### D. Hashed MAC (HMAC)

HMAC combines a cryptographic hash function with a secret key. It is the **de facto standard** for keyed-hash message authentication (RFC 2104, FIPS 198).

The HMAC construction:

$$ \text{HMAC}(K, M) = H \Big( (K \oplus opad) \;\|\; H\big((K \oplus ipad) \;\|\; M\big) \Big) $$

where:
- $H$ = embedded hash function (e.g., SHA-256)
- $K$ = secret key (padded/hashed to block size)
- $ipad$ = inner pad = $0x36$ repeated to block size $b$
- $opad$ = outer pad = $0x5C$ repeated to block size $b$
- $\|$ = concatenation
- $\oplus$ = XOR operation

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.3 — Essential Equations and Parameters**

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hash function definition | $H : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | $n$ = output bit length |
| 2 | MD5 output size | $n = 128$ bits | Obsolete, broken in 2004 |
| 3 | SHA-1 output size | $n = 160$ bits | Deprecated by NIST in 2011 |
| 4 | SHA-256 output size | $n = 256$ bits | Current standard |
| 5 | SHA-512 output size | $n = 512$ bits | High-security standard |
| 6 | HMAC security strength | $\min(L, n)$ | where $L$ = key length, $n$ = hash output bits |
| 7 | HMAC block size (SHA-256) | $b = 512$ bits | Input block size |
| 8 | Inner pad constant | $ipad = 0x36$ | Hex byte, repeated $b$ times |
| 9 | Outer pad constant | $opad = 0x5C$ | Hex byte, repeated $b$ times |
| 10 | Brute-force MAC forgery attempts | $2^n$ | where $n$ = tag length in bits |
| 11 | Birthday attack — collisions found in $k$ trials | $k \approx 1.177 \sqrt{2^n}$ | $n$ = hash output bits |
| 12 | Birthday bound — 50% collision probability | $k \approx 1.18 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ | $n$ = hash output bits |
| 13 | Probability of at least one collision | $P \approx 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/2^{n+1}}$ | For $k$ random inputs |
| 14 | SHA-256 rounds | 64 rounds | Compression function iterations |
| 15 | MD5 rounds | 64 rounds (4 groups of 16) | Compression function iterations |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Application of Message Authentication & Hashing |
|---|---|
| **Banking (NEFT, UPI, SWIFT)** | HMAC-SHA-256 authenticates every transaction; prevents fraudulent fund transfers. |
| **Software Distribution** | SHA-256 checksums verify downloaded installers (e.g., Linux ISO images). |
| **TLS 1.3 (HTTPS)** | Uses HMAC for the handshake and AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM) for payload authentication. |
| **Git Version Control** | Uses SHA-1 (being migrated to SHA-256) to identify every commit, blob, and tree. |
| **Blockchain (Bitcoin/Ethereum)** | SHA-256 and Keccak-256 hash every block and transaction; Merkle trees authenticate transactions. |
| **Code Signing** | Digital signatures authenticate software publishers; MACs authenticate firmware in embedded devices. |
| **IoT Security** | HMAC-SHA-1/256 authenticates sensor readings in constrained-device protocols (CoAP, MQTT). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Birthday Paradox and Hash Collision Probability

This is one of the **highest-yield derivations** for KTU Module 3, as it determines the effective security strength of any hash function.

### Problem Statement
Given a hash function $H$ producing $n$-bit outputs, how many random inputs $k$ must an adversary try before finding two with the same hash (a collision)?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Total number of possible hash values is $N = 2^n$.

**Step 2:** For $k$ randomly chosen inputs, the number of *pairs* is:

$$ \binom{k}{2} = \frac{k(k-1)}{2} $$

**Step 3:** Approximate the probability that a *single* pair collides. With $N = 2^n$ possible hash values:

$$ P(\text{single pair collides}) \approx \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{2^n} $$

**Step 4:** The probability that a specific pair does NOT collide is:

$$ P(\text{no collision in pair}) \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2^n} $$

**Step 5:** Assuming all $\binom{k}{2}$ pairs are *approximately independent* (a standard birthday-paradox assumption), the probability that *no* collision occurs among all pairs is:

$$ P(\text{no collision}) \approx \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^n}\right)^{k(k-1)/2} $$

**Step 6:** Apply the well-known limit $(1-x)^y \approx e^{-xy}$ for small $x$:

$$ P(\text{no collision}) \approx e^{-\frac{k(k-1)}{2 \cdot 2^n}} $$

**Step 7:** The probability of *at least one* collision is therefore:

$$ P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{k(k-1)}{2^{n+1}}} $$

**Step 8:** Solve for the value of $k$ that gives $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5$:

$$ 0.5 = 1 - e^{-\frac{k^2}{2^{n+1}}} $$

$$ e^{-\frac{k^2}{2^{n+1}}} = 0.5 $$

$$ -\frac{k^2}{2^{n+1}} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693 $$

$$ k^2 = 0.693 \times 2^{n+1} $$

$$ k = \sqrt{2 \cdot \ln(2)} \cdot 2^{n/2} $$

**Step 9:** Compute the constant: $\sqrt{2 \ln 2} = \sqrt{1.386} \approx 1.177$

$$ \boxed{k \approx 1.177 \times 2^{n/2}} $$

**Step 10 (Engineering Interpretation):** A hash function with $n$-bit output offers only **$n/2$ bits of collision resistance**, not $n$ bits.

### Numerical Example (MD5)

For MD5, $n = 128$:

$$ k \approx 1.177 \times 2^{64} \approx 2.18 \times 10^{19} \text{ hashes} $$

This number is computationally feasible with modern GPU farms (achieved in 2004 by Wang et al.). Hence MD5 is **broken for collision resistance**, though still useful for pre-image resistance in some contexts.

### Numerical Example (SHA-256)

For SHA-256, $n = 256$:

$$ k \approx 1.177 \times 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \text{ hashes} $$

This is computationally infeasible with current technology, making SHA-256 secure against collision attacks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — HMAC Construction Walkthrough

The HMAC algorithm is the textbook example of how to convert an unkeyed hash function into a keyed MAC. Let us trace its construction.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1: Key Normalization**

If the key $K$ is shorter than the block size $b$ (e.g., 512 bits for SHA-256), pad with zeros. If longer, hash it first:

$$ K_0 = \begin{cases} K \;\|\; \text{zeros} & \text{if } \vert K \vert < b \\ H(K) \;\|\; \text{zeros} & \text{if } \vert K \vert > b \end{cases} $$

The result $K_0$ is exactly $b$ bits.

**Step 2: Inner Keyed Pad**

XOR the normalized key with the inner pad constant (0x36 repeated):

$$ K_{\text{inner}} = K_0 \oplus ipad $$

Since $ipad$ consists of $b/8$ bytes equal to $0x36$, this operation flips specific bits in $K_0$.

**Step 3: Inner Hash**

Hash the concatenation of the inner-padded key with the message $M$:

$$ H_{\text{inner}} = H\big( K_{\text{inner}} \;\|\; M \big) $$

Note: The hash is computed over the entire message stream; if $M$ is longer than one block, the Merkle–Damgård iteration handles multi-block processing.

**Step 4: Outer Keyed Pad**

XOR the (same) normalized key with the outer pad constant (0x5C repeated):

$$ K_{\text{outer}} = K_0 \oplus opad $$

**Step 5: Outer Hash**

Hash the concatenation of the outer-padded key with the inner hash output:

$$ \boxed{\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\big( K_{\text{outer}} \;\|\; H_{\text{inner}} \big) = H\big( (K_0 \oplus opad) \;\|\; H\big((K_0 \oplus ipad) \;\|\; M\big) \big) } $$

**Step 6 (Why two pads?):** Using two different constants ($0x36$ and $0x5C$) ensures that the outer key and inner key are mathematically independent, even though they derive from the same secret key. This prevents **length-extension attacks** that plague naive constructions like $H(K \| M)$.

### Worked Numerical Illustration (Conceptual Block Sizes)

For SHA-256 with a 256-bit key:

$$ b = 512 \text{ bits}, \quad K_0 = K \;\|\; 256 \text{ zero bits} $$

$$ K_{\text{inner}} = K_0 \oplus (0x36 \text{ repeated 64 bytes}) $$

$$ H_{\text{inner}} = \text{SHA-256}(K_{\text{inner}} \| M), \quad \text{output} = 256 \text{ bits} $$

$$ K_{\text{outer}} = K_0 \oplus (0x5C \text{ repeated 64 bytes}) $$

$$ \text{HMAC-SHA256}(K, M) = \text{SHA-256}(K_{\text{outer}} \| H_{\text{inner}}), \quad \text{output} = 256 \text{ bits} $$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Security Strength of MAC Against Brute Force

A MAC tag of $n$ bits is theoretically vulnerable to brute-force forgery.

**Without knowing the key:** An attacker can randomly guess a valid tag. The probability of guessing any specific $n$-bit tag is:

$$ P(\text{correct guess}) = \frac{1}{2^n} $$

To find a valid (message, tag) pair by exhaustive search requires approximately:

$$ \boxed{\text{Number of forgery attempts} \approx 2^n} $$

For HMAC-SHA-256 ($n = 256$):

$$ 2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77} \text{ attempts} $$

This is computationally infeasible with all known computing paradigms (including quantum computers via Grover's algorithm, which reduces this to $2^{128}$ — still infeasible).

> [!NOTE]
> **Grover's Algorithm Impact:** A quantum adversary using Grover's search reduces the effective security of an $n$-bit MAC to roughly $n/2$ bits. Hence NIST recommends **doubling the key length** of symmetric primitives for post-quantum security, i.e., use HMAC-SHA-384 or HMAC-SHA-512.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.4 — Authentication Mechanisms Compared**

| Property | Plain Hash $H(M)$ | HMAC $\text{HMAC}(K, M)$ | MAC $C_K(M)$ | Digital Signature $S_{\text{priv}}(M)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Key type** | None (public) | Symmetric secret | Symmetric secret | Asymmetric (public+private) |
| **Provides authentication?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Provides integrity?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Provides non-repudiation?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Requires shared secret?** | No | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Reversible?** | No | No | No | Yes (verifiable with public key) |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Fast | Slow (1000x slower) |
| **Standard example** | SHA-256 | HMAC-SHA-256 | CMAC-AES | RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA |
| **Output size** | $n$ bits | $n$ bits | $n$ bits (often 64–128) | $\vert \text{key} \vert$ bits (1024–4096) |
| **Vulnerability** | Collision attacks if $n$ small | Brute-force ($2^n$) | Brute-force ($2^n$) | Math attacks (factoring, ECDLP) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram — Generic Message Authentication using MAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER
        M[Message M]
        K1[Shared Secret Key K]
        MACgen[MAC Generation Algorithm C]
        M --> MACgen
        K1 --> MACgen
        MACgen --> TAG[MAC Tag T = CK of M]
    end

    MSG[Transmitted: M concatenated with T]
    TAG --> MSG
    M --> MSG

    MSG -- transmitted over insecure channel --> MSGrecv[Received: M prime and T prime]

    subgraph RECEIVER
        Mrecv[Received Message M prime]
        K2[Same Shared Secret Key K]
        MACver[MAC Verification Algorithm V]
        Mrecv --> MACver
        K2 --> MACver
        MACver --> COMP{T prime equals V sub K of M prime?}
    end

    MSGrecv --> Mrecv
    MSGrecv --> Tprime[Received Tag T prime]
    Tprime --> COMP

    COMP -- Yes --> ACCEPT[Accept: Message Authentic]
    COMP -- No --> REJECT[Reject: Tampered or Forged]
```

## 4.2 Diagram — Hash Function Pipeline (Merkle–Damgård Construction)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Mbig[Arbitrary Length Message M]
    PAD[Padding: Append 1, zeros, and 64-bit length]
    BLOCKS[Split into fixed-size 512-bit blocks: M1, M2, ..., Mn]
    IV[Initial Hash Value IV: H0]
    CF[Compression Function f: 512-bit block + 160-bit state to 160-bit state]
    OUT[Final Hash Value H of n]

    Mbig --> PAD
    PAD --> BLOCKS
    BLOCKS --> CF
    IV --> CF
    CF --> CF2[Next iteration: H1 becomes input to f with M2]
    CF2 --> CF3[Continue until Mn processed]
    CF3 --> OUT

    style IV fill:#fff4cc
    style OUT fill:#d4f4dd
    style CF fill:#cce5ff
```

## 4.3 Diagram — HMAC Internal Architecture (Two-Layer Nested Hash)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K[Secret Key K]
    Kpad[Pad or Hash K to block size b equals 512 bits]
    K0[K0: Normalized Key]

    M[Message M]

    IPAD[ipad equals 0x36 repeated 64 times]
    OPAD[opad equals 0x5C repeated 64 times]

    XOR1[XOR: K0 XOR ipad]
    XOR2[XOR: K0 XOR opad]

    INNER[Inner Hash: SHA-256 of K_inner concatenated with M]
    OUTER[Outer Hash: SHA-256 of K_outer concatenated with Inner_Hash]

    TAG[HMAC-SHA256 Tag of M equals 256 bits]

    K --> Kpad --> K0
    K0 --> XOR1
    K0 --> XOR2
    IPAD --> XOR1
    OPAD --> XOR2
    XOR1 --> INNER
    M --> INNER
    INNER --> OUTER
    XOR2 --> OUTER
    OUTER --> TAG

    style TAG fill:#d4f4dd
    style INNER fill:#cce5ff
    style OUTER fill:#cce5ff
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Hash Function Internal Round (SHA-256 style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Block: 512 bits]
    W[Message Schedule: Expand 16 words to 64 words]
    ROUND[64 Rounds of Compression]
    ADD[Add constant Kt]
    SIGMA[Sigma Functions: bit rotation, choice, majority]
    STATE[256-bit Working State: a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h]
    NEW[New State for next block]

    A --> W --> ROUND
    ADD --> ROUND
    SIGMA --> ROUND
    STATE --> ROUND
    ROUND --> NEW
    NEW --> STATE
```

## 4.5 Diagram — Authentication Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Receiver gets message M prime with tag T prime]
    RECV[Recompute MAC using shared key K on M prime]
    COMP{Compare recomputed tag with T prime?}
    EQUAL[T prime matches recomputed value]
    NOTEQUAL[T prime differs from recomputed value]

    AUTH[Message is AUTHENTIC and INTEGRITY VERIFIED]
    REJ[REJECT: Message tampered or forged]

    START --> RECV --> COMP
    COMP -- Yes --> EQUAL --> AUTH
    COMP -- No --> NOTEQUAL --> REJ

    style AUTH fill:#d4f4dd
    style REJ fill:#f4d4d4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Message Authentication Code (MAC) and Digital Signature. List any three points of difference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — each point carries 1 mark):**

| S.No. | MAC | Digital Signature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses a **symmetric secret key** shared between sender and receiver. | Uses an **asymmetric key pair** — private key for signing, public key for verification. |
| 2 | Provides **authentication and integrity**, but **not non-repudiation** (since the receiver also knows the key and could forge). | Provides **authentication, integrity, AND non-repudiation** (only the sender possesses the private key). |
| 3 | **Faster** — uses symmetric primitives (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256). | **Slower** — uses computationally expensive asymmetric operations (e.g., RSA, ECDSA). |

*(Award full 3 marks if any 3 valid distinctions are clearly stated.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the three properties of a cryptographic hash function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each for property + explanation):**

1. **Pre-image Resistance (One-wayness):** Given a hash value $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find any message $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. This prevents an attacker from recovering the original input from its digest.

2. **Second Pre-image Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance):** Given a specific message $M_1$, it is computationally infeasible to find a different message $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_2) = H(M_1)$. This prevents forging an alternate message with the same hash as a given message.

3. **Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance):** It is computationally infeasible to find any two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. This is the strongest property and protects against malicious dual-document forgery.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**A) With neat diagrams, explain the operation of HMAC. Show the mathematical construction and discuss why two different padding constants (ipad and opad) are used. (7 marks)**

**B) An organization uses MD5 for integrity verification of its financial transaction logs. As a security consultant, evaluate the suitability of MD5 and recommend a stronger alternative. Justify your answer using the birthday paradox. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for A — HMAC Construction (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): HMAC Definition**

HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) is a mechanism that combines a cryptographic hash function with a secret key to produce a keyed message authentication code. It is standardized in **RFC 2104** and **FIPS 198**.

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\big((K_0 \oplus opad) \| H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \| M)\big)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Step-by-Step Construction**

The construction proceeds in six stages (as derived in Section 3.2):

1. **Key normalization:** Adjust $K$ to exactly $b$ bits (block size) by hashing if too long, zero-padding if too short → $K_0$.
2. **Inner pad:** $K_{\text{inner}} = K_0 \oplus ipad$, where $ipad = 0x36$ repeated $b$ times.
3. **Inner hash:** $H_{\text{inner}} = H(K_{\text{inner}} \| M)$.
4. **Outer pad:** $K_{\text{outer}} = K_0 \oplus opad$, where $opad = 0x5C$ repeated $b$ times.
5. **Outer hash:** $\text{Tag} = H(K_{\text{outer}} \| H_{\text{inner}})$.
6. **Output:** Fixed-length tag of $n$ bits.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Why Two Different Pads?**

The two different constants $0x36$ and $0x5C$ ensure that the **inner key** and **outer key** are cryptographically independent even though they share the same secret $K$.

- If both pads were identical (e.g., just $H(K \| M)$), the construction would be vulnerable to **length-extension attacks**, where an attacker who knows $H(K \| M)$ can compute $H(K \| M \| \text{padding} \| M')$ without knowing $K$.
- Using two distinct constants means that even if the attacker recovers the inner hash, they cannot easily compute the outer hash without knowing the full $K_0$.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Diagram**

Refer to the **HMAC Internal Architecture diagram in Section 4.3** for the visual representation of the two-layer nested hash structure.

**Step 5 (Bonus 1 mark for mentioning):** HMAC's security reduces to that of the underlying hash function. If SHA-256 is used as $H$, HMAC-SHA-256 has $2^{256}$ pre-image security and $2^{128}$ collision security (due to birthday paradox on outer hash).

---

### Model Solution for B — MD5 Evaluation using Birthday Paradox (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): MD5 Overview**

MD5 (Message Digest Algorithm 5) was designed by Ron Rivest in 1991. It produces a **128-bit** hash value. It was officially **deprecated for security use by NIST in 2004** after Wang et al. demonstrated practical collision attacks.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Birthday Paradox Calculation**

For a hash function with $n$-bit output, a collision can be found in approximately:

$$ k \approx 1.177 \times 2^{n/2} \text{ trials} $$

For MD5 ($n = 128$):

$$ k \approx 1.177 \times 2^{64} \approx 2.18 \times 10^{19} \text{ hash evaluations} $$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Practical Feasibility**

In 2004, Wang's team produced MD5 collisions using approximately $2^{39}$ hash operations, which is far less than $2^{64}$. Today, GPU clusters can find MD5 collisions in **seconds to minutes**. The 2012 *Flame malware* exploited an MD5 collision to forge a Microsoft code-signing certificate.

**Step 4 (1 mark): Recommendation**

**Replace MD5 with SHA-256 or SHA-3 (Keccak).** SHA-256 provides a 256-bit output, giving:

$$ k \approx 1.177 \times 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \text{ trials} $$

This is computationally infeasible with current technology. For financial transaction logs, additionally use **HMAC-SHA-256** rather than bare SHA-256 to authenticate the source.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**A) Explain the three security requirements that a message authentication scheme must fulfill. Discuss how Message Authentication Codes (MAC) and Hash Functions address each requirement. (7 marks)**

**B) Describe the working of the Merkle–Damgård construction used in hash functions like SHA-256. Explain why this construction is vulnerable to length-extension attacks and how HMAC overcomes this limitation. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for A — Security Requirements (7 marks)

**Step 1 (3 marks): Three Requirements**

1. **Message Integrity / Source Authentication:** The receiver must be able to verify that the message came from the claimed sender and was not modified in transit.
2. **Non-repudiation (optional but desirable):** The sender must not be able to later deny sending the message.
3. **Replay Protection:** The receiver must be able to detect if a previously sent message is being reused.

**Step 2 (2 marks): How MAC Addresses These**

- **MAC** uses a shared secret key $K$. Since only sender and receiver know $K$, a valid MAC tag authenticates the sender (✓) and confirms integrity (✓).
- **Limitation:** MAC does NOT provide non-repudiation — the receiver also has the key and could forge a MAC.
- For replay protection, sequence numbers or timestamps are added to the message before MAC computation.

**Step 3 (2 marks): How Hash Functions Address These**

- **Hash alone** provides only integrity. It does NOT authenticate the source because the hash is public — anyone can compute $H(M)$.
- To achieve authentication, the hash is used in combination with a key (HMAC) or digital signature.
- For example: $H(M \| K)$ is a naive MAC; $H(M)$ signed with sender's private key is a digital signature.

---

### Model Solution for B — Merkle–Damgård Construction and Length Extension (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Merkle–Damgård Construction**

The Merkle–Damgård construction builds a fixed-output hash function from a fixed-input compression function. Steps:

1. **Padding:** Append a '1' bit, then zeros, then the 64-bit length of the original message, ensuring the total is a multiple of the block size $b$.
2. **Block Division:** Split the padded message into $n$ blocks $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_n$, each of $b$ bits.
3. **Iterative Compression:** Starting from an initial hash value $H_0 = IV$, compute:
$$ H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n $$
4. **Output:** The final hash value $H_n$ is the message digest.

**Step 2 (1 mark): Application in SHA-256**

SHA-256 uses Merkle–Damgård with $b = 512$ bits, a 256-bit state, and a 64-round compression function. IV is fixed (derived from square roots of prime numbers).

**Step 3 (2 marks): Length-Extension Vulnerability**

In a naive MAC scheme $H(K \| M)$, an attacker who knows $H(K \| M)$ and $\vert M \vert$ can compute $H(K \| M \| \text{padding} \| M')$ for any attacker-chosen $M'$ without knowing $K$. This is because the Merkle–Damgård state after processing $K \| M$ is exactly the hash output, which can be used as $H_i$ for further extension.

**Step 4 (2 marks): How HMAC Overcomes This**

HMAC's two-layer construction prevents length-extension:

- The outer hash does not simply continue from the inner state — it incorporates $K_{\text{outer}}$, a separately-derived value involving $opad$.
- Even if the attacker knows the inner hash $H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \| M)$, they cannot compute the outer hash without knowing $K_0$, because the outer hash requires $H(K_0 \oplus opad \| H_{\text{inner}})$, and $K_0$ is secret.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating HMAC with simple $H(K \| M)$:** Students often write $H(K \| M)$ as "HMAC" — this is **wrong** and loses 2 marks. HMAC strictly uses the two-pad construction.
> 2. **Confusing MAC with Digital Signatures:** Many students claim MAC provides non-repudiation. It does NOT.
> 3. **Forgetting the Birthday Paradox:** When asked "why MD5 is insecure?", students often say "it's old" without quantifying the $2^{64}$ attack complexity. Always show the calculation.
> 4. **Mis-stating hash output sizes:** MD5 = 128 bits, SHA-1 = 160 bits, SHA-256 = 256 bits. Mixing these up costs marks.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** KTU ESE questions worth 7 marks require a labelled diagram. Failing to draw the HMAC structure diagram costs at least 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Message Authentication & Hash Functions**

- [ ] Message authentication ensures **integrity + source verification**, not confidentiality.
- [ ] Three core properties of cryptographic hash functions: **pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, collision resistance**.
- [ ] **Collision resistance** is the strongest property; breaking it (as in MD5/SHA-1) is the most catastrophic.
- [ ] The **Birthday Paradox** gives an $n$-bit hash only **$n/2$ bits of collision resistance**.
- [ ] **MAC** uses a symmetric secret key; provides authentication + integrity, but NOT non-repudiation.
- [ ] **Digital signatures** use asymmetric keys; provide authentication + integrity + non-repudiation.
- [ ] **HMAC** = $H((K_0 \oplus opad) \| H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \| M))$.
- [ ] $ipad = 0x36$ (inner pad constant); $opad = 0x5C$ (outer pad constant), each repeated to block size.
- [ ] **Merkle–Damgård construction** is vulnerable to **length-extension attacks**; HMAC's two-pad design defeats this.
- [ ] **MD5** = 128-bit output, broken; **SHA-1** = 160-bit output, deprecated; **SHA-256** = 256-bit output, current standard.
- [ ] **SHA-256** uses 64 rounds of compression on 512-bit blocks with a 256-bit state.
- [ ] **HMAC security** reduces to that of the underlying hash; HMAC-SHA-256 has $2^{128}$ collision security.
- [ ] **Grover's algorithm** (quantum) halves the effective security of MAC/hash primitives.
- [ ] For modern systems, prefer **HMAC-SHA-256, HMAC-SHA-384, or SHA-3 family** (Keccak-based, sponge construction).
- [ ] **Digital signature standards:** RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA; **MAC standards:** HMAC, CMAC, GMAC.
- [ ] TLS 1.3 uses **AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data)** — combining confidentiality and authentication.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Signature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Signature**: A cryptographic mechanism that provides **authenticity**, **integrity**, and **non-repudiation** of a digital message, document, or transaction. It is a mathematical scheme that uses **asymmetric key cryptography** to generate a unique alphanumeric string (the signature) bound to a specific message and a specific signer, such that any tampering with the message invalidates the signature.

A digital signature is fundamentally different from a digital certificate (which binds an identity to a public key). The digital signature binds the **identity of the signer** to the **content of the message** through a mathematical transformation that can be verified by any third party.

The three security guarantees provided by a robust digital signature scheme are:

| Property | Cryptographic Guarantee |
|----------|------------------------|
| **Authentication** | Confirms the identity of the sender/signatory |
| **Integrity** | Guarantees that the message has not been altered in transit |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Prevents the signer from later denying having signed the message |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU 2024 Syllabus Note**: Digital Signature is a **Module 3** core topic of PEECT864 (Cyber Security) and is directly linked to **Course Outcome CO3**: *Apply cryptographic techniques to ensure confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation in network security.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are signing a legally binding paper document in front of a Notary Public:

1. **Your handwritten signature** is unique to you and difficult to forge (analogous to your **private key**).
2. The **paper document** itself contains the information being signed (analogous to the **plaintext message**).
3. The **notary verifies** that the signature on the document matches your registered signature on file (analogous to the **recipient verifying** using your **public key**).
4. Once signed, **you cannot deny** that you signed it, because the notary witnessed it (analogous to **non-repudiation**).

A digital signature performs exactly this function — but in the cyber world, with mathematical precision. The signer uses their **secret private key** to "sign" a hash of the message. Anyone with the signer's widely-distributed **public key** can verify that the signature is genuine and the message is unmodified.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember**: In **encryption** with public-key cryptography, we use the *receiver's public key to encrypt* and the *receiver's private key to decrypt*. In **digital signatures**, the process is **reversed** — we use the *sender's private key to sign* and the *sender's public key to verify*.

---

## 1.3 Why Digital Signatures Are Indispensable

In the modern engineering landscape, digital signatures form the backbone of:

- **Software Distribution**: Code-signing certificates (Authenticode, Apple notarization)
- **SSL/TLS Handshakes**: Server authentication via signed certificates
- **E-Governance**: India's **Information Technology Act, 2000** legally recognizes digital signatures (under Section 3) as equivalent to physical signatures.
- **Blockchain & Cryptocurrency**: Every Bitcoin transaction is signed with **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)**.
- **Document Workflows**: PDF signing, smart contracts, code commits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: Students often confuse **digital signature** with **digital certificate**. A digital **certificate** binds a public key to an identity (issued by a CA). A digital **signature** binds a message to the signer's private key (mathematical operation). Know the difference — examiners test this distinction directly.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Operational Requirements of a Digital Signature Scheme

A secure digital signature scheme must satisfy these properties, as per standard cryptographic literature and the **NIST FIPS 186-4** standard:

- **Unforgeability**: An adversary without the private key cannot produce a valid signature for any message.
- **Authenticity**: The verifier can confirm the signer's identity cryptographically.
- **Integrity**: Any modification to the signed message causes verification to fail.
- **Non-repudiation**: The signer cannot later validly claim the signature was forged.
- **Verifiability**: Any third party with the public key can independently verify the signature.
- **Non-reusability**: A signature created for one document cannot be detached and reattached to a different document.

---

## 2.2 The Three Core Algorithms of a Digital Signature Scheme

A digital signature scheme is formally a **triple of probabilistic polynomial-time algorithms** $(G, S, V)$:

| Algorithm | Symbol | Function | Inputs | Outputs |
|-----------|--------|----------|--------|---------|
| **Key Generation** | $G$ | Creates the key pair | Security parameter $k$ | Private key $sk$, Public key $pk$ |
| **Signing** | $S$ | Produces the signature | Message $M$, Private key $sk$ | Signature $\sigma$ |
| **Verification** | $V$ | Confirms validity | Message $M$, Signature $\sigma$, Public key $pk$ | Boolean: Valid (1) or Invalid (0) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Verification Equation**: A signature $\sigma$ is valid for message $M$ under public key $pk$ if and only if $V(pk, M, \sigma) = \text{TRUE}$.

---

## 2.3 Generic Digital Signature Process

### A. Signing Process (Performed by the Sender / Signer)

1. The sender computes a **hash** $h = H(M)$ of the message $M$ using a cryptographic hash function $H$ (e.g., **SHA-256**, with output **256 bits / 64 hex characters**).
2. The sender **encrypts the hash** using their **private key** $sk_{sender}$, producing the signature $\sigma = S(sk_{sender}, h)$.
3. The signature $\sigma$ is appended to the message $M$, producing the signed message $(M \Vert \sigma)$.
4. The combined bundle is transmitted over the insecure channel.

### B. Verification Process (Performed by the Receiver / Verifier)

1. The receiver separates the message $M$ and the signature $\sigma$.
2. The receiver computes the hash $h' = H(M)$ of the received message $M$.
3. The receiver **decrypts the signature** $\sigma$ using the sender's **public key** $pk_{sender}$, recovering $h = V_{decrypt}(pk_{sender}, \sigma)$.
4. The receiver compares $h'$ and $h$. If $h' = h$, the signature is **valid**; otherwise, it is **rejected**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hash instead of signing the full message?** Public-key operations (modular exponentiation, elliptic curve point multiplication) are computationally expensive. Hashing produces a fixed-size, short digest (e.g., 256 bits) that is far cheaper to sign. Hashing also protects against chosen-plaintext attacks and provides message integrity.

---

## 2.4 RSA Digital Signature Scheme

The **RSA signature scheme** was the first practical digital signature scheme and remains widely deployed. It directly leverages the **RSA trapdoor permutation**.

### Key Generation

1. Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute the modulus $n = p \cdot q$.
3. Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$.
4. Choose a public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
5. Compute the private exponent $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

The **public key** is $(n, e)$; the **private key** is $d$ (with $p, q, \phi(n)$ kept secret).

### Signing

$$\sigma = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$$

### Verification

$$H(M) \stackrel{?}{=} \sigma^{e} \bmod n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The verification equation holds because $\sigma^{e} \bmod n = (H(M)^{d})^{e} \bmod n = H(M)^{de} \bmod n$. Since $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, by Euler's theorem $H(M)^{ed} \equiv H(M)^{1} \equiv H(M) \pmod{n}$.

### KTU Formula Sheet (RSA Signatures)

| Parameter | Formula | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Public modulus product of two primes |
| Totient | $\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$ | Number of integers coprime to $n$ |
| Key Relation | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Public-private exponent linkage |
| **Signature Generation** | $\sigma = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$ | Signer uses private exponent $d$ |
| **Signature Verification** | $H(M) \equiv \sigma^{e} \bmod n$ | Verifier uses public exponent $e$ |
| Hash Function Standard | $H : \{0,1\}^{*} \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}_{n}$ | Maps arbitrary message to $\mathbb{Z}_{n}$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Do not confuse RSA Encryption with RSA Signing!** 
> - **Encryption** (for confidentiality): $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ (receiver's public key)
> - **Signing** (for authenticity): $\sigma = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$ (sender's private key)

---

## 2.5 Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) — The NIST Standard

The **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)** was published by **NIST in 1991** as part of **FIPS 186** (now FIPS 186-4). Unlike RSA, DSA is a signature-only scheme that uses **modular exponentiation** in a prime-order subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$.

### Key Generation

1. Choose a prime $p$ of $L$ bits (where $L$ is between **1024 and 3072**, multiples of 64).
2. Choose a 160-bit prime divisor $q$ of $p - 1$.
3. Choose a generator $g$ of the order-$q$ subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$.
4. Choose a random integer $x$ such that $1 \leq x \leq q - 1$ (this is the **private key**).
5. Compute $y = g^{x} \bmod p$ (this is the **public key**).

### Signing

1. Choose a per-message random secret $k$ with $1 \leq k \leq q - 1$.
2. Compute $r = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q$.
3. Compute $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$.
4. The signature is the pair $(r, s)$.

### Verification

1. Compute $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$.
2. Compute $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q$.
3. Compute $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q$.
4. Compute $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$.
5. The signature is valid if and only if $v = r$.

### DSA vs RSA Signature Comparison

| Property | RSA Signature | DSA Signature |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Standard | PKCS #1 | FIPS 186-4 |
| Underlying Hard Problem | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm |
| Signature Size | Equal to modulus size (e.g., **2048 bits**) | Two 160-bit values ($r, s$) = **320 bits** |
| Key Sizes | $p, q \geq 1024$ bits each | $p \geq 2048$ bits, $q \geq 256$ bits (modern) |
| Speed | Verification faster than signing | Signing faster than verification |
| Patent Status | Free | Originally patented (now expired) |

---

## 2.6 Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA)

**ECDSA** is the modern successor to DSA and is the signature scheme used in **Bitcoin, Ethereum, TLS 1.3**, and most contemporary security protocols. It operates over an **elliptic curve group** $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ instead of $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$.

| ECDSA Parameter | Meaning |
|-----------------|---------|
| Curve $E$ | e.g., **NIST P-256**, **secp256k1** (Bitcoin) |
| Base point $G$ | Generator of order $n$ |
| Private key $d_A$ | Random integer in $[1, n-1]$ |
| Public key $Q_A$ | $Q_A = d_A \cdot G$ (point multiplication) |
| Signature | Pair $(r, s)$ where $r$ is the x-coordinate of $k \cdot G$ mod $n$ |

### Key Engineering Advantage

ECDSA achieves the **same security strength as RSA with much smaller key sizes**:

| Security Level | RSA Key Size | ECDSA Key Size |
|----------------|--------------|----------------|
| 128-bit | 3072 bits | 256 bits |
| 192-bit | 7680 bits | 384 bits |
| 256-bit | 15360 bits | 512 bits |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Digital Signatures

Digital signatures are foundational to:

- **Internet PKI**: Every X.509 certificate is digitally signed by a Certificate Authority.
- **Email Security**: S/MIME and PGP both use digital signatures.
- **Code Signing**: Authenticates software origin (Windows, macOS, Android).
- **Smart Cards & National IDs**: India's **Aadhaar eKYC** uses digital signatures for authentication.
- **Legal Contracts**: India's **eSign** service uses Aadhaar-linked digital signatures under IT Act 2000.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RSA Digital Signature: Exhaustive Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern**: This is the canonical 14-mark Part B question on digital signatures. Memorize this derivation format — examiners follow it strictly.

### Given Parameters (Standard KTU Numerical)

- Prime numbers: $p = 5$ and $q = 11$
- Public exponent: $e = 3$
- Message $M$: integer value such that $H(M) = m = 8$
- Hash function: Assume $H(M) = 8$

### Step 1: Compute the RSA Modulus $n$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= p \times q \\
n &= 5 \times 11 \\
n &= 55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: The modulus $n$ is the product of the two distinct large primes and forms the public arithmetic domain for all modular exponentiations.

---

### Step 2: Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(n) &= (p - 1)(q - 1) \\
\phi(n) &= (5 - 1)(11 - 1) \\
\phi(n) &= 4 \times 10 \\
\phi(n) &= 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: Euler's totient $\phi(n)$ counts the number of integers in $[1, n]$ that are coprime to $n$. This is the order of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_{n}^{*}$.

---

### Step 3: Compute the Private Exponent $d$ via Modular Inverse

We need to find $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $3 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
40 &= 3 \times 13 + 1 \\
3 &= 1 \times 3 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Back-substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 40 - 3 \times 13 \\
1 &= 40 - 3 \times 13 \\
\Rightarrow 3 \times (-13) &\equiv 1 \pmod{40} \\
\Rightarrow d &= -13 \equiv 27 \pmod{40}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification**: $3 \times 27 = 81 = 2 \times 40 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$. ✓

Thus, **private exponent $d = 27$**.

**Logic**: The private exponent $d$ is the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$. It is computed using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.

---

### Step 4: Generate the Digital Signature

The signer uses the private key $d = 27$ to sign the hash $H(M) = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= H(M)^{d} \bmod n \\
\sigma &= 8^{27} \bmod 55
\end{aligned}
$$

We compute $8^{27} \bmod 55$ using successive squaring:

$$
\begin{aligned}
8^{1} &\equiv 8 \pmod{55} \\
8^{2} &\equiv 64 \equiv 9 \pmod{55} \\
8^{4} &\equiv 9^{2} = 81 \equiv 26 \pmod{55} \\
8^{8} &\equiv 26^{2} = 676 \equiv 676 - 12(55) = 676 - 660 = 16 \pmod{55} \\
8^{16} &\equiv 16^{2} = 256 \equiv 256 - 4(55) = 256 - 220 = 36 \pmod{55}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $27 = 16 + 8 + 2 + 1$ (binary: 11011):

$$
\begin{aligned}
8^{27} &= 8^{16} \cdot 8^{8} \cdot 8^{2} \cdot 8^{1} \\
8^{27} &\equiv 36 \cdot 16 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 \pmod{55}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute stepwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
36 \cdot 16 &= 576 \equiv 576 - 10(55) = 576 - 550 = 26 \pmod{55} \\
26 \cdot 9 &= 234 \equiv 234 - 4(55) = 234 - 220 = 14 \pmod{55} \\
14 \cdot 8 &= 112 \equiv 112 - 2(55) = 112 - 110 = 2 \pmod{55}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, **the digital signature is $\sigma = 2$**.

**Logic**: The signer applies modular exponentiation with the private exponent to the message hash, producing a unique signature bound to both the message and the signer.

---

### Step 5: Verify the Digital Signature

The verifier uses the public key $(n, e) = (55, 3)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{e} \bmod n &\stackrel{?}{=} H(M) \\
2^{3} \bmod 55 &\stackrel{?}{=} 8 \\
8 \bmod 55 &\equiv 8 \pmod{55}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $2^{3} \bmod 55 = 8 = H(M)$, the signature is **VALID** ✓.

**Logic**: Verification succeeds because $\sigma^{e} = (H(M)^{d})^{e} = H(M)^{de} \equiv H(M)^{1} = H(M) \pmod{n}$ by Euler's theorem.

---

### Analytical Matrix: RSA Signature Workflow

| Stage | Operation | Key Used | Inputs | Output |
|-------|-----------|----------|--------|--------|
| **Setup** | Key Generation | — | Primes $p, q$ | $(n, e, d)$ |
| **Hash** | Hashing | — | Message $M$ | Hash $H(M)$ |
| **Sign** | $H(M)^{d} \bmod n$ | Private key $d$ | $H(M)$ | Signature $\sigma$ |
| **Verify** | $\sigma^{e} \bmod n \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$ | Public key $e$ | $\sigma, H(M)$ | Boolean |

---

## 3.2 DSA Signing and Verification: Exhaustive Worked Example

### Given Parameters

- Prime $p = 59$, prime divisor $q = 29$ (note: $29 \mid 58 = p - 1$)
- Generator $g = 3$ (of order $q$ in $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$)
- Private key $x = 7$
- Per-message random $k = 10$
- Message hash $H(M) = 26$

### Step 1: Compute the Public Key

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= g^{x} \bmod p \\
y &= 3^{7} \bmod 59 \\
y &= 2187 \bmod 59
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2187 \div 59 &= 37 \text{ remainder } 4 \\
\Rightarrow y &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Public key**: $y = 4$.

**Logic**: Public key is derived from the private key by raising the generator $g$ to the power $x$ modulo $p$.

---

### Step 2: Generate the Signature

Compute $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q \\
g^{k} &= 3^{10} \bmod 59
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute by successive squaring:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^{1} &\equiv 3 \\
3^{2} &\equiv 9 \\
3^{4} &\equiv 81 \equiv 81 - 59 = 22 \\
3^{8} &\equiv 22^{2} = 484 \equiv 484 - 8(59) = 484 - 472 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $10 = 8 + 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^{10} &= 3^{8} \cdot 3^{2} = 12 \cdot 9 = 108 \equiv 108 - 59 = 49 \pmod{59} \\
\Rightarrow r &= 49 \bmod 29 = 49 - 29 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k^{-1} \bmod q &= 10^{-1} \bmod 29
\end{aligned}
$$

Using Extended Euclidean: $10 \cdot 3 = 30 \equiv 1 \pmod{29}$, so $k^{-1} = 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 3 \cdot (26 + 7 \cdot 20) \bmod 29 \\
s &= 3 \cdot (26 + 140) \bmod 29 \\
s &= 3 \cdot 166 \bmod 29 \\
s &= 498 \bmod 29
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
498 \div 29 &= 17 \text{ remainder } 5 \\
\Rightarrow s &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Signature**: $(r, s) = (20, 5)$.

---

### Step 3: Verify the Signature

Compute modular inverse $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5 \cdot w &\equiv 1 \pmod{29} \\
5 \cdot 6 &= 30 \equiv 1 \pmod{29} \\
\Rightarrow w &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $u_1$ and $u_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= H(M) \cdot w \bmod q = 26 \cdot 6 \bmod 29 \\
26 \cdot 6 &= 156 \equiv 156 - 5(29) = 156 - 145 = 11 \pmod{29} \\
\Rightarrow u_1 &= 11 \\
u_2 &= r \cdot w \bmod q = 20 \cdot 6 \bmod 29 \\
20 \cdot 6 &= 120 \equiv 120 - 4(29) = 120 - 116 = 4 \pmod{29} \\
\Rightarrow u_2 &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q \\
v &= (3^{11} \cdot 4^{4} \bmod 59) \bmod 29
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $3^{11} \bmod 59$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^{11} &= 3^{8} \cdot 3^{2} \cdot 3^{1} = 12 \cdot 9 \cdot 3 = 324 \\
324 \bmod 59 &= 324 - 5(59) = 324 - 295 = 29
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $4^{4} \bmod 59$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4^{2} &= 16 \\
4^{4} &= 16^{2} = 256 \equiv 256 - 4(59) = 256 - 236 = 20 \pmod{59}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
29 \cdot 20 &= 580 \equiv 580 - 9(59) = 580 - 531 = 49 \pmod{59} \\
\Rightarrow v &= 49 \bmod 29 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $v = 20 = r$, **the signature is VALID** ✓.

**Logic**: DSA verification succeeds because by construction $v$ recovers the same $r$ component if the signature was honestly produced with the correct private key $x$.

---

## 3.3 Formal Proof of RSA Signature Correctness

We prove that if the signer honestly follows the protocol, the verifier will always accept.

**Given**:
- $n = p \cdot q$
- $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$
- $\sigma = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$

**To prove**: $\sigma^{e} \bmod n = H(M) \bmod n$.

**Proof**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{e} \bmod n &= (H(M)^{d})^{e} \bmod n \\
&= H(M)^{de} \bmod n
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, we can write $ed = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k \geq 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(M)^{de} \bmod n &= H(M)^{1 + k\phi(n)} \bmod n \\
&= H(M)^{1} \cdot H(M)^{k\phi(n)} \bmod n
\end{aligned}
$$

By **Euler's Theorem**, if $\gcd(H(M), n) = 1$, then:

$$
H(M)^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
H(M)^{k\phi(n)} = (H(M)^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv 1^{k} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(M)^{de} \bmod n &\equiv H(M) \cdot 1 \bmod n \\
&\equiv H(M) \bmod n \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: The proof rests on the fact that the public and private exponents are multiplicative inverses modulo $\phi(n)$, which gives the trapdoor property of RSA.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Digital Signature Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Original Document M"]
    nodeB["Hash Function H e.g. SHA-256"]
    nodeC["Message Digest H of M of fixed 256 bits"]
    nodeD["Signers Private Key sk"]
    nodeE["Signing Algorithm Signature equals H to the power d mod n"]
    nodeF["Digital Signature sigma"]
    nodeG["Sender Transmits M concatenated with sigma over network"]
    nodeH["Receiver Separates M and sigma"]
    nodeI["Hash Function H applied to M"]
    nodeJ["Computed Hash H prime of M"]
    nodeK["Signers Public Key pk"]
    nodeL["Verification Algorithm H prime equals sigma to the power e mod n"]
    nodeM["Decision Valid Signature or Rejected"]
    nodeN["Trusted CA binds pk to signer identity"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeN --> nodeK
```

**Reading the Diagram**:
- The **left pipeline** (Sender side) computes the signature using the private key.
- The **right pipeline** (Receiver side) re-computes the hash and verifies using the public key.
- The **public key** is authenticated through a **trusted Certificate Authority (CA)**, ensuring the verifier knows the public key genuinely belongs to the claimed signer.

---

## 4.2 RSA Signature Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM["Message M"]
    nodeH["Hash H of M via SHA"]
    nodeD["Private Key d"]
    nodeS["Modular Exponentiation M to the power d mod n"]
    nodeSig["Signature sigma"]
    nodeE["Public Key e"]
    nodeV["Verification sigma to the power e mod n"]
    nodeC["Comparison H prime equals H of M"]
    nodeOK["VALID Signature Accepted"]
    nodeNo["INVALID Signature Rejected"]

    nodeM --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeS
    nodeD --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeSig
    nodeSig --> nodeV
    nodeE --> nodeV
    nodeM --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeOK
    nodeC --> nodeNo
```

---

## 4.3 DSA Signature Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Global Parameters p, q, g"]
    nodeX["Private Key x random in 1 to q-1"]
    nodeY["Public Key y equals g to the power x mod p"]
    nodeM["Message M"]
    nodeK["Per-message random k"]
    nodeR["Compute r equals g to the power k mod p mod q"]
    nodeS["Compute s equals k inverse times H of M plus x times r mod q"]
    nodeSig["Signature tuple r and s"]
    nodeW["Compute w equals s inverse mod q"]
    nodeU1["u1 equals H of M times w mod q"]
    nodeU2["u2 equals r times w mod q"]
    nodeV["Compute v equals g to the power u1 times y to the power u2 mod p mod q"]
    nodeCheck["Check v equals r"]

    nodeP --> nodeY
    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeM --> nodeS
    nodeK --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeX --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeSig
    nodeSig --> nodeW
    nodeW --> nodeU1
    nodeW --> nodeU2
    nodeSig --> nodeU2
    nodeM --> nodeU1
    nodeU1 --> nodeV
    nodeU2 --> nodeV
    nodeY --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeCheck
```

---

## 4.4 Attack Surface and Threat Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeKS["Key-Only Attack Attacker knows only public key pk"]
    nodeMA["Known-Message Attack Attacker has valid signatures for chosen messages"]
    nodeCMA["Chosen-Message Attack Attacker obtains signatures for messages of choice"]
    nodeEA["Existential Forgery Produce valid signature for at least one message"]
    nodeUF["Universal Forgery Produce valid signature for any given message"]
    nodeTS["Total Break Recover the private key"]

    nodeKS --> nodeMA
    nodeMA --> nodeCMA
    nodeCMA --> nodeEA
    nodeEA --> nodeUF
    nodeUF --> nodeTS
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Existence of Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack (EUF-CMA)** is the **gold standard security notion** for digital signatures. RSA-FDH (Full Domain Hash) and ECDSA meet this under standard assumptions (RSA problem, ECDLP).

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
