## Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Digital Signature and a Digital Certificate.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | Digital Signature | Digital Certificate |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Purpose** | Binds signer's identity to a specific message | Binds a public key to an identity |
| **Created by** | Signer using private key | Certificate Authority (CA) |
| **Provides** | Authentication, Integrity, Non-repudiation | Trust establishment for public keys |
| **Example** | Signing a PDF document | X.509 SSL/TLS certificate |

A **digital signature** is a mathematical value generated by signing the hash of a message with the signer's private key. A **digital certificate** is an electronic document issued by a trusted CA that contains a public key and the identity of its owner, digitally signed by the CA itself. *(3 Marks: 1 for each differentiating dimension, 1 for example)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three properties that a secure digital signature scheme must satisfy.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer**:

1. **Authentication**: The signature must verifiably prove the identity of the signer.
2. **Integrity**: Any modification to the signed message must invalidate the signature.
3. **Non-repudiation**: The signer cannot later deny generating the signature.

*(Optional additional valid properties: Unforgeability, Verifiability by third parties, Non-reusability.)* *(3 Marks: 1 mark per property with brief explanation)*

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions follow the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Internal Choice pattern**: students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Explain the RSA Digital Signature Scheme in detail. Given $p = 7$, $q = 11$, $e = 13$, and message $M$ such that $H(M) = 5$, generate the signature and verify it.**

**(a)** Explain the RSA Digital Signature Algorithm with a neat diagram. *(7 Marks)*
**(b)** Perform the key generation, signing, and verification for the given parameters. *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a): RSA Digital Signature Algorithm *(7 Marks)*

**Introduction** *(1 mark)*:
The RSA Digital Signature Scheme, proposed by **Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman in 1978**, is the most widely used signature scheme based on the difficulty of **integer factorization**.

**Key Generation Steps** *(2 marks)*:
1. Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute $n = p \cdot q$ (modulus) and $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
3. Choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
4. Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.
5. Public key = $(n, e)$; Private key = $d$.

**Signing** *(2 marks)*:
- Compute the message hash $H(M)$ using a cryptographic hash function (SHA-256).
- Compute the signature $\sigma = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$.
- Send $(M, \sigma)$ to the receiver.

**Verification** *(2 marks)*:
- Compute $H(M)$ from the received message.
- Compute $\sigma^{e} \bmod n$.
- If $\sigma^{e} \bmod n = H(M)$, accept; otherwise, reject.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A neat diagram showing Sender (with private key) → Hash → Signature → Transmission → Receiver → Verification using Public Key must be drawn for full marks.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1: Compute modulus $n$** *(1 mark)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= p \cdot q = 7 \cdot 11 = 77
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\phi(n)$** *(1 mark)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(n) &= (p-1)(q-1) = 6 \cdot 10 = 60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute private exponent $d$** *(2 marks)*:

Find $d$ such that $13d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
60 &= 4 \cdot 13 + 8 \\
13 &= 1 \cdot 8 + 5 \\
8 &= 1 \cdot 5 + 3 \\
5 &= 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \\
3 &= 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \\
2 &= 2 \cdot 1 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Back-substitution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 3 - 1 \cdot 2 \\
&= 3 - 1 \cdot (5 - 1 \cdot 3) = 2 \cdot 3 - 5 \\
&= 2 \cdot (8 - 5) - 5 = 2 \cdot 8 - 3 \cdot 5 \\
&= 2 \cdot 8 - 3 \cdot (13 - 8) = 5 \cdot 8 - 3 \cdot 13 \\
&= 5 \cdot (60 - 4 \cdot 13) - 3 \cdot 13 = 5 \cdot 60 - 23 \cdot 13
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore: $-23 \cdot 13 \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$, so $d = -23 \equiv 37 \pmod{60}$.

**Verification**: $13 \cdot 37 = 481 = 8 \cdot 60 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$ ✓.

**Step 4: Generate the signature** *(1.5 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= H(M)^{d} \bmod n = 5^{37} \bmod 77
\end{aligned}
$$

By successive squaring:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{1} &\equiv 5 \\
5^{2} &\equiv 25 \\
5^{4} &\equiv 25^{2} = 625 \equiv 625 - 8(77) = 625 - 616 = 9 \\
5^{8} &\equiv 9^{2} = 81 \equiv 81 - 77 = 4 \\
5^{16} &\equiv 4^{2} = 16 \\
5^{32} &\equiv 16^{2} = 256 \equiv 256 - 3(77) = 256 - 231 = 25
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $37 = 32 + 4 + 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{37} &= 5^{32} \cdot 5^{4} \cdot 5^{1} = 25 \cdot 9 \cdot 5 = 1125 \\
1125 \bmod 77 &= 1125 - 14(77) = 1125 - 1078 = 47
\end{aligned}
$$

**Signature**: $\sigma = 47$.

**Step 5: Verify the signature** *(1.5 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{e} \bmod n &= 47^{13} \bmod 77
\end{aligned}
$$

By successive squaring:

$$
\begin{aligned}
47^{1} &\equiv 47 \\
47^{2} &\equiv 47 \cdot 47 = 2209 \equiv 2209 - 28(77) = 2209 - 2156 = 53 \\
47^{4} &\equiv 53^{2} = 2809 \equiv 2809 - 36(77) = 2809 - 2772 = 37 \\
47^{8} &\equiv 37^{2} = 1369 \equiv 1369 - 17(77) = 1369 - 1309 = 60
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $13 = 8 + 4 + 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
47^{13} &= 47^{8} \cdot 47^{4} \cdot 47^{1} = 60 \cdot 37 \cdot 47 \\
&= 60 \cdot 37 = 2220 \equiv 2220 - 28(77) = 2220 - 2156 = 64 \\
64 \cdot 47 &= 3008 \equiv 3008 - 39(77) = 3008 - 3003 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sigma^{e} \bmod n = 5 = H(M)$, the signature is **VALID** ✓.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain the Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) with its key generation, signing, and verification phases. Discuss the security strength of DSA compared to RSA.**

**(a)** Describe DSA with its full mathematical workflow. *(7 Marks)*
**(b)** Compare DSA with RSA on security, performance, and signature size. List two real-world applications of digital signatures. *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a): DSA Workflow *(7 Marks)*

**Introduction** *(1 mark)*:
The **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)** was published by **NIST in August 1991** as part of **FIPS 186**. Unlike RSA, DSA is a signature-only scheme (cannot be used for encryption) based on the **discrete logarithm problem**.

**Global Public Parameters** *(1 mark)*:
- A prime $p$ of $L$ bits (1024 to 3072).
- A prime divisor $q$ of $p-1$, of 160 bits.
- A generator $g$ of the order-$q$ subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$.

**Key Generation** *(1.5 marks)*:
- Choose random private key $x \in [1, q-1]$.
- Compute public key $y = g^{x} \bmod p$.

**Signing Phase** *(2 marks)*:
- Choose random per-message secret $k \in [1, q-1]$.
- $r = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q$.
- $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$.
- Signature is $(r, s)$.

**Verification Phase** *(1.5 marks)*:
- $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$.
- $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q$, $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q$.
- $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$.
- Accept if $v = r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **critical security requirement** in DSA is that the per-message secret $k$ must be **cryptographically random and never reused**. Reuse of $k$ across two different messages allows an attacker to recover the private key $x$. This was the cause of the famous **Sony PS3 ECDSA break in 2010**.

---

#### Part (b): DSA vs RSA + Applications *(7 Marks)*

**Comparison Table** *(4 marks)*:

| Criterion | RSA Signature | DSA |
|-----------|---------------|-----|
| **Underlying Hard Problem** | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm |
| **Standard** | PKCS #1 (RFC 8017) | FIPS 186-4 |
| **Signature Size** | Equal to modulus (e.g., 2048 bits) | 320 bits (two 160-bit values) |
| **Key Generation Speed** | Slower (requires prime generation) | Faster |
| **Signing Speed** | Slower | Faster |
| **Verification Speed** | Faster | Slower (two exponentiations) |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | Lower | Higher (smaller signature) |
| **Encryption Capability** | Yes (sign-then-encrypt) | No (signature-only) |
| **Security at 128 bits** | 3072-bit RSA | 256-bit DSA parameter $p$, 256-bit $q$ |

**Real-World Applications of Digital Signatures** *(3 marks)*:

1. **Software Code Signing**: Operating systems (Windows, macOS, Android) require software publishers to digitally sign executables. This ensures the code originated from a verified developer and has not been tampered with.

2. **SSL/TLS Certificates**: Every HTTPS website has a certificate digitally signed by a Certificate Authority (e.g., DigiCert, Let's Encrypt). The browser verifies this signature to authenticate the website.

3. **Email Security (S/MIME and PGP)**: Digital signatures ensure email authenticity, protecting against phishing and spoofing.

4. **E-Governance and Smart Contracts**: India's IT Act 2000 (Section 3) recognizes digital signatures for legal contracts, income tax e-filing, and Aadhaar-linked eKYC authentication.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> 
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Digital Signature questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing encryption with signing direction**: Many students write $M^e \bmod n$ as the signature, confusing the signer's *private* key with the public key. **Remember**: Sign = private exponent; Verify = public exponent.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to include the hash step**: A signature is computed on $H(M)$, not on $M$ directly. Skipping this loses 1-2 marks.
> 
> 3. **No diagram**: For the 7-mark "explain with diagram" question, **omitting the block diagram** costs at least 2 marks. Always draw Sender → Hash → Sign → Transmit → Receive → Verify.
> 
> 4. **Modular arithmetic mistakes**: Numerical RSA problems require correct Extended Euclidean computation. Show all steps; partial credit requires visible work.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up DSA's $r$ and $s$**: Many students forget that $r$ is computed from $k$ and $s$ involves the private key $x$. Memorize the formula:
>    - $r = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q$
>    - $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$
> 
> 6. **Not mentioning non-repudiation**: When asked "explain digital signatures," **always** list **Authentication, Integrity, Non-repudiation** as the three pillars.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **digital signature** is a cryptographic primitive providing **authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation** — never confuse it with a digital certificate (which is a key-binding document).
- ✅ The **signing** operation uses the **sender's PRIVATE key**; **verification** uses the sender's **PUBLIC key** (this is opposite to encryption).
- ✅ Always sign the **hash** $H(M)$, not the raw message — this provides efficiency and security against chosen-message attacks.
- ✅ **RSA Signature**: $\sigma = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$; verify: $H(M) \equiv \sigma^{e} \bmod n$. Based on **integer factorization** hardness.
- ✅ **DSA**: Signature is the pair $(r, s)$. Based on **discrete logarithm** problem. Cannot be used for encryption (signature-only).
- ✅ **ECDSA**: Modern standard using elliptic curves. Same security as RSA with much smaller keys (e.g., 256-bit ECDSA ≈ 3072-bit RSA).
- ✅ DSA requires a **per-message random nonce $k$** — reusing $k$ catastrophically reveals the private key (Sony PS3 incident).
- ✅ **Full Domain Hash (FDH)** and **Probabilistic Signature Scheme (PSS)** are modern RSA signature padding schemes (PKCS#1 v2.2) that should replace textbook RSA signatures.
- ✅ Digital signatures are legally recognized in India under **Section 3 of the IT Act, 2000** for e-governance and e-commerce.
- ✅ The **gold-standard security notion** is **EUF-CMA** (Existential Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack).
- ✅ Attacks against signatures (in increasing severity): **key-only → known-message → chosen-message → existential forgery → universal forgery → total break**.
- ✅ Standardized algorithms: **FIPS 186-4 (DSA, ECDSA)**, **PKCS#1 v2.2 (RSA-PSS, RSA-PKCS1v1.5)**, **EdDSA (Ed25519, Ed448)**.
- ✅ Remember the **four standard hash functions** used with signatures: **SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512** (SHA-2 family); **SHA-3 (Keccak)** is also standardized but not yet widely deployed in signatures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cybercrimes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cybercrimes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cybercrime

According to the **KTU 2024 Syllabus** and the standard legal/information-security literature, a **cybercrime** (also known as a *computer-oriented crime*, *computer-related crime*, or *high-tech crime*) is formally defined as:

> A criminal offence in which a **computer, networked device, or network** is used as the **instrumentality, target, or incidental component** of the crime, and which violates cyber-laws, digital privacy, intellectual property, or national information-security statutes.

Mathematically, the cyber-incident space can be expressed as a **triple tuple**:

$$
\mathcal{C} \;=\; \langle A,\; T,\; M \rangle
$$

Where:
- $A$ = **Actor / Attacker** (threat agent)
- $T$ = **Target** (person, system, data, infrastructure)
- $M$ = **Modus Operandi / Mechanism** (malware, social engineering, protocol abuse)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** A crime is classified as a *cybercrime* only when an **electronic communication device / computer network** is materially involved in its planning, execution, or victimization.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Highway Robbery" Intuition

Imagine a **real city** (the physical world) and a **parallel shadow-city** (the Internet). Just as a city has roads, banks, shops, and police stations, the Internet has **servers, routers, e-commerce sites, and CERTs**.

A **cybercrime** is exactly the *shadow-city equivalent* of an urban crime:

| Real City Crime | Cybercity Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Bank vault break-in | Database breach / SQL injection |
| Pickpocketing | Phishing / Session hijacking |
| Forging currency | Credit-card cloning / carding |
| Threatening phone call | Cyberstalking / online harassment |
| Arson | Logic bomb / ransomware encryption |
| Vandalism | Website defacement / DoS attack |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook:** The only thing that changes between a *physical* and a *cyber* crime is the **medium of execution** — the *intent, motive, and legal classification* remain identical.

---

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Pillars of Cybercrime

Every cybercrime (whether simple or sophisticated) intersects with the following three mandatory pillars:

1. **Mens Rea (Criminal Intent)** — The mental element / motive of the perpetrator (financial gain, revenge, espionage, ideology).
2. **Actus Reus (Guilty Act)** — The concrete act of using a computing device to commit the offence.
3. **Cyberlaw Violation** — The act must contravene the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008)** of India, supplemented by sections of the **Indian Penal Code (IPC, now Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita)** and **Special Laws** (Copyright Act, Trademark Act, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"I-C-L" → Intent + Computer + Law**. If all three are present, it is a cybercrime.

---

## 1.4 Why Cybercrime Matters — Statistics & Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The global cost of cybercrime was projected at **USD 8.44 trillion** in 2022 and is on track to exceed **USD 23 trillion by 2027** (industry estimates).
> - India reported over **13.9 lakh cybercrime cases** in the year 2022 (NCRB data) — one of the highest globally.
> - The **average dwell time** (time between intrusion and detection) is **~207 days** in modern enterprise networks.

These numbers establish **why** cybercrime is a separate, dedicated module under the KTU 2024 scheme — it is not a subset of general security, but a *standalone domain* with its own legal, technical, and forensic treatment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The KTU-Standard Classification of Cybercrimes

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PEECT864) categorises cybercrime using the **two-axis taxonomy**:
- **Axis-1:** *By Target* (who is harmed)
- **Axis-2:** *By Modus Operandi* (how it is done)

### 2.1.1 Classification By Target (Axis-1)

#### A. Cybercrimes Against **Individuals**

These are offences whose victim is a **single, identifiable private person**. Examples:

- **Phishing** — fraudulent emails/websites to steal credentials.
- **Identity Theft** — impersonation of another person online.
- **Cyberstalking & Cyberbullying** — repeated harassment via digital media.
- **Defamation & Morphing** — uploading tampered obscene images/videos.
- **Email Bombing / Spamming** — flooding inbox to disrupt communication.
- **DoS / DDoS against personal servers** — denying service to a user.
- **Child Pornography / CSAM** — depicting minors in sexually explicit acts.

#### B. Cybercrimes Against **Property**

Target = data, intellectual property, or financial assets.

- **Hacking / Unauthorised Access** (Sec. 66 IT Act).
- **Data Theft / Data Leakage** — exfiltration of corporate data.
- **Software Piracy & IP Violations** — copyright infringement (Copyright Act 1957).
- **Credit-card Fraud / Online Banking Fraud**.
- **Vishing / Smishing / SIM Swapping**.
- **Cryptojacking** — covert use of victim's CPU/GPU to mine crypto.
- **Ransomware** — encrypting data and demanding payment.

#### C. Cybercrimes Against **Government / Nation**

Target = state sovereignty, critical infrastructure, or public services.

- **Cyberterrorism** — politically motivated attacks causing disruption/panic.
- **Cyberwarfare** — state-sponsored attacks on another nation.
- **Critical Infrastructure Attack** — power grids, water treatment, telecom.
- **Website Defacement of Government Portals**.
- **Distribution of Anti-national / Radicalising Content**.

#### D. Cybercrimes Against **Society at Large**

Target = the public commons, moral fabric, or community trust.

- **Online Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM)**.
- **Drug Trafficking over Darknet** (e.g., Silk-Road-era markets).
- **Online Gambling / Betting Fraud**.
- **Money Laundering via Crypto** (PMLA implications).
- **Spread of Misinformation / Disinformation / Fake News**.
- **Human Trafficking** (coordinated via social platforms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says *"classify cybercrimes"*, the four-category framework **Individual / Property / Government / Society** is the *expected* answer. Memorise it.

---

### 2.1.2 Classification By Modus Operandi (Axis-2)

| MO Category | Description | Common Offence |
|---|---|---|
| **Hacking / Cracking** | Unauthorised access to computer systems | Sec. 66 IT Act |
| **Malware-Based** | Use of viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware | Sec. 43 + 66 IT Act |
| **Social Engineering** | Manipulating human psychology | Phishing, Pretexting, Baiting |
| **Cryptographic Attacks** | Breaking or abusing encryption | Brute-force, MITM, Key-logging |
| **Network-Based** | Exploiting network protocols | DoS, DDoS, ARP poisoning, DNS spoof |
| **Application-Based** | Exploiting software vulnerabilities | SQLi, XSS, RCE, Buffer overflow |
| **Hardware / Physical** | Tampering with hardware | USB drop, Side-channel, Eavesdropping |

---

## 2.2 The **Indian Legal Framework** — The KTU Mandated Statutes

### 2.2.1 The Information Technology Act, 2000 (Amended 2008)

The **IT Act** is the *primary cyberlaw of India*. KTU examiners **expect** knowledge of key sections:

| Section | Offence Description | Punishment |
|---|---|---|
| **Sec. 43** | Damage to computer / computer system without authorisation | Compensation up to ₹1 Crore |
| **Sec. 65** | Tampering with computer source documents (source-code hiding) | Up to 3 years imprisonment + fine |
| **Sec. 66** | *Hacking* — unauthorised access + damage / data theft / disruption | Up to 3 years / fine / both |
| **Sec. 66A** ⚠ *Struck down (Shreya Singhal v. UoI, 2015)* | Sending offensive messages through communication service | — (Unconstitutional) |
| **Sec. 66B** | Receiving stolen computer or communication device | Up to 3 years |
| **Sec. 66C** | Identity theft (using another person's e-signature, password, unique ID) | Up to 3 years + fine |
| **Sec. 66D** | Cheating by personation using computer resource | Up to 3 years + fine (₹1 lakh) |
| **Sec. 66E** | Publishing/ transmitting obscene material electronically | Up to 3 years |
| **Sec. 66F** | **Cyberterrorism** | Up to **life imprisonment** |
| **Sec. 67** | Publishing/ transmitting sexually explicit material | 1st offence: 3 yrs; Subsequent: 5 yrs |
| **Sec. 67A** | Depicting children in sexually explicit acts | Up to 7 years |
| **Sec. 67B** | Child pornography | Min. 5 yrs, may extend to 7 yrs + fine |
| **Sec. 69** | Government's interception powers | Rule-bound |
| **Sec. 72** | Breach of confidentiality / privacy | Up to 2 yrs + fine |
| **Sec. 79** | Intermediary safe-harbour (with conditions) | Conditional protection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often cite **Sec. 66A** in their answers. The **Supreme Court struck it down in 2015** as violative of Article 19(1)(a). Examiners award zero marks for citing it.

### 2.2.2 Complementary Indian Statutes

| Statute | Cyber-Relevant Sections |
|---|---|
| **Indian Penal Code, 1860** (now partly replaced by BNS 2023) | Sec. 420 (cheating), 463/465 (forgery), 503/507 (criminal intimidation) |
| **Indian Evidence Act, 1872** | Sec. 65B — admissibility of *electronic evidence* |
| **Copyright Act, 1957** | Sec. 51–55 — software piracy, plagiarism |
| **PMLA 2002** (Amended 2008) | Money laundering through crypto / digital wallets |

### 2.2.3 International Frameworks

- **Budapest Convention on Cybercrime (2001)** — only binding international treaty.
- **ITU Global Cybersecurity Index (GCI)**.
- **ISO/IEC 27001** — Information Security Management.
- **NIST Cybersecurity Framework**.

---

## 2.3 High-Yield Cheat Sheet — KTU Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following consolidated table is the **single most important revision artifact** for the KTU ESE on this topic. Reproduce it from memory before every exam.

| Domain | Offence Type | Legal Provision | Punishment Cap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Individual | Cyberstalking | Sec. 67 IT + IPC 509 | 3 yrs + fine |
| Individual | Identity Theft | Sec. 66C IT | 3 yrs + fine |
| Property | Hacking | Sec. 66 IT | 3 yrs + fine |
| Property | Ransomware / Data theft | Sec. 43 + 66 IT | 3 yrs + civil damages |
| Property | Credit-card fraud | Sec. 66D IT + IPC 420 | 7 yrs combined |
| Government | Cyberterrorism | Sec. 66F IT | **Life imprisonment** |
| Society | CSAM | Sec. 67B IT | 5–7 yrs + fine |
| Society | Drug trafficking (darknet) | NDPS Act + IT Act | Varies |
| Cross-cutting | Phishing / Vishing | Sec. 66D IT | 3 yrs + fine |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Understanding cybercrime is **not just an academic exercise** — it has direct industry application:

1. **Security Operations Centres (SOC)** classify incidents using these taxonomies.
2. **Insurance underwriting** uses MO-classification to price *cyber-insurance premiums*.
3. **Compliance teams** map Sec. 43/66 controls to their access-management policies.
4. **Incident responders** trigger legal action by citing specific IT-Act sections in FIRs.
5. **Forensic investigators** preserve evidence per **Sec. 65B IEA** to ensure admissibility.

---

## 2.5 The Cybercrime Attack Lifecycle (Cyber Kill Chain — Lockheed Martin Variant)

The lifecycle, mapped to KTU terminology:

1. **Reconnaissance** — gathering target info (OSINT, social-media scraping).
2. **Weaponisation** — crafting malware / payload (coupled with exploit).
3. **Delivery** — transmitting the weapon (phishing email, drive-by, USB).
4. **Exploitation** — triggering the vulnerability in the victim's system.
5. **Installation** — establishing persistence (backdoor / implant).
6. **Command & Control (C2)** — remote communication channel.
7. **Actions on Objectives** — exfiltration, encryption, destruction.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Favourite:** *"Which stage is detection easiest?"* → **Reconnaissance & Delivery** (still outside the perimeter).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Case-Style Derivations

Because Cybercrime is largely **qualitative / legal** rather than purely mathematical, the "derivation" takes the form of **structured analytical breakdowns, legal reasoning chains, and worked case-evaluations**. The following subsections provide the exhaustive analytical depth demanded by KTU's 14-mark essay-type questions.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Classifying a Cybercrime Incident

### Worked Case Scenario (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Scenario (KTU-2024 Pattern):** A person named **Ravi** receives a phone call claiming to be from his bank. The caller states that his account will be blocked unless he shares his **OTP** and **CVV**. Ravi shares them. Within 2 hours, ₹50,000 is debited from his account via online transfer. Classify this crime, identify the IT-Act sections applicable, and describe the investigation procedure.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

#### **Step 1 — Identify the Modus Operandi (MO)**

The attacker used a **voice call impersonating a legitimate entity** and extracted **One-Time Password (OTP)** and **Card Verification Value (CVV)** by deception. This is a textbook case of:

$$
\text{MO} \;=\; \text{Vishing (Voice-Phishing)} \;\subset\; \text{Social Engineering}
$$

The attacker never "hacked" the bank's system; they hacked **Ravi's human trust**. Hence this is *not* a "hacking" crime under Sec. 66 — it is **cheating by personation**.

#### **Step 2 — Identify the Crime Category (Target-based)**

The harm is to an **individual's property (money)**. Therefore:

$$
\text{Category} \;=\; \text{Cybercrime against Property (with individual victim)}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Map to IT-Act Provisions**

The applicable provisions are derived by mapping each *act* to the *legal definition*:

| Action by Attacker | Legal Mapping |
|---|---|
| Impersonating bank officer | Sec. 66D IT Act — *cheating by personation using computer resource* |
| Fraudulently obtaining CVV/OTP | Sec. 66C IT Act — *identity theft* (unique ID = financial credential) |
| Forged bank SMS / fake caller-ID | Sec. 66D read with Sec. 43 (damage to computer system) |
| Receiving stolen money | Sec. 66B (receiving stolen communication device or data value) |
| Traditional criminal law (cheating) | Sec. 420 IPC / Sec. 318 BNS (post-2024) |

#### **Step 4 — Punishment Derivation**

For Sec. 66D:

$$
P_{66D} \;=\; \text{Imprisonment up to } 3 \text{ years} \;+\; \text{Fine up to } \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000
$$

For Sec. 66C:

$$
P_{66C} \;=\; \text{Imprisonment up to } 3 \text{ years} \;+\; \text{Fine}
$$

For Sec. 420 IPC (in case of cognisable cheating):

$$
P_{420} \;=\; \text{Imprisonment up to } 7 \text{ years} \;+\; \text{Fine}
$$

Because the offences overlap, the **maximum cumulative** punishment under Sec. 66D + Sec. 420 may be applied (subject to judicial discretion and the rule against double-jeopardy under Article 20(2)).

#### **Step 5 — Investigation Procedure**

The standard 65B-compliant investigative chain is:

$$
\text{Detection} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Lodging of FIR (Sec. 154 CrPC)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Registration at cybercrime portal (cybercrime.gov.in)}
$$

$$
\;\rightarrow\; \text{Sec. 65B Certificate by Examiner} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Forensic Acquisition (Bit-stream imaging)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Analysis (Volatility / Autopsy)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Reporting to Adjudicating Officer / Court}
$$

#### **Step 6 — Sec. 65B Evidence Admissibility**

For the digital record to be admissible in court:

$$
E_{\text{admissible}} \;\iff\; \text{(i) Authentic output of computer;} \; \text{(ii) Produced at the time of regular course of operation;} \; \text{(iii) Properly certified Sec. 65B(4) certificate}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Failure to produce a valid **Sec. 65B(4) certificate** = electronic evidence is **inadmissible** in Indian courts. This is the *single most common reason* cyber-cases collapse.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cybercrime Categories

| Dimension | Hacking (Sec. 66) | Phishing (Sec. 66D) | Ransomware (Sec. 43+66) | Cyberterrorism (Sec. 66F) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Curiosity / Access | Financial gain | Extortion | Ideological / political |
| **Tool** | Exploit code | Email / SMS | Crypto-malware | Sophisticated APT |
| **Target** | Server / system | Human / endpoint | Data | Critical infrastructure |
| **Skill needed** | Medium–High | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Damage scope** | Limited–Medium | Limited | Widespread | Mass disruption |
| **Punishment** | 3 yrs + fine | 3 yrs + ₹1L | 3 yrs + civil | **Life imprisonment** |
| **Cognisable** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Bailable** | Bailable | Bailable | Non-bailable | Non-bailable |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Reasoning — Why Sec. 66F (Cyberterrorism) is the *Strongest* Section

Let $D$ = *Damage Potential* and $S$ = *Severity Class*.

$$
S_{66F} \;=\; \max\left(D_{\text{all IT-Act offences}}\right) \;\text{ because:} \;
S_{66F} \;\equiv\; (\text{intent to threaten unity, integrity, security, sovereignty of India})
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;P_{66F} \;\geq\; P_{\text{all other IT-Act sections}}\;}
$$

This is why Sec. 66F is treated as a *standalone, non-compoundable* offence and tried by **Special Courts** designated under Sec. 14 of the National Investigation Agency (NIA) Act.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Walk-through — Phishing Email Examination

A phishing attack can be modelled as a **4-tuple**:

$$
\mathcal{P} \;=\; \langle S,\; L,\; C,\; T \rangle
$$

Where:

- $S$ = **Sender domain** (e.g., `paypa1.com` — homoglyph attack).
- $L$ = **Lure / hook** (urgency, fear, reward).
- $C$ = **Credential harvesting channel** (cloned login page).
- $T$ = **Target** (victim's identity / funds).

The probability of victim compliance $P_{\text{succ}}$ can be loosely expressed as:

$$
P_{\text{succ}} \;\approx\; f(\text{lure quality}) \;\cdot\; g(\text{brand impersonation fidelity}) \;\cdot\; \frac{1}{\text{victim cyber-awareness}}
$$

Where $f, g$ are monotonically increasing functions — a poorly-crafted phishing email with typos and poor logos drops the success probability dramatically.

> [!TIP]
> This analytical model can be used in **14-mark KTU answers** to demonstrate depth when explaining social-engineering threats.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following diagrams are deliberately constructed using **alphanumeric-only node IDs** and **clean uppercase labels** (no markdown formatting inside node quotes) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

---

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Cybercrime (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cybercrime] --> B[Against Individuals]
    A --> C[Against Property]
    A --> D[Against Government]
    A --> E[Against Society]

    B --> B1[Cyberstalking]
    B --> B2[Cyberbullying]
    B --> B3[Phishing Vishing Smishing]
    B --> B4[Identity Theft Sec66C]
    B --> B5[Morphing Obscene Content Sec66E]

    C --> C1[Hacking Sec66]
    C --> C2[Data Theft Sec43 66]
    C --> C3[Software Piracy]
    C --> C4[Ransomware Crypto Attacks]
    C --> C5[Credit Card Fraud Sec66D]
    C --> C6[Cryptojacking]

    D --> D1[Cyberterrorism Sec66F]
    D --> D2[Cyberwarfare State Sponsored]
    D --> D3[Critical Infra Attack]
    D --> D4[Gov Website Defacement]

    E --> E1[Child Pornography Sec67B]
    E --> E2[Online Gambling Fraud]
    E --> E3[Darknet Drug Trafficking]
    E --> E4[Money Laundering Crypto]
    E --> E5[Fake News Disinformation]
```

---

## 4.2 Cybercrime Attack Lifecycle (Kill Chain Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    K1[Reconnaissance] --> K2[Weaponisation]
    K2 --> K3[Delivery Phishing Email]
    K3 --> K4[Exploitation Vulnerability Trigger]
    K4 --> K5[Installation Backdoor Implant]
    K5 --> K6[Command and Control Channel]
    K6 --> K7[Actions on Objectives Exfil Encrypt Destroy]

    style K1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style K4 fill:#FFA07A
    style K7 fill:#CD5C5C
```

---

## 4.3 Investigation & Forensics Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Incident Detection] --> P2[Initial Triage]
    P2 --> P3[Sec 65B Certificate Issuance]
    P3 --> P4[Chain of Custody Established]
    P4 --> P5[Forensic Imaging Bit Stream]
    P5 --> P6[Volatile Memory Capture]
    P6 --> P7[Disk and Network Forensics]
    P7 --> P8[Artifact Recovery]
    P8 --> P9[Timeline Reconstruction]
    P9 --> P10[Reporting to Adjudicating Officer]
    P10 --> P11[Court Submission Sec 65B Compliant]

    style P3 fill:#B0E0E6
    style P5 fill:#B0E0E6
    style P11 fill:#98FB98
```

---

## 4.4 Indian Legal Mapping Flow (Offence-to-Section)

```mermaid
graph TD
    O1[Hacking] --> S1[Sec 66 IT Act]
    O2[Identity Theft] --> S2[Sec 66C IT Act]
    O3[Cheating by Personation] --> S3[Sec 66D IT Act]
    O4[Obscene Material] --> S4[Sec 67 IT Act]
    O5[Child Pornography] --> S5[Sec 67B IT Act]
    O6[Cyberterrorism] --> S6[Sec 66F IT Act]
    O7[Damage to Computer] --> S7[Sec 43 IT Act]
    O8[Privacy Breach] --> S8[Sec 72 IT Act]

    O1 --> X1[3 Years Fine]
    O2 --> X2[3 Years Fine]
    O3 --> X3[3 Years 1 Lakh Fine]
    O4 --> X4[3 to 5 Years]
    O5 --> X5[5 to 7 Years]
    O6 --> X6[Life Imprisonment]
    O7 --> X7[Compensation up to 1 Crore]
    O8 --> X8[2 Years Fine]
```

---

## 4.5 Cybercrime Reporting Architecture (India-Specific)

```mermaid
graph TD
    V[Victim] --> R1[cybercrime.gov.in Portal]
    V --> R2[Local Police Station FIR Sec 154 CrPC]
    V --> R3[1930 Cyber Helpline]
    V --> R4[Women Helpline 181]
    V --> R5[Child Helpline 1098]

    R1 --> A1[CCTNS Database]
    R2 --> A1
    R1 --> A2[State Cyber Cell]
    R2 --> A2
    A1 --> A3[Adjudicating Officer]
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4[Special Court IT Act]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked *"where does a cybercrime victim in India file a complaint?"*, the **expected answer** enumerates all 5 reporting channels — not just the police.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-A carries 3 marks, expects ~80–120 word answers with **bold keywords** and *at least one section reference*.

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Define cybercrime. List any four categories of cybercrimes.*

**Model Answer:**

**Cybercrime** is a criminal activity in which a computer, network, or digital device is used as the tool, target, or incidental component of the offence. As per the IT Act, 2000 (amended 2008), it includes acts like hacking, phishing, identity theft, and cyberterrorism.

The **four categories** of cybercrime (classified by target) are:

1. **Cybercrimes against Individuals** (e.g., cyberstalking, phishing).
2. **Cybercrimes against Property** (e.g., hacking, data theft).
3. **Cybercrimes against Government** (e.g., cyberterrorism under Sec. 66F).
4. **Cybercrimes against Society at Large** (e.g., child pornography under Sec. 67B).

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
*Differentiate between phishing and vishing with examples.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Phishing | Vishing |
|---|---|---|
| **Channel** | Email / SMS (text-based) | Voice call (audio-based) |
| **Modus Operandi** | Fraudulent link sent via email | Caller impersonates bank/authority |
| **Lure** | Urgency, prize, account lock | OTP / CVV request on call |
| **Example** | Email from "fake-paypal@paypa1.com" | Call from "fake-SBI officer" asking OTP |

Both fall under **Sec. 66D IT Act** (cheating by personation) and **Sec. 66C** (identity theft) when credentials are stolen.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Pattern:** Each Module carries **one full 14-mark question** with **internal choice** (i.e., a student attempts ONE out of TWO alternatives: A or B). Marks are split as 7 + 7 between sub-parts. Examiner expects a **structured answer with sub-headings, definitions, examples, sections, diagrams, and a conclusion.**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Classify the different types of cybercrimes targeting individuals and property. Explain with at least **two real-world examples for each category** and state the relevant **IT-Act sections** with punishments. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Cyberterrorism** as defined under the **IT Act, 2008**? Discuss its essential ingredients, punishment, and how it differs from ordinary hacking. Provide two illustrative case scenarios. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Sub-Part (a)

**Definition:** Cybercrimes against individuals and property target *private persons* and *assets/data* respectively, using computing infrastructure.

**Cybercrimes Against Individuals** (examples + sections):

1. **Phishing** — fraudulent emails to steal credentials → *Sec. 66D IT Act* (3 yrs + ₹1 lakh fine).
   *Example:* The 2019 "Paytm KYC" phishing wave deceived lakhs of users into sharing OTPs.

2. **Identity Theft** — impersonation using another person's digital ID → *Sec. 66C IT Act* (3 yrs + fine).
   *Example:* Aadhaar-PAN linkage frauds where cloned biometrics were used to open bank accounts.

3. **Cyberstalking / Cyberbullying** — repeated online harassment → *Sec. 67 + IPC 509 / Sec. 79 IT Act on intermediaries*.
   *Example:* The Sagar Saxena cyberstalking case (2014) where the accused stalked a woman using fake social profiles.

**Cybercrimes Against Property** (examples + sections):

1. **Hacking / Unauthorised Access** → *Sec. 66 IT Act* (3 yrs + fine).
   *Example:* The 2016 Bangladesh Bank heist attempted to siphon $951 million via SWIFT vulnerabilities.

2. **Data Theft / Database Breach** → *Sec. 43 (compensation up to ₹1 Crore) + Sec. 66 (criminal)*.
   *Example:* The 2021 Dominos India data leak exposing 18 Crore orders.

3. **Ransomware** → *Sec. 43 + 66 (damage to computer system)*.
   *Example:* The 2017 WannaCry ransomware attack (220,000+ systems globally).

**Conclusion:** Cybercrimes against individuals violate *personal autonomy*, while those against property violate *economic trust* — both are cognisable, bailable (Sec. 66) offences, triable by the Metropolitan Magistrate / Judicial Magistrate First Class.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-Part (b)

**Definition:** As per **Section 66F of the IT Act, 2008** (inserted by the 2008 amendment, after the 26/11 Mumbai attacks):

> *"Whoever commits or intends to commit cyberterrorism — by denying access to authorised persons, or by introducing malware / computer contamination with intent to threaten the unity, integrity, security or sovereignty of India — shall be punishable with imprisonment which may extend to **life imprisonment**."*

**Essential Ingredients (Legal Elements):**

1. **Mens Rea:** Specific intent to threaten India's *unity, integrity, security, sovereignty, economic security, or strike terror in the people*.
2. **Actus Reus:** One or more of the following:
   - Denial of access to a computer resource (Sec. 66F(a)).
   - Introduction of malicious code that causes damage (Sec. 66F(b)).
   - Accessing a "protected system" (declared under Sec. 70 IT Act) for an unauthorised purpose (Sec. 66F(c)).

**Punishment:**

$$
P_{66F} \;=\; \text{Imprisonment up to \textbf{Life} + Fine}
$$

The offence is **non-bailable, non-compoundable, cognisable**, and triable by a **Special Court** (designated under the NIA Act).

**Comparison with Ordinary Hacking:**

| Parameter | Sec. 66 (Hacking) | Sec. 66F (Cyberterrorism) |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Curiosity / financial / personal | Threaten national security / sovereignty |
| **Target** | Any computer system | Critical infra / protected systems / mass harm |
| **Punishment** | Up to 3 yrs | Up to **Life imprisonment** |
| **Severity** | Bailable | Non-bailable |
| **Severity Class** | $S_{\text{low}}$ | $S_{\max}$ |

**Case Scenarios:**

- **Scenario 1 (Qualifies as 66F):** A hostile foreign-state actor penetrates the SCADA system of the Indian power grid to cause a multi-state blackout — clearly threatens economic security and sovereignty.
- **Scenario 2 (Does NOT qualify as 66F, only Sec. 66):** A teenager defaces a school website for fun — lacks the *national-security intent* required for 66F.

**Conclusion:** Sec. 66F is the **strongest weapon** in the Indian cyberlaw arsenal and must be invoked only when *national-scale harm* and *specific anti-state intent* coexist. Otherwise, the appropriate sections are Sec. 43/66.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]

**(a)** Explain the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008)** in detail. Discuss the salient offences defined under the Act with relevant **sections and punishments**. Highlight the **Sec. 65B** provision for admissibility of electronic evidence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are **cybercrimes against women and children**? Discuss the relevant **IT-Act sections and IPC sections**, and outline the **investigation and reporting mechanisms** available in India. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Sub-Part (a)

**Introduction:** The IT Act, 2000 was India's first comprehensive cyberlaw, enacted to provide **legal recognition to e-commerce, e-governance, and digital signatures**, and to **criminalise computer-mediated offences**. The 2008 amendment, enacted in the wake of the 26/11 Mumbai attacks, significantly expanded its scope and inserted Sec. 66F (Cyberterrorism).

**Salient Offences and Punishments (Consolidated Table):**

| Section | Offence | Punishment |
|---|---|---|
| 43 | Damage to computer / system without authorisation | Civil damages up to ₹1 Crore |
| 65 | Tampering with computer source documents | 3 yrs + fine |
| 66 | Hacking (unauthorised access) | 3 yrs + fine |
| 66B | Receiving stolen computer resource | 3 yrs |
| 66C | Identity theft | 3 yrs + fine |
| 66D | Cheating by personation using computer | 3 yrs + ₹1 lakh fine |
| 66E | Publishing/transmitting obscene material (privacy breach) | 3 yrs + fine |
| 66F | Cyberterrorism | **Life imprisonment** |
| 67 | Publishing obscene material | 1st: 3 yrs / Repeat: 5 yrs |
| 67A | Sexually explicit depiction of children | Up to 7 yrs |
| 67B | Child pornography | 5–7 yrs + fine |
| 69 | Government interception of communications | Rule-bound procedure |
| 72 | Breach of confidentiality/privacy | 2 yrs + fine |
| 79 | Intermediary liability (safe-harbour) | Conditional protection |

**The Sec. 65B Provision (Admissibility of Electronic Evidence):**

Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (now under Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023, Sec. 63) is the **gateway provision** for admitting electronic records (emails, logs, CCTV footage, call-detail records) as evidence in Indian courts.

The Section 65B(4) Certificate must contain:
- Identification of the electronic record.
- The manner of production (computer + storage medium).
- Details of the operator (who produced the record).
- A declaration that the record was produced during the *ordinary course of business* of the computer.
- A statement that the record has not been tampered with.

> Without a valid 65B certificate, the electronic evidence is **inadmissible** — the *Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014) and Arjun Khotkar (2020) judgments* have repeatedly upheld this.

**Conclusion:** The IT Act is the *backbone* of India's cybersecurity legal framework, but its effectiveness depends on proper **admissibility, investigation, and prosecution** — making Sec. 65B equally critical.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-Part (b)

**Introduction:** Women and children constitute the *most vulnerable* cyber-victim categories due to the *asymmetric, anonymous, and pervasive* nature of digital abuse.

**Cybercrimes Against Women:**

| Offence | Section | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Cyberstalking | IPC 509 + IT Act Sec. 67 (if obscene content) | Repeated online harassment |
| Cyberbullying / Trolling | IPC 503 / 507 + IT Act | Threats via social media |
| Obscene messaging / Morphing | IT Act Sec. 67, 67A, 66E | Tampering victim's photos |
| Email harassment | IT Act Sec. 67 | Obscene content via email |
| Cyber-defamation | IPC 499/500 + IT Sec. 79 (intermediary) | Reputation harm online |

**Cybercrimes Against Children:**

| Offence | Section | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Child Pornography / CSAM | IT Act Sec. 67B | 5–7 yrs + fine |
| Online sexual exploitation of minors | IT Act Sec. 67B + POCSO Act 2012 | Enhanced punishment |
| Cyber-bullying of minors | IT Act Sec. 67 + POCSO | Min 3 yrs |
| Online grooming | POCSO Sec. 11/12 | Specific POCSO application |
| Internet-facilitated child trafficking | IT Act + IPC 370/ITPA | Severe compounded liability |

**Reporting & Investigation Mechanisms (India):**

1. **cybercrime.gov.in** — National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal.
2. **Helpline 1930** — Financial cyber-fraud reporting.
3. **Local Police** — FIR under Sec. 154 CrPC.
4. **Cyber Cell of State Police** — specialised investigative wing.
5. **Women Helpline 181** and **Childline 1098** — for vulnerable victims.
6. **CBI / NIA** — for transnational / cyberterrorism cases.
7. **Adjudicating Officer** under Sec. 46 IT Act — for civil compensation claims.
8. **Special Court** under Sec. 14 IT Act — for criminal trial.

**Conclusion:** India has evolved a *multi-agency response framework* for cybercrime against women and children, but **awareness, swift reporting, and digital-evidence preservation** remain the most critical determinants of justice delivery.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Citing Sec. 66A:** Sec. 66A was *struck down by the Supreme Court in Shreya Singhal v. UoI (2015)*. Citing it will result in **0 marks** for that sub-part.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing "Phishing" with "Hacking":** Phishing is *social engineering*, not technical hacking. The correct section is **Sec. 66D** (cheating by personation), not Sec. 66 (hacking). Examiners mark precisely.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Ignoring Sec. 65B:** Any answer that discusses *digital evidence* without mentioning the Sec. 65B certificate is considered *incomplete*.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Memorising but not applying:** KTU tests *application*, not rote learning. If a question gives a scenario, you must **identify** the section, **justify** the choice, and **state** the punishment. Skipping the justification loses half marks.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Spelling mistakes in section numbers** (e.g., 66E vs 66F vs 67A vs 67B). These are considered *factual errors* and result in direct mark deduction.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Forgetting reporting channels:** When asked "how does one report cybercrime?", many students only mention the police. KTU expects the *complete ecosystem* (1930 helpline, cybercrime.gov.in, state cyber cell, special courts).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> Use this checklist 10 minutes before entering the exam hall. Tick each box mentally.

### A. Conceptual Knowledge

- ☐ Cybercrime = computer-mediated offence violating cyberlaw.
- ☐ Three pillars: **Intent + Computer + Law (I-C-L)**.
- ☐ Four target categories: **Individual / Property / Government / Society**.
- ☐ Seven Modus Operandi categories: Hacking, Malware, Social Engineering, Crypto, Network, Application, Hardware.
- ☐ Kill Chain stages: **Recon → Weaponise → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Act**.

### B. Legal Knowledge (MUST MEMORISE)

- ☐ **Sec. 43** — civil damages (compensation up to ₹1 Crore).
- ☐ **Sec. 66** — Hacking (3 yrs + fine).
- ☐ **Sec. 66C** — Identity Theft (3 yrs + fine).
- ☐ **Sec. 66D** — Cheating by Personation (3 yrs + ₹1L).
- ☐ **Sec. 66E** — Obscene material (privacy breach) — 3 yrs.
- ☐ **Sec. 66F** — Cyberterrorism (**Life imprisonment**).
- ☐ **Sec. 67** — Publishing obscene material (3–5 yrs).
- ☐ **Sec. 67A/67B** — Children in sexually explicit acts (**5–7 yrs**).
- ☐ **Sec. 72** — Privacy breach (2 yrs + fine).
- ☐ **Sec. 79** — Intermediary safe harbour.
- ☐ **Sec. 65B IEA / Sec. 63 BSA 2023** — electronic evidence admissibility.

### C. Statutory Knowledge

- ☐ **IT Act, 2000** (amended 2008) — primary cyberlaw.
- ☐ **IPC** (now BNS 2023) — for traditional crimes digitised.
- ☐ **Indian Evidence Act / BSA 2023** — for Sec. 65B certificate.
- ☐ **Copyright Act 1957** — software piracy.
- ☐ **PMLA 2002** — money laundering via crypto.
- ☐ **POCSO Act 2012** — child protection online.

### D. Process / Investigation Knowledge

- ☐ Sec. 65B Certificate required for digital evidence admissibility.
- ☐ Reporting channels: **cybercrime.gov.in, 1930, 181, 1098, local police, state cyber cell**.
- ☐ Forensic procedure: **Detection → Triage → Imaging → Analysis → Reporting**.
- ☐ **Chain of custody** is non-negotiable.

### E. Awareness Triggers

- ☐ **Budapest Convention (2001)** — international cybercrime treaty.
- ☐ **Shreya Singhal v. UoI (2015)** — struck down Sec. 66A.
- ☐ **Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014)** — Sec. 65B necessity.
- ☐ **Sec. 70 Protected Systems** — critical for cyberterrorism case law.
- ☐ **24×7 helpline 1930** for financial cyber fraud.

### F. One-Liners for Quick Recall

- ☐ *"Sec. 66 = Hacking."*
- ☐ *"Sec. 66F = Life in prison — Cyberterrorism."*
- ☐ *"Sec. 67B = Children in explicit content = 5–7 years."*
- ☐ *"Sec. 65B = Digital evidence gatekeeper."*
- ☐ *"Sec. 66A = DEAD (Shreya Singhal 2015)."*

> **Final Note:** The Cybercrime module is a *high-yield* topic in the KTU 2024 ESE — students who master the **section-number → offence → punishment** triad (the "**SOP triangle**") typically secure full or near-full marks. Master the four-category target taxonomy, the kill-chain, and the reporting ecosystem, and you are exam-ready.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Cybercrime
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Cybercrime

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cybercrime** is broadly defined as any criminal activity, offense, or unlawful act in which a **computer, networked device, or computer network** is either used as a **tool**, **target**, or **medium** of commission. Under the **Indian Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008)**, cybercrimes are classified as cognizable, non-bailable, and punishable offenses that compromise the **CIA Triad** — **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** of digital assets.

The **United Nations Manual on the Prevention and Control of Computer-Related Crime (1994)** recognizes cybercrimes under two broad categories:

1. **Computer Crime** — offenses committed *against* computer systems and data.
2. **Computer-Related Crime / Computer Abuse** — offenses committed *using* computer systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Cybercrime like "Digital Trespassing"**
> Imagine a highly secured physical bank vault. Traditional theft = physically breaking in with tools. **Cybercrime = sitting in a café across the street and remotely manipulating the vault's electronic lock through radio signals, or tricking the bank manager into revealing the combination via a convincing phone call (social engineering).** The vault, the manager, and the money are all still physically present — but the *crime* has been executed through an intangible digital channel.

A useful mental model is the **"Who, What, How" Triad** of Cybercrime:

| W-H-H Element | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **WHO** (Actor) | The perpetrator (hacker, insider, organized group, nation-state) | "BlackCat" Ransomware Gang |
| **WHAT** (Target) | Person, Property, Government, or Society at large | Aadhaar Database |
| **HOW** (Means) | Tool, technique, or vulnerability exploited | Phishing email + credential reuse |

## 1.3 KTU Syllabus Highlight: Classification Framework

For KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation, students must remember the **Three-Tier Classification** that the Indian IT Act and global bodies (FBI, INTERPOL) follow:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Major Categories of Cybercrime (KTU 2024 — High Priority):**
> 1. **Cybercrime Against Persons**
> 2. **Cybercrime Against Property**
> 3. **Cybercrime Against Government / Society**

This classification is the **backbone of most KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions** on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Cybercrime

### **Category 1: Cybercrime Against Persons**

These are offenses targeting the **psychological, financial, and personal integrity** of an individual.

#### **2.1.1 Phishing**
- **Definition:** A social-engineering attack where an attacker masquerades as a legitimate, trusted entity to trick victims into revealing **sensitive information** (usernames, passwords, OTPs, card numbers).
- **Variants:**
  * **Spear Phishing** — Targeted at a specific individual/organization.
  * **Whaling** — Targeted at senior executives ("C-suite").
  * **Vishing** — Voice-based phishing via phone calls.
  * **Smishing** — SMS-based phishing.
  * **Pharming** — Redirects users from legitimate websites to malicious ones via DNS poisoning.
- **Key Metric:** The **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** is the percentage of recipients who click the malicious link.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Exploits human psychology (*authority bias, urgency, fear*) rather than technical vulnerabilities.

#### **2.1.2 Identity Theft & Impersonation**
- **Identity Theft:** Stealing Personally Identifiable Information (PII) — Aadhaar, PAN, credit-card data — to impersonate the victim for financial gain.
- **Identity Fraud:** The **actual misuse** of stolen identity (a subset or consequence of theft).
- **IT Act Reference:** **Section 66C** — Punishment for identity theft (up to **3 years imprisonment + ₹1 lakh fine**).

#### **2.1.3 Cyberstalking & Cyberbullying**
- **Cyberstalking:** Repeated online harassment using emails, social media, or tracking software to intimidate, threaten, or frighten a victim. The *digital equivalent of physical stalking*.
- **Cyberbullying:** Specifically targets minors/teens — intimidation, shaming, or humiliation through digital platforms.
- **IT Act Reference:** **Section 66E** (Violation of privacy), **Section 67** (Publishing obscene material).

#### **2.1.4 Child Pornography / CSAM (Child Sexual Abuse Material)**
- Distribution, creation, or possession of sexually explicit material involving minors.
- **IT Act Section 67B** prescribes rigorous imprisonment (**7 years to life**).

#### **2.1.5 Online Fraud & Scams**
- **Nigerian 419 Scam, Lottery Fraud, Job Fraud, Matrimonial Fraud, UPI Frauds.**

---

### **Category 2: Cybercrime Against Property**

These are offenses against digital assets, intellectual property, and financial holdings.

#### **2.1.6 Hacking / Unauthorized Access**
- **Definition:** Illegal access to a computer system, network, or database **without permission**.
- **IT Act Section 66:** Up to **3 years imprisonment or ₹5 lakh fine (or both)**.
- **Types:**
  * **Black Hat Hacking** — Malicious intent.
  * **White Hat Hacking** — Ethical / authorized penetration testing.
  * **Grey Hat Hacking** — Violates rules but without malicious intent.
  * **Script Kiddie** — Uses pre-built tools without deep understanding.
  * **Hacktivism** — Politically/socially motivated hacking (e.g., Anonymous).

#### **2.1.7 Malware (Malicious Software)**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Malware is a *family term* — it is NOT a single attack.** KTU expects the following sub-classification:

| Malware Type | Behavior | Notable Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Attaches to a legitimate program; needs user activation | ILOVEYOU, Melissa |
| **Worm** | Self-replicating; spreads via networks without user action | Blaster, Code Red |
| **Trojan Horse** | Disguised as legitimate software | Zeus, Emotet |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts files, demands ransom for decryption key | WannaCry, LockBit |
| **Spyware** | Silently monitors user activity | CoolWebSearch |
| **Adware** | Unwanted pop-up advertisements | Fireball |
| **Rootkit** | Hides deep in OS, gains privileged control | Alureon |
| **Keylogger** | Records keystrokes to steal credentials | Olympic Vision |
| **Bot / Botnet** | Infected machine controlled remotely (zombie) | Mirai Botnet |
| **Fileless Malware** | Operates in memory only; no file footprint | PowerShell-based attacks |
| **Logic Bomb** | Triggers payload on specific condition/event | — |

#### **2.1.8 Denial-of-Service (DoS) & Distributed DoS (DDoS)**
- **Goal:** Make a service unavailable by overwhelming it with traffic.
- **Mechanism:** Flooding bandwidth, exhausting CPU/RAM, or exploiting protocol weaknesses.
- **KPI:** Measured in **Gbps (Gigabits per second)** of attack traffic.
- **Mathematical Model:**

$$\text{Effective Load} = L_{\text{legitimate}} + L_{\text{malicious}}$$

When $L_{\text{malicious}} \gg L_{\text{legitimate}}$, the system reaches **saturation** and refuses service to legitimate users.

- **Famous Example:** **Mirai Botnet DDoS on Dyn (October 21, 2016)** — Peak traffic of **~1.2 Tbps**, disrupted Twitter, Netflix, Reddit across the US/Europe.
- **DoS vs DDoS:**

| Feature | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| **Source Count** | Single | Multiple (often thousands) |
| **Mitigation** | Easy (block source IP) | Hard (distributed traffic) |
| **Amplification** | Low | High (uses reflector networks) |
| **Botnet Required** | No | Yes |

#### **2.1.9 Data Breach & Data Exfiltration**
- Unauthorized copying, transfer, or retrieval of sensitive data.
- **IT Act Section 43A** (Compensation for failure to protect data).

#### **2.1.10 Software Piracy & IP Violation**
- Unauthorized copying, distribution, or modification of copyrighted software.
- **Indian Copyright Act, 1957** + **IT Act Section 65** (Tampering with computer source documents).

---

### **Category 3: Cybercrime Against Government / Society**

#### **2.1.11 Cyberterrorism**
- Politically/ideologically motivated attacks on **critical information infrastructure** (CII) — power grids, defense systems, financial networks.
- **Goal:** Cause mass panic, disruption, or destruction.

#### **2.1.12 Cyberwarfare**
- State-sponsored attacks against another nation's digital infrastructure.
- **Examples:** Stuxnet (US-Israel vs Iran, 2010), Sony Pictures Hack (North Korea, 2014).

#### **2.1.13 Distributed Denial of Truth / Disinformation**
- **Fake News**, **Deepfakes**, and **Propaganda** campaigns to manipulate public opinion.
- **Example:** Influence operations during elections via social media bots.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas and Metrics for KTU 2024 Cybercrime Problems**

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Attack Volume | $V = R \times t$ | $R$ = packet rate (pps), $t$ = duration (s) |
| DDoS Magnitude | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i$ | $B_i$ = bandwidth of bot $i$; Total in **Gbps** |
| Click-Through Rate (Phishing) | $CTR = \dfrac{C}{S} \times 100\%$ | $C$ = clicks, $S$ = sent emails |
| Breach Severity | $S = N \times V$ | $N$ = records, $V$ = per-record value (₹) |
| Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum t_d}{N}$ | $t_d$ = detection time per incident |
| Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum t_r}{N}$ | $t_r$ = response time per incident |
| Malware Spread (SIR Model) | $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I$ | $\beta$ = infection rate, $\gamma$ = recovery rate |
| Botnet Size | $\mid B \mid$ (cardinality) | Number of compromised hosts |
| Hash Collision Resistance | $n = 2^{k/2}$ (birthday bound) | $k$ = hash length in bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The **SIR (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered)** epidemiological model borrowed from public health is used by cybersecurity firms (e.g., Kaspersky, Symantec) to forecast **worm propagation rates** in large networks. This is a true cross-disciplinary application frequently cited in KTU Part B questions.

## 2.3 The Why and How — Operational Insight

- **Why do attackers prefer cybercrime?** *Asymmetric economics* — A phishing email costs **\$0** to send but can yield **thousands of dollars**; the per-incident ROI is unmatched.
- **How does it work?** Attacks typically follow the **Cyber Kill Chain** (Lockheed Martin model): **Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → Command & Control → Actions on Objectives.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Mathematical Modeling of a Worm Spread

Let us derive the **basic reproduction number $R_0$** for a network worm — a parameter the KTU examiner often asks in 14-mark Part B questions.

### **Step 1: Define the Population Compartments**

Let:
- $S(t)$ = Number of **Susceptible** (vulnerable, uninfected) hosts at time $t$.
- $I(t)$ = Number of **Infected** hosts at time $t$.
- $R(t)$ = Number of **Recovered/Patched** hosts at time $t$.
- $N$ = Total hosts in the network, with $S + I + R = N$.

### **Step 2: State the Rate Equations**

The **mass-action incidence** assumes infection rate is proportional to the product of susceptible and infected hosts:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot \frac{S \cdot I}{N}$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot \frac{S \cdot I}{N} - \gamma \cdot I$$

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- $\beta$ = Effective contact rate (scan rate × vulnerability exploitation probability).
- $\gamma$ = Recovery/patching rate (inverse of mean recovery time).
- $\dfrac{S \cdot I}{N}$ = Probability that a contact occurs between a susceptible and an infected host.

### **Step 3: Compute the Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$**

At the start of an outbreak, $S \approx N$ (almost all hosts are susceptible). Substituting $S = N$ into the $I$ equation:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} \approx \beta I - \gamma I = (\beta - \gamma) \cdot I$$

Solving this ODE:

$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{(\beta - \gamma) \cdot t}$$

**Step 3a — Logical interpretation:** The worm will grow **exponentially** if and only if $\beta > \gamma$.

**Step 3b — Define $R_0$:** The basic reproduction number is the average number of secondary infections produced by one infected host in a fully susceptible population:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma}$$

**Step 3c — Critical threshold:** The infection dies out when $R_0 < 1$; it spreads when $R_0 > 1$.

### **Step 4: Work Out a Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)**

> **Problem:** A network has $N = 10{,}000$ hosts. A new worm scans $S_{\text{rate}} = 100$ hosts/second and successfully exploits **30%** of vulnerable targets. The patching team restores infected machines at rate $\gamma = 2$ per hour. Determine whether the worm will spread.

**Step 4a — Convert units to common time base.**

$$\beta = S_{\text{rate}} \times p_{\text{exploit}} = 100 \times 0.30 = 30 \text{ infections/hour}$$

**Step 4b — Compute $R_0$.**

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} = \frac{30}{2} = 15$$

**Step 4c — Interpretation.**

Since $R_0 = 15 \gg 1$, the worm spreads aggressively. One infected host produces on average **15 new infections** before being patched.

**Step 4d — Compute the doubling time.**

The early exponential growth factor is:

$$r = \beta - \gamma = 30 - 2 = 28 \text{ per hour}$$

Doubling time:

$$T_d = \frac{\ln 2}{r} = \frac{0.693}{28} \approx 0.0247 \text{ hours} \approx 89 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conclusion:** The worm would double the infected population every **~89 seconds**, severely compromising availability.

## 3.2 DDoS Saturation Analysis

**Problem:** A server has capacity $C = 10 \text{ Gbps}$. Legitimate traffic averages $L = 2 \text{ Gbps}$. A botnet of $n = 50{,}000$ bots attacks, each generating $b = 0.4 \text{ Mbps}$ of traffic. Will the server saturate?

**Step 1 — Compute total malicious traffic.**

$$L_{\text{malicious}} = n \times b = 50{,}000 \times 0.4 \text{ Mbps} = 20{,}000 \text{ Mbps} = 20 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total offered load.**

$$L_{\text{total}} = L + L_{\text{malicious}} = 2 + 20 = 22 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with capacity.**

Since $L_{\text{total}} = 22 \text{ Gbps} \gg C = 10 \text{ Gbps}$, the server is **saturated** by a factor of:

$$\text{Saturation Ratio} = \frac{L_{\text{total}}}{C} = \frac{22}{10} = 2.2$$

**Step 4 — Effective throughput for legitimate users (using fair-queueing approximation).**

$$L_{\text{eff}} \approx L \times \frac{C}{L_{\text{total}}} = 2 \times \frac{10}{22} \approx 0.91 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Conclusion:** Legitimate users experience only **~9% of their normal throughput** — effectively a **DoS**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Attack Types

| Dimension | Phishing | Malware | DoS/DDoS | Identity Theft |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CIA Triad Violated** | Confidentiality | All three | Availability | Confidentiality, Integrity |
| **Primary Vector** | Email / SMS | Email / Drive-by / USB | Network Flooding | Data Dump / Phishing |
| **Human Factor Required** | High (must click) | Medium (must execute) | Low (automated) | Low |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Hard (looks legitimate) | Medium (signature-based fails for zero-day) | Easy (traffic anomaly) | Very Hard |
| **Typical Motive** | Credential theft | Varies (espionage to ransomware) | Disruption / Extortion | Financial fraud |
| **IT Act Section (India)** | 66C, 66D | 43, 65, 66 | 66F (Cyberterrorism if critical infra) | 66C, 66D |
| **Average Time to Detect** | **~95 days** | **~200+ days** | **Minutes** | **Months-Years** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Cybercrime

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cybercrime Taxonomy] --> B[Against Persons]
    A --> C[Against Property]
    A --> D[Against Government and Society]

    B --> B1[Phishing and Social Engineering]
    B --> B2[Identity Theft]
    B --> B3[Cyberstalking and Cyberbullying]
    B --> B4[Online Fraud and Scams]
    B --> B5[Child Exploitation CSAM]

    C --> C1[Hacking Unauthorized Access]
    C --> C2[Malware Family]
    C --> C3[DoS and DDoS]
    C --> C4[Data Breach and Exfiltration]
    C --> C5[Software Piracy and IP Theft]

    D --> D1[Cyberterrorism]
    D --> D2[Cyberwarfare]
    D --> D3[Disinformation and Deepfakes]
    D --> D4[Attacks on Critical Infrastructure]

    C2 --> C2A[Virus]
    C2 --> C2B[Worm]
    C2 --> C2C[Trojan]
    C2 --> C2D[Ransomware]
    C2 --> C2E[Spyware]
    C2 --> C2F[Rootkit]
    C2 --> C2G[Keylogger]
    C2 --> C2H[Botnet]
```

## 4.2 Cyber Kill Chain (Attack Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reconnaissance] --> A2[Weaponization]
    A2 --> A3[Delivery]
    A3 --> A4[Exploitation]
    A4 --> A5[Installation]
    A5 --> A6[Command and Control C2]
    A6 --> A7[Actions on Objectives]
    A7 --> A8{Impact}
    A8 --> A8A[Data Theft]
    A8 --> A8B[Service Disruption]
    A8 --> A8C[Financial Loss]
    A8 --> A8D[Reputation Damage]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Phishing Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Attacker Prepares Fake Login Page] --> P2[Crafts Email with Malicious Link]
    P2 --> P3[Email Sent to Victim List]
    P3 --> P4{Victim Clicks Link}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Fake Page Loads on Browser]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Email Filtered or Ignored]
    P5 --> P7[Victim Enters Credentials]
    P7 --> P8[Credentials Posted to Attacker Server]
    P8 --> P9[Attacker Logs into Real Account]
    P9 --> P10[Fraudulent Transactions or Data Theft]
    P6 --> P11[Attack Failed - No Impact]
```

## 4.4 CIA Triad Mapping with Cybercrime Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CIA[Cybercrime Impact on CIA Triad]
    CIA --> C[Confidentiality Breached]
    CIA --> I[Integrity Breached]

    C --> C1[Phishing]

    I --> I1[Malware Modification]

```

    DM --> L6[Layer 6 User Awareness]

    L1 --> L1A[Firewalls]
    L1 --> L1B[IDS IPS]

    L2 --> L2A[VLAN Segmentation]

    L3 --> L3A[Antivirus]

    L4 --> L4A[WAF]
