<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

### **Q1. Define cybercrime. List any four types of cybercrimes with one-line examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Cybercrime is any unlawful act where a computer or network is the **tool, target, or medium** of crime, violating the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).

**Four Types:**
1. **Phishing** — Fraudulent emails to steal credentials.
2. **Malware** — Malicious software like viruses, worms, ransomware.
3. **Identity Theft** — Misuse of stolen PII for fraud.
4. **Cyberstalking** — Repeated online harassment of an individual.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

### **Q2. Differentiate between DoS and DDoS attacks. State the relevant IT Act section.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Single system | Multiple distributed systems (botnet) |
| Mitigation | Easy (block source) | Difficult (distributed) |
| Volume | Lower | Very high (Gbps–Tbps) |

**IT Act Section 66F** — Cyberterrorism (when targeting critical infrastructure), punishable with **imprisonment up to life**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

### **Question A (14 Marks):** *Comprehensive Question*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify cybercrime in detail with examples for each category. Explain how the **IT Act 2000 (amended 2008)** addresses these categories.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **types of malware** with a comparison table. Explain why **ransomware** has become the most financially damaging category in recent years.

### **Detailed Model Answers:**

#### **Answer to (a) — Classification + IT Act Mapping**

Cybercrime is classified into three broad categories as per the **IT Act 2000** and international frameworks:

**1. Cybercrime Against Persons** — Targets individual safety, dignity, and privacy.
- *Phishing, Identity Theft (Sec 66C), Cyberstalking (Sec 66E), Online Fraud (Sec 66D), Child Exploitation (Sec 67B).*
- **Example:** A fraudster sending fake UPI collect requests.

**2. Cybercrime Against Property** — Targets digital assets and IP.
- *Hacking (Sec 66), Malware (Sec 43), DoS (Sec 66F), Data Breach (Sec 43A), Software Piracy (Sec 65).*
- **Example:** The 2017 WannaCry ransomware attack encrypted hospital data in the UK NHS.

**3. Cybercrime Against Government / Society** — Targets national security and public order.
- *Cyberterrorism (Sec 66F), Cyberwarfare, Disinformation campaigns, Attacks on CII.*
- **Example:** Stuxnet worm sabotaging Iranian nuclear centrifuges.

**IT Act Penalty Summary:**

| Section | Offense | Punishment |
|---|---|---|
| 43 | Damage to computer system | Compensation up to ₹1 Crore |
| 65 | Tampering with source code | Up to 3 years + ₹2 lakh fine |
| 66 | Hacking (basic) | Up to 3 years + ₹5 lakh fine |
| 66C | Identity Theft | Up to 3 years + ₹1 lakh fine |
| 66D | Cheating using computer | Up to 3 years + ₹1 lakh fine |
| 66E | Violation of privacy | Up to 3 years + ₹2 lakh fine |
| 66F | Cyberterrorism | Life imprisonment |
| 67B | Child pornography | 7 years to life |

---

#### **Answer to (b) — Malware Deep-Dive + Ransomware Analysis**

Malware is software designed to **damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access** to a system.

**Comparison Table:**

| Type | Self-Replicate | Trigger | Goal | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Virus | Needs host | User action | Damage data | ILOVEYOU |
| Worm | Yes (autonomous) | Network scan | Spread fast | Blaster |
| Trojan | No | User deception | Backdoor | Emotet |
| Ransomware | Optional | Phishing/USB | Extortion | WannaCry |
| Spyware | No | Bundled install | Steal data | CoolWebSearch |
| Rootkit | No | Pre-existing infection | Stealth control | Alureon |

**Why Ransomware is Most Damaging (Reasons):**

1. **Direct monetization** — Cryptocurrency payments (Bitcoin, Monero) are untraceable.
2. **Asymmetric encryption** — Modern ransomware uses **RSA-2048 / AES-256** keys, making brute-force decryption infeasible.
3. **RaaS Model** — Ransomware-as-a-Service allows low-skill criminals to lease ransomware kits (e.g., REvil, LockBit).
4. **Double/Triple Extortion** — Encrypt + exfiltrate + threaten public leak / DDoS the victim.
5. **Critical-target preference** — Hospitals, utilities, and government agencies pay quickly due to downtime cost.

**Example:** **WannaCry (2017)** infected 230,000+ computers in 150 countries in **one day**, causing **\$4–8 billion** damages.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Cyber Kill Chain** model proposed by Lockheed Martin. Illustrate each phase with a real-world example (e.g., APT attack or phishing campaign).

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **Identity Theft**? Discuss its various types, working mechanism, and countermeasures. Mention relevant **IT Act provisions**.

### **Detailed Model Answers:**

#### **Answer to (a) — Cyber Kill Chain**

The **Cyber Kill Chain** is a 7-stage model that describes the **anatomy of a cyberattack** from the attacker's perspective. By mapping defenses to each stage, defenders can **break the chain** and prevent the attack.

**The 7 Phases:**

1. **Reconnaissance** — Gathering information about the target (OSINT, Shodan, social media profiling).
   - *Example:* Attacker scrapes LinkedIn to identify IT admins at a target company.

2. **Weaponization** — Coupling exploit with a deliverable payload.
   - *Example:* Attacker creates a PDF with an embedded exploit (CVE-2017-0199) and a backdoor.

3. **Delivery** — Transmitting the weapon to the victim.
   - *Example:* Email phishing with malicious PDF attachment.

4. **Exploitation** — Triggering the vulnerability in the target system.
   - *Example:* Victim opens PDF; exploit runs in Adobe Reader.

5. **Installation** — Installing malware/backdoor for persistent access.
   - *Example:* Trojan drops Cobalt Strike beacon on disk.

6. **Command and Control (C2)** — Establishing a channel to remotely control the compromised system.
   - *Example:* Beacon "calls home" to attacker's C2 server over HTTPS.

7. **Actions on Objectives** — Achieving the actual goal.
   - *Example:* Lateral movement, data exfiltration, ransomware deployment.

**Defensive Mapping:** Each stage can be disrupted — e.g., email gateways (Stage 3), EDR (Stage 5), network monitoring (Stage 6).

---

#### **Answer to (b) — Identity Theft Comprehensive**

**Definition:** Identity Theft is the **deliberate acquisition and use** of another individual's Personally Identifiable Information (PII) — name, Aadhaar, PAN, bank details — to commit fraud or deception, typically for financial gain.

**Working Mechanism (4-step model):**

1. **Acquisition** — Phishing, data breaches, dumpster diving, dark-web purchase.
2. **Processing** — Validating the stolen data via automated tools.
3. **Utilization** — Opening fraudulent accounts, making unauthorized purchases, applying for loans.
4. **Cover-up** — Using anonymization, fake IDs, or cryptocurrency to avoid traceback.

**Types of Identity Theft:**

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Financial** | Bank fraud, credit-card fraud, loan fraud |
| **Medical** | Using victim's insurance to obtain treatment |
| **Criminal** | Providing victim's ID when arrested |
| **Synthetic** | Combining real + fake data to create a new identity |
| **Child** | Using a minor's SSN/Aadhaar — often undetected for years |
| **Tax** | Filing fraudulent returns using stolen PAN/SSN |

**Countermeasures:**
- Strong, unique passwords + MFA.
- Regularly monitor credit reports / bank statements.
- Use of tokenization and encryption for stored PII.
- Dark-web monitoring services.
- Public awareness and reporting via **cybercrime.gov.in**.

**IT Act Provisions:**
- **Section 66C** — Identity Theft (3 years + ₹1 lakh fine).
- **Section 66D** — Cheating by personation using computer resource.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing DoS with DDoS** — Students often describe DDoS as "many DoS attacks." Correct answer: *DDoS is a coordinated, distributed attack from multiple sources, often via a botnet.* Marks lost: 2–3.
> 2. **Forgetting IT Act Sections** — A 14-mark answer without Section numbers is treated as incomplete. Always cite **at least 3 specific sections** in any Part B cybercrime question.
> 3. **Mixing Virus and Worm** — A *virus* needs a host program and user action; a *worm* is self-replicating across networks. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Conflating "Identity Theft" with "Identity Fraud"** — Theft = stealing data; Fraud = using it. Many students skip this nuance and lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the CIA Triad mapping** — Every cybercrime answer is **incomplete** without identifying which of C, I, A is breached. Examiners often allocate 1–2 marks explicitly for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Types of Cybercrime**

- **Definition:** Cybercrime = any offense where a computer/network is *tool, target, or medium* — violates the **CIA Triad**.
- **3 Main Categories:** Against **Persons**, **Property**, **Government/Society**.
- **Phishing** is the **#1 initial-access vector** worldwide; remember its 5 variants (Spear, Whaling, Vishing, Smishing, Pharming).
- **Malware Family:** Virus vs Worm vs Trojan — know the **trigger** and **spread** mechanism of each.
- **Ransomware** is the most financially damaging; uses **asymmetric encryption**; monetized via cryptocurrency.
- **DoS vs DDoS:** DoS = single source, easy to block; DDoS = distributed via botnet, hard to block.
- **Identity Theft (Sec 66C)** vs **Cheating by Personation (Sec 66D)** — different sections, different intents.
- **Cyber Kill Chain** — 7 phases from Reconnaissance to Actions on Objectives; map defenses to each phase.
- **Cyberterrorism (Sec 66F)** — most severe offense under IT Act, life imprisonment, applies to CII attacks.
- **Hacker Classes:** Black Hat, White Hat, Grey Hat, Script Kiddie, Hacktivist — know motivation of each.
- **Key DDoS Metric:** Measured in **Gbps**; Mirai Botnet peaked at **~1.2 Tbps**.
- **SIR Model:** Use $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$ — infection spreads if $R_0 > 1$.
- **Always cite IT Act Section numbers** in any 14-mark answer — examiners reward specificity.
- **Defense-in-Depth:** Perimeter → Network → Endpoint → Application → Data → User awareness.
- **CSAM (Sec 67B)** — strictest punishment; **7 years to life imprisonment**.
- **Quick Recognition Rule:** If a question mentions *emails + credentials* → **Phishing (66C/66D)**; if *encryption + ransom* → **Ransomware (66F)**; if *overwhelming traffic* → **DoS/DDoS (66F)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hacking
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hacking

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Hacking

**Hacking** is the unauthorized or authorized practice of gaining access to, manipulating, or exploiting vulnerabilities in computer systems, networks, software, or digital devices with the intent of understanding, modifying, or subverting their normal behavior. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEECT864 – Cyber Security, Module 4)**, hacking encompasses the identification and exploitation of weaknesses in information systems — both for malicious intent (Black Hat) and defensive/auditing purposes (White Hat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Hacking is the process of finding and exploiting weaknesses (vulnerabilities) in a computer system, application, network, or embedded device to gain unauthorized or authorized access to data, resources, or system control.*

### Related Formal Terms

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|------|----------------|
| **Hacker** | An individual who exploits system vulnerabilities to achieve a goal (malicious or constructive). |
| **Cracker** | A hacker with purely malicious intent — breaks systems for personal gain or damage. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in a system that can be exploited to compromise security. |
| **Exploit** | A piece of code, technique, or sequence of commands that takes advantage of a vulnerability. |
| **Payload** | The malicious component delivered by an exploit to perform the actual attack. |
| **Attack Vector** | The path or means by which an attacker gains unauthorized access. |
| **Threat** | Any potential danger that could compromise system security. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine your house has multiple locked doors, windows, and a chimney. A **hacker** is someone who:

1. **Walks around the house first** (looks at the doors, windows, locks, and structure) — this is called *Reconnaissance*.
2. **Tests each opening** (pushes doors, peeks through windows) — this is *Scanning*.
3. **Picks a lock or finds an unlocked window** — this is *Gaining Access*.
4. **Moves in, hides evidence, and may leave a back door open for later visits** — this is *Maintaining Access and Clearing Tracks*.

A **White Hat hacker** is like a **licensed locksmith** who is hired by you to test your locks and tell you which ones are weak. A **Black Hat hacker** is a **burglar** who breaks in to steal. A **Grey Hat hacker** is someone in between — perhaps a curious neighbor who checks your doors without permission but tells you afterward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hacking is not inherently illegal.** It becomes a crime only when done *without authorization* and *with malicious intent*. Authorized hacking (penetration testing, bug bounty programs) is a legitimate and high-paying profession.

## 1.3 Classification of Hackers

Based on **intent, authorization, and skill level**, hackers are broadly classified into the following categories:

### 1.3.1 By Intent / Hat Color

| Type | Hat Color | Authorization | Intent | Example |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|---------|
| **White Hat Hacker** | White | Authorized (legal permission) | Defensive — finds vulnerabilities to fix them | Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH), Penetration Tester |
| **Black Hat Hacker** | Black | Unauthorized | Malicious — steals data, damages systems, extorts money | Ransomware groups, identity thieves |
| **Grey Hat Hacker** | Grey | Sometimes unauthorized but no malicious damage | Finds vulnerabilities and may disclose publicly | Independent security researchers |
| **Red Hat Hacker** | Red | Authorized (offensive) | Aggressively attacks Black Hat hackers | Government cyber-warfare teams |
| **Blue Hat Hacker** | Blue | External (invited) | Tests software pre-release | Microsoft BlueHat program bug testers |
| **Hacktivist** | — | Unauthorized | Politically/socially motivated attacks | Anonymous, LulzSec |

### 1.3.2 By Skill Level

| Type | Skill Level | Description |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Elite Hacker** | Expert | Innovator, creates new exploit techniques |
| **Script Kiddie** | Novice | Uses pre-built tools without deep understanding |
| **Neophyte / Noob** | Beginner | Learning the basics |
| **Lamer** | Minimal | Pretends skill, often engages in online harassment |

### 1.3.3 By Motivation

| Type | Primary Motivation |
|------|-------------------|
| **Financial Gain** | Monetary theft, ransomware, fraud |
| **Espionage** | Nation-state or corporate intelligence |
| **Hacktivism** | Political or ideological agenda |
| **Revenge** | Insider attacks, personal grudges |
| **Notoriety** | Fame, reputation among hacker community |
| **Curiosity** | Pure intellectual exploration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The distinction between *Ethical Hacking* and *Cracking* lies in **AUTHORIZATION** and **INTENT**. Always mention both dimensions when asked.

## 1.4 The Modern Threat Landscape

The 2024 cyber threat landscape is dominated by:
- **Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS)** — rented ransomware toolkits
- **AI-powered attacks** — using LLMs for phishing and code generation
- **Supply chain attacks** — compromising software updates (e.g., SolarWinds)
- **Zero-day exploits** — vulnerabilities unknown to the vendor
- **IoT botnets** — billions of insecure connected devices

> [!NOTE]
> **Hacking vs. Cracking — KTU Board Convention:** In KTU answer scripts, always use the precise term. "Cracking" refers to breaking copy-protection or passwords with malicious intent. "Hacking" is the broader umbrella including ethical exploration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases of Hacking (KTU Core Framework)

The standard **hacking methodology** taught in KTU's ethical hacking syllabus follows the **five-phase penetration testing model** adopted from EC-Council's CEH curriculum:

### Phase 1: Reconnaissance (Footprinting)

**Definition:** The preparatory phase where an attacker gathers information about the target before launching any attack.

**Two Sub-Types:**

| Type | Description | Tools |
|------|-------------|-------|
| **Passive Reconnaissance** | Information gathered without directly interacting with the target | Google dorks, WHOIS lookup, Shodan, social media, job postings |
| **Active Reconnaissance** | Direct probing of the target system | Nmap, ping sweeps, traceroute, banner grabbing |

**Information Gathered:**
- IP addresses, DNS records, network topology
- Employee names, emails (for social engineering)
- Operating systems, open ports, running services
- Physical location, business relationships

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Studies show that **80–90%** of attack success depends on the quality of reconnaissance. The famous IBM X-Force report states that attackers often spend weeks or months in this phase.

### Phase 2: Scanning & Enumeration

**Definition:** Actively probing the target to identify live hosts, open ports, services, and vulnerabilities.

**Three Scanning Stages:**
1. **Port Scanning** — Identifying open/closed/filtered ports
2. **Network Scanning** — Mapping live hosts and topology
3. **Vulnerability Scanning** — Detecting known CVEs

**Common Tools:**
- **Nmap** — Network mapper for port and OS detection
- **Nessus / OpenVAS** — Vulnerability scanners
- **Nikto** — Web server scanner
- **Burp Suite** — Web application scanner

**Key Nmap Scan Types (KTU Favorite Question):**

| Scan Type | TCP Flag | Stealth Level | Use Case |
|-----------|----------|---------------|----------|
| **TCP Connect Scan** | Full SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK | Loud | Complete connection, logged by target |
| **SYN Scan (Half-Open)** | SYN only | Stealth | Default scan, doesn't complete handshake |
| **NULL Scan** | No flags | Very stealthy | Detects Unix systems |
| **FIN Scan** | FIN only | Stealthy | Bypasses some firewalls |
| **Xmas Scan** | FIN+PSH+URG | Very stealthy | Lights up flags like a Christmas tree |
| **ACK Scan** | ACK only | Stealthy | Firewall rule mapping |
| **UDP Scan** | UDP packets | — | Finds UDP services |

### Phase 3: Gaining Access (Exploitation)

**Definition:** The actual intrusion phase where the attacker exploits a discovered vulnerability to enter the system.

**Common Attack Techniques:**

| Technique | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Password Attacks** | Brute force, dictionary, rainbow tables, credential stuffing |
| **Vulnerability Exploitation** | Using CVEs, zero-days, buffer overflows |
| **Social Engineering** | Phishing, pretexting, baiting, tailgating |
| **Man-in-the-Middle** | Intercepting communications |
| **Malware Injection** | Trojans, backdoors, rootkits, worms |
| **Session Hijacking** | Stealing session tokens |
| **SQL Injection** | Database exploitation |
| **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** | Browser-side code injection |

### Phase 4: Maintaining Access

**Definition:** After gaining entry, the attacker ensures persistent access for future use.

**Techniques Used:**
- Installing **backdoors** and **rootkits**
- Creating new user accounts
- Planting **scheduled tasks/cron jobs**
- Using **reverse shells** and **bind shells**
- Establishing **command-and-control (C2)** channels
- Pivoting to other systems in the network

### Phase 5: Clearing Tracks (Covering Up)

**Definition:** Removing evidence of the intrusion to avoid detection.

**Methods:**
- Clearing logs (Windows Event Logs, syslog, bash_history)
- Disabling antivirus and audit mechanisms
- Hiding files using **Alternate Data Streams (ADS)**, steganography
- Tunneling traffic through legitimate services
- Manipulating timestamps of planted files

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** When answering hacking-related questions, always structure your answer around these **five phases** — it is the most common framework expected in board exams.

## 2.2 Common Hacking Attack Categories

### 2.2.1 Password-Based Attacks

**Password Cracking** is the process of recovering or guessing passwords from stored data or live transmission.

**Methods:**

| Method | Description | Time Complexity |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Brute Force** | Tries every possible combination | O(2ⁿ) for n-bit key |
| **Dictionary Attack** | Tries words from a wordlist | O(W) where W is wordlist size |
| **Rainbow Tables** | Pre-computed hash-to-password lookup | O(1) lookup |
| **Hybrid Attack** | Dictionary + rules (e.g., appending "123") | O(W × R) |
| **Credential Stuffing** | Reuses leaked username/password pairs | Depends on database size |
| **Social Engineering** | Phishing or guessing from personal info | Trivial |
| **Keylogger** | Captures keystrokes on victim's machine | Real-time |

### 2.2.2 Sniffing & Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)

**Sniffing** is the capture of unencrypted network traffic.

**Tools:** Wireshark, tcpdump, Cain & Abel, Ettercap

**MITM Attack Flow:**
1. Attacker positions between client and server
2. ARP spoofing / DNS spoofing / rogue AP is used
3. All traffic is relayed through attacker
4. Credentials, cookies, and sensitive data are harvested

### 2.2.3 Social Engineering

The art of manipulating human psychology rather than breaking code.

**Six Principles (Robert Cialdini):**
1. **Reciprocity** — Give something to get something
2. **Commitment** — Small yes leads to bigger yes
3. **Social Proof** — "Everyone is doing it"
4. **Authority** — Impersonating officials
5. **Liking** — Friendliness and rapport
6. **Scarcity** — "Limited time offer"

### 2.2.4 Malware-Based Attacks

| Malware Type | Behavior | Example |
|--------------|----------|---------|
| **Virus** | Attaches to legitimate programs | ILOVEYOU, Melissa |
| **Worm** | Self-replicates across networks | Blaster, Conficker |
| **Trojan** | Disguises as legitimate software | Zeus, Emotet |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts data, demands payment | WannaCry, LockBit |
| **Spyware** | Secretly monitors user activity | CoolWebSearch |
| **Adware** | Displays unwanted advertisements | Gator |
| **Rootkit** | Hides deep within OS kernel | Stuxnet, Sony BMG |
| **Keylogger** | Records keystrokes | Olympic Vision |
| **Bot/Botnet** | Network of compromised machines | Mirai botnet |
| **Fileless Malware** | Lives only in memory, no file on disk | Poweliks |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Quantitative Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU numerical/derivation questions on hacking, password strength, and brute force complexity.

### 2.3.1 Password Strength Metrics

**Shannon Entropy of a Password:**

$$
H = - \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2(p_i)
$$

where $N$ is the character set size and $p_i$ is the probability of each character.

**For a uniformly random password of length $L$ from a charset of size $N$:**

$$
H = L \cdot \log_2(N) \text{ bits}
$$

**Keyspace (Total Combinations):**

$$
K = N^L
$$

where $N$ = character set size, $L$ = password length.

**Cracking Time:**

$$
T_{crack} = \frac{N^L}{R \cdot 2}
$$

where $R$ = guesses per second (hash rate), and division by 2 accounts for average case (50% of keyspace searched).

### 2.3.2 Network & Scanning Metrics

**Port Scan Detection Rate:**

$$
D = \frac{\text{Detected Scans}}{\text{Total Scans}} \times 100\%
$$

**Risk Score (CVSS-style):**

$$
\text{Risk} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood}
$$

**Mean Time to Detection (MTTD):**

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Detection Time}_i}{n}
$$

**Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Response Time}_i}{n}
$$

### 2.3.3 Brute Force Search Space Table

| Charset $N$ | Characters Included | For 8-char Password |
|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 26 | lowercase only | $26^8 \approx 2.09 \times 10^{11}$ |
| 52 | lower + upper | $52^8 \approx 5.34 \times 10^{13}$ |
| 62 | lower + upper + digits | $62^8 \approx 2.18 \times 10^{14}$ |
| 95 | All printable keyboard | $95^8 \approx 6.63 \times 10^{15}$ |

### 2.3.4 Attack Surface Calculation

$$
\text{Attack Surface} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Entry Points}_i \times \text{Permission Level}_i \times \text{Value of Resource}_i)
$$

A larger attack surface implies higher risk and easier exploitation.

### 2.3.5 Network Bandwidth & DoS Metrics

**Bandwidth Consumption (DoS):**

$$
B_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \cdot t_i
$$

**Packets per Second (PPS):**

$$
\text{PPS} = \frac{\text{Total Packets}}{\text{Time Interval}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by penetration testers to estimate how long an 8-character password will resist a brute-force attack using GPU clusters. The entropy value directly drives password policy design in enterprise security.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Password Cracking Time for Brute Force

**Problem Statement:** A user creates a password of length **8 characters** using only **lowercase English letters (a–z)**. An attacker uses a GPU capable of **10 billion (10¹⁰) hashes per second**. Calculate:
1. The total keyspace size
2. The entropy of the password in bits
3. The worst-case time to crack the password
4. The average time to crack the password

### Given Information

$$
L = 8 \text{ (password length)}, \quad N = 26 \text{ (lowercase charset)}, \quad R = 10^{10} \text{ hashes/sec}
$$

### Step 1: Calculate the Total Keyspace

The total number of possible password combinations is the number of $L$-length sequences drawn from a charset of size $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= N^L \\
&= 26^8 \\
&= (26^4)^2 \\
&= (456976)^2 \\
&= 208{,}827{,}064{,}576 \\
&\approx 2.088 \times 10^{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

The keyspace $K$ represents every possible 8-character lowercase password. **Approximately 208 billion combinations.**

### Step 2: Calculate Password Entropy in Bits

For a uniformly random password, the entropy is the base-2 logarithm of the keyspace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \log_2(K) = \log_2(26^8) \\
&= 8 \cdot \log_2(26) \\
&= 8 \times 4.7004 \\
&= 37.603 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

The entropy $H = 37.603$ bits means an attacker needs, on average, $2^{37.603}$ ≈ $2.088 \times 10^{11}$ guesses.

**Step-by-Step Cross-Check Using the Direct Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= L \cdot \log_2(N) \\
&= 8 \times \log_2(26) \\
&= 8 \times \frac{\ln(26)}{\ln(2)} \\
&= 8 \times \frac{3.258}{0.6931} \\
&= 8 \times 4.7004 \\
&= 37.603 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Worst-Case Cracking Time

The worst case assumes the correct password is the **last one** tried:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{worst} &= \frac{K}{R} \\
&= \frac{2.088 \times 10^{11}}{10^{10}} \\
&= 20.88 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

In the worst case, the attacker needs about **20.88 seconds** with this GPU.

### Step 4: Calculate Average-Case Cracking Time

On average, the correct password is found after examining **half** the keyspace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{K}{2 \cdot R} \\
&= \frac{2.088 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= \frac{2.088 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= 10.44 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Analytical Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** An 8-character lowercase-only password can be cracked in **less than 11 seconds on average** by a modern GPU. This is why **NIST SP 800-63B** (2024) recommends a **minimum of 12 characters** with mixed case, digits, and symbols.

### Step 5: Compare with Strong Password (Mixed Charset)

If the password uses **all 95 printable ASCII characters** instead:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K' &= 95^8 \\
&= 6.634 \times 10^{15} \\
T_{avg}' &= \frac{6.634 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= 3.317 \times 10^5 \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 3.84 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

Increasing the charset from 26 to 95 increased cracking time by a factor of $\approx 31{,}760$.

### Step 6: Final Comparative Table

| Password Profile | Keyspace $K$ | Entropy $H$ (bits) | Avg. Crack Time (10¹⁰ H/s) |
|------------------|--------------|--------------------|------------------------------|
| 8-char lowercase | $2.09 \times 10^{11}$ | 37.6 | **10.4 seconds** |
| 8-char mixed case | $5.34 \times 10^{13}$ | 45.6 | **2,667 seconds (44 min)** |
| 8-char full ASCII | $6.63 \times 10^{15}$ | 52.6 | **3.84 days** |
| 12-char lowercase | $9.54 \times 10^{16}$ | 56.4 | **1.34 hours** |
| 12-char full ASCII | $5.40 \times 10^{23}$ | 79.0 | **859,000 years** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway for KTU Answers:** Length matters exponentially. Doubling password length is far more effective than doubling charset size.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Entropy for Non-Uniform Passwords

**Problem:** A password creation policy enforces:
- 60% of users choose from the top 1,000 most common passwords
- 30% choose dictionary words
- 10% use random characters

**Calculate the entropy:**

### Solution

Apply the Shannon entropy formula:

$$
H = -\sum_{i=1}^{3} p_i \log_2(p_i)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -[0.6 \cdot \log_2(0.6) + 0.3 \cdot \log_2(0.3) + 0.1 \cdot \log_2(0.1)] \\
&= -[0.6 \cdot (-0.737) + 0.3 \cdot (-1.737) + 0.1 \cdot (-3.322)] \\
&= -[(-0.4422) + (-0.5211) + (-0.3322)] \\
&= -[-1.2955] \\
&= 1.2955 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective entropy ≈ 1.3 bits.** This means an attacker only needs, on average, $2^{1.3} \approx 2.46$ guesses — showing how catastrophic user behavior is to password security.

## 3.3 Derivation: Risk Score Calculation (CVSS-Inspired)

**Problem:** A server has:
- Impact score: 8.5
- Likelihood score: 7.0
- Availability multiplier: 1.2

**Calculate the Risk Score.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk} &= \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Availability} \\
&= 8.5 \times 7.0 \times 1.2 \\
&= 71.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- Risk score > 70 = **Critical** (immediate action required)
- Risk score 40–70 = **High**
- Risk score 20–40 = **Medium**
- Risk score < 20 = **Low**

## 3.4 Derivation: Man-in-the-Middle ARP Spoofing Logic

In an ARP spoofing MITM, the attacker sends forged ARP replies to the network, claiming their MAC address owns the target's IP.

**Legitimate ARP Table (Normal State):**

| IP Address | MAC Address |
|------------|-------------|
| 192.168.1.1 (Router) | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA |
| 192.168.1.5 (Victim) | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB |
| 192.168.1.99 (Attacker) | CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC |

**After ARP Spoof (Poisoned State):**

| IP Address | MAC Address |
|------------|-------------|
| 192.168.1.1 (Router) | **CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC** (poisoned!) |
| 192.168.1.5 (Victim) | **CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC** (poisoned!) |
| 192.168.1.99 (Attacker) | CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC |

The attacker now receives all traffic meant for both the router and the victim, enabling interception, modification, and relay.

**Detection Probability:**

$$
P_{detect} = 1 - \left(1 - P_{entry}\right)^n
$$

where $P_{entry}$ = probability of detection per ARP entry, $n$ = number of poisoned entries.

For $P_{entry} = 0.4$ and $n = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{detect} &= 1 - (1 - 0.4)^2 \\
&= 1 - (0.6)^2 \\
&= 1 - 0.36 \\
&= 0.64 = 64\%
\end{aligned}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Explain the different categories of hackers based on their intent.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Hackers are classified based on their **intent and authorization** into the following categories:

1. **White Hat Hackers (1 mark):** Ethical hackers authorized by the system owner to identify vulnerabilities through penetration testing and security audits. They help improve security. Example: Certified Ethical Hackers (CEH).

2. **Black Hat Hackers (1 mark):** Malicious hackers who break into systems without authorization to steal data, cause damage, or gain financial profit. They are criminals under IT laws like the IT Act 2000.

3. **Grey Hat Hackers (1 mark):** Hackers who may violate laws but without malicious intent. They often find vulnerabilities and disclose them publicly, sometimes asking for a fee.

**Supplementary (for depth):** Red Hat (offensive defenders), Blue Hat (external bug testers), Hacktivists (ideology-driven).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**What is password cracking? Explain any two password cracking techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Password Cracking (1 mark):** Password cracking is the process of recovering or guessing passwords from stored data or transmitted data, typically by an attacker attempting to gain unauthorized access.

**Techniques:**

1. **Brute Force Attack (1 mark):** Tries every possible combination of characters until the correct password is found. Time complexity grows exponentially with password length. Example: A 6-digit PIN has $10^6 = 1$ million combinations.

2. **Dictionary Attack (1 mark):** Uses a precompiled list (wordlist) of common passwords, names, and dictionary words to guess the password. Much faster than brute force but only effective against weak passwords.

**Supplementary:** Rainbow Tables (precomputed hashes), Hybrid Attack, Keylogger, Social Engineering.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

#### **(a)** Explain the five phases of hacking with suitable examples and tools used in each phase. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** What is social engineering? Explain any three types of social engineering attacks with real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The five phases of hacking form the standard methodology used by penetration testers and attackers alike:

**Phase 1: Reconnaissance (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Gathering information about the target without alerting them.
- **Passive:** Searching WHOIS records, Google dorks, social media profiles (LinkedIn, Facebook), Shodan.
- **Active:** Direct probing using `ping`, `traceroute`, email harvesting.
- **Example:** Attacker searches LinkedIn for the target company's IT staff, then queries WHOIS for domain registration details.

**Phase 2: Scanning & Enumeration (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Identifying live hosts, open ports, services, and vulnerabilities.
- **Tools:** Nmap (port scanning), Nessus (vulnerability scanning), Netcat, Nikto.
- **Example:** Running `nmap -sS -O 192.168.1.0/24` to discover hosts and OS types.

**Phase 3: Gaining Access (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Exploiting discovered vulnerabilities to enter the system.
- **Methods:** SQL injection, buffer overflow, password cracking, phishing.
- **Example:** Exploiting CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue) to gain remote access to Windows systems.

**Phase 4: Maintaining Access (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Establishing persistent entry for future use.
- **Methods:** Installing backdoors, rootkits, creating rogue user accounts, setting up reverse shells via `netcat`.
- **Example:** Deploying a Meterpreter payload via Metasploit to maintain persistent access.

**Phase 5: Clearing Tracks (1 mark)**
- **Definition:** Removing evidence of intrusion.
- **Methods:** Clearing Windows Event Logs, deleting bash_history, disabling audit logs, timestomping with `timestomp`.
- **Example:** Attacker uses `wevtutil cl Security` to clear Windows Security logs.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition of Social Engineering (1.5 marks):**
Social engineering is the art of manipulating human psychology to obtain confidential information, access, or valuables, rather than breaking into systems through technical means. It exploits the weakest link in security — the human element.

**Three Types of Social Engineering Attacks:**

1. **Phishing (2 marks):**
   - **Description:** Fraudulent emails, messages, or websites that impersonate legitimate entities to steal credentials or financial data.
   - **Types:** Email phishing, spear phishing (targeted), whaling (targeting executives), vishing (voice), smishing (SMS).
   - **Real-World Example:** The 2016 phishing attack on John Podesta (Hillary Clinton's campaign chairman) where a fake Google login page captured his credentials, leading to major email leaks.

2. **Pretexting (2 marks):**
   - **Description:** The attacker fabricates a fictitious scenario (pretext) to steal personal information.
   - **Real-World Example:** An attacker calls an employee pretending to be from IT support, claiming they need the employee's password to perform urgent maintenance. The attacker uses the credentials to access confidential files.

3. **Baiting (1.5 marks):**
   - **Description:** Leaving infected USB drives or links in physical locations where victims are likely to find and use them.
   - **Real-World Example:** Attackers dropped USB drives labeled "Confidential Salary Data 2024" in a company's parking lot. Employees plugged them into office computers, unknowingly installing malware that gave attackers remote access.

**Supplementary Countermeasures:** Security awareness training, multi-factor authentication (MFA), verification procedures, least-privilege policies.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

#### **(a)** Explain the different types of malware with examples. Compare worms and viruses in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Explain Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack with a diagram. Discuss ARP spoofing as an MITM technique. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Types of Malware (5 marks):**

1. **Virus:** A malicious program that attaches itself to legitimate files or programs and spreads when the host file is executed. Requires user action. *Example: ILOVEYOU, Melissa.*

2. **Worm:** Self-replicating malware that spreads autonomously across networks without user intervention. Exploits network vulnerabilities. *Example: Blaster, Conficker, Stuxnet.*

3. **Trojan Horse:** Disguises itself as legitimate software but carries malicious payload. Does not self-replicate. *Example: Emotet banking trojan.*

4. **Ransomware:** Encrypts victim files and demands ransom (often cryptocurrency) for decryption key. *Example: WannaCry (2017), LockBit, REvil.*

5. **Spyware:** Secretly monitors user activity and transmits data to attacker. *Example: CoolWebSearch, Pegasus.*

6. **Rootkit:** Hides deep within the OS kernel, providing persistent privileged access while evading detection. *Example: Sony BMG rootkit, Stuxnet's payload.*

7. **Adware:** Displays unwanted advertisements; often bundled with free software. *Example: Gator.*

8. **Keylogger:** Records keystrokes to capture passwords and sensitive data. *Example: Olympic Vision keylogger.*

9. **Bot/Botnet:** Network of compromised computers controlled remotely (Command & Control). *Example: Mirai IoT botnet.*

10. **Fileless Malware:** Lives only in memory (RAM); leaves no file artifacts on disk. *Example: Poweliks.*

**Comparison: Worms vs Viruses (2 marks):**

| Feature | Virus | Worm |
|---------|-------|------|
| **Self-Replication** | Requires host file | Self-replicating |
| **User Action** | Required (open file) | Not required |
| **Spread Mechanism** | File sharing, USB, email attachments | Network vulnerabilities, automatic |
| **Speed of Spread** | Slow, depends on user | Fast, automated |
| **Primary Target** | Files on local system | Networks and connected systems |
| **Payload Carrier** | Yes, attaches to programs | Often self-contained |
| **Example** | ILOVEYOU | Blaster, WannaCry (worm component) |

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition of MITM (1.5 marks):**
A **Man-in-the-Middle attack** is a cyberattack where the attacker secretly intercepts and possibly alters communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other. The attacker sits "in the middle" of the conversation, capable of eavesdropping, modifying, or injecting malicious content.

**MITM Attack Flow Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Client] <----> [Attacker] <----> [Server]
              (intercepts all)
```

The attacker achieves MITM through one or more of the following techniques:
- ARP Spoofing (LAN-based)
- DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning
- Rogue Wi-Fi Access Point
- SSL Stripping
- Email hijacking

**ARP Spoofing (2.5 marks):**

**ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)** maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network. ARP spoofing exploits this by sending forged ARP messages onto the LAN, associating the attacker's MAC address with the IP address of the legitimate gateway (router) or another host.

**Step-by-Step ARP Spoofing Attack:**

1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker uses `nmap` or `arpscan` to identify victims on the LAN.

2. **Enable IP Forwarding:** Attacker enables IP forwarding on their machine so victim traffic still reaches the gateway (otherwise DoS occurs).

3. **Send Forged ARP Replies:** Attacker uses `arpspoof` or `ettercap` to broadcast ARP replies like:
   - "192.168.1.1 (Router) has MAC CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC (Attacker's MAC)"
   - "192.168.1.5 (Victim) has MAC CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC (Attacker's MAC)"

4. **Traffic Interception:** All victim traffic intended for the router now flows through the attacker's machine.

5. **Sniffing and Manipulation:** Attacker uses Wireshark to capture credentials and can use `sslstrip` to downgrade HTTPS to HTTP.

6. **Forwarding:** Attacker's machine forwards traffic to the real router (so the victim doesn't notice).

**Countermeasures:**
- Static ARP entries on critical hosts
- ARP spoofing detection tools (e.g., XArp, ARPWatch)
- Use HTTPS/TLS everywhere
- Network segmentation
- 802.1X port-based authentication

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Hacking Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Hacker" with "Cracker":** A student who writes only about "criminal hackers" loses 1 mark. Always distinguish authorized vs unauthorized.
>
> 2. **Skipping the Five Phases:** For 14-mark questions on hacking methodology, students who list only 3 phases lose 3–4 marks. **Always present all 5 phases.**
>
> 3. **Tools without context:** Writing "Nmap, Metasploit, Wireshark" without explaining what they do or how they fit into which phase loses marks. Each tool must be tied to a specific phase.
>
> 4. **Vague MITM explanation:** "The attacker sits in the middle" without specifying ARP spoofing or DNS spoofing loses 2 marks. **Mechanism matters.**
>
> 5. **No countermeasures:** Any attack question without a defense section is considered incomplete by KTU examiners. Always end with **at least 2–3 countermeasures.**
>
> 6. **Mixing malware types:** Calling a worm a virus, or describing ransomware as "just malware," loses accuracy marks.
>
> 7. **Diagrams:** Internal choice questions (14 marks) often award **2 marks for diagrams**. A well-labeled MITM or hacking-cycle diagram can make the difference between an A and B grade.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Hacking**

### Key Definitions to Memorize

- ✅ **Hacking** — Finding and exploiting system vulnerabilities (authorized or unauthorized)
- ✅ **Hacker** — An individual who hacks; classified by intent (hat color) and skill
- ✅ **Cracker** — Hacker with purely malicious intent
- ✅ **Vulnerability** — Weakness in a system
- ✅ **Exploit** — Code/technique that uses a vulnerability
- ✅ **Payload** — Malicious component of an exploit

### The Five Phases of Hacking (MUST MEMORIZE)

- ✅ **Phase 1:** Reconnaissance (Passive + Active)
- ✅ **Phase 2:** Scanning & Enumeration (Nmap, Nessus)
- ✅ **Phase 3:** Gaining Access (Exploitation)
- ✅ **Phase 4:** Maintaining Access (Backdoors, Rootkits)
- ✅ **Phase 5:** Clearing Tracks (Log deletion, Timestomping)

### Hacker Classifications

- ✅ White Hat (good, authorized) | Black Hat (bad, unauthorized) | Grey Hat (mixed)
- ✅ Red Hat (offensive defense) | Blue Hat (pre-release testing) | Hacktivist (ideology)
- ✅ Script Kiddie (uses tools) | Elite Hacker (creates tools)

### Key Attacks to Memorize

- ✅ **Password Attacks:** Brute Force, Dictionary, Rainbow Tables, Hybrid, Credential Stuffing, Keylogger
- ✅ **Network Attacks:** Sniffing, MITM (ARP Spoofing, DNS Spoofing), Session Hijacking
- ✅ **Social Engineering:** Phishing, Spear Phishing, Whaling, Vishing, Smishing, Pretexting, Baiting, Tailgating
- ✅ **Malware Types:** Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware, Spyware, Adware, Rootkit, Keylogger, Botnet, Fileless
- ✅ **Web Attacks:** SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF, Directory Traversal

### Key Formulas to Memorize

- ✅ $K = N^L$ (Keyspace)
- ✅ $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ (Entropy in bits for uniform password)
- ✅ $T_{avg} = \frac{N^L}{2R}$ (Average crack time)
- ✅ $H = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)$ (Shannon Entropy)
- ✅ $\text{Risk} = \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Availability}$

### Key Nmap Scans to Memorize

- ✅ SYN Scan (stealth) | NULL/FIN/Xmas (firewall evasion) | Connect (loud)

### Famous Real-World Incidents to Reference in Answers

- ✅ WannaCry (2017) — Ransomware via EternalBlue
- ✅ Stuxnet (2010) — Nation-state worm targeting Iran's nuclear program
- ✅ SolarWinds (2020) — Supply chain attack
- ✅ Mirai Botnet (2016) — IoT DDoS using default credentials
- ✅ Yahoo Breach (2013–2014) — 3 billion records compromised
- ✅ ILOVEYOU (2000) — Email worm causing $15B in damages

### Countermeasures to Mention in Every Answer

- ✅ Security awareness training (against social engineering)
- ✅ Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
- ✅ Firewalls, IDS/IPS, WAF
- ✅ Regular patching and updates
- ✅ Principle of least privilege
- ✅ Defense-in-depth strategy
- ✅ Encryption (in transit and at rest)
- ✅ Periodic penetration testing and red team exercises

### KTU 2024 Exam Tips

- ✅ Always structure long answers with **headings and bullet points**.
- ✅ Include at least **one diagram** for 14-mark questions (adds ~2 marks).
- ✅ Use **table comparisons** (Worm vs Virus, White Hat vs Black Hat) — examiners love them.
- ✅ End every attack explanation with **countermeasures**.
- ✅ Mention **real-world examples** for higher marks (e.g., "Like the WannaCry attack of 2017...").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attack Vectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Attack Vectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attack Vector (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> An *attack vector* is the **pathway, method, or channel** through which an adversary delivers a malicious payload, exploits a vulnerability, or gains unauthorized access to a target system, network, application, or human user. It represents the *convergence point* between a **threat actor's capability**, a **system's vulnerability**, and the **mechanism of exploitation** required to compromise the Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA triad) of an information asset.

The formal KTU taxonomy classifies attack vectors along **three orthogonal dimensions**:

1. **Technical Dimension** — Code- or protocol-mediated exploitation (e.g., buffer overflow, SQLi, malware).
2. **Human (Social Engineering) Dimension** — Cognitive and procedural manipulation of people (e.g., phishing, pretexting, baiting).
3. **Physical Dimension** — Tangible world contact with assets (e.g., USB drop, tailgating, shoulder surfing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms used in KTU Examinations:** *Threat Vector*, *Attack Pathway*, *Vector of Attack*, *Intrusion Vector*, *Cyber Kill Chain Stage 3 (Delivery)*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Break-In" Mental Model

Imagine your home is a **corporate network** with locked doors, windows, and a security alarm.

| 🏠 House Element | 🖥️ Cyber Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Front door (main entry) | Public web server / SSH port |
| Unlocked window | Misconfigured cloud storage (S3 bucket) |
| Tricked housekeeper | Social engineering (phishing) |
| Forged house key | Credential theft / password spray |
| Thief sneaking in with a delivery | Supply-chain attack (SolarWinds-style) |
| Burst of mail overwhelming mailbox | DDoS flood |
| Tail-gating behind a resident | Insider threat / physical piggybacking |

Just as a burglar will choose the **path of least resistance** (an open window rather than a reinforced door), a cyber adversary selects the **attack vector with the highest success probability and lowest detection risk**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Attack Vectors Matter in KTU Exams:** Every answer in Module 4 is anchored to the *threat × vulnerability × impact* triple. Recognizing the **vector** tells you *how* the attack reaches the system; recognizing the **vulnerability** tells you *what* is weak; recognizing the **impact** tells you *why* it matters. Master this triangle, and you master the module.

---

## 1.3 The Three Cardinal Properties of an Attack Vector

Every attack vector in the KTU 2024 scheme is evaluated against these three metrics, which are marked in **bold** because they recur in every Part B question:

- **Reachability** — Can the adversary practically reach the target via this channel?
- **Exploitability** — How easily can the channel be weaponized?
- **Detectability** — How likely are existing controls to flag the attempt?

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often confuse **Attack Vector** with **Threat** or **Vulnerability**.
> - *Threat* = who/what wants to harm you (the burglar).
> - *Vulnerability* = the weakness they will use (unlocked window).
> - *Attack Vector* = **the path/method used to deliver the exploit** (climbing the trellis). 
> All three together constitute *Risk*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Big Picture" — Anatomy of an Attack

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to articulate the **lifecycle of an attack** in a coherent, structured form. Every attack, regardless of vector, follows a **5-stage intellectual chain**:

### Stage 1 — Reconnaissance (Passive / Active)
The adversary gathers intelligence about the target — open ports, employee names on LinkedIn, DNS records, leaked credentials on the dark web.
- *Passive:* Google dorking, Shodan, Wayback Machine.
- *Active:* Nmap scans, banner grabbing, social engineering calls.

### Stage 2 — Weaponization
The adversary pairs a **vulnerability** with a **payload**. Classic examples: macro-laden Office document + CVE-2017-11882 exploit; PDF + embedded JavaScript; USB stick + autorun.inf.

### Stage 3 — Delivery (The Vector Itself)
The attack vector is *delivered* to the victim. This is the KTU module's centre of gravity.

### Stage 4 — Exploitation
The payload triggers the vulnerability. Privilege escalation, code execution, or credential disclosure follows.

### Stage 5 — Action on Objectives
Data exfiltration, ransomware encryption, lateral movement, persistence.

---

## 2.2 Comprehensive Taxonomy — The Master List

> [!NOTE]
> The following classification is the **definitive KTU 2024 reference** for Module 4. Memorize the bolded terms verbatim.

### A. Technical Attack Vectors

| # | Vector | Mechanism | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Malware (Virus/Worm/Trojan/Ransomware/Spyware/Rootkit)** | Self-propagating or dropper code | Endpoints, servers |
| 2 | **Phishing (Email)** | Deceptive messages with malicious links | Human users |
| 3 | **Spear Phishing / Whaling** | Targeted phishing of executives | C-suite, admins |
| 4 | **Drive-by Download** | Browser exploit via compromised site | Web users |
| 5 | **SQL Injection (SQLi)** | Malicious SQL in input fields | Databases |
| 6 | **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** | Injected JS in trusted pages | Web app users |
| 7 | **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** | Tricked authenticated state change | Web app users |
| 8 | **Remote Code Execution (RCE)** | Arbitrary command execution | Servers, IoT |
| 9 | **Buffer Overflow (Stack/Heap)** | Memory corruption → shellcode | Native binaries |
| 10 | **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** | Traffic interception / modification | LAN, Wi-Fi |
| 11 | **ARP Spoofing / DNS Poisoning** | L2/L3 redirection | Local networks |
| 12 | **DDoS / DoS** | Resource exhaustion | Web infrastructure |
| 13 | **Zero-Day Exploit** | Unpatched vulnerability | All systems |
| 14 | **Credential Stuffing** | Replay of leaked credentials | Web logins |
| 15 | **Password Spraying / Brute Force** | Low-and-slow credential guessing | Authentication systems |
| 16 | **Session Hijacking** | Theft of session cookies/tokens | Web apps |
| 17 | **Rogue Access Point / Evil Twin** | Spoofed wireless network | Mobile users |
| 18 | **Bluetooth / NFC Attacks (BlueBorne)** | Proximity wireless exploit | Mobile devices |
| 19 | **IoT Botnet Recruitment** | Default-credential compromise | Smart devices |
| 20 | **Cloud Misconfiguration Exploitation** | Public S3 / open security groups | Cloud assets |
| 21 | **API Abuse (BOLA/BFLA)** | Broken Object Level Authorization | REST APIs |
| 22 | **Container Escape** | Breaking out of Docker/Kubernetes | Cloud-native |
| 23 | **Supply-Chain Compromise** | Trojanized upstream dependency | Build pipeline |
| 24 | **Kernel / Firmware Implant** | Low-level persistence | Hardware |
| 25 | **Living-off-the-Land (LotL)** | Misuse of native tools (PowerShell, WMI) | Windows systems |

### B. Human / Social Engineering Vectors

| # | Vector | Psychological Lever |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Phishing** | Urgency / fear |
| 2 | **Spear Phishing** | Personalization / trust |
| 3 | **Vishing (Voice Phishing)** | Authority / voice urgency |
| 4 | **Smishing (SMS Phishing)** | Curiosity / mobile attention |
| 5 | **Pretexting** | Fabricated scenario |
| 6 | **Baiting** | Greed / curiosity |
| 7 | **Tailgating / Piggybacking** | Politeness / social conformity |
| 8 | **Quid Pro Quo** | Favors-for-access |
| 9 | **Honey Trap** | Romantic manipulation |
| 10 | **CEO Fraud / BEC** | Executive impersonation |

### C. Physical Attack Vectors

| # | Vector | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **USB Drop / BadUSB** | Auto-execution on pickup |
| 2 | **Shoulder Surfing** | Visual credential capture |
| 3 | **Skimming (ATM)** | Card reader overlay |
| 4 | **Hardware Keylogger** | Inline keyboard capture |
| 5 | **Tampering / Device Theft** | Direct physical access |
| 6 | **EM / Side-Channel Eavesdropping** | Van Eck phreaking, TEMPEST |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **only quantitative formulas** the KTU 2024 scheme expects you to apply in Module 4 numerical/analytical questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Classic risk triple | Each ∈ [0, 1] | Quantitative risk assessment |
| 2 | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Single Loss Expectancy | Asset Value in ₹, EF ∈ [0,1] | Insurance / loss modelling |
| 3 | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | ARO = events/year | Budget justification |
| 4 | $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}$ | Return on Security Investment | Ratio (dimensionless) | Security ROI pitch |
| 5 | $\text{Attack Surface} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_i \times A_i \times V_i)$ | Cumulative exposure | E=entry points, A=accessibility, V=value | Architecture hardening |
| 6 | $\text{ISS} = 1 - (1-C)(1-I)(1-A)$ | CVSS Impact Sub-Score (v3.1) | C, I, A ∈ {0, 0.22, 0.56} | Vulnerability scoring |
| 7 | $\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS}$ (Scope Unchanged) | CVSS Impact | ISS from above | CVSS computation |
| 8 | $\text{Impact} = 7.52 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.02)^{15}$ (Scope Changed) | CVSS Impact (changed scope) | ISS from above | CVSS computation |
| 9 | $\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}$ | CVSS Exploitability | Metric weights | CVSS computation |
| 10 | $\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10)\bigr)$ | Final CVSS v3.1 | If Impact ≤ 0, score = 0 | Severity triage |
| 11 | $P(\text{compromise}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)$ | Compromise probability (independent vectors) | p_i = vector i success probability | Red-team modelling |
| 12 | $\text{MTTC} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \text{ (Exponential model)}$ | Mean Time to Compromise | λ = attacker skill × exposure | Threat modelling |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of a Security Analyst's risk report**. In your career, RO SI will justify your firewall purchase to management; CVSS will triage your vulnerability queue; Attack-Surface will guide your architecture review.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application

In practice, every enterprise has a **Vector Inventory** maintained in a GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance) tool. The goal of a Cyber Security engineer is to *reduce the Attack Surface* by:

1. **Eliminating** unreachable attack vectors (close unused ports, decommission legacy apps).
2. **Hardening** reachable vectors (patching, MFA, WAF rules).
3. **Detecting** active exploitation (SIEM, EDR, NDR, UEBA).
4. **Responding** to successful breaches (SOAR playbooks, IR retainers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The two derivations below are the *exact* numerical style KTU 2024 ESE expects in 14-mark Part B questions.** Practice writing them out by hand before the exam.

---

## 3.1 Derivation A — CVSS v3.1 Base Score for a Real Attack Vector

**Scenario:** A vulnerability analyst at Infosys has flagged an **SQL Injection** flaw in the company's customer portal. The analyst assigns the following CVSS v3.1 metrics:

- Attack Vector (AV) = **Network (N)** $\rightarrow$ weight $= 0.85$
- Attack Complexity (AC) = **Low (L)** $\rightarrow$ weight $= 0.77$
- Privileges Required (PR) = **None (N)** $\rightarrow$ weight $= 0.85$
- User Interaction (UI) = **None (N)** $\rightarrow$ weight $= 0.85$
- Confidentiality (C) = **High (H)** $\rightarrow$ weight $= 0.56$
- Integrity (I) = **High (H)** $\rightarrow$ weight $= 0.56$
- Availability (A) = **None (N)** $\rightarrow$ weight $= 0.00$
- Scope (S) = **Unchanged (U)**

We must compute the **CVSS Base Score** to determine severity.

### Step 1 — Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - (1 - C)(1 - I)(1 - A)
$$

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - (1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.00)
$$

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - (0.44)(0.44)(1.00)
$$

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - 0.1936 = 0.8064
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Impact value (Scope Unchanged branch)

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS}
$$

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.8064
$$

$$
\text{Impact} = 5.177088 \approx 5.18
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Exploitability

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
$$

Multiplying left-to-right:

$$
8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987
$$

$$
6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.37999
$$

$$
5.37999 \times 0.85 = 4.57299
$$

$$
4.57299 \times 0.85 = 3.88704
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} \approx 3.89
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Base Score

Since $\text{Impact} = 5.18 > 0$, the score is non-zero:

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10)\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min(5.18 + 3.89,\ 10)\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min(9.07,\ 10)\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}(9.07) = 9.1
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Severity

| CVSS Range | Severity Label |
|---|---|
| 0.0 | None |
| 0.1 – 3.9 | Low |
| 4.0 – 6.9 | Medium |
| 7.0 – 8.9 | High |
| **9.0 – 10.0** | **Critical** |

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** $\text{BaseScore} = 9.1 \Rightarrow$ **CRITICAL** severity. Patch within 24 hours. This is the most common KTU Part B mark-allocating calculation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) and ROSI

**Scenario:** A Kochi-based BPO firm holds customer PII valued at **₹50,00,000**. The CISO estimates a **phishing-induced data breach** would expose **80%** of this asset, and that the probability of such an incident is **once every 2 years**. Implementing an **email-gateway anti-phishing solution** costs **₹5,00,000** per year, and reduces the breach probability to **once every 10 years**. Compute the **ALEs** before and after, and the **ROSI**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 40{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute ALE Before Control (ARO = 0.5)

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}}
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 20{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR/yr}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute ALE After Control (ARO = 0.1)

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = 4{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR/yr}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Annual Savings (Risk Reduction)

$$
\Delta\text{ALE} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR/yr}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

$$
\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\Delta\text{ALE} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{16{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{11{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 2.2
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\text{ROSI} = 220\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** For every ₹1 spent on the anti-phishing gateway, the firm saves ₹2.20 in avoided risk. **Strong business case — deploy immediately.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Aggregate Compromise Probability Across Multiple Vectors

**Scenario:** An air-gapped SCADA network is exposed to four independent attack vectors, each with the following annual compromise probability: Phishing (0.05), USB drop (0.02), Insider (0.03), Supply-chain (0.01). Compute the **aggregate annual compromise probability** assuming independence.

$$
P(\text{compromise}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)
$$

$$
P = 1 - (1 - 0.05)(1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.03)(1 - 0.01)
$$

$$
P = 1 - (0.95)(0.98)(0.97)(0.99)
$$

$$
P = 1 - 0.8945 \approx 0.1055
$$

$$
P \approx 10.55\%\ \text{per year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often *add* the probabilities (0.11) which is mathematically incorrect when events are independent — the correct operation is the **complement product**. This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss if forgotten.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Top 10 Attack Vectors

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **single most useful revision artefact** for KTU 2024 Module 4. Cover every cell in your head before the exam.

| Vector | CIA Pillar Breached | Attack Surface | Detectability | Common Mitigation | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phishing** | C, I | Email gateway | Medium | DMARC, FIDO2, training | 2016 DNC hack |
| **SQL Injection** | C, I | Web app | Low–Medium | Parameterized queries, WAF | 2017 Equifax |
| **Ransomware** | A, I (C if leak) | Endpoint | High | Backups, EDR, segmentation | 2021 Colonial Pipeline |
| **DDoS** | A | Network edge | High | Anycast, scrubbing, rate limit | 2016 Dyn/Mirai |
| **Zero-Day** | C, I, A | OS / App | Very Low | Virtual patching, Zero Trust | 2021 ProxyLogon |
| **Insider Threat** | C, I, A | Internal | Low | DLP, UEBA, least privilege | Snowden 2013 |
| **Supply-Chain** | C, I, A | Build pipeline | Very Low | SBOM, code signing | 2020 SolarWinds |
| **MITM** | C, I | LAN / Wi-Fi | Medium | TLS 1.3, HSTS, cert pinning | 2015 Lenovo Superfish |
| **Credential Stuffing** | C | Auth portal | Medium | MFA, rate limit, breach feeds | 2018 Dunkin' Donuts |
| **XSS** | C, I, A (deface) | Web app | Low | Output encoding, CSP | 2018 British Airways |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Mapping a Vector to the Kill Chain

Consider the **2017 Equifax breach** (Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638):

1. **Reconnaissance:** Shodan query for `struts2` banner.
2. **Weaponization:** Public exploit code on Metasploit.
3. **Delivery (Vector):** Internet-facing web server (technical vector, unpatched software).
4. **Exploitation:** RCE via Content-Type header.
5. **Installation:** Web-shell dropped.
6. **C2:** Outbound HTTPS to attacker C2.
7. **Action on Objectives:** Lateral movement → 147 million PII records exfiltrated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The vector was *technical* (unpatched web app), but the root cause was *procedural* (failure to apply a known patch).** This is why KTU examiners love asking "identify the vector AND the control that should have prevented it."

# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ✅ Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define *Attack Vector*. Classify attack vectors into three primary categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An *Attack Vector* is the path or method used by a threat actor to gain unauthorized access to a system or deliver a malicious payload, exploiting a specific vulnerability to compromise Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA).

The three primary categories are:

1. **Technical Vector** — Exploits software, hardware, or network protocols. *Example:* SQL Injection against a web application.
2. **Human / Social Engineering Vector** — Manipulates human psychology. *Example:* Phishing email that tricks a user into revealing credentials.
3. **Physical Vector** — Requires tangible world access. *Example:* USB drop attack in a corporate parking lot.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for classification with examples.)*

---

### ✅ Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between *Phishing* and *Spear Phishing*. Mention one mitigation control for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Phishing | Spear Phishing |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Broad, mass-mailing | Specific individual or group |
| Personalization | Generic content | Uses OSINT (name, role, project) |
| Volume | Thousands of emails | Few, hand-crafted emails |
| Success Rate | Low | High (up to 4× higher click rate) |
| Example | "Your bank account is locked" | "Hi Asha, please review the attached Q3 forecast" |

**Mitigations:**
- Phishing: **DMARC, DKIM, SPF** email authentication + spam filtering.
- Spear Phishing: **Security awareness training**, FIDO2 hardware keys, **out-of-band verification** for financial requests.

*(1.5 marks for difference, 1.5 marks for mitigations.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE provides an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. You must answer **ONE**. Practice both alternatives below.

---

### ✅ Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Cyber Kill Chain** proposed by Lockheed Martin, identifying which stage represents the *Attack Vector*. List **five technical attack vectors** with real-world examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vulnerability in an e-commerce server has the following CVSS v3.1 metrics:  
`AV:N / AC:L / PR:N / UI:N / C:H / I:H / A:N / S:U`  
Compute the **Base Score**, and state the **severity rating**. Show all steps. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

**Cyber Kill Chain Stages (5 marks):**

1. **Reconnaissance** — Adversary gathers intel (Shodan, LinkedIn).
2. **Weaponization** — Pairs a CVE exploit with a payload (e.g., malicious macro).
3. **Delivery** — **THIS IS THE ATTACK VECTOR.** Email, USB, web, etc.
4. **Exploitation** — Payload triggers the flaw.
5. **Installation** — Malware persists on the host.
6. **Command and Control (C2)** — Beacons back to attacker infrastructure.
7. **Actions on Objectives** — Data theft, encryption, sabotage.

**Five Technical Attack Vectors (2 marks — 0.4 each):**

1. **SQL Injection** — Example: 2017 Equifax breach (Struts CVE-2017-5638 / SQLi variant).
2. **Phishing Email** — Example: 2016 DNC email leak.
3. **Drive-by Download** — Example: 2017 XcodeGhost via infected IDE.
4. **Ransomware** — Example: 2017 WannaCry (EternalBlue exploit).
5. **Supply Chain** — Example: 2020 SolarWinds Orion backdoor.

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

Metric values:  
AV = 0.85, AC = 0.77, PR = 0.85, UI = 0.85, C = 0.56, I = 0.56, A = 0.00, Scope = Unchanged.

**Step 1 — ISS:**

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - (1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0) = 1 - 0.44 \times 0.44 = 0.8064
$$

**Step 2 — Impact (Scope Unchanged):**

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.8064 = 5.18
$$

**Step 3 — Exploitability:**

$$
\text{Expl.} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 = 3.89
$$

**Step 4 — Base Score:**

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}(\min(5.18 + 3.89, 10)) = \text{roundUp}(9.07) = 9.1
$$

**Severity Rating:** **CRITICAL (9.0 – 10.0 range).** Patch within 24 hours.

*(1 mark ISS, 2 marks Impact, 2 marks Exploitability, 1 mark BaseScore, 1 mark Severity label.)*

---

### ✅ Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss **social engineering** as an attack vector. Explain the **six principles of influence** (Cialdini) that attackers exploit. Give an example for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A healthcare firm in Thiruvananthapuram holds patient records valued at ₹80,00,000. A ransomware attack would expose 75% of the asset, expected to occur **3 times per year**. The firm is considering a **backup + EDR solution costing ₹6,00,000 per year** that would reduce the occurrence to **once every 5 years**. Compute the **ALEs before and after**, the **annual savings**, and the **ROSI**. Recommend whether to deploy. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Social Engineering (2 marks):** A non-technical attack vector that manipulates human psychology to bypass technical controls. It is the *most successful* initial-access vector in 70%+ of breaches (Verizon DBIR 2023).

**Cialdini's Six Principles of Influence (5 marks — 0.8 each for principle + example):**

1. **Reciprocity** — *"I sent you a gift card, now send me the file."* Free USB drives dropped in lobby.
2. **Commitment & Consistency** — Phishing that begins with a tiny "yes" and escalates.
3. **Social Proof** — "Hi, your colleague John already approved this invoice." Imitates prior behaviour.
4. **Authority** — Email impersonating CEO, calls from "Microsoft support."
5. **Liking** — Attacker builds rapport over LinkedIn before sending malicious link.
6. **Scarcity / Urgency** — "Your account will be closed in 1 hour — click now."

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — SLE (1 mark):**

$$
\text{SLE} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 = 60{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 2 — ALE Before (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — ALE After (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2 = 12{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Savings (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta\text{ALE} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}68{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 5 — ROSI (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{1{,}68{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = \frac{1{,}62{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 27
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = 2700\%
$$

**Step 6 — Recommendation (1 mark):** **Deploy immediately.** For every ₹1 invested, the firm saves ₹27 in avoided risk. The control pays for itself 27-fold.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Attack Vector questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Threat, Vulnerability, and Vector.** If the question asks for the *vector*, do not answer with a *vulnerability*. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 2. **Skipping CVSS derivation steps.** Examiners award step marks. Writing only the final answer gets 1 mark; showing all four steps gets 7. (Loss: up to 6 marks)
> 3. **Wrong rounding in CVSS.** Use `roundUp` to **1 decimal place**, not `round` or `floor`. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting the Scope branch.** If Scope = Changed, the Impact formula is the *15th-power* equation, not `6.42 × ISS`. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 5. **Conflating ROSI sign convention.** Some textbooks subtract Cost from Savings, others from ALE. KTU 2024 follows: $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\Delta\text{ALE} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}}$.
> 6. **Writing the answer as a single sentence.** Use **bullet points, headings, and sub-steps** to make valuation easy.
> 7. **Forgetting units** in ALE/SLE (always write **INR/yr** or **₹/year**). (Loss: 0.5 mark)
> 8. **Not drawing the kill-chain diagram** when explicitly asked. (Loss: 2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — go through this in the last 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** Attack Vector = *How* the attack is delivered. Distinct from Threat (*Who*) and Vulnerability (*What*).
- [ ] **Three Categories:** Technical, Human (Social Engineering), Physical.
- [ ] **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** has 7 stages. **Stage 3 — Delivery — IS the attack vector.**
- [ ] **MITRE ATT&CK** has 14 enterprise tactics. Initial Access (TA0001) is where most vectors begin.
- [ ] **CVSS v3.1 Base Score formula** is a 4-step calculation: ISS → Impact → Exploitability → BaseScore.
- [ ] **CVSS Metric weights to memorize:** AV (N=0.85), AC (L=0.77), PR (N=0.85), UI (N=0.85), C/I/A (H=0.56).
- [ ] **Severity Bands:** 0.1–3.9 Low, 4.0–6.9 Medium, 7.0–8.9 High, 9.0–10.0 Critical.
- [ ] **ALE = SLE × ARO.** ROSI = (ΔALE − Cost) / Cost. Always show units.
- [ ] **Compromise probability** across independent vectors uses the **complement product** formula: $1 - \prod (1 - p_i)$.
- [ ] **Cialdini's 6 principles** of social engineering: Reciprocity, Commitment, Social Proof, Authority, Liking, Scarcity.
- [ ] **Top 10 vectors** (Phishing, SQLi, Ransomware, DDoS, Zero-Day, Insider, Supply-Chain, MITM, Credential Stuffing, XSS) — know the *real-world breach* example for each.
- [ ] **DMARC / DKIM / SPF** defeat email-borne vectors.
- [ ] **FIDO2 / Hardware keys** defeat credential phishing.
- [ ] **Patching + Virtual Patching + WAF** defeat technical exploit vectors.
- [ ] **MFA + Least Privilege + Segmentation** defeat lateral-movement vectors.
- [ ] **Always draw a diagram** for 7-mark sub-questions on the Kill Chain or MITRE ATT&CK.

---

### 🔚 End of Module 4 — Attack Vectors Notes
