```mermaid
    D2B --> D3B
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define cyberspace. Mention any two realms of the cyber world with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Cyberspace, as defined by the U.S. Department of Defense and adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus, is *"the notional environment in which digitized information is communicated over computer networks."* It is a globally distributed, interactive domain comprising the Internet, intranets, computer systems, and telecommunications networks.
>
> Two realms of the cyber world:
> 1. **Personal Realm** — Example: An individual's Gmail account, used for personal communication and storage. The primary threat is phishing and identity theft.
> 2. **Organizational / Enterprise Realm** — Example: A company's internal intranet hosting employee records and CRM. The primary threat is insider threat and Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs).
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks each for the two realms with example — total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between the Surface Web, Deep Web, and Dark Web.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Surface Web | Deep Web | Dark Web |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indexing | Indexed by search engines | Not indexed | Not indexed + deliberately hidden |
| Accessibility | Via standard browsers | Via login / authenticated access | Only via Tor / I2P |
| Content | Public websites, news, blogs | Email, banking, academic DBs | Illegal markets, whistleblower sites |
| Approx. size | 4-5% of Internet | 90-95% of Internet | A small subset of Deep Web |

*(1 mark per row, 3 rows = 3 marks. Alternatively, 1 mark for definition of each + 0.5 mark for the key example = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> The most common mistake students make in the 14-mark *"Realms of the Cyber World"* question is to **merely list** the realms without (a) explaining the **layered architecture** (physical / logical / social), (b) showing a **comparative table** with threats, and (c) discussing the **digital divide** or **realm-crossing** as an integrative conclusion. A pure list will fetch at most 7-8 marks out of 14.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Option 1*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023-style]**

**(a)** *Define the concept of Cyberspace. Explain in detail the major realms of the Cyber World, with suitable examples and the primary threats faced in each realm.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the three-layer architecture (Physical, Logical, Social) of Cyberspace. How does the C-I-A (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) triad apply differently across the Personal, Financial, and Government realms?* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition of Cyberspace (1.5 marks):**
Cyberspace is the global, interactive, and notional environment in which digital information is created, exchanged, and processed across interconnected computer networks. The term was coined by **William Gibson** in his 1984 novel *Neuromancer*, but it has since evolved into a formal technical and legal concept. It is *borderless, persistent, and decentralized* — three properties that distinguish it from the physical world.

**Major Realms of the Cyber World (5 marks — 0.5 mark per realm + 0.5 mark for example + 0.5 mark for threat, with 1 mark for a concluding statement):**

1. **Personal Realm** — Examples: personal email, social media profile, digital wallet. *Threats: phishing, identity theft.*
2. **Social / Community Realm** — Examples: Facebook groups, Discord servers, Reddit. *Threats: cyberbullying, misinformation, deepfakes.*
3. **Organizational / Enterprise Realm** — Examples: corporate intranets, ERP systems, internal email. *Threats: insider threats, ransomware, APTs.*
4. **Government / E-Governance Realm** — Examples: Aadhaar, DigiLocker, GST portal, defense networks. *Threats: nation-state attacks, defacement, espionage.*
5. **Financial / E-Commerce Realm** — Examples: net-banking, UPI apps, crypto exchanges. *Threats: UPI fraud, card skimming, money laundering.*
6. **Cloud and IoT Realm** — Examples: AWS, smart home devices, IIoT. *Threats: misconfigured buckets, botnets (Mirai), supply-chain attacks.*
7. **Dark Web / Underground Realm** — Examples: Tor .onion markets, RaaS platforms. *Threats: illegal trade, data dumps.*
8. **Virtual / Metaverse Realm** — Examples: Meta Horizon, VR chat worlds. *Threats: avatar theft, VR phishing.*

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** A modern user simultaneously inhabits multiple realms, making *integrated, cross-realm cyber security* essential.

---

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Three-Layer Architecture (3.5 marks):**

1. **Physical Layer:** The tangible hardware — servers, routers, fiber-optic cables, satellites, smartphones, IoT sensors. *Example:* Airtel's undersea cable connecting Kerala to Singapore.
2. **Logical Layer:** The software and protocols that make communication possible — TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS, operating systems, applications. *Example:* When you type `https://www.ktu.ac.in`, DNS resolves the domain to an IP, and HTTPS encrypts the session.
3. **Social Layer:** The human and policy layer — users, their behavior, ethics, cyber laws, organizational policies. *Example:* India's IT Act 2000 and the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023.

**C-I-A Triad Applied Differently (3.5 marks):**

| Realm | Dominant C-I-A Priority | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal** | **Confidentiality** | Privacy of personal messages, photos, and identity is paramount. A breach leads to identity theft and personal harm. |
| **Financial** | **Confidentiality + Integrity** | Both secrecy of card data *and* accuracy of transaction records are critical. A tampered transaction record causes real monetary loss. |
| **Government** | **Integrity** | Integrity of electoral rolls, tax records, and sovereign data is paramount. A corrupted database (even if available and confidential) breaks public trust. |

**Concluding statement (built into the table):** While the C-I-A triad is universal, the *priority* of each pillar shifts based on the realm's purpose, threat model, and legal obligations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Option 2*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024-style]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of the Digital Divide. Discuss its three levels and its impact on the equitable access to the Realms of the Cyber World.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *What is the Dark Web? Explain how it operates, the technology behind it (Tor/I2P), and discuss whether it is entirely illegal. Provide two legitimate use cases.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Concept of Digital Divide (1 mark):**
The **digital divide** refers to the *gap between individuals, households, businesses, and geographic regions* with respect to their **access to, use of, or benefit from Information and Communication Technologies (ICTs)**. It is a multi-dimensional inequality that affects participation in the digital economy, education, and democratic processes.

**Three Levels (4.5 marks):**

1. **First-Level Divide — Access Divide (1.5 marks):** This is the most basic divide — *do you have a device (smartphone/computer) and a working internet connection?* A fisherman in coastal Alappuzha with a basic feature phone is in a different category from a software engineer in Infopark, Kochi, with a 5G connection and a laptop. *KTU connection:* This divide is being bridged in Kerala through initiatives like **Kerala Fibre Optic Network (KFON)**, which aims to provide free internet to BPL families.

2. **Second-Level Divide — Usage Divide (1.5 marks):** Even with access, *do you have the skills to use technology meaningfully?* A person may have a smartphone but only use it for WhatsApp. They cannot, for example, file a GST return, attend a video lecture, or apply for a government job online. This *digital literacy* gap is the second-level divide.

3. **Third-Level Divide — Outcomes Divide (1.5 marks):** The *outcomes* divide asks: *do you reap tangible benefits from technology?* A rural student with internet access and digital skills can apply for scholarships, access MOOCs (NPTEL, SWAYAM), and improve employment prospects. A person lacking either access or skills cannot. The *outcomes* are the ultimate measure of digital equity.

**Impact on the Realms of the Cyber World (1.5 marks):** The digital divide determines *which realms a person can inhabit*. A digitally excluded person exists only in (at most) a narrow Personal realm, with no participation in E-Governance, Financial, or Cloud realms — denying them access to subsidies, banking, and online education. This *widens economic and social inequality*, making digital divide a *cybersecurity and human rights* issue, not just a technology issue.

---

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Definition of Dark Web (1 mark):**
The **Dark Web** is a small, deliberately hidden subset of the Deep Web that is accessible only through specialized software like **Tor (The Onion Router)**, **I2P (Invisible Internet Project)**, or **Freenet**. It is not indexed by standard search engines, and user identities / server locations are anonymized through multiple layers of encryption.

**How Tor Works — Technology Behind Anonymity (3 marks):**

When a user accesses a `.onion` website via Tor, their request is encrypted in **three layers** (hence "onion") and routed through **three randomly selected relay nodes**:

1. **Guard / Entry Node:** Knows the user's IP address, but not the destination.
2. **Middle Relay:** Knows neither the source nor the destination, only the previous and next hop.
3. **Exit Node:** Knows the destination, but not the original source IP.

Each node can only decrypt **one layer**, so no single node has the full picture. The user's IP is hidden from the destination, and the destination's IP is hidden from the user. This is the core *anonymity guarantee* of Tor.

**I2P and Freenet (1 mark):** I2P is similar to Tor but optimized for *hidden services* (services inside the network), while Freenet focuses on *censorship-resistant distributed storage*.

**Is the Dark Web Entirely Illegal? (1 mark):** **No.** While it has a notorious reputation due to illegal marketplaces (Silk Road successor markets, ransomware-as-a-service portals), the Dark Web itself is *not illegal technology*. Accessing Tor is legal in most jurisdictions, including India.

**Two Legitimate Use Cases (1 mark):**

1. **Whistleblowing and Journalism:** Platforms like *SecureDrop* (used by *The New York Times*, *The Guardian*) allow whistleblowers (e.g., in a corrupt organization) to share documents with journalists without revealing their identity — critical for press freedom in authoritarian regimes.
2. **Privacy and Circumventing Censorship:** Citizens in countries with heavy internet censorship (e.g., journalists in Iran or China) use Tor to access the open Internet and communicate safely with the outside world. Human rights organizations like the *Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF)* actively promote Tor for this purpose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concluding Note for Examiner:** The *technology* (Tor) is neutral; it is the *application* that determines legality — a principle analogous to "the knife is not evil, but its use can be."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullets for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [ ] **Cyberspace** = notional, global, interactive digital environment (Gibson coined the term, 1984).
- [ ] **Realms** are 8 in number as per the KTU 2024 syllabus: Personal, Social, Organizational, Government, Financial, Cloud/IoT, Dark Web, Virtual/Metaverse.
- [ ] **Three-Layer Architecture** of cyberspace: **Physical → Logical → Social** (memorize this sequence).
- [ ] **C-I-A Triad** = **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** — universal across realms, but *priorities differ* by realm.
- [ ] **Surface Web** = ~4-5% of Internet, indexed by Google.
- [ ] **Deep Web** = ~90-95%, *not indexed* (email, banking portals, academic DBs).
- [ ] **Dark Web** = a small *subset* of Deep Web, accessible via Tor/I2P/Freenet.
- [ ] **Tor** = The Onion Router; uses 3-layer encryption across 3 relay nodes (Guard, Middle, Exit).
- [ ] **Dark Web is not entirely illegal** — used by whistleblowers, journalists, and citizens under censorship.
- [ ] **Digital Divide** has 3 levels: **Access → Usage → Outcomes** (remember this A-U-O sequence).
- [ ] **DPDP Act 2023** = India's modern data protection law (replaces portions of IT Act 2000 for personal data).
- [ ] **IT Act 2000** = India's primary cyber law; Section 66A was *struck down* in *Shreya Singhal v. UoI* (2015) — a frequently asked legal trivia point.
- [ ] **CERT-In** = Indian Computer Emergency Response Team; its 2022 directives mandate 6-hour incident reporting by companies.
- [ ] **Realm-crossing** is the modern reality — a single attack can touch 3+ realms (e.g., phishing on social → financial theft → laundering on dark web).
- [ ] **Defense-in-depth** is the recommended security strategy: Perimeter → Network → Host → Data → Application → Social (people).
- [ ] **IoT realm** is special because devices are often *unpatchable* and ship with default credentials — making them perfect botnet recruits.
- [ ] **Metaverse / Virtual realm** introduces new attack vectors: avatar theft, VR phishing, motion-sickness DoS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a 14-mark question on this topic appears, structure your answer in **3 parts**: (1) **Definition + Layers**, (2) **Realm-by-Realm Table with Examples and Threats**, (3) **Integrative Conclusion** (Digital Divide, Realm-Crossing, or C-I-A Priority Shifts). This structure consistently scores 12+/14.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** A Brief History of the Internet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Internet (KTU 2024 Definition):** The *Internet* is a global, decentralized, packet-switched network of networks that uses the standardized **Internet Protocol Suite (TCP/IP)** to interconnect billions of computing devices worldwide. It is an *internetwork* — a meta-network formed by the autonomous interconnection of thousands of smaller, independently administered networks using a common addressing and routing schema.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT864 – Cyber Security)**, understanding the Internet's lineage is not merely historical curiosity — it is the foundation for grasping *why* modern vulnerabilities (like BGP hijacking, IP spoofing, DNS poisoning) exist. Every weakness in today's cyber landscape traces its origin to design decisions made during specific historical phases.

## 1.2 The Four Generations of the Internet (KTU Categorization)

> [!NOTE]  
> For exam purposes, the Internet's evolution is typically categorized into **four distinct generations**, each characterized by its dominant protocol, application set, and security posture.

| Generation | Era | Defining Characteristic | Dominant Protocol |
|------------|-----|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Web 1.0 (Read-Only)** | 1991 – 2003 | Static HTML pages, one-way information flow | HTTP/1.0, IPv4 |
| **Web 2.0 (Read-Write)** | 2004 – 2015 | User-generated content, social media | HTTP/1.1, REST APIs |
| **Web 3.0 (Semantic)** | 2016 – Present | Decentralization, blockchain, AI agents | HTTP/2, QUIC, IPv6 |
| **Web 4.0 (Symbiotic)** | Emerging | IoT, brain-computer interfaces, ambient computing | IPv6, 6LoWPAN, MQTT |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]  
> **Analogy — The Internet as a Global Postal System:**  
> Imagine the Internet as a worldwide postal service.  
> - **Your computer** = a house with a unique postal address (**IP address**).  
> - **Packets** = letters you send, broken into smaller envelopes if they are too large (**packet switching**).  
> - **Routers** = post offices that read the *destination address* on each envelope and forward it toward the next post office closer to the recipient.  
> - **TCP/IP** = the universally agreed-upon rulebook that all post offices follow so that letters can traverse any country.  
> - **DNS** = the phonebook that maps easy-to-remember names (like `google.com`) to postal addresses (like `142.250.190.46`).  
> - **The Internet itself** = the entire global postal infrastructure, owned by no single government but operated by countless cooperating agencies.

This analogy explains *why* the Internet is decentralized (no single owner), *how* data moves (small packets through routers), and *why* security is hard (every "post office" along the way can theoretically open your envelope — unless you **encrypt** it).

## 1.4 Key Foundational Metrics & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following standards and constants define the Internet's operational envelope. **Bolded terms are must-know for KTU examinations.**

- **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** — RFC 793, standardized in **1981**.  
- **IP (Internet Protocol)** — IPv4 standardized via **RFC 791** (1981), IPv6 via **RFC 8200** (2017).  
- **Port Number Range** — **0 to 65535** (16-bit unsigned integer).  
- **IPv4 Address Space** — $2^{32} =$ **4,294,967,296** unique addresses.  
- **IPv6 Address Space** — $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ unique addresses.  
- **Maximum IPv4 Packet Size** — **65,535 bytes** (16-bit length field).  
- **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)** — typically **1,500 bytes** on Ethernet.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes

## 2.1 The Evolutionary Phases — Detailed Breakdown

> [!NOTE]  
> The Internet did not emerge overnight. Its development followed **five major evolutionary phases**, each solving a critical bottleneck of the previous phase. From a KTU 2024 perspective, each phase also introduced a corresponding *new attack surface*, which is why cyber security as a discipline exists.

### Phase 1 — The Cold War Catalyst (1957 – 1969)

- **1957** — The USSR launches **Sputnik**, prompting the US to create the **Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA)** under the Department of Defense.
- **1962** — J.C.R. Licklider of MIT proposes the **"Intergalactic Computer Network"** concept — a globally interconnected set of computers.
- **1969** — The **ARPANET** goes live with **four nodes**: UCLA, Stanford Research Institute, UC Santa Barbara, and the University of Utah. The first message ever sent was the word **"LOGIN"** — and the system crashed after transmitting **"LO"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Cyber Security Implication:** ARPANET was designed for *resilience* against nuclear attack through **packet switching** and **distributed routing**. However, it assumed all users were trusted academics — there was **zero authentication or encryption**, a foundational vulnerability that persists today.

### Phase 2 — The Protocol Wars (1970 – 1983)

- **1970** — **Network Control Protocol (NCP)** becomes ARPANET's first host-to-host protocol.
- **1974** — Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn publish *"A Protocol for Packet Network Intercommunication"* — the blueprint for **TCP/IP**.
- **1983** — **January 1st** is flagged as *"Flag Day"*: ARPANET switches from NCP to **TCP/IP**, marking the birth of the modern Internet.

> [!TIP]  
> **Why TCP/IP Won:** TCP/IP's *datagram* design was robust, vendor-neutral, and stateless at the network layer — qualities NCP lacked. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

### Phase 3 — Democratization & The World Wide Web (1983 – 1995)

- **1983** — **Domain Name System (DNS)** is introduced by Paul Mockapetris (RFC 882, RFC 883).
- **1989** — Tim Berners-Lee at CERN proposes the **World Wide Web**.
- **1991** — The **first website** goes live at `http://info.cern.ch`.
- **1993** — The **Mosaic browser** (later Netscape) brings graphical browsing to the masses.
- **1995** — Commercial ISPs begin offering consumer dial-up access; **NSFNet** backbone is decommissioned.

### Phase 4 — The Commercial Explosion (1995 – 2007)

- **1998** — **Google** launches, transforming how information is indexed.
- **2004** — **Web 2.0** emerges: Facebook, Gmail, YouTube, Wikipedia.
- **2007** — Apple launches the **iPhone**, catalyzing the mobile Internet era.

### Phase 5 — Cloud, Mobile, and the IoT Surge (2007 – Present)

- **2010s** — Cloud computing (AWS, Azure, GCP) becomes mainstream; **Software as a Service (SaaS)** dominates enterprise IT.
- **2015+** — IoT devices explode in number; **IPv6** adoption accelerates due to IPv4 exhaustion.
- **2020s** — Remote work normalizes post-COVID; **Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)** becomes the de facto security framework.
- **2023+** — Generative AI tools become primary attack reconnaissance platforms.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following table consolidates every formula, constant, and key date you must memorize for KTU PEECT864 Module 4.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Engineering Unit | Cyber Security Significance |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|------------------------------|
| IPv4 address count | $N_{v4} = 2^{32}$ | **4,294,967,296** addresses | IPv4 exhaustion occurred Feb 2011 (IANA) |
| IPv6 address count | $N_{v6} = 2^{128}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | Effectively unlimited |
| Header overhead (IPv4) | $H_{v4} = 20$ bytes (min) | Bytes | Bandwidth tax per packet |
| Header overhead (IPv6) | $H_{v6} = 40$ bytes (fixed) | Bytes | Larger headers, better extensibility |
| Max IPv4 packet size | $P_{max} = 2^{16} - 1$ | **65,535 bytes** | Limits single-payload transmission |
| Standard Ethernet MTU | $MTU_{eth} = 1500$ | Bytes | Forces fragmentation beyond this |
| Fragment offset units | $8$ bytes per unit | Bytes | Determines reassembly position |
| ARPANET launch year | **1969** | Year | 4-node initial deployment |
| TCP/IP standardization | **1983** (RFC 791/793) | Year | Flag Day transition |
| WWW invention | **1989** (CERN) | Year | Berners-Lee's proposal |
| Public launch of WWW | **1991** | Year | `info.cern.ch` goes live |
| IPv4 exhaustion (IANA) | **February 3, 2011** | Date | Last /8 blocks allocated |

> [!TIP]  
> **Pipeline Latency Formula (frequently tested):** The *Round-Trip Time* (RTT) for a packet traversing $n$ hops each of distance $d_i$ at propagation speed $v$ is:
>
> $$RTT = 2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{d_i}{v} + 2 \cdot \frac{L}{B} + T_{queue}$$
>
> Where $L$ is payload size, $B$ is bandwidth, and $T_{queue}$ is queueing delay.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Conceptual Derivations

> [!NOTE]  
> Since "A Brief History of the Internet" is a historical-conceptual topic rather than a purely mathematical one, the *derivations* below are **logical-analytical reconstructions** of the key design decisions that shaped the Internet. Each step explicitly justifies *why* a particular architectural choice was made.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Why Packet Switching Replaced Circuit Switching

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** because it explains the foundational trade-off of all data networking.

### Step 1 — Define the Two Paradigms

**Circuit Switching (used in traditional telephone networks):**
A dedicated physical path is established between sender and receiver for the entire duration of the call. The path is reserved even during silence.

**Packet Switching (used in the Internet):**
Data is broken into small, independently routed packets. No dedicated path is reserved; packets share network resources statistically.

### Step 2 — Define Utilization Efficiency

Let $T_{call}$ be the total call duration and $T_{silence}$ be the average silence duration during a voice call. For circuit switching, the channel is occupied for the full $T_{call}$, but information actually flows only during $T_{call} - T_{silence}$.

**Efficiency of Circuit Switching:**

$$\eta_{cs} = \frac{T_{call} - T_{silence}}{T_{call}} = 1 - \frac{T_{silence}}{T_{call}}$$

For a typical voice call where $T_{silence} \approx 0.60 \cdot T_{call}$:

$$\eta_{cs} = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40 = 40\%$$

### Step 3 — Define Packet Switching Efficiency

For packet switching, statistical multiplexing allows the channel to carry traffic from multiple users during the silence gaps. The theoretical efficiency approaches:

$$\eta_{ps} = \frac{\text{Total useful payload bits transmitted}}{\text{Total channel capacity}} \rightarrow \text{up to } 90\%$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Cyber Security Trade-off

The same statistical multiplexing that gives $\eta_{ps} > \eta_{cs}$ also creates the *attack surface*:

$$\text{Attack Surface} \propto \text{Number of Independent Decision Points}$$

Packet switching routes each packet independently through routers. Each router is a **decision point** that can be:
- Eavesdropped (passive attack)
- Modified (active attack / MITM)
- Dropped (denial-of-service)
- Spoofed (IP spoofing attack)

$$\text{Number of Routers per Path} = R_{path} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j$$

where $r_j = 1$ if node $j$ is a router, $0$ otherwise. Thus:

$$\text{Total Exposure} = R_{path} \times P_{attack}$$

where $P_{attack}$ is the per-router compromise probability. **This is why end-to-end encryption (TLS/IPsec) was eventually mandated — to neutralize $R_{path}$ as a multiplicative threat.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Generation-Wise Threat Evolution

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This matrix maps each generation of the Internet to its dominant threat vector. **Highly examinable.**

| Generation | Era | Dominant Application | Dominant Threat Vector | Standard Mitigation |
|------------|-----|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| ARPANET era | 1969 – 1983 | Remote login, file transfer | Physical wiretapping | Physical security only |
| Early Internet | 1983 – 1995 | Email, Usenet, Telnet | Password sniffing, spoofing | One-time passwords (S/Key) |
| Web 1.0 | 1995 – 2003 | Static websites | Web defacement, CGI exploits | WAFs, input validation |
| Web 2.0 | 2004 – 2015 | Social media, SaaS | XSS, CSRF, SQL injection | OWASP Top 10 frameworks |
| Mobile / Cloud | 2010 – 2020 | Mobile apps, APIs | Mobile malware, API abuse | OAuth 2.0, MFA, JWT |
| IoT / Web 3.0 | 2015 – Present | Smart devices, blockchain | Botnets (Mirai), ransomware, supply-chain | Zero Trust, SBOM, EDR |
| AI-era | 2023 – Present | LLM agents, automation | AI-driven phishing, deepfakes, prompt injection | AI red-teaming, watermarking |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Reconstruction: The TCP/IP Design Decision Tree

> [!TIP]  
> Understanding *why* TCP/IP was designed the way it was helps you answer KTU's "design questions" that ask you to justify architectural choices.

**Step 1 — Problem Statement:**  
How do we interconnect heterogeneous networks (ARPANET, Packet Radio, Packet Satellite) without a central coordinator?

**Step 2 — Constraint:**  
Networks differ in topology, MTU, latency, and reliability. A one-size-fits-all link-layer protocol is impossible.

**Step 3 — Solution:**  
Apply the *hourglass* model — define a single, minimal **network layer (IP)** that hides all link-layer differences, and place intelligence at the **edges** (hosts), not the **core** (routers).

**Step 4 — Implementation:**  
- **IP** handles addressing and routing (best-effort, unreliable).  
- **TCP** handles reliability on top of IP (connection-oriented, reliable).  
- **UDP** handles lightweight, unreliable transport for real-time apps.

**Step 5 — Cyber Security Consequence:**  
Because IP is *best-effort* and *connectionless*, there is no built-in authentication of source addresses. This is the **root cause** of:
- IP spoofing (RFC 2267 mitigation: BCP 38 ingress filtering)  
- Smurf attacks  
- BGP prefix hijacking

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following questions are modelled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns for the course **PEECT864 – Cyber Security**, Module 4. Mark distribution and cognitive level (per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**) are indicated.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the role of ARPANET in the evolution of the Internet. Why was packet switching chosen over circuit switching in its design?**  
**Cognitive Level:** Understand (Bloom Level 2) | **CO Mapping:** CO4

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

ARPANET, funded by the U.S. Department of Defense's Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA), became operational in **1969** with four nodes (UCLA, SRI, UCSB, University of Utah). It was the **first operational packet-switched network** and served as the testbed for the protocols that would later become the Internet.

**Packet switching was chosen over circuit switching for three reasons:** *(1 mark each)*

1. **Resilience to failure** — In circuit switching, a single link failure breaks the entire call. In packet switching, packets can be **dynamically rerouted** around failed nodes, fulfilling ARPA's original goal of a *survivable* communications network under nuclear attack.
2. **Higher channel utilization** — Statistical multiplexing allows the same channel to carry traffic from many users, eliminating the 60% silence-time waste of voice circuits. Efficiency approaches **90%** versus ~**40%** for circuit switching.
3. **Burst-friendly for data** — Computer traffic is inherently *bursty*. Packet switching transmits only when there is data, making it ideal for asynchronous digital communication, whereas circuit switching wastes reserved bandwidth during idle periods.

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In Part B answers, students often *list* events chronologically without explaining the *causal link* between them. Marks are awarded for **causal reasoning** ("Event A caused Problem B, which led to Solution C"). A mere timeline will fetch only **6–7 of 14 marks**. Always narrate the *why*.

---

### 🅰️ Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**"The modern Internet is the result of decades of iterative protocol evolution." Discuss this statement with reference to the transition from NCP to TCP/IP, the invention of DNS, and the emergence of the World Wide Web.**  
**(14 Marks)** | **Cognitive Level:** Analyze (Bloom Level 4) | **CO Mapping:** CO4

#### Part (a) — NCP to TCP/IP Transition (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Limitations of NCP:**  
The Network Control Protocol (NCP), standardized in 1970, was designed for a **single, homogeneous network** (ARPANET). It had no mechanism for **inter-networking** heterogeneous networks. By the late 1970s, ARPANET had to interconnect with **Packet Radio Net (PRNET)** and **Packet Satellite Net (SATNET)**, and NCP could not handle this.

**Step 2 — Cerf-Kahn Contribution (1974):**  
Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn's paper *"A Protocol for Packet Network Intercommunication"* introduced the concept of a **gateway** (today's router) and a **universal datagram** service. This became the foundation of **TCP/IP**.

**Step 3 — Layered Hourglass Model:**  
TCP/IP separates concerns into four layers, with IP as the narrow waist of the hourglass. **Any** link-layer technology and **any** application can ride on top, enabling exponential growth.

**Step 4 — Flag Day (1 January 1983):**  
ARPANET officially switched from NCP to TCP/IP. This is considered the **birthday of the Internet**. Backward compatibility was impossible, so the cutover was instantaneous across all nodes.

**Cyber Security Implication (½ mark):**  
The best-effort, connectionless nature of IP introduced the **IP spoofing vulnerability**, which persists today and is mitigated only by **BCP 38 / RFC 2827 ingress filtering**.

#### Part (b) — DNS and the World Wide Web (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — The Addressing Problem:**  
Before DNS, hosts were addressed by flat numerical labels in a file called `HOSTS.TXT`, maintained centrally by SRI-NIC. As the network grew beyond a few hundred hosts, this became unscalable.

**Step 2 — DNS Solution (1983, RFC 882/883):**  
Paul Mockapetris designed DNS as a **distributed, hierarchical, cached** name-to-IP mapping system. Key innovations:
- **Hierarchical zones** (root → TLD → authoritative) for administrative delegation.
- **Caching** for performance.
- **Anycast** (added later) for DDoS resilience.

**Step 3 — Cyber Security Implications of DNS:**  
- **DNS spoofing / cache poisoning** (Kaminsky bug, 2008).
- **DNS amplification DDoS** (e.g., 2016 Mirai-driven Dyn attack).
- **DNSSEC** (RFC 4033) introduced cryptographic chain-of-trust.

**Step 4 — The World Wide Web (1989 – 1991):**  
Tim Berners-Lee, frustrated with information fragmentation at CERN, proposed three technologies:
1. **HTTP** — HyperText Transfer Protocol
2. **HTML** — HyperText Markup Language
3. **URL** — Uniform Resource Locator

The first website launched in 1991. CERN released the WWW into the **public domain** in April 1993 — a decision that catalyzed the Web's explosive global growth.

**Step 5 — Mosaic & the Browser Revolution:**  
The 1993 release of NCSA Mosaic (later Netscape Navigator) made the Web **graphical** and accessible to non-technical users, triggering the first dot-com wave (1995–2000).

---

### 🅱️ Question B: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**Explain the evolution of the Internet across its four generations. For each generation, identify the dominant cyber security threat and the corresponding defense strategy.**  
**(14 Marks)** | **Cognitive Level:** Analyze (Bloom Level 4) | **CO Mapping:** CO4

#### Part (a) — Web 1.0 and Web 2.0 (7 Marks)

**Web 1.0 (1991 – 2003):**  
- **Characteristic:** Static, read-only HTML pages served over HTTP/1.0.  
- **Dominant Threats:** Web defacement, CGI-bin exploits, server-side buffer overflows (e.g., Code Red, Nimda worms of 2001).  
- **Defense:** Perimeter firewalls, intrusion detection systems (IDS), input validation, security patches via CERT/CC advisories.

**Web 2.0 (2004 – 2015):**  
- **Characteristic:** User-generated content, AJAX, REST APIs, social platforms.  
- **Dominant Threats:** Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF), SQL Injection (OWASP Top 10).  
- **Defense:** OWASP Top 10 awareness, Content Security Policy (CSP), parameterized queries, OAuth 2.0 for delegated authorization.

#### Part (b) — Web 3.0 and Web 4.0 (7 Marks)

**Web 3.0 (2016 – Present):**  
- **Characteristic:** Decentralized, semantic, blockchain-based, AI-driven.  
- **Dominant Threats:** Smart contract reentrancy attacks (e.g., 2016 DAO hack, $60M loss), cryptocurrency exchange breaches, wallet private-key theft, DeFi flash loan attacks.  
- **Defense:** Smart contract audits (e.g., CertiK, OpenZeppelin), hardware wallets, multi-party computation (MPC), zero-knowledge proofs (zk-SNARKs).

**Web 4.0 (Emerging, 2020s – 2030s):**  
- **Characteristic:** Symbiotic computing — IoT, brain-computer interfaces, ambient AI, digital twins.  
- **Dominant Threats:** Botnet-driven DDoS (e.g., Mirai 2016, 1.2 Tbps), ransomware-as-a-service (e.g., Colonial Pipeline 2021), supply-chain attacks (e.g., SolarWinds 2020), AI-driven phishing and deepfake social engineering.  
- **Defense:** **Zero Trust Architecture** (NIST SP 800-207), Software Bill of Materials (SBOM), Extended Detection & Response (XDR), post-quantum cryptography (NIST PQC standards 2024), AI red-teaming.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**  
> 1. ✘ Writing only *names* of generations without explaining **causal technology shift**.  
> 2. ✘ Listing threats without linking to a **specific defense mechanism**.  
> 3. ✘ Confusing *Web 2.0* with *Web 3.0* — examiners will deduct **2 full marks**.  
> 4. ✘ Omitting **real-world incident references** (Code Red, DAO hack, SolarWinds).  
> 5. ✘ Failing to mention **NIST SP 800-207** as the current Zero Trust reference.

- [ ] **Packet switching** chosen for resilience, efficiency (~90% vs ~40%), and burst-friendly data flow.  
- [ ] **IPv4 address space** = $2^{32} =$ **4.29 billion**; exhausted by IANA on **3 February 2011**.  
- [ ] **IPv6 address space** = $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ — effectively unlimited.

- [ ] **Current defense paradigm: Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)** + **post-quantum cryptography (NIST PQC 2024)**.  
- [ ] **Hourglass model** of TCP/IP — IP is the narrow waist; all apps and all link technologies converge on it.  
- [ ] **Key cyber incidents to remember by year:** Code Red (2001), Kaminsky DNS bug (2008), Mirai botnet (2016), DAO hack (2016), SolarWinds (2020), Colonial Pipeline (2021), Log4Shell (2021).  
- [ ] **Remember the analogy:** Internet = global postal service; IP address = postal address; Router = post office; DNS = phonebook; Encryption = sealed envelope.  

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recognizing and Defining Computer Crime
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recognizing and Defining Computer Crime

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Crime (Cybercrime):** A criminal act in which a computer, a networked device, or a computer network is the *target*, the *instrument*, or the *repository* of evidence related to the offence, carried out with the deliberate intent to cause unauthorized harm, manipulate data, defraud entities, or compromise the **CIA Triad** — **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** of digital assets.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a "computer" is legally interpreted under the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (India)**, Section 2(1)(i)*, as *any electronic, magnetic, optical, or other high-speed data processing device which performs logical, arithmetic, and memory functions, and includes any software, firmware, or associated data*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault Without Walls"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine a bank vault that has no walls, no locked doors, and no guards. Anyone with the right key (a password, a backdoor, or a stolen credential) can walk in from any country in the world, at any time, and either steal money, change the ledger, or destroy the vault. That is exactly what a computer crime is — a *physical-world crime transposed into a borderless digital environment* where the **perpetrator and victim can be continents apart**, and the weapon is *information itself*.

## 1.3 Why "Recognizing" a Computer Crime is a Skill

Recognizing computer crime is fundamentally different from recognizing a physical crime:

| Aspect | Physical Crime | Computer Crime |
|---|---|---|
| **Witnesses** | Often present | Rarely present |
| **Physical Evidence** | Tangible (fingerprints, blood) | Volatile (logs, packets, memory dumps) |
| **Detection Time** | Usually immediate | Often discovered months/years later |
| **Jurisdiction** | Clear geographic boundary | Cross-border, multi-jurisdictional |
| **Crime Scene** | Static | Dynamic and reproducible |

## 1.4 The Three Operational Roles of a Computer in Crime

> [!NOTE]
> A computer can play **one or more** of the following three roles in any cybercrime. Examiners use this triad to *classify and define* an offence under the IT Act 2000/2008 (amended).

1. **Computer as a TOOL (Instrument):** The criminal uses the computer to commit the crime (e.g., sending phishing emails, hosting illegal content, drafting a forgery).
2. **Computer as a TARGET:** The computer system itself is the victim (e.g., DDoS attack, ransomware encryption, SQL injection).
3. **Computer as Evidence Storage:** The computer stores proof of the crime (e.g., hard drive containing fraud records, child exploitation material).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of "Defining" a Computer Crime

For any act to be legally and academically *defined* as a computer crime, it must satisfy **four conditions**. KTU examiners frequently test this logical framework.

### Pillar 1 — Actus Reus (The Guilty Act)
A deliberate, volitional act performed using a computing device. Mere *thought* is not a crime; an executable action must exist (clicking, uploading, executing code).

### Pillar 2 — Mens Rea (Criminal Intent)
The perpetrator must have *knowledge* and *intention* to cause harm, OR *reckless disregard* for the consequences. The Indian IT Act punishes acts done "dishonestly" or "fraudulently" under **Section 66, 66C, 66D, 66E, 66F**.

### Pillar 3 — Causation (Harm or Loss)
Actual damage must occur — financial loss, data destruction, reputational harm, identity theft, or breach of national security. *Attempted* crimes are also punishable under **Section 66B** (punishment for dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource).

### Pillar 4 — Jurisdictional Nexus
The act must have a legally recognizable link to a computer system, network, or electronic record under the relevant statute (IT Act 2000/2008, IPC, Indian Telegraph Act, or Special Laws).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Classification Matrix

The following consolidated table is the **most-tested framework** for Part A and Part B questions on this topic:

| Sl. No. | Category of Computer Crime | Description | IT Act / IPC Reference |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hacking / Unauthorized Access** | Gaining entry into a system without permission | Sec 66, 43(a) IT Act |
| 2 | **Data Theft / Data Leakage** | Copying, exfiltrating, or selling confidential data | Sec 66, 66B IT Act |
| 3 | **Malware Propagation** | Distributing viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware | Sec 43(c), 66 IT Act |
| 4 | **Phishing & Social Engineering** | Fraudulently obtaining credentials using deception | Sec 66D IT Act, IPC 420 |
| 5 | **Identity Theft** | Impersonating another person's digital identity | Sec 66C IT Act |
| 6 | **Cyberstalking & Obscenity** | Harassment or publishing obscene material online | Sec 66E, 67, 67A, 67B IT Act |
| 7 | **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)** | Overwhelming a server to deny service to legitimate users | Sec 66F (if against critical info. infra) |
| 8 | **Cyberterrorism** | Attack threatening sovereignty, unity, or security of India | Sec 66F IT Act (Life imprisonment) |
| 9 | **Cryptojacking & Botnets** | Hijacking compute resources without consent | Sec 43, 66 IT Act |
| 10 | **Electronic Forgery & Tampering** | Altering electronic records fraudulently | Sec 463 / 477A IPC, Sec 65 IT Act |
| 11 | **E-Commerce Fraud / Online Scams** | Cheating using digital payment platforms | Sec 66D IT Act, Sec 420 IPC |
| 12 | **Insider Threat / Privilege Abuse** | Misuse of authorized access by an employee | Sec 43A, 66 IT Act |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip (High-Yield):** Notice the strict correlation between the **type of crime** and the **section of the IT Act**. Examiners expect students to quote the *correct section number* along with the *correct offence description*. Memorising this mapping alone can secure 3–5 marks.

## 2.3 Why Recognizing Computer Crime is Hard — The *Five Dimensions of Ambiguity*

When an investigator looks at a log file or a complaint, ambiguity arises from five orthogonal dimensions. KTU 2024 expects students to articulate these clearly.

$$
D_{crime} = \{ D_{perpetrator},\; D_{victim},\; D_{location},\; D_{time},\; D_{evidence} \}
$$

Where each dimension $D_i$ contributes a degree of uncertainty:

- **$D_{perpetrator}$** — Identity hidden behind Tor, VPNs, proxy chains, spoofed MAC/IP.
- **$D_{victim}$** — May be a person, organization, critical infrastructure, or the public at large.
- **$D_{location}$** — Server in Country A, attacker in Country B, victim in Country C.
- **$D_{time}$** — Crime in 2019, discovered in 2024. Logs rotated. Evidence stale.
- **$D_{evidence}$** — Volatile (RAM), fragile (metadata), distributed (cloud, blockchain).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recognizing and defining computer crime is **not** purely a legal exercise — it has direct engineering consequences:

1. **Network Design:** Recognizing that DDoS is a crime informs the design of **rate-limiting, scrubbing centers, and BGP blackholing**.
2. **System Hardening:** Recognizing insider threats leads to **RBAC, least privilege, audit logging**.
3. **Incident Response:** Defining the crime precisely (e.g., "Sec 66 IT Act — unauthorized access") triggers a specific **chain of custody** and reporting protocol under the **IT Act 2008, Section 69** and the **Cyber Crime Coordination Centre (I4C)** framework.
4. **Digital Forensics:** The **first step** of the *Identification → Preservation → Collection → Examination → Analysis → Presentation* (IPCEPAP) model depends on correct recognition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Linkage:** A B.Tech graduate in CSE/IT/ECE will encounter this framework when configuring a **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** tool, performing a **forensic acquisition** with tools like *FTK Imager* or *EnCase*, or filing a **First Information Report (FIR)** under the IT Act.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Logical Framework & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The "Recognition Decision Tree" — Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

A computer crime is *recognized* by following a deterministic decision tree. This is the *operational algorithm* used by a **Cyber Forensic Investigator (CFI)** or a **SOC Analyst (L1/L2)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Note:** Although this is a non-programming conceptual topic, the *decision logic* is presented as a formal ordered tuple $(S_0, T, F, A)$ where $S_0$ is the initial state, $T$ is the transition trigger, $F$ is the formal recognition function, and $A$ is the final assignment to an IT Act section.

### Step 1 — Trigger Detection (Initial State $S_0$)
The process begins when an *indicator* of compromise (IoC) is observed. An IoC is any observable that, with high confidence, suggests a computer-related incident has occurred.

$$
S_0 = \{ \text{IoC}_{alert} \}
$$

Examples of $S_0$:
- A SIEM alerts on **1000 failed logins from a single IP** → Brute-force suspected.
- A user reports an **unauthorized transaction** from a banking app.
- An IDS detects **EICAR test signature** in email attachment.
- A **CCTV shows tampering** with a server rack (physical-cyber crime).

### Step 2 — Trigger Validation (Transition $T$)
A raw alert is *not* a crime. The SOC must rule out false positives.

$$
T : S_0 \rightarrow S_1 \quad \text{iff} \quad \text{Confidence}(S_0) \geq \tau
$$

Where $\tau$ is the **threshold of suspicion** (typically 0.7 for an L1 SOC analyst). If the alert is below $\tau$, it is closed as *Benign True Positive* or *False Positive*. If above, it is escalated.

### Step 3 — Formal Recognition Function $F$
The recognition function $F$ maps the validated incident to a *category* and *statute*:

$$
F : S_1 \rightarrow \{ \text{Category}_i,\; \text{IT Act Section}_j \}
$$

The category is selected by evaluating the incident against the **Four Pillars (Actus Reus, Mens Rea, Causation, Jurisdictional Nexus)** discussed in Section 2.1.

$$
\text{Category}_i = \arg\max_{i \in \{1..12\}} \left[ P(\text{Pillar}_1 \cap \text{Pillar}_2 \cap \text{Pillar}_3 \cap \text{Pillar}_4 \;\vert\; S_1) \right]
$$

The argmax above simply means: *choose the category for which all four pillars are most completely satisfied.*

### Step 4 — Final Assignment $A$
The recognized crime is assigned a formal label $(C, S, L, T_{discovered})$ where:
- $C$ = Crime category (from the 12-item matrix).
- $S$ = Statute reference (IT Act section, IPC section).
- $L$ = Loss magnitude (financial, reputational, operational).
- $T_{discovered}$ = Time of discovery (UTC timestamp).

### Step 5 — Notification & Documentation
Under **IT Act Section 69** and the rules notified by **CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team)** in 2022, certain categories of incidents must be reported to CERT-In within **6 hours** of detection.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Mapping an Incident

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** *An employee of a bank in Kochi receives an email that appears to be from RBI. The link takes her to a fake portal. She enters her netbanking credentials. ₹2,00,000 is transferred to an unknown account in Delhi the next morning.*

Let us apply the recognition decision tree step-by-step.

| Step | Logical Deduction | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trigger:** Money deducted without authorization. | $S_0$ established. |
| 2 | **Mens Rea check:** Unknown party designed deceptive email + fake portal. | Intent confirmed. |
| 3 | **Actus Reus check:** Phishing email was sent, fake portal was hosted, credentials were exfiltrated. | Action confirmed. |
| 4 | **Causation check:** Direct loss of ₹2,00,000. | Harm confirmed. |
| 5 | **Jurisdictional Nexus check:** Email, portal, and transaction all involved electronic records and a computer network. | Nexus confirmed. |
| 6 | **Category assignment** from the 12-item matrix. | **Category 4 (Phishing) + Category 12 (E-Commerce Fraud)**. |
| 7 | **Statute mapping.** | **Sec 66D IT Act** (Cheating by personation using computer resource) **+ Sec 420 IPC**. |
| 8 | **Final label.** | $A = (\text{Phishing Fraud},\; \text{Sec 66D + 420 IPC},\; \text{₹2,00,000},\; T_{discovered})$ |
| 9 | **Notification obligation.** | Bank must inform CERT-In + file FIR at jurisdictional cyber cell. |

## 3.3 The Definition of Computer Crime — Formalized

Synthesizing the four pillars, a **formal definition** can be expressed as the conjunction:

$$
\text{ComputerCrime} \;\equiv\; \bigwedge_{k=1}^{4} P_k \quad \text{where} \quad P_k \in \{0, 1\}
$$

$$
\text{ComputerCrime} \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3 \cdot P_4 = 1 \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Here $P_k = 1$ means the $k^{th}$ pillar is satisfied. *If even one pillar is zero, the act is not a computer crime in the strict legal sense — it may be a civil wrong, a tort, or a non-cyber crime.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:** This Boolean formulation is identical in structure to the *Access Control Triad* in computer security (Subject × Object × Action), and to the *AND-gate logic* used in firewall rule processing. Recognizing this pattern helps students recall the concept using familiar logic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four-Pillar Recognition Framework (Conceptual Map)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs use the alphanumeric prefix convention. No reserved keywords are used. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode([COMPUTER CRIME RECOGNITION ENTRY]) --> pillar1{PILLAR 1 ACTUS REUS GUILTY ACT}
    pillar1 -- YES --> pillar2{PILLAR 2 MENS REA CRIMINAL INTENT}
    pillar1 -- NO --> notCrime1[NOT A COMPUTER CRIME MAY BE CIVIL WRONG]
    pillar2 -- YES --> pillar3{PILLAR 3 CAUSATION ACTUAL HARM}
    pillar2 -- NO --> notCrime2[NOT A COMPUTER CRIME INSUFFICIENT INTENT]
    pillar3 -- YES --> pillar4{PILLAR 4 JURISDICTIONAL NEXUS ELECTRONIC RECORD}
    pillar3 -- NO --> notCrime3[ATTEMPT OR PREPARATION ONLY]
    pillar4 -- YES --> classifyNode[FORMAL CLASSIFICATION TO IT ACT SECTION]
    pillar4 -- NO --> notCrime4[NOT COVERED UNDER IT ACT TRY IPC]
    classifyNode --> outputLabel((ASSIGN FINAL LABEL CATEGORY SECTION LOSS TIME))
    outputLabel --> notifyCert[NOTIFY CERT IN WITHIN 6 HOURS IF CRITICAL]
    notifyCert --> firStep[FILE FIR AT JURISDICTIONAL CYBER CELL]
```

## 4.2 The Three Operational Roles of a Computer (Tool × Target × Evidence)

```mermaid
graph LR
    roleTool[COMPUTER AS TOOL INSTRUMENT]
    roleTarget[COMPUTER AS TARGET VICTIM]
    roleEvidence[COMPUTER AS EVIDENCE REPOSITORY]
    roleTool --> ex1[EXAMPLE PHISHING EMAIL SENT]
    roleTool --> ex2[EXAMPLE HOSTING ILLEGAL CONTENT]
    roleTarget --> ex3[EXAMPLE DDOS ATTACK ON SERVER]
    roleTarget --> ex4[EXAMPLE RANSOMWARE ENCRYPTION]
    roleEvidence --> ex5[EXAMPLE HARD DRIVE WITH FRAUD RECORDS]
    roleEvidence --> ex6[EXAMPLE CLOUD STORAGE WITH STOLEN DATA]
    ex1 --> crime(COMPUTER CRIME)
    ex2 --> crime
    ex3 --> crime
    ex4 --> crime
    ex5 --> crime
    ex6 --> crime
```

## 4.3 The IPCEPAP Forensic Recognition Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    identNode[STEP 1 IDENTIFICATION OF INCIDENT]
    identNode --> preservNode[STEP 2 PRESERVATION OF EVIDENCE]
    preservNode --> collNode[STEP 3 COLLECTION BIT STREAM IMAGE]
    collNode --> examNode[STEP 4 EXAMINATION USING FORENSIC TOOLS]
    examNode --> analyNode[STEP 5 ANALYSIS CORRELATION WITH CRIME CATEGORY]
    analyNode --> presNode[STEP 6 PRESENTATION IN COURT OF LAW]
    analyNode -.-> reportCert[PARALLEL REPORT TO CERT IN]
    reportCert -.-> presNode
```

## 4.4 Classification of Computer Crimes — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    crimeRoot[COMPUTER CRIMES CLASSIFICATION]
    crimeRoot --> branchA[AGAINST PERSONS]
    crimeRoot --> branchB[AGAINST PROPERTY]
    crimeRoot --> branchC[AGAINST ORGANIZATION]
    crimeRoot --> branchD[AGAINST SOCIETY OR NATION]
    branchA --> childA1[PHISHING]
    branchA --> childA2[IDENTITY THEFT SEC 66C]
    branchA --> childA3[CYBERSTALKING SEC 66E]
    branchB --> childB1[HACKING SEC 66]
    branchB --> childB2[DATA THEFT SEC 43]
    branchB --> childB3[DDOS SEC 66F IF CRITICAL INFRA]
    branchC --> childC1[INSIDER THREAT SEC 43A]
    branchC --> childC2[MALWARE PROPAGATION SEC 43C]
    branchC --> childC3[RANSOMWARE ATTACK]
    branchD --> childD1[CYBER TERRORISM SEC 66F]
    branchD --> childD2[PUBLISHING OBSCENE MATERIAL SEC 67]
    branchD --> childD3[SOCIAL MEDIA HATE CRIMES]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Answer in 80–120 words. Direct, definition-style. Each carries 3 marks as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern.*

### Question A.1  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define computer crime. List any four categories of computer crime with one-line examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Computer crime is any illegal act in which a computer is the *tool*, *target*, or *evidence repository*, and which violates the **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA Triad)** of digital information. Four categories are: (1) **Hacking** — unauthorized access to a system (e.g., cracking a Wi-Fi password); (2) **Phishing** — fraudulent emails to steal credentials (e.g., fake bank login page); (3) **Malware Propagation** — distributing viruses/worms (e.g., email attachment trojan); (4) **Identity Theft** — impersonating another person online (e.g., using someone else's Aadhaar for KYC). Each violates the IT Act 2000/2008 and corresponding IPC sections. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for categories)*

---

### Question A.2  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the four pillars required to legally define an act as a computer crime.**

**Model Answer:**
The four pillars are: (i) **Actus Reus** — a deliberate, volitional act using a computer; (ii) **Mens Rea** — criminal intent, knowledge, or reckless disregard; (iii) **Causation** — actual harm, loss, or damage occurred; (iv) **Jurisdictional Nexus** — a legally recognizable link to a computer, network, or electronic record under the IT Act 2000/2008. All four must be satisfied simultaneously for an act to qualify as a computer crime; if any one pillar is missing, the act may be a civil wrong or a non-cyber crime. *(3 marks: 0.75 per pillar)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question B.1  (A)  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 — Set A]`

> **Answer all sub-parts. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various ways in which a computer is used as a *tool*, a *target*, and an *evidence repository* in the commission of computer crimes. Cite at least two IT Act sections in support of your answer.  *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a neat classification framework, explain any **six** major categories of computer crime. For each category, give one real-world example and the relevant section of the IT Act 2000/2008.  *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — *7 Marks*

**Introduction (1 mark):** A computer plays one or more of three operational roles in any cybercrime. Recognizing this role is the *first step* in classifying an offence under the Indian IT Act 2000/2008.

**Computer as a TOOL (2 marks):** The criminal uses the computer to *commit* the crime, but the target is human or organizational. Examples: (i) Sending a phishing email that steals banking credentials — punishable under **Section 66D IT Act** (cheating by personation using computer resource) and **Section 420 IPC**; (ii) Hosting a fake e-commerce website that defrauds customers — covered under **Section 66D IT Act** and **Section 463/465 IPC** (forgery).

**Computer as a TARGET (2 marks):** The computer system itself is the victim. Examples: (i) A DDoS attack on a bank's payment gateway that denies service to legitimate users — punishable under **Section 66F IT Act** if it is against critical information infrastructure, with imprisonment up to life; (ii) Ransomware encrypting a hospital's database — covered under **Section 43(c)** for damage to computer systems and **Section 66** for computer-related offences.

**Computer as EVIDENCE REPOSITORY (2 marks):** The computer is neither tool nor target but stores *proof* of a crime. Examples: (i) A seized hard drive containing child sexual abuse material — relevant to **Section 67B IT Act** and **POCSO Act 2012**; (ii) A server log file showing fraudulent financial transactions — admissible under **Section 65B Indian Evidence Act** (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023) as electronic evidence.

**Conclusion (Extra):** Real-world cases often involve *overlapping* roles — the same computer may be the tool (used to send phishing), the target (compromised), and the evidence (logs).

#### Model Solution for (b) — *7 Marks*

| # | Category | Real-World Example | IT Act Section |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hacking / Unauthorized Access** | Attacker breaks into a college ERP system to change marks | **Sec 66 IT Act** + Sec 43(a) |
| 2 | **Phishing & Identity Theft** | Fake IRCTC email steals login credentials of a user | **Sec 66D** + **Sec 66C** IT Act |
| 3 | **Malware / Ransomware Attack** | WannaCry ransomware encrypts hospital records | **Sec 43(c)** + **Sec 66** IT Act |
| 4 | **DDoS Attack** | Botnet floods an e-commerce site on Diwali sale | **Sec 66F** (if critical infra) |
| 5 | **Cyberstalking / Obscenity** | Man harasses a woman on Instagram with morphed images | **Sec 66E** + **Sec 67** IT Act |
| 6 | **E-Commerce Fraud** | Seller on OLX takes advance for non-existent iPhone | **Sec 66D** + **Sec 420 IPC** |

**Brief Explanation (any 1 mark per category, total 6 marks for the 6 categories and 1 mark for the framework intro):**
1. *Hacking* — Actus Reus: access without permission; Mens Rea: intent to access; IT Act Sec 66 prescribes imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹5 lakh.
2. *Phishing* — Deceptive communication to obtain credentials; Sec 66D mandates up to 3 years imprisonment and fine up to ₹1 lakh.
3. *Malware* — Self-propagating code damages data; Sec 43(c) provides civil compensation; Sec 66 provides criminal penalty.
4. *DDoS* — Volumetric attack denying service; Sec 66F for attacks on critical information infrastructure is non-bailable and triable by a Special Court.
5. *Cyberstalking* — Persistent online harassment; Sec 66E (violation of privacy) and Sec 67 (obscenity) are commonly invoked.
6. *E-Commerce Fraud* — Use of digital platforms to cheat; Sec 66D IT Act + Sec 420 IPC.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Quoting the wrong section number** (e.g., citing Sec 66D for hacking) costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Failing to cite the IPC section** alongside the IT Act section for compound offences (e.g., phishing is *both* Sec 66D and Sec 420 IPC) costs 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Writing the category but not giving an example** — the model answer requires *both*; missing example costs 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Vague answers like "cybercrime is bad"** — KTU examiners award 0 marks for definitions that lack technical/legal precision.
> 5. **Not distinguishing "as tool" vs "as target"** — examiners test this distinction explicitly. Memorise the three-role framework.

---

### Question B.1  (B)  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 — Set B]`

> **Answer all sub-parts. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

**(a)** "An act is not a computer crime unless four specific pillars are satisfied." Justify this statement by explaining each pillar in detail with a suitable example.  *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **IPCEPAP** forensic model. How does the *identification* stage of this model relate to the act of *recognizing* a computer crime? Discuss the challenges of recognizing computer crimes across jurisdictions.  *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — *7 Marks*

**Statement (0.5 mark):** True. An act is recognized as a computer crime only when **all four pillars** are simultaneously satisfied; absence of any one pillar alters the legal classification.

**Pillar 1 — Actus Reus (1.5 marks):** *Guilty Act.* A voluntary, conscious action must have been performed using a computer. Example: An attacker actively runs an exploit script against a server. Merely *thinking* about hacking is not a crime, but *clicking* "Run" is.

**Pillar 2 — Mens Rea (1.5 marks):** *Criminal Intent.* The actor must have intended to cause harm, or acted with reckless disregard. Example: An employee knowingly copies the customer database to a personal USB drive with the intent to sell it — intent is proven.

**Pillar 3 — Causation (1.5 marks):** *Actual Harm.* Real damage or loss must have occurred. Example: A DDoS attack causes a measurable downtime of 4 hours, resulting in lost sales. *Attempted* DDoS without effect may still be punishable under Sec 66B, but causation strengthens the prosecution.

**Pillar 4 — Jurisdictional Nexus (1.5 marks):** *Link to a Computer/Network.* The act must be connected to a computer system or electronic record. Example: Threatening emails sent via Gmail — Gmail is a computer resource, so Sec 66 IT Act applies. If the threat was hand-delivered, IT Act does not apply, though IPC 503/506 may.

**Counter-Example (0.5 mark):** A disgruntled employee *thinks* about deleting the database (no Actus Reus). Result: *not* a crime. A competitor *accidentally* crashes your server through a misconfigured load balancer (no Mens Rea). Result: not a crime, though a civil tort may apply.

#### Model Solution for (b) — *7 Marks*

**Introduction to IPCEPAP (1 mark):** The **IPCEPAP** model is the standard digital forensics framework: **Identification, Preservation, Collection, Examination, Analysis, Presentation**. It guides the investigator from recognizing an incident to presenting findings in court.

**Six Stages (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Identification** — Recognise that a computer crime has occurred; identify the *scope* (single system vs. enterprise-wide).
2. **Preservation** — Secure the crime scene; prevent alteration; document chain of custody.
3. **Collection** — Acquire bit-stream forensic images using tools like *FTK Imager*, *EnCase*, *dd*.
4. **Examination** — Search and extract relevant artefacts (logs, registry, deleted files, RAM).
5. **Analysis** — Correlate artefacts; reconstruct timeline; tie to IT Act section.
6. **Presentation** — Produce a court-admissible report citing Sec 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023).

**Identification ↔ Recognition Link (1.5 marks):** Identification is *synonymous* with recognition — it is the *first and most critical* step. Without correct identification, the wrong evidence is preserved, the wrong statute is applied, and the case collapses. The *Four Pillars framework* (Actus Reus, Mens Rea, Causation, Nexus) IS the identification tool.

**Cross-Jurisdictional Challenges (1.5 marks):**
- **Trans-border data flow:** Logs hosted on AWS US-East may be outside Indian jurisdiction; MLAT (Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty) needed.
- **Conflicting laws:** An act legal in one country (e.g., active vulnerability research) may be illegal in another.
- **Anonymity:** Tor/VPN routes traffic through multiple countries, complicating attribution.
- **Sovereignty issues:** Servers in a non-cooperative nation cannot be seized without diplomatic channels.
- **Time-sensitive evidence:** Cloud logs may be rotated in 30 days, faster than the legal process.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Writing IPCEPAP without explaining each stage** — examiners expect *all six* stages; missing one costs ~0.5 mark.
> 2. **Not connecting Identification to the Four Pillars** — this is a tested *integration question*; weak linkage costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Listing cross-jurisdictional challenges without examples** — give at least *two concrete examples* (e.g., Tor, AWS data residency).
> 4. **Confusing IT Act with IPC** — IPC applies to *traditional* crimes, IT Act applies to *computer-related* crimes; mixing up costs marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following 12 points constitute the *minimum* high-yield knowledge for scoring full marks on this topic. Memorise verbatim before the exam.**

1. **Definition:** Computer Crime = Computer as **TOOL** + **TARGET** + **EVIDENCE** + Violation of **CIA Triad**.
2. **Four Pillars:** Actus Reus → Mens Rea → Causation → Jurisdictional Nexus. *All four must hold simultaneously.*
3. **IT Act 2000/2008 is the primary statute** in India; IT Act does NOT apply if no computer/network is involved — then IPC applies.
4. **Key Section Mapping (MUST memorise):**
   - **Sec 66** → Hacking / general computer-related offence
   - **Sec 66B** → Receiving stolen computer resource
   - **Sec 66C** → Identity theft
   - **Sec 66D** → Cheating by personation (phishing / e-fraud)
   - **Sec 66E** → Violation of privacy
   - **Sec 66F** → Cyberterrorism (attacks on critical info. infrastructure)
   - **Sec 43** → Civil compensation (damage to systems, data)
   - **Sec 67 / 67A / 67B** → Obscenity (progressive severity)
   - **Sec 65** → Tampering with computer source documents
   - **Sec 69** → Government interception powers
5. **Three Roles of a Computer in a Crime:** TOOL (criminal uses it), TARGET (system is the victim), EVIDENCE (data storage). Real crimes often involve overlapping roles.
6. **IPCEPAP Forensic Model:** Identification → Preservation → Collection → Examination → Analysis → Presentation. This is the *canonical* forensic pipeline.
7. **Why Recognition is Difficult:** Five-dimensional ambiguity — Perpetrator, Victim, Location, Time, Evidence are all uncertain.
8. **CERT-In Reporting Rule (2022):** Cyber incidents must be reported to CERT-In within **6 hours** of detection.
9. **Volatile vs Persistent Evidence:** RAM is volatile (lose on power-off); disk logs are persistent; cloud logs depend on retention policy.
10. **Cross-Jurisdiction is the rule, not the exception.** Most cybercrimes span ≥ 2 countries, requiring MLAT, Budapest Convention, or bilateral treaties.
11. **Engineering Linkage:** Recognition drives *SIEM rule design*, *incident response playbooks*, and *digital forensic acquisition* — the B.Tech graduate will use this framework professionally.
12. **Examiner's Heuristic:** A 14-mark answer must contain — *Definition + 4 Pillars + 6 Categories with Sections + 1 Real-World Example + 1 Diagram/Flowchart + 1 Counter-Example* to be considered complete.

> **All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE Examination! 🚀**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contemporary Crimes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Contemporary Crimes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Contemporary Crimes** (also termed *modern or cyber-enabled crimes*) refer to unlawful acts, offences, or misdemeanors committed using digital devices, computer networks, the Internet, or any electronic communication medium as the instrumentality, target, or both. These crimes exploit the inherent vulnerabilities of the modern digital ecosystem — including its anonymity, reach, automation, and interconnectedness — to inflict harm on individuals, organizations, governments, and critical infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Contemporary crimes* are evolving forms of criminal activity that have emerged alongside digital technologies, social media platforms, e-commerce systems, and the Internet of Things (IoT). They blur the traditional boundaries of physical, geographical, and jurisdictional law enforcement, demanding updated legal frameworks, advanced forensic techniques, and proactive cyber-hygiene practices.

### Categories of Contemporary Cyber Crimes (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| Sl. No. | Category | Defining Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Crimes Against Persons** | Target individuals (cyberstalking, cyberbullying, identity theft) |
| 2 | **Crimes Against Property** | Target digital/physical assets (fraud, phishing, ransomware) |
| 3 | **Crimes Against Government** | Cyberterrorism, digital espionage, state-sponsored attacks |
| 4 | **Crimes Against Society** | Drug trafficking, prostitution rings, hate speech, piracy |
| 5 | **Computer-as-Instrument Crimes** | Computer is the *tool* used (DoS, malware distribution) |
| 6 | **Computer-as-Target Crimes** | Computer/network is the *victim* (intrusion, data breach) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Invisible Pickpocket"**
> Imagine a thief who can reach into your pocket from across the world, copy your wallet, replace the cards with fakes, and vanish — all within milliseconds — leaving no fingerprints, no broken locks, and no physical evidence. **That is contemporary crime in the digital age.** Just as pickpocketing evolved with technology (from physical sleight-of-hand to RFID skimmers and now NFC relay attacks), crime itself has *digitally transformed*, exploiting the trust users place in their devices, networks, and online identities.

**Why does this matter?**
- A KTU graduate engineer will, in practice, build or manage systems that *defend* against these crimes.
- Software that you code today may become the *target* of a future attack — understanding attacker psychology is non-negotiable.
- Many of these crimes have **no physical victim in proximity**, making them psychologically easier for perpetrators and harder for law enforcement to prosecute.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & High-Yield Constants

> [!TIP]
> The following globally-recognised metrics and statutes underpin the legal and technical evaluation of contemporary crimes in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **IT Act, 2000 (India)** — Sections 43, 65, 66, 66A–F, 67, 69, 72 define penalties for unauthorized access, data theft, obscenity, and interception.
- **IPC Sections 503/507** — Cover criminal intimidation and anonymous communication.
- **POCSO Act, 2012** — Specifically addresses online child sexual abuse material (CSAM).
- **GDPR (EU)**, **CCPA (California)** — Data protection frameworks (max fine: **€20 million or 4% global turnover**).
- **FBI IC3 Report metric (2023)** — Reported cyber-crime losses exceeded **USD 12.5 billion** in a single year.
- **Symantec ISTR metric** — Global spam rate in botnet traffic: ~**85.9%** of total email traffic.
- **NIST SP 800-63B** — Defines digital identity assurance levels (AAL1, AAL2, AAL3).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of Contemporary Crimes

Contemporary crimes can be dissected into a **four-stage operational pipeline** that attackers, fraudsters, and digital offenders universally follow:

### Stage 1: Reconnaissance
- **Goal:** Gather information about the target (individual, system, or organization).
- **Techniques:** Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT), social media profiling, WHOIS lookups, network scanning (`nmap`), dumpster diving (physical analog).
- **Why it matters:** Reconnaissance accounts for ~**70% of attack time** in advanced persistent threat (APT) models.

### Stage 2: Weaponization
- **Goal:** Convert gathered intel into a deliverable attack vector.
- **Examples:** Phishing emails, malicious attachments, weaponized USB drives, deepfake voice calls, fake mobile apps.
- **Output:** A *payload + delivery mechanism* combination.

### Stage 3: Delivery & Exploitation
- **Goal:** Deploy the weapon to the victim.
- **Vectors:** Email, SMS (smishing), voice call (vishing), social media DMs, malicious advertisements (malvertising), supply-chain compromise.

### Stage 4: Action on Objectives & Monetization
- **Goal:** Realize the criminal benefit.
- **Forms:** Financial fraud, data exfiltration, ransom demand, blackmail, reputational damage, sale on darknet marketplaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Most exam questions on contemporary crimes test your ability to *map* a real-world incident (e.g., a fake job-offer scam) back to these four stages. Memorize the pipeline — it is a guaranteed 7-marker in Part B.

## 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Contemporary Crimes (Module 4 Core Content)

### 2.2.1 Phishing and its Variants
Phishing is the *socio-technical* practice of impersonating a trusted entity to extract sensitive credentials, financial data, or authentication tokens.

| Variant | Medium | Sophistication |
|---|---|---|
| **Email Phishing** | Bulk email campaigns | Low |
| **Spear Phishing** | Targeted emails to specific individuals | Medium |
| **Whaling** | Targets senior executives / CXOs | High |
| **Smishing** | SMS-based phishing | Medium |
| **Vishing** | Voice-call phishing (VoIP spoofing) | High |
| **Pharming** | DNS poisoning / hosts-file hijack | High |
| **Clone Phishing** | Replicates a legitimate, previously-delivered email | High |
| **Evilginx / AitM Phishing** | Adversary-in-the-middle reverse-proxy | Expert |

### 2.2.2 Identity Theft & Financial Fraud
- **Identity Theft:** Unauthorized acquisition and use of another person's identifying information (PII) — name, Aadhaar/PAN, date of birth, biometric data.
- **Synthetic Identity Fraud:** Combining real and fabricated information to create a *new* fictitious identity (a USD 6 billion+ annual loss category in the US).
- **Account Takeover (ATO):** Credential-stuffing attacks using leaked databases.

### 2.2.3 Cyberstalking & Cyberbullying
- **Cyberstalking:** Repeated, obsessive online harassment involving threats, false allegations, identity exposure (doxing), and location tracking.
- **Cyberbullying:** Repeated aggression toward minors or vulnerable individuals on social platforms (Instagram, TikTok, Discord).
- **Legal note:** India IT Act §66A was struck down (Shreya Singhal v. Union of India, 2015) — replaced by §507 IPC + specific State laws.

### 2.2.4 Sexting, Online Predators, and CSAM
- **Sexting:** Self-production and sharing of sexually explicit imagery, especially dangerous for minors.
- **Online Predator Behavior:** Grooming patterns (Graduation Model by Lanning, 2010) — *friendship forming → relationship forming → risk assessment → exclusivity → sexual stage → conclusion*.
- **CSAM (Child Sexual Abuse Material):** Distribution is a cognizable, non-bailable offence under POCSO Act 2012 + IT Act §67B.

### 2.2.5 Cyberterrorism & Cyberwarfare
- **Definition (Denning, 2000):** *“Unlawful attacks and threats of attack against computers, networks, and the information stored therein, done to intimidate or coerce a government or its people in furtherance of political or social objectives.”*
- **Distinguishing features:** Political motive, intent to cause mass harm, target critical infrastructure (power grids, water systems, financial markets).

### 2.2.6 Ransomware
- A malware sub-class that encrypts victim data and demands payment (typically cryptocurrency) for decryption.
- **Notable families:** WannaCry (2017), NotPetya (2017), Ryuk (2018), Conti (2020), LockBit (2022–24).
- **Average recovery cost (2023):** **USD 1.85 million per incident** (excluding ransom).

### 2.2.7 Drug Trafficking & Illicit Marketplaces
- **Darknet markets:** Tor-based platforms (e.g., historical *Silk Road*, *AlphaBay*, *Hydra Market*) facilitate anonymous trade in narcotics, weapons, stolen data, and counterfeit currency.
- **Cryptocurrency:** Monero (XMR) and Zcash (ZEC) preferred for *privacy-by-default* transactions.

### 2.2.8 Money Laundering via Cryptocurrency
A three-stage process formalized in the **FATF (Financial Action Task Force)** framework:

$$
\text{Criminal Proceeds} \xrightarrow{\text{Placement}} \text{Financial System} \xrightarrow{\text{Layering}} \text{Complex Web} \xrightarrow{\text{Integration}} \text{Legitimate Economy}
$$

- **Placement:** Converting cash to crypto via ATMs or exchanges.
- **Layering:** Tumbling / mixing services (e.g., Tornado Cash) to obscure trail.
- **Integration:** Withdrawal as “clean” funds via real estate, NFTs, or shell companies.

### 2.2.9 Digital Piracy & Intellectual Property Theft
- Unauthorized reproduction, distribution, or streaming of copyrighted content.
- **Software piracy cost (global, 2022 BSA study):** > **USD 46 billion** in unlicensed software usage.

### 2.2.10 Emerging Frontier Crimes
- **Deepfake-enabled fraud:** Synthetic audio/video used for CEO-impersonation scams (USD 25 million Hong Kong case, 2024).
- **AI-powered social engineering:** LLMs crafting hyper-personalized spear-phishing at scale.
- **IoT botnets:** Mirai (2016), Mozi (2020) — millions of compromised IoT devices launching DDoS.
- **Cloud-jacking:** Hijacking cloud compute/storage for crypto-mining (cryptojacking).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the *most testable* metrics, frameworks, and statutory references for the KTU ESE. The quantities are universally accepted; *memorize* the bold numbers.

| Concept | Framework / Equation | Standard Value / Range | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NIST Identity Assurance** | AAL1 / AAL2 / AAL3 | AAL1 (single-factor), AAL3 (hardware crypto + verifier impersonation resistant) | Often asked as 3-mark |
| **GDPR Max Fine** | $F_{\max} = \max(0.04 \times R, \ 20 \text{ M€})$ | **20 M€ or 4% annual global turnover** | Always quote as 4% or €20M (whichever is higher) |
