- **NIST 800-88 Purge Level Compliant**
- **No Media Damage:** Does not affect NAND cell endurance

**Disadvantages:**

- **Requires Self-Encrypting Drive (SED) hardware support**
- **Key Management:** MEK must be properly randomized per device; vendor implementation flaws can compromise security
- **Post-Quantum Concerns:** Future quantum computers (running Grover's algorithm) may reduce AES-256 effective key strength to 128 bits, which is still secure but should be monitored

**Conclusion:** Cryptographic Erase is the most efficient and reliable method for modern storage media, particularly SSDs, provided the drive supports hardware encryption with proper key destruction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Contaminants and Destruction**

- **Data Contaminants:** Residual digital artifacts (slack space, swap, metadata, registry) persisting after deletion
- **Three NIST Sanitization Levels:**
  - **Clear** → Logical overwrite (keyboard-level protection)
  - **Purge** → Cryptographic erase / degauss (state-of-the-art lab attack protection)
  - **Destroy** → Physical disintegration (absolute protection)
- **Authoritative Standards:**
  - **NIST SP 800-88 Rev.1** (current global standard)
  - **DoD 5220.22-M** (legacy 3-pass standard, now superseded)
  - **Gutmann Method** (35-pass, historical/legacy)
- **HDD vs. SSD — Critical Distinction:**
  - HDD: Overwriting is reliable
  - SSD: Wear-leveling makes overwriting unreliable → use **ATA Secure Erase** or **Cryptographic Erase**
- **Degaussing Formula:** $H_{\text{applied}} \geq 2 \times H_c$
- **Cryptographic Erase Security:** AES-256 → $2^{255}$ operations required to break → infeasible
- **Single-Overwrite Sufficiency:** Modern PRML/EPRML drives → 1-pass overwrite gives $P_{\text{recovery}} \approx 10^{-12}$
- **Physical Destruction Methods:** Shredding (< 5mm), pulverization, melting, incineration
- **Compliance Domains:** HIPAA, PCI-DSS, GDPR (Article 17 — Right to Erasure), WEEE Directive
- **Common Exam Trap:** Confusing *deletion* (file system pointer removed) with *destruction* (data physically/logically eliminated)
- **Memory Aid:** **"D-C-P-D"** = **D**elete → **C**lear → **P**urge → **D**estroy (escalating sanitization strength)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indian IT Act 2000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT864 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Indian IT Act 2000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Indian IT Act 2000: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Information Technology Act, 2000** (also known as the **IT Act, 2000** or **ITA-2000**) is the primary cyber law of the Republic of India. It was enacted by the Parliament of India on **9 June 2000** and notified on **17 October 2000**. The Act is the brainchild of the **Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)**, Government of India, and it was designed to provide the legal infrastructure for **e-commerce, e-governance, and digital signatures** in India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Citation:** The Information Technology Act, 2000 (Act No. 21 of 2000), published in the Gazette of India (Extraordinary) on 9th June 2000. It came into force on 17 October 2000.

The Act primarily amends the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)**, the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872**, the **Bankers' Books Evidence Act, 1891**, and the **Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934**. The principal object of the Act is to carry out transactions electronically and to facilitate **Electronic Governance** by means of reliable electronic records.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine the year 1999. A gentleman signs a paper cheque and hands it to a shopkeeper. The shopkeeper trusts the signature because he can see the ink, the paper, and the unique flourish of the handwritten name. Now imagine the same gentleman sends an **email** saying, "I will pay you ₹50,000." 

**Question:** Is that legal? Is it a crime if the shopkeeper delivers the goods and the customer denies it?

In 1999, the answer was murky — Indian law did not clearly recognize a digital document as evidence equivalent to a paper document. The IT Act 2000 solved this problem by legally saying:

> *"An electronic record and a digital signature are legally equivalent to a paper document and a handwritten signature."*

In short, the **IT Act 2000 gave legal recognition, validity, and enforceability to digital signatures, electronic records, and electronic contracts** — laying the foundation for India's entire digital economy (UPI, e-signing, Aadhaar e-KYC, online taxation, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Founding Inspiration:** The IT Act 2000 was largely modeled on the **United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL) Model Law on Electronic Commerce (1996)**, making India one of the earliest major economies to adopt a comprehensive digital law framework.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| **Term** | **Meaning in Layperson's Language** |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Electronic Record** | Any information generated, stored, or communicated in electronic form (e.g., an email, a PDF, a database entry). |
| **Digital Signature** | A mathematical scheme used to verify the authenticity of a digital message — equivalent to a physical signature. |
| **Asymmetric Crypto System** | A pair of cryptographic keys (Public Key + Private Key) used to encrypt and decrypt data. |
| **Certifying Authority (CA)** | A trusted third party (like eMudhra, NIC, Sify) that issues Digital Signature Certificates (DSC). |
| **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** | The Government-appointed authority that licenses and supervises all CAs in India. |
| **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** | A quasi-judicial body that hears appeals against orders of the Adjudicating Officer. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Salient Features of the IT Act 2000

The IT Act 2000 can be broadly divided into **13 Chapters** containing **94 Sections** (originally) and **4 Schedules**. The architecture is designed to be a **technology-neutral, facilitative, and protective** legislation.

1. **Legal Recognition of Electronic Records** (Chapter II, Sections 3–10): Grants legal status to electronic communications.
2. **Legal Recognition of Digital Signatures** (Chapter III, Sections 11–14): Defines what constitutes a valid digital signature.
3. **Regulation of Certifying Authorities** (Chapter IV, Sections 17–34): Governs who can issue digital certificates.
4. **Penalties and Adjudication** (Chapter IX, Sections 43–47): Deals with compensation for damages.
5. **Offences and Penalties** (Chapter XI, Sections 65–78): Criminal liability for cyber offences.
6. **Cyber Appellate Tribunal** (Chapter X, Sections 48–64): Appellate mechanism.
7. **Miscellaneous** (Chapter XII & XIII): Interception, blocking, and final provisions.

## 2.2 Historical Timeline of the IT Act 2000

| **Year** | **Event** | **Significance** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1996** | UNCITRAL Model Law on E-Commerce | Inspired India to draft its own law. |
| **1998** | First draft of IT Bill | Department of Electronics prepared initial draft. |
| **1999** | IT Bill introduced in Parliament | On the last day of the 12th Lok Sabha. |
| **9 June 2000** | IT Act receives Presidential assent | Act No. 21 of 2000 enacted. |
| **17 Oct 2000** | IT Act came into force | Major provisions made operational. |
| **2008** | **IT Amendment Act 2008** | Massive overhaul, added Sections 66A, 66B, 66C, 66D, 66E, 66F, 67, 67A, 67B, 69, 69A, 69B, etc. |
| **2009** | Rules notified (IT Rules 2009) | Implementation rules for cyber café, DSCs, etc. |
| **2011** | IT Amendment Act 2011 | Minor amendments to Section 66A and procedural changes. |
| **2015** | Shreya Singhal v. Union of India | Supreme Court **struck down Section 66A** as unconstitutional. |
| **2018** | **Personal Data Protection Bill** introduced (later withdrawn in 2022) | India's GDPR-equivalent. |
| **2023** | **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** | New comprehensive privacy law enacted. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The IT Act 2000 is **NOT static**. Every KTU question will expect you to mention the **2008 Amendment**, as more than 80% of practical cyber offences are prosecuted under the amended sections.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Section Cheat Sheet (Memorize This!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table forms the backbone of nearly every KTU question on the IT Act. Memorize Section numbers, offence names, and punishments.**

| **Section** | **Offence / Provision** | **Punishment** | **Bailability & Cognizability** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sec 43** | Damage to computer, computer system, etc. | Compensation up to **₹1 Crore** to victim | Civil (no criminal court) |
| **Sec 44** | Failure to furnish documents/returns | Fine up to **₹1,500 per day** (max ₹15,000) | Adjudication by Officer |
| **Sec 45** | Failure to assist in decryption | Imprisonment up to **7 years** + fine | Cognizable, Bailable, Non-compoundable |
| **Sec 65** | Tampering with computer source documents | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹2 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable, Triable by Magistrate |
| **Sec 66** | Computer-related offences (hacking with dishonest intent) | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹5 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable, Triable by Magistrate |
| **Sec 66A** | Sending offensive messages through communication service, etc. (struck down in 2015) | **REPEALED by Supreme Court** in *Shreya Singhal* | — |
| **Sec 66B** | Dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource or communication device | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable, Triable by Magistrate |
| **Sec 66C** | **Identity theft** (fraudulently using another's electronic signature, password, etc.) | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 66D** | **Cheating by personation** using communication device/computer | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 66E** | **Voyeurism** (publishing/sending private image of a person without consent) | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, + fine up to **₹2 lakh** | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 66F** | **Cyber Terrorism** | Imprisonment up to **life imprisonment** | Cognizable, Non-bailable, Triable by Court of Session |
| **Sec 67** | Publishing/passing obscene material in electronic form | First offence: 3 years + ₹5 lakh fine; Subsequent: 5 years + ₹10 lakh fine | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 67A** | Publishing sexually explicit material | First offence: 5 years + ₹10 lakh; Subsequent: 7 years + ₹20 lakh | Cognizable, Non-bailable |
| **Sec 67B** | Publishing child pornography in electronic form | First offence: 5 years + ₹10 lakh; Subsequent: 7 years + ₹20 lakh | Cognizable, Non-bailable |
| **Sec 68** | Failure/refusal to comply with orders of Controller | Imprisonment up to **2 years**, or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both | Non-cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 69** | Government power to intercept, monitor, decrypt information | Imprisonment up to **7 years**, + fine | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 69A** | Government power to block public access to websites (IT Rules 2009) | — | Administrative (no direct punishment) |
| **Sec 69B** | Power to monitor and collect traffic data / metadata | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 70** | Unauthorised access to protected system (e.g., defence, government) | Imprisonment up to **10 years**, + fine | Cognizable, Non-bailable |
| **Sec 71** | Misrepresentation to obtain Digital Signature Certificate | Imprisonment up to **2 years**, or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 72** | Breach of confidentiality / privacy by service provider | Imprisonment up to **2 years**, or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 72A** | Disclosure of information in breach of lawful contract | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹5 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 73** | Publishing Digital Signature Certificate with false particulars | Imprisonment up to **2 years**, or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 74** | Publication of Digital Signature Certificate for fraudulent purpose | Imprisonment up to **2 years**, or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable |
| **Sec 75** | Offences committed outside India by any person (Extraterritorial jurisdiction) | As per relevant section | Extraterritorial application |
| **Sec 78** | Power of police officer (Inspector and above) to investigate | Investigation authority | Procedural |
| **Sec 79** | **Safe Harbour / Network Service Provider Exemption** | Conditional immunity to intermediaries | — |
| **Sec 80** | Compounding of offences | Allowed up to Section 61 | Procedural |
| **Sec 81** | No court inferior to that of Metropolitan Magistrate / JMFC shall try offences | — | Procedural |
| **Sec 84** | Power of Central Government to make rules | Rule-making authority | Administrative |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Do NOT confuse **Section 66 (Hacking)** with **Section 66C (Identity Theft)**. Section 66 requires *dishonest or fraudulent intent*, whereas Section 66C is purely about *possession or use* of another person's credentials.

## 2.4 Section 79: The Safe Harbour Doctrine (Intermediary Liability)

**Section 79** is one of the most commercially significant provisions. It grants **conditional immunity** to intermediaries (e.g., WhatsApp, Google, Facebook, Twitter/X, YouTube, ISPs) for third-party content hosted on their platforms.

> **Conditions for Safe Harbour (must satisfy all):**
> 1. The intermediary's role is limited to providing access to a communication system (mere conduit).
> 2. The intermediary does not initiate the transmission.
> 3. The intermediary does not select the receiver of the transmission.
> 4. The intermediary does not select or modify the information contained in the transmission.
> 5. The intermediary observes due diligence and follows Government guidelines (the **IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021**).

> **Loss of Safe Harbour:** If the intermediary fails to remove unlawful content upon receiving **actual knowledge** (e.g., a court order or government notification), it loses immunity and may be held liable for the content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Every web app, e-commerce platform, cloud service, and social media platform operating in India must architect its systems around Section 79 + IT Rules 2021. This is a **direct application** of IT Act knowledge in software engineering jobs.

## 2.5 Adjudicating Officer & Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)

- **Adjudicating Officer (AO):** Appointed by the Central Government, holds enquiry for **Section 43 damages**. Holds rank equivalent to District Judge. Decides civil claims.
- **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT):** Established under **Section 48** of the IT Act 2000 to hear appeals against AO's orders and other statutory authorities.
  - **Composition:** Chairperson + other members.
  - **Powers:** Equivalent to a Civil Court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
  - **Vacancy & Current Status:** The original CAT was **abolished** by the Finance Act 2017, and its jurisdiction was transferred to the **Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)**. However, TDSAT has been handling these appeals since 2017.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Jurisdictional Walkthrough

## 3.1 Working of a Digital Signature: Step-by-Step (Asymmetric Cryptography)

The IT Act 2000 explicitly recognizes the **Asymmetric Crypto System** (Public Key Infrastructure) under **Section 3**. The steps below trace the lifecycle of a digital signature, the exact technical process recognized as legally binding under the Act.

> **Step 1: Key Pair Generation**
> The sender (say, Mr. A) uses an approved cryptographic algorithm (RSA, DSA, or ECC) to generate a **Key Pair** consisting of:
> - **Private Key** (kept secret by Mr. A, stored on his e-token or smart card).
> - **Public Key** (mathematically linked to the private key, distributed openly via a Digital Signature Certificate).

> **Step 2: Hashing the Document**
> The original electronic record (e.g., a contract.pdf) is fed into a **Hash Function** (e.g., SHA-256), which produces a fixed-length **Message Digest** (e.g., 256-bit hash). This is a unique "fingerprint" of the document.

> **Step 3: Signing (Encryption of Hash)**
> Mr. A uses his **Private Key** to encrypt the hash value. The encrypted hash is now called the **Digital Signature**. It is appended to the document and sent to Mr. B.

> **Step 4: Transmission**
> The signed document + Digital Signature are transmitted to Mr. B over the Internet (or any electronic medium).

> **Step 5: Verification (Decryption of Hash)**
> Mr. B uses Mr. A's **Public Key** (from the DSC) to decrypt the signature, retrieving the original hash. Concurrently, Mr. B's software recomputes the hash of the received document using the same hash function.

> **Step 6: Comparison and Result**
> If the **two hashes match** (decrypted hash == recomputed hash), the signature is **VALID**. This proves two things:
> 1. **Authentication:** The document was indeed signed by the holder of the private key (Mr. A).
> 2. **Integrity:** The document was not altered after signing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Effect (Section 3(2) of IT Act 2000):** *“Notwithstanding anything contained in any other law for the time being in force, after the Commencement of this Act, the following shall be deemed to be validly discharged, namely the use of affixing digital signatures with effect from the date and time indicated by the date and time stamp affixed by the system in accordance with the provisions of this Act.”*

## 3.2 Certifying Authority (CA) Hierarchy — Jurisdictional Walkthrough

Under **Sections 17–25** of the IT Act 2000, the digital trust infrastructure is built on a strict 3-tier hierarchy. Let us trace the flow:

**Tier 1: Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**
- Appointed by the **Central Government** under **Section 17**.
- The CCA is the apex regulator. It supervises the activities of all Certifying Authorities, lays down rules, and handles complaints.
- The Office of the CCA is part of the Ministry of Electronics & Information Technology (MeitY).

**Tier 2: Certifying Authorities (CAs)**
- Licensed by the CCA under **Section 21**.
- Examples in India: **eMudhra, Sify Technologies, (n)Code Solutions, National Informatics Centre (NIC)**.
- Each CA must maintain a **Repository of Digital Signature Certificates** containing all DSCs issued and revoked by it (**Section 20**).
- CAs are required to use hardware/software that meets **Information Technology Security Guidelines** issued by the Department of IT.

**Tier 3: Subscribers (End Users)**
- Any person who purchases/uses a Digital Signature Certificate from a CA.
- Each DSC contains: Subscriber's name, Public Key, Validity period, Name of issuing CA, Signature of CA, Serial number, and Usage limits.

> **Flow of Trust:**
> Subscriber trusts the CA → CA trusts the CCA → CCA is appointed by the Government → Government derives its authority from the IT Act 2000 → IT Act derives its authority from the Constitution of India.

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 24** mandates a **Security Guideline Compliance Audit** — any CA can be audited by the CCA at any time.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix of Amendments and Landmark Judgments

| **Aspect** | **Original IT Act 2000** | **After 2008 Amendment** | **After Shreya Singhal (2015)** | **After 2017 (Finance Act)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope of Cyber Offences** | Limited (only Sections 65, 66) | Expanded massively (66A–66F, 67A, 67B, 69A, 69B) | Section 66A struck down (free speech violation) | New offences and changes to penalties |
| **Cyber Terrorism** | Not defined | Introduced (Section 66F) | Upheld | Still in force |
| **Powers of Police** | Basic | Strengthened (Sec 78) | Upheld | Upheld |
| **Adjudicating Officer** | Adjudicates only Sec 43 | AO enhanced powers | Upheld | Upheld |
| **Cyber Appellate Tribunal** | Operational | Operational | Operated until 2017 | Abolished, jurisdiction to TDSAT |
| **Intermediary Liability** | Mentioned briefly | Detailed in Section 79 | Interpretation refined | IT Rules 2021 introduced Grievance Officers, Traceability |
| **Data Protection** | No specific provision | Sections 43A, 72A introduced | Upheld | New DPDP Act 2023 |

## 3.4 Worked Example: How Section 66F (Cyber Terrorism) is Invoked

> **Scenario:** A hacker from a hostile nation-state breaches the Indian Railways reservation system and exfiltrates 100 million passenger records, demanding ransom.

> **Step 1:** Identify the **computer system** — Indian Railways reservation server (a "critical information infrastructure" under **Section 70**).
> **Step 2:** Identify the **intent** — terrorizing the population, destabilizing the nation (e.g., threatening to release sensitive data and disrupt train scheduling).
> **Step 3:** Map the act to the section: Section 66F(1) — *"Whoever commits cyber terrorism shall be punishable with imprisonment which may extend to imprisonment for life."*
> **Step 4:** Trial by **Special Court** under **Section 78**.
> **Step 5:** The accused is also liable under the **Indian Penal Code (now Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023)** for related offences like criminal intimidation, extortion, and dismembering national security.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "imprisonment up to 14 years" for Section 66F. This is WRONG. The punishment is **"imprisonment which may extend to life imprisonment"** — meaning there is NO upper limit; the court can award a life term. Some references also mention the death penalty for extreme cases (since the 2008 Amendment aligned it with the UAPA framework).

## 4.2 Digital Signature Workflow (Cryptographic Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sender["SENDER Mr. A"]
    PrivKey["PRIVATE KEY<br/>Kept Secret"]
    HashFunc["HASH FUNCTION<br/>SHA 256"]

    HashVal["MESSAGE DIGEST<br/>256 bit hash"]
    Encrypt["ENCRYPT HASH<br/>with Private Key"]
    Sig["DIGITAL SIGNATURE<br/>Encrypted Hash"]
    Bundle["SIGNED DOCUMENT<br/>Doc plus Signature"]
    Net["INTERNET TRANSMISSION"]
    Receiver["RECEIVER Mr. B"]
    PubKey["PUBLIC KEY<br/>from DSC"]
    Decrypt["DECRYPT SIGNATURE<br/>with Public Key"]
    HashVal2["RECEIVED HASH<br/>Extracted"]
    HashFunc2["HASH FUNCTION<br/>SHA 256 on Received Doc"]
    HashVal3["RECOMPUTED HASH<br/>of Received Doc"]
    Compare{"HASH<br/>MATCH?"}
    Valid["VALID SIGNATURE<br/>Authenticated and Intact"]

    Encrypt --> Sig

    Sender --> Bundle

    Net --> Receiver
    Receiver --> Decrypt
    PubKey --> Decrypt
    Decrypt --> HashVal2
    Bundle --> HashFunc2
    HashFunc2 --> HashVal3
    HashVal2 --> Compare
    HashVal3 --> Compare
    Compare --> Valid
    Compare --> Invalid
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** *Define the term "Digital Signature" as per the Information Technology Act,2000. Mention the section that provides its legal recognition.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> As per **Section 2(1)(p)** of the IT Act 2000, a **Digital Signature** means authentication of any electronic record by a subscriber by means of an electronic method or procedure in accordance with the provisions of the Act.
> Legal recognition of digital signatures is provided under **Section 3**, which states that any subscriber may authenticate any electronic record by such digital signature. **Section 5** further grants that legal recognition to digital signatures, and **Section 6** prescribes the conditions under which a digital signature shall be deemed to be a secure digital signature.
> *(For full marks, mention Section 3, Section 5, and the Asymmetric Crypto System link.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** *What is a Certifying Authority (CA)? Name the authority that regulates CAs in India.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Certifying Authority (CA)** is a person who has been granted a license to issue **Digital Signature Certificates (DSCs)** under **Section 21** of the IT Act 2000. Examples include eMudhra, NIC, and Sify Technologies.
> The authority that regulates CAs is the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**, appointed by the **Central Government** under **Section 17** of the Act. The CCA's office functions under the **Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)** and is responsible for supervising the activities of all CAs, handling complaints, and ensuring compliance with the Information Technology Security Guidelines.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, ESE Pattern]**

### ⭐ OPTION A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Q.A.(a)** Explain the salient features of the Information Technology Act,2000. Discuss the legal recognition of electronic records and digital signatures under the Act. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A.(b)** Discuss in detail the various penalties and offences defined under the IT Act 2000 (as amended in 2008), with reference to Sections 43, 65, 66, 66C, 66D, 66E, 66F, 67, and 67B. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ Model Solution for Q.A.(a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Information Technology Act, 2000 (Act No. 21 of 2000) is India's pioneering cyber law, enacted on 9 June 2000 and enforced on 17 October 2000. It was primarily designed to provide legal recognition to e-commerce, e-governance, and digital signatures.

**Salient Features (3 marks):**
1. **Legal Recognition:** It grants legal validity to electronic records, digital signatures, and electronic contracts, amending the Indian Evidence Act 1872, IPC 1860, Bankers' Books Evidence Act 1891, and RBI Act 1934.
2. **Regulatory Framework:** Establishes the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** to license and supervise Certifying Authorities (CAs) that issue Digital Signature Certificates.
3. **Technology-Neutral:** Unlike many country-specific laws, the IT Act uses the generic term "electronic record" rather than specifying technologies, making it future-proof.
4. **Facilitative Approach:** Focuses on enabling e-commerce and e-governance rather than restricting digital activities.
5. **Extra-Territorial Jurisdiction (Section 75):** Applies to any offence or contravention committed outside India by any person, irrespective of nationality.
6. **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT):** Provides an appellate forum for redressal of grievances and appeals against orders of the Adjudicating Officer.

**Legal Recognition of Electronic Records (2 marks):**
- **Section 3:** Any subscriber may authenticate any electronic record by digital signature.
- **Section 4:** Where any law requires information to be in writing or typed, the same requirement is satisfied if the information is rendered or made available in an electronic form and is accessible for subsequent reference.
- **Section 7:** Subscriber's duty of care — protecting private keys, taking reasonable precautions.
- **Section 8:** Deemed acknowledgement — receipt of electronic records through designated systems.
- **Section 9:** Presumption that any electronic record is genuine if it is accompanied by a valid digital signature and proper security procedures were followed.

**Legal Recognition of Digital Signatures (1 mark):**
- **Section 5:** Legal recognition of digital signatures.
- **Section 6:** Conditions under which a digital signature is deemed **secure** — using an asymmetric crypto system + hash function + prescribed security procedure.

---

#### ⭐ Model Solution for Q.A.(b):

**Section 43 — Penalty for Damage to Computer System (1 mark):**
Whoever accesses, downloads, introduces viruses, damages, disrupts, denies access, tampers with, or manipulates any computer resource without permission is liable to pay compensation not exceeding **₹1 Crore** to the affected party. This is **civil in nature**, adjudicated by the **Adjudicating Officer**, not a criminal court.

**Section 65 — Tampering with Computer Source Documents (1 mark):**
Whoever knowingly or intentionally conceals, destroys, alters, or causes another to do so with any computer source code, network, computer database, or programme is punishable with imprisonment up to **3 years**, or a fine up to **₹2 lakh**, or both. The offence is **cognizable and bailable**.

**Section 66 — Computer-Related Offences (Hacking) (1 mark):**
Whoever, with dishonest or fraudulent intent, commits hacking (any of the acts in Section 43) is punishable with imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹5 lakh**, or both. Section 66 is essentially the **criminal version** of Section 43.

**Section 66C — Identity Theft (1 mark):**
Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses the electronic signature, password, or any other unique identification feature of any other person is guilty of identity theft. Punishment: imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh**. Example: Using someone else's Aadhaar or PAN credentials to open a bank account.

**Section 66D — Cheating by Personation (1 mark):**
Whoever cheats by personation using a communication device or computer resource is liable. Punishment: imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh**. Example: Posing as a bank official on a video call to extract OTPs.

**Section 66E — Voyeurism (0.5 mark):**
Publishing or transmitting images of a private area of any person without consent is punishable with imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹2 lakh**. Introduced in 2013 to address the rising "MMS scandals."

**Section 66F — Cyber Terrorism (0.5 mark):**
Whoever commits cyber terrorism (denial of access, unauthorised access to critical infrastructure with intent to threaten the unity, integrity, security, or sovereignty of India) is punishable with imprisonment **up to life**. This is the most severe section in the IT Act.

**Section 67 — Publishing Obscene Material (1 mark):**
Whoever publishes, transmits, or causes to be transmitted obscene material in electronic form is punishable with first offence: 3 years + ₹5 lakh fine; subsequent: 5 years + ₹10 lakh fine.

**Section 67B — Child Pornography (1 mark):**
Whoever publishes, transmits, or stores child pornography in electronic form is punishable with first offence: 5 years + ₹10 lakh fine; subsequent: 7 years + ₹20 lakh fine. This is **non-bailable** and considered a serious offence.

**Conclusion:** The 2008 Amendment transformed the IT Act from a simple regulatory law into a comprehensive criminal code for cyberspace, addressing modern concerns like cyber terrorism, voyeurism, identity theft, and child exploitation.

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q.B.(a)** Explain the role and powers of the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** and the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** under the IT Act 2000. Discuss the impact of the **Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)** judgment. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B.(b)** Explain the concept of **"Safe Harbour"** under Section 79 of the IT Act 2000. Discuss the obligations of intermediaries under the **Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ Model Solution for Q.B.(a):

**Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA) — Roles and Powers (3 marks):**

The CCA is appointed by the **Central Government** under **Section 17** of the IT Act 2000 and functions under the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology. The CCA serves as the apex regulator of the entire Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) ecosystem in India.

**Functions of CCA:**
1. **Licensing Certifying Authorities (Section 21):** The CCA grants licenses to eligible entities to operate as CAs. The license specifies the digital signature operations they may perform.
2. **Supervision (Section 18):** The CCA exercises supervision over the activities of CAs to ensure compliance with the Act and the Information Technology Security Guidelines.
3. **Certification Practice Statement (Section 34):** CAs must publish their practice statements; the CCA reviews them.
4. **Public Key Repository (Section 20):** The CCA maintains a database of public keys for verification purposes.
5. **Investigation of Contraventions (Section 19):** The CCA may investigate any contravention of the Act by any person.
6. **Recommend Security Guidelines:** The CCA recommends Information Technology Security Guidelines to the Central Government.
7. **Adjudication of Disputes:** The CCA adjudicates disputes between CAs and subscribers.

**Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT) — Structure and Powers (2.5 marks):**

The CAT was originally established under **Section 48** of the IT Act 2000 to provide an appellate mechanism against orders of the Adjudicating Officer.

**Composition (Section 49):**
- **Chairperson:** A person who is, or has been, a **Judge of a High Court**.
- **Members (optional):** Persons of ability, integrity, and standing with special knowledge of, and experience in, IT, telecommunication, IPR, etc.

**Powers of CAT (Section 58):**
- Powers of a **Civil Court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, including power to summon, examine witnesses, require discovery and production of documents, and receive evidence on affidavit.
- Subject to the **Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973** (now Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023) for offences punishable with fine exceeding ₹1 lakh.

**Jurisdiction:**
- Appeals against any order of the **Adjudicating Officer**.
- Appeals against orders of the **Controller of Certifying Authorities**.
- Appeals against any order of the **Central Government or State Government** under the Act.

**Status after 2017 (Finance Act):**
The Cyber Appellate Tribunal was **abolished** by the **Finance Act, 2017**, and its powers were transferred to the **Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)**.

**Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) — Impact (1.5 marks):**

The Supreme Court of India, in this landmark judgment, **struck down Section 66A** of the IT Act (as amended in 2008) as unconstitutional and void, ruling that it violated **Article 19(1)(a)** (Freedom of Speech and Expression) and **Article 14** (Right to Equality) of the Constitution.

**Key observations by the Court:**
- Section 66A was **vague and overbroad** — words like "annoying," "inconvenient," "grossly offensive" did not have clearly defined meanings, leading to arbitrary application.
- The Court emphasized that the **"chilling effect"** on free speech was sufficient ground to strike down a law, even if most prosecutions under it were legitimate.
- The Court clarified the concept of **"actual knowledge"** for intermediaries, leading to the **Safeguards Rule 2021** which requires intermediaries to act on court orders or government notifications, not mere user complaints, to lose safe harbour.
- The judgment saved the rest of the IT Act 2000 from being struck down by applying the **doctrine of severability**.

---

#### ⭐ Model Solution for Q.B.(b):

**Concept of Safe Harbour (3.5 marks):**

**Section 79** of the IT Act 2000 (as amended in 2008) provides **conditional immunity** ("safe harbour") to **Network Service Providers (NSPs)** or **intermediaries** for third-party content hosted, transmitted, or stored on their platforms. The intent is to encourage the growth of e-commerce and digital platforms without making them strictly liable for everything their users do.

**Definition of "Intermediary" (Section 2(1)(w) — inserted in 2008 Amendment):**
> "Intermediary means any person who on behalf of another person receives, stores, or transmits that message or provides any service with respect to that message."

Examples: Telecom operators, ISPs, search engines, online marketplaces, social media platforms, cloud storage providers, payment gateways.

**Conditions for Safe Harbour (Section 79(1) and (2)):**
1. The intermediary's role is limited to providing access to a communication system over which third-party information is transmitted or temporarily stored.
2. The intermediary does not initiate the transmission, select the receiver, or select/modify the information.
3. The intermediary acts within the time prescribed by the regulations.
4. The intermediary **observes due diligence** and follows the guidelines prescribed by the Central Government.

**Loss of Safe Harbour (Section 79(3)):**
If the intermediary, upon receiving **actual knowledge** (i.e., a court order or government notification under Section 69) or upon being notified by an affected person, fails to expeditiously remove or disable access to the unlawful content, it loses its immunity and can be held liable for that content.

**IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 (3.5 marks):**

Notified on **25 February 2021**, these rules replaced the earlier 2011 Intermediary Rules and added substantial obligations:

**Grievance Redressal Mechanism (Rule 3(2)):**
- Every intermediary must appoint a **Grievance Officer** (with contact details published on the website/app).
- Complaints must be acknowledged within **24 hours** and resolved within **15 days**.
- The Grievance Officer must be a senior employee residing in India.

**Content Removal (Rule 3(1)(d)):**
- Content must be removed within **36 hours** of receiving a court order or government notification.
- For non-consensual intimate imagery: removal within **24 hours** of complaint.

**Traceability of Originators (Rule 4(2)):**
- Significant Social Media Intermediaries (SSMIs) with **50 lakh+ registered users** in India must enable **traceability of the first originator** of messages, but only in cases related to:
  - Sovereignty, integrity, or security of India.
  - Public order.
  - Rape or sexually explicit content.
- This is the **"first originator"** requirement that has been contested on encryption grounds.

**Categories of Intermediaries under the 2021 Rules:**
1. **Intermediary** (general)
2. **Significant Social Media Intermediary (SSMI)** (≥50 lakh users)
3. **Online Curated Content Providers (OCCPs)** — e.g., Netflix, Amazon Prime

**Additional Obligations for SSMIs:**
- Appoint a **Chief Compliance Officer**, **Nodal Contact Person**, and **Resident Grievance Officer**, all Indian residents.
- Enable **identity verification** of users through voluntary mechanisms (like KYC-based verification).
- Publish **monthly compliance reports**.

**Digital Media Ethics Code:**
- A three-tier grievance redressal structure for news media, OTT platforms, and digital content.
- Self-regulatory bodies → Inter-Departmental Committee → Oversight by the Ministry of I&B.

**Criticisms:**
- Mandatory traceability conflicts with end-to-end encryption (used by WhatsApp, Signal).
- Potential for **government overreach** without judicial oversight.
- Compliance burden on smaller startups.

**Conclusion:** Section 79, combined with the 2021 Rules, creates a balance between **platform liability** and **freedom of expression**, while imposing robust obligations on intermediaries to maintain order in the Indian cyberspace.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT write "imprisonment up to 14 years" for Section 66F.** It is **"imprisonment which may extend to life."**
> 2. **Do NOT confuse Section 66 with Section 66C/D/E.** They are different specific offences. Section 66 is the general "hacking" clause.
> 3. **Do NOT say CAT is "currently active."** It was **abolished in 2017** by the Finance Act; jurisdiction was transferred to **TDSAT**.
> 4. **Do NOT forget to mention the 2008 Amendment** — most offences are from the amended version.
> 5. **Do NOT use Section 66A in answers** — it has been **struck down** by the Supreme Court.
> 6. **Always cite Section numbers** in your answer. KTU examiners reward precision.
> 7. **Section 43 is CIVIL, not criminal.** It is adjudicated by an officer, not a Magistrate. Many students incorrectly call it "criminal prosecution."

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your final 1-night revision before the KTU exam.**

- ⭐ **Full Title:** Information Technology Act, 2000 (Act No. 21 of 2000).
- ⭐ **Enacted:** 9 June 2000 | **Enforced:** 17 October 2000.
- ⭐ **Primary Ministry:** Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY).
- ⭐ **Total Chapters (Original):** 13 | **Total Sections (Original):** 94 | **Schedules:** 4.
- ⭐ **Major Amendment:** IT Amendment Act 2008 — added Sections 66A–66F, 67A, 67B, 69A, 69B, 70, 72A, 79.
- ⭐ **Section 3:** Legal recognition of electronic records via digital signature.
- ⭐ **Section 4:** Electronic records are valid substitutes for paper documents.
- ⭐ **Section 5:** Legal recognition of digital signatures.
- ⭐ **Section 6:** Conditions for a **secure digital signature** (asymmetric crypto + hash + secure procedure).
- ⭐ **Section 17:** Establishes the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**.
- ⭐ **Section 21:** CAs must obtain a license from the CCA to issue DSCs.
- ⭐ **Section 43:** Civil liability — compensation up to **₹1 Crore** for damage to computer systems.
- ⭐ **Section 65:** Tampering with computer source documents — 3 years / ₹2 lakh.
- ⭐ **Section 66:** Computer-related offences (hacking) with dishonest intent — 3 years / ₹5 lakh.
- ⭐ **Section 66A:** **REPEALED** by Supreme Court in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015).
- ⭐ **Section 66B:** Dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource — 3 years / ₹1 lakh.
- ⭐ **Section 66C:** Identity theft — 3 years / ₹1 lakh.
- ⭐ **Section 66D:** Cheating by personation using computer — 3 years / ₹1 lakh.
- ⭐ **Section 66E:** Voyeurism — 3 years / ₹2 lakh.
- ⭐ **Section 66F:** **Cyber Terrorism** — life imprisonment (most severe).
- ⭐ **Section 67:** Obscene material in electronic form — 3–5 years / ₹5–10 lakh.
- ⭐ **Section 67A:** Sexually explicit material — 5–7 years / ₹10–20 lakh (non-bailable).
- ⭐ **Section 67B:** Child pornography — 5–7 years / ₹10–20 lakh (non-bailable).
- ⭐ **Section 70:** Unauthorised access to **protected systems** (e.g., defence, government) — 10 years.
- ⭐ **Section 72:** Breach of confidentiality by service provider — 2 years / ₹1 lakh.
- ⭐ **Section 75:** **Extra-territorial jurisdiction** — applies to offences committed outside India.
- ⭐ **Section 78:** Investigation by an officer not below the rank of **Inspector of Police**.
- ⭐ **Section 79:** **Safe Harbour** for intermediaries, conditional upon following due diligence.
- ⭐ **Section 84:** Power of the Central Government to frame rules.
- ⭐ **CAT (Cyber Appellate Tribunal):** Abolished by **Finance Act 2017**; jurisdiction now with **TDSAT**.
- ⭐ **Digital Signature:** Uses **Asymmetric Crypto System** + **Hash Function** (SHA-256, etc.).
- ⭐ **Hash Function:** Maps any input to a fixed-length output (e.g., 256 bits); used for integrity verification.
- ⭐ **Private Key:** Kept secret, used to sign; **Public Key:** Distributed openly, used to verify.
- ⭐ **DSC (Digital Signature Certificate):** Contains subscriber's name, public key, validity, CA's signature, serial number.
- ⭐ **Licensed CAs in India:** eMudhra, NIC, Sify Technologies, (n)Code Solutions, CDAC.
- ⭐ **IT Rules 2021:** Imposed 36-hour content removal, Grievance Officer, Chief Compliance Officer, traceability for SSMIs.
- ⭐ **Adjudicating Officer:** Decides **civil** claims under Section 43, not criminal prosecutions.
- ⭐ **Special Court:** Designated by Central/State Government for trial of offences under the Act.
- ⭐ **Critical Information Infrastructure:** Protected under **Section 70** — sectors include banking, telecom, power, defence.
- ⭐ **PENALTY RECALL:** Section 43 = Civil; Section 66 = Criminal (3 yrs); Section 66F = Life Imprisonment; Section 70 = 10 Years.
- ⭐ **Important Case Law:** Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) — struck down Section 66A; *MC Mehta v. Union of India* (2010) — authorized IT Act for CII.

> **🎯 One-line Memory Aid:** *"The IT Act 2000 = Three Pillars = Legal Recognition of e-Documents (S.3-10) + Certification Authority Framework (S.17-34) + Cyber Offences and Penalties (S.43-78)."*
